topics.title: Signals and Signal Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signals and Signal Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

A **signal** is any physical quantity that varies with time, space, or any other independent variable, and conveys information about the behavior or state of a physical system. Mathematically, a signal is a function of one or more independent variables. In the context of biomedical engineering, a signal is a measurable physiological activity that represents the structural or functional state of a living system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** A signal $x(t)$ is a function that maps an independent variable (typically time $t$ or a spatial coordinate) to a dependent variable (amplitude). It carries information about the phenomenon being observed.

A **biomedical signal** is a specific class of signals that originate from living organisms. These signals are recorded as time-varying voltages, currents, pressures, sounds, or other physical parameters that reflect the activity of biological systems.

## 1.2 What is Signal Processing?

**Signal processing** is the discipline of analyzing, modifying, and synthesizing signals to extract useful information, enhance the quality of the signal, or to transform it into a form suitable for transmission, storage, or further analysis. In biomedical engineering, signal processing is applied to physiological signals to remove noise, detect features, classify conditions, and aid in clinical diagnosis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Biomedical Signal Processing is a subdomain of signal processing that focuses on signals of biological origin, with the central objective of extracting clinically relevant information for diagnostic, therapeutic, and monitoring purposes.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a doctor with a **stethoscope** placed on a patient's chest. The stethoscope picks up the heartbeat sound — a tiny pressure wave traveling through the air, which represents the mechanical action of the heart. This sound wave is a *signal* (pressure varying with time). However, the raw sound is weak, contains noise (breathing, environment, bowel sounds), and needs to be *processed* to be useful. You "process" the signal mentally: your brain filters out the noise, focuses on the lub-dub rhythm, counts the beats per minute, and decides if it's normal or abnormal.

In the electronic/engineering world, this entire chain is automated: a microphone (transducer) converts the sound into an electrical signal $\rightarrow$ an **amplifier** boosts it $\rightarrow$ **filters** remove noise $\rightarrow$ a **digitizer** samples it $\rightarrow$ a **processor (microcontroller/DSP)** analyzes it $\rightarrow$ the result (e.g., heart rate = 72 bpm) is displayed.

| Term | Real-World (Doctor) | Engineering Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Heart sound | Original physical phenomenon | Information source |
| Sound wave through air | Pressure varying with time | Continuous-time signal |
| Stethoscope | Sound collector | Transducer / Sensor |
| Your ear nerves | Conversion to electrical pulses | ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) |
| Your brain | Pattern recognition | DSP algorithm / Processor |
| Diagnosis | Decision-making | Classification / Decision block |

## 1.4 Need for Signal Processing in Biomedical Applications

Biomedical signals are inherently contaminated with noise, artifacts, and interference. Some critical reasons why signal processing is indispensable:

- **Noise Removal:** Powerline interference (50 Hz in India), baseline wander, motion artifacts.
- **Feature Extraction:** Identifying R-peaks in ECG, sleep spindles in EEG, motor unit action potentials in EMG.
- **Data Compression:** Reducing storage requirements for long-duration ECG/EEG recordings.
- **Pattern Recognition:** Classifying heart rhythms (normal, AF, VT, VF).
- **Telemetry & Remote Monitoring:** Enabling real-time transmission over networks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physiological Sampling Frequencies (Clinical Practice):**
> - **ECG (Electrocardiogram):** $f_s = \mathbf{500\ Hz}$ (bandwidth $\approx 0.05$ to $150$ Hz)
> - **EEG (Electroencephalogram):** $f_s = \mathbf{256}$ to $\mathbf{1000\ Hz}$ (bandwidth $\approx 0.5$ to $100$ Hz)
> - **EMG (Electromyogram):** $f_s = \mathbf{1000}$ to $\mathbf{2000\ Hz}$ (bandwidth $\approx 20$ to $500$ Hz)
> - **PCG (Phonocardiogram):** $f_s = \mathbf{1000\ Hz}$ (bandwidth $\approx 20$ to $2000$ Hz)
> - **PPG (Photoplethysmogram):** $f_s = \mathbf{100}$ to $\mathbf{500\ Hz}$

These values are vital — under-sampling causes **aliasing** (loss of clinical information), which is a punishable conceptual error in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Signals

Signals can be classified along multiple orthogonal axes. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects a thorough command of these classifications.

### A. Based on the Nature of the Independent Variable

**1. Continuous-Time Signal:** Defined for every value of time $t$ in a continuous interval. The amplitude may also be continuous.
- Mathematically: $x(t)$ is defined $\forall\ t \in \mathbb{R}$ (or a continuous subset).
- Example: The actual electrical voltage output of an ECG electrode.

**2. Discrete-Time Signal:** Defined only at specific, discrete instants of time. Usually obtained by sampling a continuous-time signal at uniform intervals $T_s$.
- Mathematically: $x[n] = x(nT_s)$, where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- Example: The digitized ECG stored in a computer file.

**3. Analog Signal:** Both the independent variable (time) and the dependent variable (amplitude) are continuous.
- Example: Output of a microphone, an unfiltered ECG waveform.

**4. Digital Signal:** Both the independent variable (time) and the amplitude are discrete. Amplitude is quantized to a finite set of levels.
- Example: A 12-bit digitized ECG sampled at 500 Hz stored in memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinction to Remember:** All analog signals are continuous-time, but NOT all continuous-time signals are analog. A continuous-time signal with discrete amplitudes (e.g., a square wave with levels $\pm 1$) is continuous-time but not analog. Similarly, a discrete-time signal with continuous amplitude is possible (e.g., $x[n] = \sin(0.1n)$ — time is discrete, amplitude is continuous).

### B. Based on Determinism

**1. Deterministic Signal:** Can be described by a known mathematical expression for all time. The future values are predictable.
- Example: $x(t) = 5\cos(2\pi 50 t)$ — the 50 Hz powerline interference.

**2. Random Signal (Stochastic Signal):** Cannot be described by an explicit mathematical function; described using probability distributions and statistics.
- Example: EEG background activity, EMG noise.

### C. Based on Repetitiveness

**1. Periodic Signal:** Repeats itself after a fixed period $T$ (continuous-time) or $N$ (discrete-time).

$$x(t) = x(t + T)\quad \text{for all } t$$

$$x[n] = x[n + N]\quad \text{for all } n$$

The smallest positive $T$ (or $N$) is called the **fundamental period**.

- Example: A clean sinusoidal ECG baseline (synthetic); normal sinus rhythm (quasi-periodic).

**2. Aperiodic (Non-Periodic) Signal:** Does not repeat with any finite period.
- Example: A single QRS complex in an ECG; a single evoked response.

### D. Based on Energy and Power

This is a critical classification for KTU exams. For a continuous-time signal $x(t)$:

**Total Energy:**

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$$

**Average Power:**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$$

For discrete-time $x[n]$:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$$

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N + 1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$$

**Classification Rule:**

- **Energy Signal:** $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$.
- **Power Signal:** $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$.
- Neither energy nor power signal: If both $E$ and $P$ are infinite (e.g., $x(t) = t$).

> [!TIP]
> **Key trick for periodic signals:** A periodic signal is always a power signal, never an energy signal (since it extends forever, its energy is infinite). Compute its power using only one period:

$$P = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$$

### E. Based on Symmetry

**1. Even Signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$. Example: $x(t) = \cos(\omega t)$.

**2. Odd Signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$. Example: $x(t) = \sin(\omega t)$.

**Decomposition Theorem:** Any signal can be written as a sum of its even and odd parts:

$$x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$$

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

## 2.2 Elementary Standard Signals

These are the building blocks used to construct and represent any biomedical signal.

| Signal | Definition (Continuous) | Definition (Discrete) | Sketch |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit Step** $u(t)$ | $1$ for $t \geq 0$, $0$ for $t < 0$ | $u[n] = 1$ for $n \geq 0$, $0$ for $n < 0$ | Jump from 0 to 1 at $t=0$ |
| **Unit Impulse** $\delta(t)$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t)dt = 1$, $\delta(t) = 0$ for $t \neq 0$ | $\delta[n] = 1$ for $n=0$, $0$ otherwise | Spike of unit area at $t=0$ |
| **Unit Ramp** $r(t)$ | $t$ for $t \geq 0$, $0$ for $t < 0$ | $r[n] = n$ for $n \geq 0$, $0$ for $n < 0$ | Linear rise from origin |
| **Exponential** | $x(t) = e^{at} u(t)$ | $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ | Growth ($a>0$) or decay ($a<0$) |
| **Sinusoidal** | $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | $x[n] = A\cos(\Omega n + \phi)$ | Oscillatory, periodic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sampling Property of Impulse:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\delta(t - t_0)\, dt = x(t_0)$. This is used to "pick out" a single value of a signal — the foundation for the **sampling theorem**.

## 2.3 Basic Signal Operations

1. **Time Shifting:** $x(t - t_0)$ shifts the signal right by $t_0$ (delay if $t_0 > 0$, advance if $t_0 < 0$).
2. **Time Scaling:** $x(at)$ compresses the signal by factor $a$ (if $a > 1$), expands if $0 < a < 1$. Time reversal: $x(-t)$.
3. **Amplitude Scaling:** $A \cdot x(t)$ multiplies the amplitude.
4. **Addition:** $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$.
5. **Multiplication:** $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ (modulation).

## 2.4 Biomedical Signal Characteristics

Biomedical signals have unique features that distinguish them from other engineering signals:

- **Low amplitude and low frequency:** Most bioelectric signals are in the $\mu V$ to $mV$ range and below 10 kHz.
- **Non-stationarity:** Their statistical properties (mean, variance) change with time — a single ECG cycle differs from the next in subtle ways.
- **High susceptibility to noise:** Powerline interference (50 Hz in India), electrode contact noise, motion artifacts.
- **Subject variability:** Same signal varies from person to person and even beat to beat.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula set** for Module 1. Commit every entry to memory before the exam.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy of CT signal | $E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | Joules (normalized) |
| 2 | Power of CT signal | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | Watts (normalized) |
| 3 | Energy of DT signal | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Finite for energy signal |
| 4 | Power of DT signal | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$ | Finite for power signal |
| 5 | Power of periodic CT signal | $P = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$ | Use one period only |
| 6 | Power of sinusoidal $A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ | $P = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$ | Independent of $\omega, \phi$ |
| 7 | Power of complex exponential $Ae^{j\omega t}$ | $P = \vert A \vert^{2}$ | Magnitude squared |
| 8 | Even part | $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | Always even |
| 9 | Odd part | $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | Always odd |
| 10 | Energy of $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$, $a>0$ | $E = \frac{1}{2a}$ | Energy signal |
| 11 | Power of $x(t) = A$ (DC signal) | $P = A^{2}$ | Power signal |
| 12 | Power of $x(t) = \cos(t)$ | $P = \frac{1}{2}$ | Power signal |
| 13 | Impulse area | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1$ | Sifting property |
| 14 | Sampling frequency (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | To avoid aliasing |
| 15 | Sampling period | $T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Time between samples |

## 2.6 Why This Matters in Engineering

These classifications and formulas are not abstract — they have direct engineering consequences:

- A **periodic** ECG indicates arrhythmia; an **aperiodic** artifact may indicate noise — classification is the first step of any diagnostic algorithm.
- The **energy/power** distinction determines whether you use a power spectral density (PSD) plot or an energy spectral density plot for analysis.
- The **even/odd decomposition** simplifies Fourier transform computations (the sine series uses odd extensions, cosine series uses even).
- **Standard sampling rates** must be applied correctly; an under-sampled ECG could be misread as AF, leading to misdiagnosis — a real-world safety concern.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy of a Decaying Exponential

**Problem:** Find the energy of $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ for $a > 0$ and determine its classification.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The signal is defined as:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} e^{-at} & t \geq 0 \\ 0 & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

By definition of total energy for a continuous-time signal:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$$

Substitute $x(t)$:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert e^{-at}u(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$$

The unit step $u(t)$ is zero for $t < 0$ and one for $t \geq 0$, so the integration limits reduce:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{0}^{T} e^{-2at}\, dt$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left[ \frac{e^{-2at}}{-2a} \right]_{0}^{T}$$

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( \frac{e^{-2aT}}{-2a} - \frac{e^{0}}{-2a} \right)$$

For $a > 0$, $e^{-2aT} \to 0$ as $T \to \infty$:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{2a}\right) \right) = \frac{1}{2a}$$

Since $E = \frac{1}{2a}$ is finite and positive (for $a > 0$), and consequently $P = 0$, the signal is a **pure energy signal**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical "energy signal" example. Most exam problems on energy classification use this exact form. If $a < 0$, the signal grows unbounded and is neither an energy nor a power signal.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power of a Sinusoidal Signal

**Problem:** Compute the average power of $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ and prove it is a power signal.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The fundamental period is $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$.

For a periodic signal, average power is computed over one period:

$$P = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} A^{2} \cos^{2}(\omega t + \phi)\, dt$$

Use the trigonometric identity:

$$\cos^{2}(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$$

Substitute:

$$P = \frac{A^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega t + 2\phi)}{2}\, dt$$

$$P = \frac{A^{2}}{2T} \int_{0}^{T} \bigl[ 1 + \cos(2\omega t + 2\phi) \bigr]\, dt$$

$$P = \frac{A^{2}}{2T} \left[ t + \frac{\sin(2\omega t + 2\phi)}{2\omega} \right]_{0}^{T}$$

For $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$, the sine term at both limits equals $\sin(2\phi) - \sin(2\phi) = 0$ (since $2\omega T = 4\pi$, the sine of integer multiples of $2\pi$ added to $2\phi$ equals $\sin(2\phi)$).

Therefore:

$$P = \frac{A^{2}}{2T} \cdot T = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$$

The energy over infinite duration is:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt \to \infty$$

So the signal is a **power signal** with average power $P = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Even-Odd Decomposition

**Problem:** Decompose $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ into its even and odd parts.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

By the decomposition theorem:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \quad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

Compute $x(-t) = e^{at}u(-t)$. So:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{e^{-at}u(t) + e^{at}u(-t)}{2}$$

$$x_o(t) = \frac{e^{-at}u(t) - e^{at}u(-t)}{2}$$

Substituting the definitions of $u(t)$ and $u(-t)$:

$$x_e(t) = \begin{cases} \frac{e^{-at}}{2} & t > 0 \\ \frac{1}{2} & t = 0 \\ \frac{e^{at}}{2} & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

$$x_o(t) = \begin{cases} \frac{e^{-at}}{2} & t > 0 \\ 0 & t = 0 \\ -\frac{e^{at}}{2} & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

Verification: $x_e(t) = x_e(-t)$ and $x_o(t) = -x_o(-t)$. Confirmed.

> [!NOTE]
> The even part is symmetric about the y-axis; the odd part is anti-symmetric. This decomposition is the basis of the **Fourier cosine and sine series**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power of a Discrete-Time Sinusoidal Sequence

**Problem:** Find the average power of $x[n] = A\cos(\Omega_0 n)$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

For a discrete-time periodic signal with period $N$:

$$P = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x[n] \vert^{2}$$

$$P = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A^{2} \cos^{2}(\Omega_0 n)$$

Using $\cos^{2}(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$P = \frac{A^{2}}{2N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \bigl[ 1 + \cos(2\Omega_0 n) \bigr]$$

The sum of a cosine over a complete period of integer cycles is zero, so:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \cos(2\Omega_0 n) = 0$$

Therefore:

$$P = \frac{A^{2}}{2N} \cdot N = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$$

Same as the continuous-time result, but the frequency $\Omega_0$ must be a rational multiple of $\pi$ for true periodicity.

## 3.5 Worked-Out Example (Standard KTU Style)

**Problem:** Determine whether the following signals are energy signals, power signals, or neither. Compute the relevant quantity.
1. $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$
2. $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$
3. $x[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n]$

**Solution:**

**Signal 1:** From Section 3.1, with $a = 2$:

$$E = \frac{1}{2a} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ J (normalized)}$$

**Classification: Energy signal.** $P = 0$.

**Signal 2:** $A = 5$, $\omega = 100\pi$, so from Section 3.2:

$$P = \frac{A^{2}}{2} = \frac{25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ W (normalized)}$$

**Classification: Power signal.** $E = \infty$.

**Signal 3:** Discrete-time energy:

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{n}$$

This is a geometric series with first term $1$ and ratio $\frac{1}{4} < 1$:

$$E = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{4}} = \frac{1}{\frac{3}{4}} = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Classification: Energy signal.** $P = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Signals

The following Mermaid diagram presents a **block-level classification map** of signals as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. This is a high-yield schematic frequently drawn in board examinations.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Signals] --> rootB[Continuous Time]
    rootA --> rootC[Discrete Time]
    rootA --> rootD[Multidimensional]

    rootB --> nodeE[Analog CT]
    rootB --> nodeF[Quantized CT]

    rootE --> nodeG[Deterministic]
    rootE --> nodeH[Random]
    rootF --> nodeI[Periodic]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Aperiodic]
    nodeF --> nodeK[Energy Signal]
    nodeF --> nodeL[Power Signal]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Even]
    nodeF --> nodeN[Odd]
    nodeF --> nodeO[Neither]

    rootC --> nodeP[Digital DT]
    rootC --> nodeQ[Sampled Data]

    nodeP --> nodeR[Periodic]
    nodeP --> nodeS[Aperiodic]
    nodeP --> nodeT[Energy Signal]
    nodeP --> nodeU[Power Signal]

    rootD --> nodeV[Image Signal]
    rootD --> nodeW[Video Signal]
    rootD --> nodeX[EEG Topographic Map]

    style rootA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style rootB fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style rootC fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style rootD fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeF fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeG fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeH fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeI fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeJ fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeK fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeL fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeM fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeN fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeO fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeP fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeQ fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style nodeR fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeS fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeT fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000
    style nodeU fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000

```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read:** The root splits into three domains of the independent variable. The second level shows signal types by amplitude (analog vs digital). The third level is the property-based classification (determinism, periodicity, energy/power, symmetry). In an exam, even a partial tree earns partial credit.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: General Biomedical Signal Processing Chain

The Mermaid block diagram below shows the **functional flow** of how a raw physiological signal is acquired, processed, and used for clinical decision-making. This is the most important architecture you will need to draw in Module 1 and beyond.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA[Patient Physiological Source] --> srcB[Transducer / Electrode]
    srcB --> srcC[Preamplifier]
    srcC --> srcD[Analog Filter Anti Aliasing]
    srcD --> srcE[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    srcE --> srcF[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    srcF --> srcG[Feature Extraction Block]
    srcG --> srcH[Classification / Diagnosis Block]
    srcH --> srcI[Display / Alarm / Therapy]

    noiseA[Powerline 50 Hz] -.interference.-> srcC
    noiseB[Motion Artifact] -.interference.-> srcB
    noiseC[Thermal Noise] -.interference.-> srcE

    style srcA fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style srcB fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style srcC fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
    style srcD fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style srcE fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style srcF fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style srcG fill:#7030a0,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style srcH fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style srcI fill:#404040,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style noiseA fill:#a6a6a6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style noiseB fill:#a6a6a6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style noiseC fill:#a6a6a6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-by-Block Role:**
> 1. **Source:** Heart, brain, muscle — the origin of the signal.
> 2. **Transducer/Electrode:** Converts biological energy (mechanical, chemical, electrical) into voltage.
> 3. **Preamplifier:** Boosts the tiny $\mu V$–$mV$ signals (high CMRR critical).
> 4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Low-pass filter to limit bandwidth to satisfy Nyquist criterion.
> 5. **ADC:** Samples and quantizes the signal.
> 6. **DSP:** Applies digital filters, transforms (FFT), and adaptive algorithms.
> 7. **Feature Extraction:** Detects R-peaks, RR-interval, QRS width, etc.
> 8. **Classification:** Applies rule-based or ML models for diagnosis.
> 9. **Output:** Display, alarm, or therapeutic device (e.g., pacemaker).

## 4.3 Diagram: Even-Odd Decomposition Process

The following flow diagram illustrates the algebraic decomposition of any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ into its even and odd components.

```mermaid
graph LR
    partA[Input Signal x of t] --> partB[Time Reversed x of minus t]
    partA --> partC[Add Operation]
    partB --> partC
    partC --> partD[Divide by 2]
    partD --> partE[Even Part x sub e of t]
    partA --> partF[Subtract Operation]
    partB --> partF
    partF --> partG[Divide by 2]
    partG --> partH[Odd Part x sub o of t]
    partE --> partI[Sum x sub e plus x sub o equals x of t]
    partH --> partI
    partI --> partJ[Reconstructed Original Signal]

    style partA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style partB fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style partC fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
    style partD fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style partE fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style partF fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000
    style partG fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define a signal. Classify signals based on the nature of the independent and dependent variables, with one biomedical example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **signal** is a physical quantity that varies with time, space, or another independent variable and carries information about a system. Mathematically, it is a function of one or more independent variables.

**Classification:**

| Type | Independent Variable | Dependent Variable (Amplitude) | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Analog** | Continuous | Continuous | Raw electrode output of ECG before ADC |
| **Discrete-time** | Discrete | Continuous | Sampled ECG values before quantization |
| **Digital** | Discrete | Discrete | 12-bit ECG stored in computer memory |
| **Quantized continuous-time** | Continuous | Discrete | Output of a sample-and-hold circuit |

A practical ECG monitor first produces an **analog** signal at the electrode, then converts it into **discrete-time** by sampling, and finally into a fully **digital** form by quantization — all three forms exist momentarily in the chain.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for classification with examples]**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between an energy signal and a power signal. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Energy Signal | Power Signal |
|---|---|---|
| Total energy $E$ | $0 < E < \infty$ (finite) | $E = \infty$ |
| Average power $P$ | $P = 0$ | $0 < P < \infty$ (finite) |
| Duration | Finite-energy transients | Infinite-duration periodic/aperiodic |
| Example 1 | $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ (a>0) | $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t)$ |
| Example 2 | $x(t) = e^{-\vert t \vert}$ | $x(t) = A$ (DC constant) |

**Key rule:** A periodic signal is **always a power signal** (never an energy signal). A signal decaying to zero as $t \to \pm\infty$ is **typically an energy signal**.

**[3 Marks: 1 for table + 1 for rule + 1 for examples]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]

**(a) Classify biomedical signals. Explain the typical characteristics (amplitude, frequency, determinism) of ECG, EEG, and EMG signals.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the functional building blocks of a typical biomedical signal processing system. State the role of the anti-aliasing filter.** **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Biomedical Signals:**

Biomedical signals are broadly classified into two categories:

1. **Bioelectric signals:** Originate from the electrical activity of excitable cells (nerve, muscle). Examples: ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG (electrooculogram), ERG (electroretinogram).
2. **Biochemical, mechanical, acoustic, and other signals:** Examples include blood pressure (mechanical), phonocardiogram (acoustic), respiratory flow (fluid dynamic), blood oxygen saturation (photoelectric).

**Detailed Characteristics of Three Major Bioelectric Signals:**

| Property | ECG | EEG | EMG |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Electrical activity of cardiac muscle (myocardium) | Electrical activity of cortical neurons | Electrical activity of skeletal muscle fibers |
| **Typical amplitude** | $0.5$ to $\mathbf{5\ mV}$ | $10$ to $\mathbf{100\ \mu V}$ | $50\ \mu V$ to $\mathbf{5\ mV}$ |
| **Frequency range** | $0.05$ to $\mathbf{150\ Hz}$ | $0.5$ to $\mathbf{100\ Hz}$ | $20$ to $\mathbf{500\ Hz}$ |
| **Standard sampling rate** | $\mathbf{500\ Hz}$ | $256$ to $\mathbf{1000\ Hz}$ | $\mathbf{1000}$ to $\mathbf{2000\ Hz}$ |
| **Number of leads/channels** | 12-lead standard | 16 to 256 channels | Variable (surface vs needle) |
| **Nature** | Quasi-periodic | Highly non-stationary, stochastic | Stochastic burst-like |
| **Diagnostic use** | Arrhythmia, MI, ischemia | Epilepsy, sleep disorders, brain death | Neuropathy, myopathy, muscle fatigue |

> [!NOTE]
> The very low amplitude of EEG ($\mu V$ range) makes it the most challenging to acquire, requiring **high CMRR amplifiers ($> 100$ dB)** and **shielded rooms**.

**Sub-part (a) marks: 2 for classification + 5 for detailed table comparison.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram** (refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.2 above).

**Detailed Explanation of Each Block:**

1. **Patient Physiological Source:** The biological origin (heart, brain, muscle). Generates the primary signal of interest.
2. **Transducer / Electrode:** Converts the physiological variable into a proportional electrical signal. ECG uses $\mathrm{Ag/AgCl}$ surface electrodes.
3. **Preamplifier:** High-input-impedance, high-CMRR instrumentation amplifier. Boosts the $\mu V$-$mV$ signal to a usable level (a few volts).
4. **Anti-Aliasing Filter (Low-Pass Filter):** A low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c < f_s / 2$ where $f_s$ is the sampling rate. It ensures that the signal is **band-limited** to avoid **aliasing** during sampling.
5. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Performs sampling and quantization. Common resolutions: 8, 12, 16 bits. The bit resolution sets the dynamic range.
6. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** Microcontroller/DSP chip. Implements digital filters, FFT, statistical computations, compression.
7. **Feature Extraction Block:** Computes clinically relevant features (e.g., R-peak amplitude, RR-interval, QRS width, RMS value of EMG).
8. **Classification / Diagnosis Block:** Uses rule-based thresholds or trained machine-learning models to assign a diagnostic label.
9. **Output:** Display on monitor, alarm generation, or activation of a therapeutic device.

**Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter:**

According to the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, a signal must be sampled at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to be reconstructable. If higher-frequency components exist (e.g., EMG above 500 Hz), they fold back into the lower frequencies during sampling, causing **aliasing** — a phenomenon where the sampled signal has a **false low-frequency component** that does not exist in the original.

For example, a 480 Hz EMG signal sampled at 1000 Hz aliases to $1000 - 480 = 520$ Hz, then folded to $480$ Hz — corrupting the clinical interpretation. The anti-aliasing filter (typically a 4th or higher-order Butterworth low-pass) ensures all frequency content is below $f_s/2$ before sampling, thus preventing this artifact.

**Sub-part (b) marks: 3 for block diagram + 2 for block-by-block explanation + 2 for the role of the anti-aliasing filter.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) Define energy and power of a continuous-time signal. Determine whether $x(t) = e^{-3t}u(t)$ is an energy signal, power signal, or neither. Compute the relevant quantity.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Explain the advantages and disadvantages of digital signal processing over analog signal processing. Why is digital processing preferred in modern biomedical instruments?** **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definitions:**

The **total energy** of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$$

The **average power** is:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^{2}\, dt$$

**Problem: Classify $x(t) = e^{-3t}u(t)$.**

The signal equals $e^{-3t}$ for $t \geq 0$ and $0$ otherwise. Compute the total energy:

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{0}^{T} \bigl(e^{-3t}\bigr)^{2}\, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{0}^{T} e^{-6t}\, dt$$

$$E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left[ \frac{e^{-6t}}{-6} \right]_{0}^{T} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( \frac{e^{-6T}}{-6} - \frac{1}{-6} \right)$$

As $T \to \infty$, $e^{-6T} \to 0$, so:

$$E = 0 + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{6} \text{ unit}$$

Since $0 < E < \infty$, the signal is an **energy signal**, and the average power is $P = 0$.

**Verification:** The formula $E = \frac{1}{2a}$ with $a = 3$ gives $E = \frac{1}{6}$. Confirmed.

**Sub-part (a) marks: 2 for definitions + 4 for step-by-step evaluation + 1 for classification.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Advantages of Digital Signal Processing (DSP):**

1. **Programmability & Flexibility:** Algorithms can be changed via software updates without modifying hardware — unlike analog circuits requiring physical re-design.
2. **Precision & Repeatability:** Digital computations are exact and deterministic; no drift due to temperature, aging, or component tolerance.
3. **Ease of Storage & Transmission:** Digital samples can be stored indefinitely, transmitted over networks, and reproduced exactly.
4. **Advanced Algorithms:** Sophisticated techniques (adaptive filtering, wavelets, neural networks) are feasible only in the digital domain.
5. **Integration & Cost:** A single DSP chip can replace many analog components, reducing size, weight, and power consumption.
6. **Multichannel Operation:** Multiple signals (e.g., 12-lead ECG, 64-channel EEG) can be processed in parallel with synchronization.

**Disadvantages of DSP:**

1. **Bandwidth Limitation:** The ADC has finite sampling rate; very high-frequency signals (e.g., ultrasound RF, $f > 10$ MHz) are difficult to digitize in real time.
2. **Quantization Error:** The ADC rounds amplitudes to discrete levels, introducing noise. Higher bit depth (16, 24 bits) increases cost.
3. **Latency:** Sampling, processing, and reconstruction introduce a small but finite delay — problematic in closed-loop control (e.g., pacemakers).
4. **Power Consumption:** High-speed ADCs and DSP chips consume considerable power — challenging in wearable/implants.
5. **Complexity:** Requires skilled designers and software validation.

**Why Digital is Preferred in Modern Biomedical Instruments:**

- Clinical decisions require **high reliability and accuracy** — DSP offers deterministic, drift-free behavior.
- **Long-term patient monitoring** (Holter, ICU) demands data storage, transmission, and offline review — all digital-native operations.
- **Telemedicine and AI-driven diagnosis** require digital data at the backend.
- The cost-performance ratio of DSP chips has dropped dramatically, making digital processing economically superior.
- Regulatory standards (FDA, CE) favor digital implementations that can be version-controlled and validated.

**Sub-part (b) marks: 3 for advantages + 2 for disadvantages + 2 for justification of preference.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $E$ and $P$ formulas:** Many students write $E = \frac{1}{T}\int |x(t)|^2 dt$ — this is the *power* formula for periodic signals, not energy. Memorize the **full-limit** definition of $E$ and the **average** definition of $P$.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit step:** When evaluating $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$, students sometimes integrate from $-\infty$ to $\infty$ without realizing $u(t)$ zeroes the negative half. The integral should run from $0$ to $\infty$.
> 3. **Saying "a periodic signal is an energy signal":** It is NEVER an energy signal — a periodic signal's energy is infinite. It is always a power signal.
> 4. **Missing the anti-aliasing filter explanation:** Students describe the ADC but omit why filtering must occur *before* sampling. Without the anti-aliasing explanation, expect a 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Mixing up bit resolution vs sampling rate:** 12-bit ADC means 4096 levels; it does NOT refer to sampling rate. The sampling rate is a separate parameter $f_s$.
> 6. **Units of energy and power:** Always state the units (Joules, Watts, or "normalized units") — omitting units loses 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 7. **Even-odd decomposition sign error:** A common mistake is $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ (sign-flipped). Triple-check the formula before writing the final answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Signals and Signal Processing**

- A **signal** is a function carrying information; a **biomedical signal** originates from a living system.
- **Continuous-time** (CT) signals are defined for all $t$; **discrete-time** (DT) signals are defined only at samples $t = nT_s$.
- **Analog** = continuous time + continuous amplitude; **digital** = discrete time + discrete amplitude.
- **Deterministic** signals have a known mathematical form; **random** signals require statistical description.
- A **periodic** signal satisfies $x(t) = x(t + T)$ for all $t$ — the smallest positive $T$ is the **fundamental period**.
- **Energy signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, $P = 0$. **Power signal:** $0 < P < \infty$, $E = \infty$.
- For a periodic signal: $P = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T |x(t)|^2 dt$. For $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$: $P = A^2/2$.
- For $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ with $a > 0$: $E = \frac{1}{2a}$, and the signal is an **energy signal**.
- **Even part:** $x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$. **Odd part:** $x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$.
- **Standard biomedical sampling rates:** ECG $\approx 500$ Hz, EEG $\approx 256$–$1000$ Hz, EMG $\approx 1000$–$2000$ Hz.
- **Nyquist theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. **Anti-aliasing filter** is mandatory before the ADC.
- **Biomedical signal processing chain:** Source $\rightarrow$ Transducer $\rightarrow$ Preamplifier $\rightarrow$ Anti-aliasing filter $\rightarrow$ ADC $\rightarrow$ DSP $\rightarrow$ Feature extraction $\rightarrow$ Classification $\rightarrow$ Output.
- **ECG amplitude:** $0.5$–$5$ mV. **EEG amplitude:** $10$–$100$ $\mu$V. **EMG amplitude:** $50\ \mu V$–$5$ mV.
- **DSP advantages:** Programmability, precision, storage, advanced algorithms, integration.
- **DSP disadvantages:** Bandwidth limit, quantization error, latency, power, complexity.
- **Impulse** $\delta(t)$: area $= 1$, **sifting property**: $\int x(t)\delta(t - t_0)dt = x(t_0)$.
- **Unit step** $u(t)$: jumps from $0$ to $1$ at $t = 0$. It is the integral of $\delta(t)$.
- **Standard signals** to know cold: $u(t), \delta(t), r(t), e^{-at}u(t), A\cos(\omega t), A\sin(\omega t), e^{j\omega t}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** When asked to classify a signal as energy/power, ALWAYS compute both $E$ and $P$. Even if you correctly identify it as one, writing "and $P = 0$" or "and $E = \infty$" shows the examiner that you understand the full theory. This single sentence can tip a 2-mark answer to a 3-mark answer in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization and Classification of Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characterization and Classification of Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal? (KTU Formal Definition)

In the context of the **PCBMT501 - Biomedical Signal Processing** syllabus, a **signal** is formally defined as a function that conveys information about the state or behavior of a physical phenomenon, typically expressed as a variable that carries quantitative data about an event or a system.

Mathematically, a signal is a mapping from an independent variable (most commonly **time**, denoted $t$ or $n$) to a dependent variable (amplitude, magnitude, or intensity). In biomedical engineering, this function almost always represents a physiological measurement obtained from a patient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBMT501 Module 1):**
> A *signal* is a physical quantity that varies with time, space, or another independent variable, by which information is conveyed. In biomedical systems, a signal is typically a record of a physiological activity, such as electrical (ECG, EEG, EMG), mechanical (blood pressure waveform), or chemical (oxygen saturation) activity.

### Standard Notational Conventions Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Meaning | Physical Sense |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $x(t)$ | Continuous-time signal | Defined for every real instant of time |
| $x(n)$ | Discrete-time signal | Defined only at integer sample indices |
| $X(f)$ | Continuous-time Fourier Transform | Frequency-domain representation |
| $X(\omega)$ | Discrete-time Fourier Transform | Angular-frequency domain |

The independent variable **$t$** represents **continuous time** (in seconds), and the independent variable **$n$** represents **discrete sample index** (dimensionless integer). The dependent variable amplitude is generally expressed in **volts (V)**, **millivolts (mV)**, or **microvolts (µV)** for biomedical electrical signals.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Heartbeat as a Storybook Signal**
> Think of a signal as a *story told by your body*. The **ECG (electrocardiogram)** is the story of your heart's electrical activity. Every peak, valley, and flat segment is a *letter* or *word* describing a specific event. When you record this story with a machine, the machine "writes" it down as a continuous wiggly line on paper. This wiggly line, in mathematical language, is your **signal**. By *classifying* signals, we are learning to "read" these storybooks of the body — distinguishing a healthy heartbeat from an abnormal one.

To make the idea even more concrete: imagine a **thermometer** recording your body temperature once every second for one hour. The collection of these temperature readings plotted against time is a **discrete-time signal**. Now imagine a **mercury thermometer** that continuously traces a curve on a moving strip of paper — that is a **continuous-time signal**. Both carry the same information (your temperature history), but they are represented in fundamentally different ways. This distinction — continuous versus discrete — is the *first and most fundamental classification* of signals in the KTU Module 1 syllabus.

## 1.3 Why Do We Classify Signals?

Classification is the foundation of all signal processing. Every processing technique — filtering, compression, spectral analysis, feature extraction — depends on the *type* of signal we are dealing with. Applying a **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** to a continuous signal directly is mathematically invalid; applying a power calculation to an energy signal will give wrong units. Therefore, **characterization precedes processing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Recalled from KTU Engineering Mathematics (Mandatory Background):**
> - **Time:** measured in **seconds (s)**, symbol $t$.
> - **Frequency:** measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **radians/second (rad/s)**, symbol $f$ or $\omega$ where $\omega = 2\pi f$.
> - **Amplitude:** measured in **volts (V)** for electrical biomedical signals, often in **mV** or **µV** (ECG is typically 1 mV peak-to-peak, EEG is typically 10–100 µV).
> - **Energy unit:** **joules (J)**, but in signal processing the normalized energy $E$ is dimensionless when load resistance is assumed as 1 Ω.
> - **Power unit:** **watts (W)**, but the normalized average power $P$ is also treated as dimensionless under the 1 Ω assumption.

## 1.4 The Five Primary Classifications of Signals (KTU Module 1 Syllabus Scope)

The KTU 2024 scheme Module 1 of PCBMT501 specifically requires the student to master the following five primary classifications. Each is treated in depth in the next section.

1. **Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Signals** — based on the nature of the independent variable.
2. **Analog vs Digital Signals** — based on the nature of both the independent variable AND the amplitude.
3. **Deterministic vs Random Signals** — based on the predictability of values.
4. **Periodic vs Aperiodic Signals** — based on repetition over time.
5. **Energy vs Power Signals** — based on whether the signal has finite energy or finite average power.

Additionally, two symmetric classifications are introduced as supplementary properties required for advanced operations like Fourier decomposition:
6. **Even vs Odd Signals** — based on symmetry about the vertical axis.
7. **Causal, Non-Causal, and Anti-Causal Signals** — based on the time-domain support.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:**
> When asked "Classify the signal" in the KTU End Semester Examination, the student is expected to provide a *complete* classification covering all relevant categories, not just one. A signal like $\sin(100\pi t)$ is simultaneously: **continuous-time, analog, deterministic, periodic, and power-type**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification 1 — Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time Signals

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time Signal $x(t)$

A **continuous-time signal** is one whose independent variable $t$ takes any real value in a given interval, typically $(-\infty, \infty)$ or a subset thereof. The amplitude may be continuous (analog) or quantized (but the time axis remains continuous).

**Operational logic behind the definition:**
- The signal is defined for *every* instant of time, not just at specific points.
- A formal mathematical function $x: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is required.
- The signal is plotted as a *smooth continuous curve*.

**Biomedical Examples:**
- **ECG recorded on chart paper** with a stylus tracing a continuous curve.
- **Blood pressure waveform** from a catheter transducer.
- **EEG analog voltage** directly from scalp electrodes.

### 2.1.2 Discrete-Time Signal $x(n)$

A **discrete-time signal** is one whose independent variable $n$ takes only discrete values, almost always integer values $\mathbb{Z} = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$. It is obtained by *sampling* a continuous-time signal at uniform intervals $T_s$.

**Operational logic behind sampling:**
- Sampling theorem (Nyquist–Shannon) requires the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the original signal.
- The sampling interval $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$.
- The discrete signal is represented as $x(n) = x(nT_s)$, a sequence of numbers.

**Biomedical Examples:**
- **Digitized ECG** stored in a Holter monitor.
- **MRI slice data** stored as a 2-D array of pixel intensities.
- **Pulse-oximeter readings** transmitted once per second to a central monitoring station.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Distinction Matters in Practice:**
> All modern digital biomedical instruments (patient monitors, ECG machines with displays, MRI scanners) internally work with discrete-time signals. The Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) at the front-end of every such device performs the sampling operation. Understanding this distinction is essential for topics in Modules 2, 3, and 4 of PCBMT501.

## 2.2 Classification 2 — Analog vs Digital Signals

This is a *related but distinct* classification from continuous vs discrete. The full taxonomy is:

| Type | Time | Amplitude | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Analog | Continuous | Continuous | ECG from electrodes |
| Continuous-Time Quantized | Continuous | Quantized (discrete levels) | Output of a sample-and-hold circuit |
| Sampled-Data | Discrete | Continuous | Sampled analog signal before quantization |
| Digital | Discrete | Quantized (discrete levels) | ECG stored in computer memory |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:**
> "A signal is discrete in time but continuous in amplitude" is the description of a *sampled-data signal*, not a digital signal. A signal is truly *digital* only when BOTH the time and amplitude are discrete. This is a classic 3-mark KTU question.

## 2.3 Classification 3 — Deterministic vs Random Signals

### 2.3.1 Deterministic Signal

A **deterministic signal** is one whose values are completely specified by a mathematical function, a lookup table, or a well-defined rule. There is **no uncertainty** about its past, present, or future values.

**Examples:** $x(t) = 5\sin(2\pi 50 t)$, $x(n) = 0.8^n$ for $n \geq 0$.

### 2.3.2 Random Signal

A **random signal** (also called *stochastic*) cannot be described by an explicit mathematical function. Its values are governed by probability distributions and statistical properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation).

**Biomedical Examples (extremely important for PCBMT501):**
- **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** — highly random-looking, must be analyzed statistically.
- **EMG (Electromyogram)** during weak voluntary contraction — pseudo-random burst patterns.
- **Noise in any biomedical signal** — thermal noise in electrodes, 50/60 Hz powerline interference.
- **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** under resting conditions — exhibits stochastic behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of This Distinction:**
> Deterministic signals are analyzed with classical transforms (Fourier, Laplace, Z). Random signals require statistical signal processing tools: autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$, power spectral density (PSD) $S_x(f)$, ensemble averages, and ergodicity theorems. This is a strong Module 3 and Module 4 topic in PCBMT501.

## 2.4 Classification 4 — Periodic vs Aperiodic Signals

### 2.4.1 Periodic Signal

A **continuous-time signal** $x(t)$ is periodic with period $T$ if:

$$x(t + T) = x(t) \quad \text{for all } t \in \mathbb{R}$$

The **fundamental period** $T_0$ is the smallest positive value of $T$ satisfying this condition. If no such $T$ exists, the signal is **aperiodic**.

A **discrete-time signal** $x(n)$ is periodic with period $N$ if:

$$x(n + N) = x(n) \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Here the fundamental period $N$ must be a **positive integer**.

### 2.4.2 The Critical Difference in Periodicity Tests

For **continuous-time sinusoids** $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$, the period is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ and *any* real value of $T$ works. A sum of two sinusoids is periodic **only if the ratio of their frequencies is a rational number**.

For **discrete-time sinusoids** $x(n) = A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$, periodicity requires:

$$\frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{k}{N} \quad \text{for some integers } k, N$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:**
> A discrete-time sinusoid with $\omega_0 = 0.3$ rad (an irrational multiple of $2\pi$) is **NOT periodic**, even though a continuous-time sinusoid with the same $\omega_0$ *is* periodic. This counterintuitive result is a frequent KTU question setter's favorite. Always reduce the ratio $\dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$ to lowest terms and check if it equals a rational number.

### 2.4.3 Biomedical Examples of Periodic and Aperiodic Signals

- **Periodic:** ECG R-R interval under resting sinus rhythm, arterial blood pressure waveform, respiration waveform in steady breathing.
- **Aperiodic:** Evoked potentials (e.g., visual evoked response), single ECG beat in isolation, transient EMG response to a stimulus.

## 2.5 Classification 5 — Energy vs Power Signals

This classification is based on the *normalised energy* and *normalised average power* of the signal, assuming a unit resistance of 1 Ω.

### 2.5.1 Total Normalised Energy $E$

For a continuous-time signal $x(t)$:

$$E \triangleq \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

For a discrete-time signal $x(n)$:

$$E \triangleq \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert^2$$

A signal is an **energy signal** if and only if $0 < E < \infty$.

### 2.5.2 Average Normalised Power $P$

For a continuous-time signal $x(t)$:

$$P \triangleq \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

For a discrete-time signal $x(n)$:

$$P \triangleq \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N + 1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x(n) \vert^2$$

A signal is a **power signal** if and only if $0 < P < \infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Theorem Linking Energy and Power Signals:**
> - Every energy signal has $P = 0$ (because $E$ is finite, so $E/2T \to 0$ as $T \to \infty$).
> - Every power signal has $E = \infty$ (because integrating $\vert x(t) \vert^2$ over all time gives infinity).
> - **A signal cannot be both an energy signal and a power signal simultaneously** (with rare pathological exceptions like the zero signal).
> - **Many signals are neither** (e.g., $x(t) = t$, which has $E = \infty$ AND $P = \infty$).

### 2.5.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU 2024 PCBMT501 student must memorize for Module 1 examinations. Note carefully: the symbol `\vert x(t) \vert^2` denotes the squared magnitude, used in place of the standard pipe notation to keep the markdown table syntax intact.

| Property | Continuous-Time $x(t)$ | Discrete-Time $x(n)$ | Energy/Power Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total Energy $E$ | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $\displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert^2$ | Energy signal: $0 < E < \infty$ |
| Average Power $P$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | $\displaystyle\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x(n) \vert^2$ | Power signal: $0 < P < \infty$ |
| Periodicity Test | $x(t+T) = x(t)$ for all $t$ | $x(n+N) = x(n)$ for all $n$, $N \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ | Periodic if such $T$ or $N$ exists |
| Fundamental Period | $T_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_0}$ | $N_0 = \dfrac{2\pi k}{\omega_0}$, $k, N_0 \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ | Used in Fourier analysis |
| Even Symmetry | $x(t) = x(-t)$ | $x(n) = x(-n)$ | Symmetric about vertical axis |
| Odd Symmetry | $x(t) = -x(-t)$ | $x(n) = -x(-n)$ | Anti-symmetric about vertical axis |
| Even Component | $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_e(n) = \dfrac{x(n) + x(-n)}{2}$ | Always an even function |
| Odd Component | $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$ | $x_o(n) = \dfrac{x(n) - x(-n)}{2}$ | Always an odd function |
| Causal Signal | $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | $x(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Real-time implementable |
| Sinusoid Energy | $E = \infty$, $P = A^2/2$ | $E = \infty$, $P = A^2/2$ | Power signal |

## 2.6 Supplementary Properties — Even/Odd Decomposition and Causality

### 2.6.1 Why Even/Odd Decomposition?

**The core 'why':** Every real signal can be uniquely expressed as the sum of an even part and an odd part: $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$. This decomposition is the gateway to the **Fourier Series**, because the even part of a signal correlates with the *cosine coefficients* and the odd part with the *sine coefficients* in the trigonometric Fourier series expansion. This is *the* most important link between Module 1 (signal characterization) and Module 2 (Fourier analysis) in PCBMT501.

### 2.6.2 Causality in Biomedical Context

A signal $x(t)$ is **causal** if it is zero for all $t < 0$. Most real-time physiological monitoring systems are causal — they cannot "see into the future". However, when we store an ECG recording and analyze it offline, the segment to the left of our analysis window is *already recorded*, so we can use non-causal filters. This distinction becomes important in Module 3 (filter design).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is explicitly written out below. The student should be able to reproduce each derivation in the KTU examination by following the logic row by row.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Orthogonality of Even and Odd Components

**Statement to prove:** The even component $x_e(t)$ and the odd component $x_o(t)$ of a real signal $x(t)$ are **orthogonal** over the symmetric interval $[-T, T]$, meaning:

$$\int_{-T}^{T} x_e(t) \cdot x_o(t) \, dt = 0$$

**Why we are proving this:** Orthogonality of the even and odd parts guarantees that the total energy of the signal equals the sum of the energies of its even and odd parts, *without any cross-term*. This is a fundamental result that simplifies energy calculations in Fourier analysis.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Begin with the definition of the cross-product of even and odd components.**

We compute the integral $I = \displaystyle\int_{-T}^{T} x_e(t) \cdot x_o(t) \, dt$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the standard definitions.**

By definition, $x_e(t) = \dfrac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}$ and $x_o(t) = \dfrac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$. Therefore:

$$I = \int_{-T}^{T} \left[ \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \right] \cdot \left[ \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \right] dt$$

**Step 3 — Multiply out the product of the two binomials.**

$$\left[ \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \right] \cdot \left[ \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} \right] = \frac{1}{4} \left[ x(t) + x(-t) \right] \left[ x(t) - x(-t) \right]$$

Using the algebraic identity $(a+b)(a-b) = a^2 - b^2$ with $a = x(t)$ and $b = x(-t)$:

$$= \frac{1}{4} \left[ x^2(t) - x^2(-t) \right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the integral.**

$$I = \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{1}{4} \left[ x^2(t) - x^2(-t) \right] dt$$

**Step 5 — Split the integral into two parts.**

$$I = \frac{1}{4} \int_{-T}^{T} x^2(t) \, dt - \frac{1}{4} \int_{-T}^{T} x^2(-t) \, dt$$

**Step 6 — Apply the substitution $\tau = -t$ in the second integral.**

When $t = -T$, $\tau = T$. When $t = T$, $\tau = -T$. Also $dt = -d\tau$, so the limits of integration are swapped, cancelling the minus sign:

$$\int_{-T}^{T} x^2(-t) \, dt = \int_{T}^{-T} x^2(\tau) \cdot (-d\tau) = \int_{-T}^{T} x^2(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 7 — Recognize that both integrals are identical.**

The first integral is $\displaystyle\int_{-T}^{T} x^2(t) \, dt$ and the second (after substitution) is also $\displaystyle\int_{-T}^{T} x^2(\tau) \, d\tau$, which is the same quantity. Let this common value be $J$.

**Step 8 — Conclude the proof.**

$$I = \frac{1}{4} J - \frac{1}{4} J = 0$$

Hence $x_e(t)$ and $x_o(t)$ are orthogonal over any symmetric interval $[-T, T]$, and in particular over $(-\infty, \infty)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy of a Rectangular Pulse (Energy Signal Example)

**Problem:** A discrete-time signal is defined as $x(n) = A$ for $0 \leq n \leq N-1$ and $x(n) = 0$ elsewhere. Find the total energy $E$ and determine whether this is an energy signal or power signal.

**Why we are deriving this:** A rectangular pulse is the canonical example of an *energy signal* and appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper as a 3-mark sub-part. The derivation also illustrates the convergence of an infinite sum to a finite value.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write down the energy formula for a discrete-time signal.**

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of $x(n)$ into the summation.**

Since $x(n) = A$ only for $0 \leq n \leq N-1$ and zero everywhere else, the only nonzero terms in the infinite sum occur over the finite range $0 \leq n \leq N-1$:

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert A \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A^2$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the constant $A^2$ from the summation.**

$$E = A^2 \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} 1$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the sum of $N$ ones.**

The sum $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} 1$ counts the number of terms from $n = 0$ to $n = N-1$, which is exactly $N$ terms.

$$E = A^2 \cdot N = NA^2$$

**Step 5 — Verify the energy-signal condition.**

Since $0 < NA^2 < \infty$ for any finite positive $A$ and finite positive integer $N$, the signal has finite nonzero energy. Therefore, $x(n)$ is an **energy signal**.

**Step 6 — Compute the average power for completeness.**

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{NA^2}{2N+1} = \frac{A^2}{2} \cdot \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N}{N+1/2} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{A^2}{2} \cdot 1$$

Wait — this calculation requires care. As $N$ grows, the signal $x(n)$ is defined only over a *fixed* window $0 \leq n \leq N-1$ where the upper limit $N-1$ refers to the *signal parameter*, not the limit variable. Let us rename the signal's window length as $L$ to avoid confusion with the limit variable, and let the limit variable be $M$:

$$P = \lim_{M \to \infty} \frac{1}{2M+1} \sum_{n=-M}^{M} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \lim_{M \to \infty} \frac{1}{2M+1} \cdot L A^2 = LA^2 \cdot \lim_{M \to \infty} \frac{1}{2M+1} = 0$$

So $P = 0$, confirming that the rectangular pulse is *purely* an energy signal. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Power of a Sinusoid (Power Signal Example)

**Problem:** Find the average power of the continuous-time signal $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ and classify the signal.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write down the power formula.**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) \vert^2 dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric power-reduction identity.**

The identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \dfrac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$ gives:

$$A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \frac{A^2}{2} \left[ 1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the integral.**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{A^2}{2} \left[ 1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \right] dt$$

**Step 4 — Distribute the integral.**

$$P = \frac{A^2}{4T} \cdot \lim_{T \to \infty} \left[ \int_{-T}^{T} 1 \, dt + \int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt \right]$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the first integral.**

$$\int_{-T}^{T} 1 \, dt = 2T$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the second integral.**

$$\int_{-T}^{T} \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt = \left[ \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2\omega_0} \right]_{-T}^{T} = \frac{\sin(2\omega_0 T + 2\phi) - \sin(-2\omega_0 T + 2\phi)}{2\omega_0}$$

This is a bounded sinusoid of magnitude at most $\dfrac{2}{2\omega_0} = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0}$.

**Step 7 — Combine the results and take the limit.**

$$P = \frac{A^2}{4T} \cdot \lim_{T \to \infty} \left[ 2T + \text{bounded term} \right] = \frac{A^2}{4T} \cdot \left[ 2T + 0 \right] \text{ as } T \to \infty$$

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{2TA^2}{4T} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Conclude the classification.**

The average power is $P = \dfrac{A^2}{2}$, which is finite and nonzero. The total energy is $E = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt = \infty$. Hence the sinusoidal signal is a **power signal**. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> The result $P = A^2/2$ does NOT depend on the frequency $\omega_0$ or the phase $\phi$ — it depends only on the amplitude. This makes intuitive sense because the time-average of $\cos^2$ is always $1/2$, regardless of how fast the cosine oscillates. This is why any sinusoid of the form $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ has the same average power as $A\sin(\omega_0 t + \phi)$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Periodicity Test for Discrete-Time Sinusoid

**Problem:** Determine the fundamental period of the discrete-time signal $x(n) = \cos\left(\dfrac{3\pi}{7} n + \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the periodicity condition.**

For $x(n)$ to be periodic, there must exist a positive integer $N$ such that:

$$x(n + N) = x(n) \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the signal form.**

$$\cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{7}(n+N) + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \cos\left(\frac{3\pi}{7} n + \frac{3\pi N}{7} + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the periodicity requirement.**

For the cosine to return to its original value, the added phase $\dfrac{3\pi N}{7}$ must be an integer multiple of $2\pi$:

$$\frac{3\pi N}{7} = 2\pi k \quad \text{for some integer } k$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the ratio $N/k$.**

$$\frac{N}{k} = \frac{14}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the smallest integers $k$ and $N$ that satisfy this ratio.**

We need integers $k$ and $N$ such that $N/k = 14/3$. The smallest such pair is $k = 3$ and $N = 14$.

**Step 6 — Verify by substitution.**

$$\frac{3\pi \cdot 14}{7} = 6\pi = 3 \cdot 2\pi \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Conclude the fundamental period.**

The fundamental period is $N_0 = 14$ samples, and the signal is periodic with this period.

**Step 8 — Cross-check the continuous-time analog.**

If this were a continuous-time signal $x(t) = \cos(3\pi t/7 + \pi/4)$, the period would be $T = 2\pi / (3\pi/7) = 14/3$ seconds. The continuous-time signal is periodic with period $14/3$ s (a non-integer number of seconds). The discrete-time version, however, is periodic only because we sample it at unit interval and the resulting sample indices form an integer period. $\blacksquare$
