# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Define a deterministic signal and a random signal. Give one biomedical example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **deterministic signal** is a signal whose values are completely and exactly described by a well-defined mathematical expression, table, or rule, with no uncertainty attached to any of its values. Every past, present, and future value is known in advance once the mathematical function is specified. *Biomedical example:* the clean ECG waveform of a normal sinus rhythm, ideally modeled as a sum of sinusoids and triangular pulses.

A **random signal** is a signal whose future values cannot be predicted exactly but can be described using probability distributions and statistical averages (mean, variance, autocorrelation, power spectral density). *Biomedical example:* the EEG signal recorded from scalp electrodes, which exhibits quasi-random oscillatory behavior in the alpha, beta, theta, and delta bands and must be characterized by statistical methods.

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 1]`

**State the conditions for a signal to be classified as an energy signal and a power signal. Why can a signal not be both?**

**Model Answer:**

A signal $x(t)$ is an **energy signal** if its total normalised energy is finite and nonzero:

$$0 < E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$$

A signal $x(t)$ is a **power signal** if its average normalised power is finite and nonzero:

$$0 < P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt < \infty$$

**Why a signal cannot be both:** For an energy signal, $E$ is finite, so the power becomes $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \dfrac{E}{2T} = 0$. For a power signal, $P$ is nonzero, so the energy becomes $E = \lim_{T \to \infty} 2T \cdot P = \infty$. The conditions $P = 0$ and $0 < P < \infty$ are mutually exclusive, and $E < \infty$ and $E = \infty$ are mutually exclusive. Hence a signal cannot satisfy both classifications simultaneously. The zero signal $x(t) = 0$ is the lone exception that vacuously satisfies both with $E = 0$ and $P = 0$, but it carries no information.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**a)** Classify the continuous-time signal $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t) + 2\sin(50\pi t + \pi/3)$ with respect to: (i) continuous-time vs discrete-time, (ii) analog vs digital, (iii) deterministic vs random, (iv) periodic vs aperiodic, (v) energy vs power. Justify each classification with one line of reasoning. (7 marks)

**b)** Compute the total energy and average power of the discrete-time signal $x(n) = (0.5)^n u(n)$, where $u(n)$ is the unit step function. State whether it is an energy signal, power signal, or neither. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**(i) Continuous-time:** $x(t)$ is defined for every real $t \in \mathbb{R}$, with no sampling mentioned. Hence $x(t)$ is a **continuous-time signal**.

**(ii) Analog:** The amplitude of $x(t)$ is a continuous real number taking all values between the maximum and minimum of the sum of two sinusoids, with no quantization mentioned. Hence $x(t)$ is an **analog signal**.

**(iii) Deterministic:** $x(t)$ is a sum of two pure sinusoids with known amplitudes, frequencies, and phases. Every value is mathematically determined. Hence $x(t)$ is a **deterministic signal**.

**(iv) Periodic:** The fundamental period of $5\cos(100\pi t)$ is $T_1 = 2\pi / (100\pi) = 1/50$ s, and that of $2\sin(50\pi t + \pi/3)$ is $T_2 = 2\pi / (50\pi) = 1/25$ s. The ratio $T_1/T_2 = (1/50)/(1/25) = 1/2$, which is a rational number. Hence the sum is periodic with fundamental period $T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = 1/25$ s (the larger of the two, when the ratio is rational). Therefore $x(t)$ is **periodic**.

**(v) Power signal:** A pure sinusoid of amplitude $A$ has average power $A^2/2$. The first component contributes $5^2/2 = 12.5$ and the second contributes $2^2/2 = 2$ (the cross-term averages to zero for sinusoids of different frequencies). Total power $P = 12.5 + 2 = 14.5$, which is finite and nonzero, while the total energy $E = \infty$. Hence $x(t)$ is a **power signal**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute the total energy.**

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^{2n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n$$

Using the geometric series formula $\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^n = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$E = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the average power.**

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x(n) \vert^2 = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Classify the signal.**

Since $0 < E = 4/3 < \infty$ and $P = 0$, the signal $x(n) = (0.5)^n u(n)$ is an **energy signal**. (The signal is also causal, real-valued, and deterministic, but those are not the energy/power classification.)

### Question B-B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**a)** For the discrete-time signal $x(n) = \cos(0.3\pi n)$, determine whether the signal is periodic. If periodic, find the fundamental period. (7 marks)

**b)** Decompose the discrete-time signal $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1\}$ (centered at $n = 0$) into its even and odd components. Verify the decomposition by reconstructing $x(n)$ from $x_e(n)$ and $x_o(n)$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Set up the periodicity condition.**

For periodicity, we require a positive integer $N$ such that $x(n + N) = x(n)$ for all $n$:

$$\cos(0.3\pi(n + N)) = \cos(0.3\pi n)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cosine periodicity requirement.**

The cosine returns to its value when the added phase $0.3\pi N$ is an integer multiple of $2\pi$:

$$0.3\pi N = 2\pi k \quad \text{for some integer } k \geq 1$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $N$.**

$$N = \frac{2\pi k}{0.3\pi} = \frac{2k}{0.3} = \frac{20k}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Find the smallest integer $N$.**

For $N$ to be a positive integer, $20k$ must be divisible by 3. The smallest positive integer $k$ satisfying this is $k = 3$, giving $N = 20$. Let us verify: $N/k = 20/3$, so the smallest integers are $N = 20, k = 3$.

**Step 5 — Verify the period.**

$$0.3\pi \cdot 20 = 6\pi = 3 \cdot 2\pi \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**

The signal is **periodic** with fundamental period $N_0 = 20$ samples.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students incorrectly conclude that the signal is aperiodic because the ratio $0.3\pi / (2\pi) = 0.15$ is *not* a rational number with small denominator. But $0.15 = 3/20$, which IS a rational number. Always reduce the fraction to lowest terms before deciding. If the ratio $\omega_0 / (2\pi)$ is a rational number $p/q$ in lowest terms, the fundamental period is $q$. Here, $0.3\pi/(2\pi) = 0.15 = 3/20$, so $q = 20$, giving $N_0 = 20$. Students who answer "not periodic" will lose 4 to 5 marks.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Write the signal explicitly with index labels.**

The signal $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1\}$ is centered at $n = 0$, so:

$$x(-3) = 1, \quad x(-2) = 2, \quad x(-1) = 3, \quad x(0) = 4, \quad x(1) = 3, \quad x(2) = 2, \quad x(3) = 1$$

**Step 2 — Compute the time-reversed signal $x(-n)$.**

By the property $x(-n)$ at index $n$ equals $x(n)$ at index $-n$:

$$x(-(-3)) = x(3) = 1, \quad x(-(-2)) = x(2) = 2, \quad x(-(-1)) = x(1) = 3, \quad x(-0) = x(0) = 4$$

So $x(-n)$ as a function of $n$:

$$x(-3) = 1, \quad x(-2) = 2, \quad x(-1) = 3, \quad x(0) = 4, \quad x(1) = 3, \quad x(2) = 2, \quad x(3) = 1$$

In this case, $x(-n) = x(n)$ because the signal is symmetric. The even component will therefore equal the original signal and the odd component will be zero.

**Step 3 — Compute the even component $x_e(n) = [x(n) + x(-n)] / 2$.**

$$x_e(-3) = \frac{1+1}{2} = 1, \quad x_e(-2) = \frac{2+2}{2} = 2, \quad x_e(-1) = \frac{3+3}{2} = 3, \quad x_e(0) = \frac{4+4}{2} = 4$$

$$x_e(1) = 3, \quad x_e(2) = 2, \quad x_e(3) = 1$$

So $x_e(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1\}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the odd component $x_o(n) = [x(n) - x(-n)] / 2$.**

$$x_o(-3) = \frac{1-1}{2} = 0, \quad x_o(-2) = 0, \quad x_o(-1) = 0, \quad x_o(0) = 0$$

$$x_o(1) = 0, \quad x_o(2) = 0, \quad x_o(3) = 0$$

So $x_o(n) = \{0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\}$.

**Step 5 — Verify the reconstruction.**

$$x_e(n) + x_o(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1\} + \{0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0\} = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1\} = x(n) \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**

Since $x(n) = x(-n)$ for all $n$, the signal is **purely even** with zero odd component. This is consistent with the symmetric triangular shape of the original signal.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> The problem in Question B-B(b) deliberately uses a symmetric signal to test whether the student can recognise a special case. Some students will mechanically compute $x_e$ and $x_o$ without noticing the symmetry, which is acceptable if done correctly, but a faster student should immediately identify $x_o(n) = 0$ from the symmetry. Also, ensure that the time-reversal $x(-n)$ is correctly indexed — a common mistake is to write $x(-3) = 1$ as $x(3) = 1$ or to confuse the bracket indices. Always re-label explicitly before computing.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following is a high-density rapid-revision checklist. A KTU 2024 PCBMT501 student should be able to reproduce this checklist from memory in the examination hall.

- [ ] A **signal** is a function of an independent variable (usually time) that carries information.
- [ ] A signal is **continuous-time** $x(t)$ if defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$; **discrete-time** $x(n)$ if defined only for integer $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- [ ] A signal is **analog** if continuous in BOTH time and amplitude; **digital** if discrete in BOTH; **sampled-data** if discrete time but continuous amplitude.
- [ ] A signal is **deterministic** if described by a known function; **random** if described by probability distributions and statistical averages.
- [ ] A signal is **periodic** with period $T$ (or $N$) if $x(t+T) = x(t)$ (or $x(n+N) = x(n)$). For continuous sinusoids, period is $T_0 = 2\pi / \omega_0$.
- [ ] For **discrete-time sinusoids**, periodicity requires $\omega_0 / (2\pi) = k/N$ for integers $k, N$. The fundamental period is the smallest $N$ satisfying this.
- [ ] **Energy signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, where $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ (continuous) or $E = \sum \vert x(n) \vert^2$ (discrete).
- [ ] **Power signal:** $0 < P < \infty$, where $P = \lim (1/2T) \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ (continuous) or $P = \lim (1/(2N+1)) \sum \vert x(n) \vert^2$ (discrete).
- [ ] **A signal cannot be both energy and power** (except the zero signal).
- [ ] Average power of $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ is **$A^2 / 2$** — independent of frequency and phase.
- [ ] **Even signal:** $x(t) = x(-t)$. **Odd signal:** $x(t) = -x(-t)$. Any signal decomposes as $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.
- [ ] $x_e(t) = [x(t) + x(-t)]/2$ and $x_o(t) = [x(t) - x(-t)]/2$ are orthogonal over any symmetric interval.
- [ ] **Causal signal:** $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$. Most real-time biomedical monitoring is causal.
- [ ] **Biomedical examples to remember:** ECG is deterministic + periodic + power signal; EEG is random + aperiodic + power signal; a single evoked potential is deterministic + aperiodic + energy signal.
- [ ] **Sampling theorem (preview for Module 2):** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing. Sampling interval $T_s = 1/f_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:**
> When asked to "classify" a signal, always provide ALL applicable classifications in a single bullet list. When asked to find the fundamental period of a discrete-time sinusoid, always reduce the ratio $\omega_0 / (2\pi)$ to lowest terms $p/q$ — the fundamental period is $q$. When asked to find the energy or power, ALWAYS show the integration/summation steps and the limit process explicitly. The KTU board examiner allocates 1 to 2 marks for the *final answer* and the remaining 5 to 6 marks for the *methodology*. A correct final answer with no work shown will earn at most 2 of the 7 marks allocated to that part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elementary Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elementary Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Elementary Signals** are the fundamental, mathematically idealized building blocks used to construct, analyze, and represent any arbitrary continuous-time or discrete-time signal. They form the **orthogonal basis** upon which all linear time-invariant (LTI) system analysis rests.

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing**, elementary signals are essential because complex physiological signals (like ECG, EEG, EMG, and PCG) can be mathematically decomposed into combinations of these idealized waveforms. This decomposition allows clinicians and engineers to perform **feature extraction, denoising, and pattern recognition** on biological waveforms.

The standard classification of elementary continuous-time signals under the KTU 2024 Scheme includes:

1. **Unit Step Signal** $u(t)$
2. **Unit Impulse Signal** $\delta(t)$
3. **Unit Ramp Signal** $r(t)$
4. **Exponential Signal** $e^{at}$
5. **Sinusoidal Signal** $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$
6. **Complex Exponential Signal** $e^{j\omega t}$
7. **Signum Function** $\text{sgn}(t)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Per the KTU 2024 PCBMT501 Module 1 syllabus, students are required to mathematically define each elementary signal, sketch its waveform, and establish the **derivative and integral relationships** that interconnect them. These relations are frequently tested in Part A (3-mark) questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a kitchen**. Before cooking a complex dish, you must first master individual ingredients — salt, sugar, vinegar, oil. You learn the exact taste, smell, and behavior of each ingredient in isolation. Once mastered, you can combine them in precise proportions to create thousands of recipes.

**Elementary signals are the "ingredients" of signal processing.** Just as every dish is a combination of basic tastes, every biomedical signal (an ECG trace, a brain wave, a heart sound) is a combination of these basic waveforms. The **impulse** is like pure salt (instantaneous), the **step** is like adding a constant spice (sudden level change), the **ramp** is like a slow simmer (gradual increase), and the **sine wave** is like a rhythmic heartbeat (oscillatory).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Sampling Rate ($f_s$):** $\geq \mathbf{2 f_{max}}$ (Nyquist Criterion) — measured in Hertz (Hz)
> - **Angular Frequency ($\omega$):** $\omega = 2\pi f$ — measured in radians per second (rad/s)
> - **Time Constant ($\tau$):** For exponential decay $e^{-t/\tau}$, $\tau$ governs decay speed — measured in seconds (s)
> - **Signals are dimensionless in pure math**, but in biomedical context, amplitudes carry units: **mV (ECG/EEG), $\mu$V (EMG), mmHg (BP)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Unit Step Signal $u(t)$

### Mathematical Definition

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

### Step-by-Step Logic
- **Why?** Models a signal that "switches on" abruptly at $t = 0$ and remains at a constant amplitude forever after. In biomedical terms, it approximates the sudden activation of a nerve stimulus or a digital trigger pulse in a pacemaker.
- **How?** It is a piecewise function — a single discontinuity at the origin. For $t < 0$, the signal has zero amplitude. For $t \geq 0$, it holds a constant value of 1.

### Shifted Step Signal

$$
u(t - t_0) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq t_0 \\ 0, & t < t_0 \end{cases}
$$

This represents a step that is "delayed" by $t_0$ seconds.

## 2.2 Unit Impulse Signal $\delta(t)$

### Mathematical Definition

$$
\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$

with the defining property:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t) \, dt = 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The impulse is a **theoretical idealization** — an infinitely tall, infinitesimally narrow spike whose total area (strength) equals 1. In practice, an ECG QRS complex or a neuronal action potential is often *approximated* by an impulse train for analytical purposes.

### Properties of Impulse Signal

- **Sampling Property:** $\displaystyle \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)$
- **Multiplication Property:** $x(t) \cdot \delta(t) = x(0) \cdot \delta(t)$
- **Even Function:** $\delta(-t) = \delta(t)$
- **Scaling:** $\delta(at) = \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} \delta(t)$

## 2.3 Unit Ramp Signal $r(t)$

### Mathematical Definition

$$
r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

Alternatively, $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$.

### Step-by-Step Logic
- **Why?** Models a continuously growing signal starting from zero. In medical instrumentation, a ramp is used to characterize the **linearity of amplifier response** during calibration.
- **How?** A ramp is the **integral of a step** and the **derivative of a parabola**.

## 2.4 Exponential Signal

$$
x(t) = A e^{at}
$$

### Cases of Interest

| Parameter Regime | Behavior | Biomedical Example |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $a > 0$ | Growing exponential | Tumor growth modeling |
| $a < 0$ | Decaying exponential | Drug concentration in bloodstream |
| $a = 0$ | Constant DC signal | Baseline drift in ECG |

## 2.5 Sinusoidal Signals

$$
x(t) = A \sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{or} \quad x(t) = A \cos(\omega t + \phi)
$$

where:
- $A$ = **Amplitude** (peak value)
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = **Angular frequency** (rad/s)
- $\phi$ = **Phase shift** (radians)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The ECG waveform is a quasi-periodic superposition of multiple sinusoids. The Fourier Series decomposition breaks an ECG into its constituent sinusoids, each representing a specific physiological event (P-wave, QRS, T-wave).

## 2.6 Complex Exponential Signal

$$
x(t) = e^{j\omega t} = \cos(\omega t) + j\sin(\omega t)
$$

This is the **unifying form** that connects exponentials and sinusoids via Euler's identity. Every LTI system has eigenfunctions of the form $e^{j\omega t}$, making this signal the cornerstone of frequency-domain analysis.

## 2.7 Signum Function

$$
\text{sgn}(t) = \begin{cases} +1, & t > 0 \\ 0, & t = 0 \\ -1, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Signal Name | Mathematical Form | Derivative | Integral |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Unit Step $u(t)$ | $1$ for $t \geq 0$, else $0$ | $\delta(t)$ | $r(t)$ |
| Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$ | $\infty$ at $t=0$, area = 1 | $\delta'(t)$ (doublet) | $u(t)$ |
| Unit Ramp $r(t)$ | $t$ for $t \geq 0$, else $0$ | $u(t)$ | $t^2/2$ for $t \geq 0$ |
| Exponential $e^{at}$ | $A e^{at}$ | $a A e^{at}$ | $(A/a) e^{at} + C$ |
| Sinusoid $A\sin(\omega t)$ | $A\sin(\omega t)$ | $A\omega\cos(\omega t)$ | $-(A/\omega)\cos(\omega t)$ |
| Signum $\text{sgn}(t)$ | $+1$ for $t>0$, $-1$ for $t<0$ | $2\delta(t)$ | $\vert t \vert$ |

### Inter-Relationships (Most Critical for KTU Exams)

$$
\delta(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}
$$

$$
u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

$$
r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau) \, d\tau = t \cdot u(t)
$$

$$
\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Step-Impulse Relationship

We derive the relationship $\delta(t) = \dfrac{du(t)}{dt}$ rigorously.

### Step 1 — Start with the Unit Step Definition

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Recognize the Discontinuity

The function $u(t)$ is **discontinuous** at $t = 0$, jumping from $0$ to $1$ instantaneously. Classical differentiation fails at a jump discontinuity. We need the **generalized derivative** (distribution theory).

### Step 3 — Use the Rectangular Pulse Approximation

Define a smooth approximation to the step:

$$
u_\epsilon(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < -\epsilon \\ \frac{1}{2\epsilon}\left(t + \epsilon\right), & -\epsilon \leq t \leq \epsilon \\ 1, & t > \epsilon \end{cases}
$$

This is a trapezoidal pulse that transitions from $0$ to $1$ over a width of $2\epsilon$.

### Step 4 — Differentiate the Approximation

$$
\frac{du_\epsilon(t)}{dt} = \begin{cases} 0, & t < -\epsilon \\ \frac{1}{2\epsilon}, & -\epsilon < t < \epsilon \\ 0, & t > \epsilon \end{cases}
$$

This is a **rectangular pulse** of height $\dfrac{1}{2\epsilon}$ and width $2\epsilon$.

### Step 5 — Compute the Area Under the Derivative

$$
\text{Area} = \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \times 2\epsilon = 1
$$

The area is **independent of $\epsilon$** and equals 1.

### Step 6 — Take the Limit as $\epsilon \to 0$

As $\epsilon \to 0$, the rectangle becomes infinitely tall and infinitely narrow, but its area remains 1. This is precisely the **Dirac delta**:

$$
\lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{du_\epsilon(t)}{dt} = \delta(t)
$$

### Step 7 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{\frac{du(t)}{dt} = \delta(t)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: The Ramp-Step Relationship

### Step 1 — Integrate the Unit Step

By definition:

$$
r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate the Integral in Two Regions

**Case A: $t < 0$**

$$
r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} 0 \, d\tau = 0
$$

**Case B: $t \geq 0$**

$$
r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} 0 \, d\tau + \int_{0}^{t} 1 \, d\tau = 0 + (t - 0) = t
$$

### Step 3 — Combine into a Compact Form

$$
\boxed{r(t) = t \cdot u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ t, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Signum Function in Terms of Step Function

### Step 1 — Express the Signum Function

$$
\text{sgn}(t) = \begin{cases} -1, & t < 0 \\ 0, & t = 0 \\ +1, & t > 0 \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Build Using Step Functions

We can construct the $+1$ portion using $u(t)$ and the $-1$ portion using $u(-t)$:

$$
\text{sgn}(t) = u(t) - u(-t)
$$

### Step 3 — Derive the Alternative Form

Note that $u(t) + u(-t) = 1$ for $t \neq 0$ (one of them is always 1, the other always 0).

Therefore:

$$
u(-t) = 1 - u(t)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{sgn}(t) = u(t) - [1 - u(t)] = 2u(t) - 1
$$

### Step 4 — Final Compact Form

$$
\boxed{\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Time-Shifted Rectangular Pulse Using Step Functions

A rectangular pulse of width $T$ starting at $t = 0$ can be constructed by **subtracting two shifted step functions**:

### Step 1 — Write the Pulse Definition

$$
p(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

### Step 2 — Construct Using Steps

A step "turns on" at $t = 0$, and another step "turns off" at $t = T$:

$$
p(t) = u(t) - u(t - T)
$$

### Step 3 — Verify

- For $t < 0$: $u(t) = 0$ and $u(t-T) = 0$ → $p(t) = 0$ ✓
- For $0 \leq t < T$: $u(t) = 1$ and $u(t-T) = 0$ → $p(t) = 1$ ✓
- For $t \geq T$: $u(t) = 1$ and $u(t-T) = 1$ → $p(t) = 0$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Application:** A rectangular pulse train is used to model the **square-wave stimulation pulses** in Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation (TENS) units and neuromuscular electrical stimulators.

## 3.5 Derivation: The Sampling Property of the Impulse

### Step 1 — Statement

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)
$$

### Step 2 — Use the Sifting Argument

The impulse $\delta(t - t_0)$ is zero everywhere except at $t = t_0$. Thus, the integrand $x(t)\delta(t - t_0)$ is zero everywhere except at $t = t_0$.

### Step 3 — Extract the Value of $x(t)$ at $t = t_0$

Since $x(t)$ is continuous at $t_0$ (assumed), it can be replaced by its value $x(t_0)$ within the integral:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t_0) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - t_0) \, dt
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Area Property of Impulse

Since the area of $\delta(t - t_0)$ is 1:

$$
x(t_0) \cdot 1 = x(t_0)
$$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$
\boxed{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Elementary Signal Generation Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the **hierarchical structure of elementary signals** and their derivative/integral inter-relationships. This block diagram is essential for KTU Module 1 problems involving signal manipulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impulse Function delta of t] -->|Integration| B[Step Function u of t]
    B -->|Integration| C[Ramp Function r of t]
    C -->|Integration| D[Parabolic Function t squared by 2]
    A -->|Differentiation| E[Doublet delta prime of t]
    B -->|Differentiation| A
    C -->|Differentiation| B
    B -->|Time Shift| F[Delayed Step u of t minus t0]
    A -->|Time Shift| G[Shifted Impulse delta of t minus t0]
    B -->|Linear Combination| H[Rectangular Pulse]
    H -->|u of t minus u of t minus T| I[Width T Pulse]
    B -->|Signum Construction| J[Signum Function]
    J -->|2u of t minus 1| B
    K[Exponential e power at] -->|Special Case| L[Sinusoid cos wt plus j sin wt]
    L -->|Euler Formula| M[Complex Exponential e power jwt]
```

## 4.2 Waveform Sketches — Mermaid Representation

Since Mermaid does not natively support continuous waveform plotting, the following **block-level schematic** represents the logical states and transitions of each elementary signal over time.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STEP["Unit Step u of t"]
        S1[Region t less than 0: Amplitude 0] --> S2[Jump at t equals 0]
        S2 --> S3[Region t greater than 0: Amplitude 1]
    end
    subgraph IMPULSE["Unit Impulse delta of t"]
        I1[Region t less than 0: Value 0] --> I2[Spike at t equals 0: Infinite Height, Area 1]
        I2 --> I3[Region t greater than 0: Value 0]
    end
    subgraph RAMP["Unit Ramp r of t"]
        R1[Region t less than 0: Value 0] --> R2[Linear Growth for t greater than 0]
        R2 --> R3[Slope equals 1, Passes Through Origin]
    end
    subgraph EXP["Exponential e power at"]
        E1[If a greater than 0: Growth] --> E2[If a less than 0: Decay]
        E2 --> E3[If a equals 0: Constant DC]
    end
```

## 4.3 Signal Transformation Block Diagram

The following diagram maps the **mathematical operations** that connect one elementary signal to another, providing a quick visual reference for problem-solving.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OP1[Differentiation Operator d/dt] -->|Acts On| SIG1[Unit Step]
    SIG1 -->|Result| RES1[Unit Impulse]
    OP2[Integration Operator Integral] -->|Acts On| SIG2[Unit Impulse]
    SIG2 -->|Result| RES2[Unit Step]
    OP3[Time Shift t0] -->|Acts On| SIG3[Any Elementary Signal]
    SIG3 -->|Result| RES3[Shifted Version]
    OP4[Linear Combination] -->|Acts On| SIG4[Two Step Functions]
    SIG4 -->|Result| RES4[Rectangular Pulse]
    OP5[Scaling by Amplitude A] -->|Acts On| SIG5[Sinusoid]
    SIG5 -->|Result| RES5[Amplified Sinusoid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When a KTU question asks to "express the given waveform using elementary signals," follow this pipeline: (1) Identify jump discontinuities → place impulses; (2) Identify level changes → place step functions; (3) Identify linear slopes → place ramp functions; (4) Combine with appropriate time shifts and amplitude scalings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Define the unit impulse signal. State and prove its sampling property.**

**Model Answer:**

The unit impulse signal $\delta(t)$ is defined as:

$$
\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$

with the property:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t) \, dt = 1
$$

**Sampling Property:**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0)
$$

**Proof:** Since $\delta(t - t_0)$ is zero everywhere except at $t = t_0$, the integrand $x(t)\delta(t - t_0)$ contributes only at $t = t_0$. Extracting the constant value $x(t_0)$:

$$
x(t_0) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0) \cdot 1 = x(t_0)
$$

Hence proved. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Express the signum function in terms of the unit step function and sketch both waveforms.**

**Model Answer:**

The signum function is expressed as:

$$
\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1
$$

**Verification:**
- For $t < 0$: $u(t) = 0$, so $\text{sgn}(t) = 0 - 1 = -1$ ✓
- For $t \geq 0$: $u(t) = 1$, so $\text{sgn}(t) = 2 - 1 = +1$ ✓

**Sketch:** The signum function is $-1$ for all negative time, jumps to $+1$ at $t = 0$, and remains $+1$ thereafter. The unit step is $0$ for $t < 0$ and jumps to $1$ at $t = 0$. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Define the following elementary signals with neat sketches: (i) Unit step, (ii) Unit impulse, (iii) Unit ramp. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical relationships $\delta(t) = \dfrac{du(t)}{dt}$ and $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ with proper justification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**(i) Unit Step Signal $u(t)$:**

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

The waveform shows a horizontal line at amplitude 0 for $t < 0$, a vertical jump of magnitude 1 at $t = 0$, and a horizontal line at amplitude 1 for $t \geq 0$.

**(ii) Unit Impulse Signal $\delta(t)$:**

$$
\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty, & t = 0 \\ 0, & t \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$

with $\displaystyle \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t) \, dt = 1$.

The waveform shows a single vertical arrow of height equal to the area (i.e., strength 1) located exactly at $t = 0$, with zero amplitude everywhere else.

**(iii) Unit Ramp Signal $r(t)$:**

$$
r(t) = \begin{cases} t, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}
$$

The waveform shows zero amplitude for $t < 0$ and a straight line with slope 1 passing through the origin for $t \geq 0$. **(7 marks — 2+2+3 for definitions and sketches)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Derivation of $\delta(t) = \dfrac{du(t)}{dt}$:**

The unit step $u(t)$ has a jump discontinuity of magnitude 1 at $t = 0$. To differentiate a discontinuous function, we use the **distribution theory** approach via the rectangular pulse approximation.

Consider a smooth approximation:

$$
u_\epsilon(t) = \frac{1}{2\epsilon}\left[t + \epsilon\right] \quad \text{for} \quad -\epsilon \leq t \leq \epsilon
$$

Differentiating:

$$
\frac{du_\epsilon(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\epsilon} \quad \text{for} \quad -\epsilon \leq t \leq \epsilon
$$

This is a rectangle of width $2\epsilon$ and height $\dfrac{1}{2\epsilon}$, with area = 1.

Taking the limit:

$$
\lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{du_\epsilon(t)}{dt} = \delta(t)
$$

**Derivation of $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$:**

The ramp is defined as the integral of the step:

$$
r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Case 1: $t < 0$**

$$
r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} 0 \, d\tau = 0
$$

**Case 2: $t \geq 0$**

$$
r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} 0 \, d\tau + \int_{0}^{t} 1 \, d\tau = t
$$

Combining:

$$
r(t) = t \cdot u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ t, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

**(7 marks — 4 for impulse-step derivation + 3 for ramp-step derivation)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Express a rectangular pulse of width $T$ and amplitude $A$ starting at $t = 0$ using elementary signals. Sketch the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 2u(t) - 3u(t-2) + u(t-4)$ is given. Sketch $x(t)$ and find its value at $t = 1$, $t = 3$, and $t = 5$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

A rectangular pulse of width $T$ and amplitude $A$ is "on" between $t = 0$ and $t = T$.

**Construction using step functions:**

$$
p(t) = A \left[ u(t) - u(t - T) \right]
$$

**Verification:**
- For $t < 0$: Both steps are 0, so $p(t) = 0$
- For $0 \leq t < T$: First step is 1, second is 0, so $p(t) = A \cdot 1 = A$
- For $t \geq T$: Both steps are 1, so $p(t) = A(1 - 1) = 0$

**Sketch:** A horizontal rectangle of height $A$ extending from $t = 0$ to $t = T$, with zero amplitude elsewhere. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Evaluating at key time points:**

**At $t = 1$:**

- $u(1) = 1$
- $u(1 - 2) = u(-1) = 0$
- $u(1 - 4) = u(-3) = 0$

$$
x(1) = 2(1) - 3(0) + 1(0) = 2
$$

**At $t = 3$:**

- $u(3) = 1$
- $u(3 - 2) = u(1) = 1$
- $u(3 - 4) = u(-1) = 0$

$$
x(3) = 2(1) - 3(1) + 1(0) = 2 - 3 = -1
$$

**At $t = 5$:**

- $u(5) = 1$
- $u(5 - 2) = u(3) = 1$
- $u(5 - 4) = u(1) = 1$

$$
x(5) = 2(1) - 3(1) + 1(1) = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0
$$

**Sketch Description:**
- For $t < 0$: $x(t) = 0$
- For $0 \leq t < 2$: $x(t) = 2$ (only the first step contributes)
- For $2 \leq t < 4$: $x(t) = 2 - 3 = -1$ (first two steps contribute)
- For $t \geq 4$: $x(t) = 2 - 3 + 1 = 0$ (all three steps contribute and cancel)

**(7 marks — 3 for values + 4 for sketch and intervals)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the area property of the impulse** — Many students write $\delta(t) = \infty$ without specifying that the integral equals 1. This costs **1 mark** in Part A and can lose **2 marks** in Part B.
>
> 2. **Confusing $u(0)$** — By convention, $u(0) = 1$ (not 0 or 0.5). This is the standard KTU convention and forgetting it leads to off-by-one errors in pulse construction problems.
>
> 3. **Wrong sign in $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$** — Some students write $\text{sgn}(t) = 1 - 2u(t)$, which gives the **opposite** waveform. Always verify at $t = -1$ and $t = 1$.
>
> 4. **Mixing up $r(t)$ with $t$** — The ramp $r(t)$ is defined as $t \cdot u(t)$, NOT just $t$. For $t < 0$, $r(t) = 0$, not negative values.
>
> 5. **Missing the scaling property** — $\delta(at) = \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} \delta(t)$ is frequently tested. The absolute value bars are **essential** — omitting them costs full marks.
>
> 6. **Incorrect time-shift direction** — $u(t - 3)$ is a step delayed by 3 units (turns on at $t = 3$), not a step that turns on at $t = 0$ and continues for 3 units.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Elementary Signals**
>
> - **Unit Step $u(t)$:** Equals 1 for $t \geq 0$, else 0. Discontinuous jump at origin.
> - **Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$:** Infinite at $t=0$, zero elsewhere, area = 1. Sampling property extracts signal values.
> - **Unit Ramp $r(t)$:** Equals $t$ for $t \geq 0$, else 0. Linear growth with unit slope.
> - **Exponential $e^{at}$:** Growth if $a > 0$, decay if $a < 0$, DC if $a = 0$.
> - **Sinusoids:** $A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ and $A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$ are periodic with period $T = 2\pi/\omega$.
> - **Complex Exponential:** $e^{j\omega t} = \cos(\omega t) + j\sin(\omega t)$ via Euler's formula. Eigenfunction of LTI systems.
> - **Signum Function:** $\text{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1$. Takes values $+1$ and $-1$.
> - **Critical Relationships:** $\delta(t) = du(t)/dt$, $u(t) = \int \delta(t) dt$, $r(t) = \int u(t) dt = t \cdot u(t)$.
> - **Pulse Construction:** Rectangular pulse = $A[u(t) - u(t-T)]$.
> - **Scaling Rule:** $\delta(at) = \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} \delta(t)$ — always include absolute value.
> - **Time Shift:** $f(t - t_0)$ is a **rightward** shift (delay) by $t_0$ units.
> - **Biomedical Context:** ECG/EEG signals can be decomposed into sums of these elementary waveforms using Fourier analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Signal Processing Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Signal Processing Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCBMT501 – Biomedical Signal Processing (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, *Typical Signal Processing Operations* refers to the standardized, sequential set of mathematical and computational transformations applied to raw bioelectric and biomechanical signals (such as **ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, PCG, and BP signals**) to extract clinically meaningful information. These operations form the foundational toolkit of every biomedical instrumentation pipeline and typically include:

- **Signal Amplification** (Instrumentation Amplifiers)
- **Filtering** (Analog and Digital: LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF)
- **Sampling and Quantization** (A/D Conversion)
- **Signal Transformation** (Fourier, Laplace, Z-Transforms)
- **Correlation and Convolution** (Time-domain analysis)
- **Signal Averaging** (Ensemble averaging for SNR enhancement)
- **Spectral Analysis** (FFT, Power Spectral Density)
- **Feature Extraction and Classification**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The phrase *Typical Signal Processing Operations* explicitly refers to the operations performed on deterministic and stochastic signals to extract diagnostic information. Mastery of these operations is essential for answering Module 1 long-answer questions (14 marks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor listening to heart sounds** through a stethoscope in a noisy emergency room:
- The **raw heart sound** is buried in ambient noise.
- You **amplify** the heart sound (signal amplification).
- You mentally **filter out** the high-frequency clatter (filtering).
- You **repeat the listening** several times and mentally average them to identify a faint murmur (signal averaging).
- You **compare** today's heartbeat with a reference pattern (correlation).
- You **decompose** the sound into its pitch and timbre components (Fourier analysis).

This exact sequence — **Amplify → Filter → Sample → Transform → Analyze** — is the *typical signal processing chain* applied to every biomedical signal before clinical interpretation.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in Biomedical Signal Processing

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard metrics govern the design and evaluation of all biomedical signal processing operations (as per KTU 2024 Module 1 emphasis):

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Range (Bioelectric) |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Frequency | $F_s$ | **250 Hz – 10 kHz** |
| Signal Bandwidth | $BW$ | **0.05 Hz – 1500 Hz** |
| Signal Amplitude | $A$ | **µV to mV range** |
| Input SNR | $SNR_{in}$ | **0 dB – 40 dB** |
| Quantization Bits | $n$ | **8 – 16 bits** |
| Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $CMRR$ | **> 80 dB** |

The **typical ECG bandwidth** is **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** with amplitude around **1 mV**, while the **EEG bandwidth** extends from **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz** with amplitudes in the **µV range**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block-by-Block Breakdown

### **Operation 1: Signal Amplification**
- **Why**: Bioelectric signals are tiny (µV–mV); they must be boosted to usable voltage levels (V).
- **How**: Instrumentation amplifiers (3-Op-Amp topology) with high **CMRR** and high input impedance are used.
- **Gain Equation**:

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$$

### **Operation 2: Filtering**
- **Why**: Remove physiological and environmental noise (50/60 Hz line interference, baseline wander, motion artifacts).
- **How**: Apply analog (RC, active) or digital (FIR, IIR) filters.
- **Transfer Function**:

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$$

### **Operation 3: Sampling and Quantization**
- **Why**: Convert continuous-time analog signals to discrete digital form for computer analysis.
- **How**: An ADC samples at $F_s$ and quantizes to $2^n$ levels.
- **Nyquist Criterion**:

$$F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$$

### **Operation 4: Signal Transformation (Fourier)**
- **Why**: Convert time-domain signal to frequency-domain for spectral analysis.
- **How**: Apply Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) or its fast algorithm (FFT).
- **DFT Equation**:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$$

### **Operation 5: Convolution**
- **Why**: Model the output of an LTI system when a signal passes through it.
- **How**: Compute the convolution of input signal $x[n]$ with impulse response $h[n]$.

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n-k]$$

### **Operation 6: Correlation**
- **Why**: Measure similarity between two signals (cross-correlation) or with itself at different time shifts (autocorrelation).
- **How**: Apply the correlation formula.

$$R_{xy}[\tau] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, y[n+\tau]$$

### **Operation 7: Signal (Ensemble) Averaging**
- **Why**: Improve SNR by averaging multiple repetitions of a periodic event-related signal (e.g., evoked potentials).
- **How**: Time-locked averaging of M trials.

$$s_{avg}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} x_m[n]$$

### **Operation 8: Windowing**
- **Why**: Reduce spectral leakage in FFT analysis when a signal is not exactly periodic in the observation window.
- **How**: Multiply the signal by a window function $w[n]$ (Hamming, Hanning, Blackman, Rectangular).

$$x_w[n] = x[n] \cdot w[n]$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Mandatory for Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – High-Yield Formula Table for Module 1.** Memorize these verbatim for full marks.

| Operation | Core Formula | Key Symbol / Variable | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amplifier Gain (INA) | $A_v = 1 + 2R_1 / R_{gain}$ | $R_1$, $R_{gain}$ | Unitless |
| Nyquist Rate | $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ | $F_s$, $F_{max}$ | Hz |
| Quantization Step | $\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | $V_{FS}$: Full-scale voltage; $n$: bits | Volts |
| DFT | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}$ | $k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ | – |
| IDFT | $x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] e^{j 2\pi kn / N}$ | $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ | – |
| Convolution | $y[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] h[n-k]$ | $x[n]$: input; $h[n]$: impulse response | – |
| Cross-Correlation | $R_{xy}[\tau] = \sum_{n} x[n] y[n+\tau]$ | $\tau$: lag | – |
| Autocorrelation | $R_{xx}[\tau] = \sum_{n} x[n] x[n+\tau]$ | – | – |
| Ensemble Average | $s_{avg}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} x_m[n]$ | $M$: number of trials | – |
| SNR Improvement (Averaging) | $SNR_{out} = M \cdot SNR_{in}$ | $M$: number of averages | dB / linear |
| Hamming Window | $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi n / N)$ | $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$ | – |
| Hanning Window | $w[n] = 0.5 \left( 1 - \cos(2\pi n / N) \right)$ | – | – |
| Power Spectral Density | $P[f] = \frac{\vert X[f] \vert^2}{N}$ | $\vert X[f] \vert$: magnitude | Watts/Hz |
| Z-Transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | $z = r e^{j\omega}$ | – |
| FIR Filter Output | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} b_k x[n-k]$ | $b_k$: filter coefficients | – |

> **Note on Table:** In the *Convolution* row above, the symbol $\vert$ denotes the magnitude operator (e.g., $\vert X[f] \vert$). This is *not* a table column separator.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These operations are **indispensable** in the design of:
- **Patient monitoring systems** (ICU monitors, Holter recorders)
- **Implantable devices** (pacemakers, cochlear implants)
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)**
- **Medical imaging pre-processing** (CT, MRI denoising)
- **Rehabilitation robotics** (EMG-based prosthetic control)
- **Telehealth devices** (wearable ECG patches)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU examinations, questions often interlink multiple operations. For example: *"Explain how the FFT is used in the spectral analysis of an EEG signal to detect alpha rhythms."* This requires knowledge of **Sampling → Windowing → FFT → Power Spectrum**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SNR Improvement of Ensemble Averaging

**Problem Setup:**
A noisy bioelectric signal $x_m[n]$ is the sum of the true signal $s[n]$ and zero-mean uncorrelated noise $n_m[n]$:

$$x_m[n] = s[n] + n_m[n]$$

The ensemble average is:

$$s_{avg}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} x_m[n]$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1: Substitute the model of $x_m[n]$:

$$s_{avg}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \big( s[n] + n_m[n] \big)$$

Step 2: Split the summation into signal and noise components:

$$s_{avg}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} s[n] + \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} n_m[n]$$

Step 3: Since $s[n]$ is the same deterministic signal across all trials, it factors out:

$$s_{avg}[n] = s[n] \cdot \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} 1 + \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} n_m[n]$$

Step 4: The first term becomes simply $s[n]$:

$$s_{avg}[n] = s[n] + \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} n_m[n]$$

Step 5: Define the averaged noise as $\bar{n}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} n_m[n]$.

Step 6: The variance of the averaged noise is:

$$\sigma_{\bar{n}}^{2} = \frac{1}{M^{2}} \sum_{m=1}^{M} \sigma_{n}^{2} = \frac{M \cdot \sigma_{n}^{2}}{M^{2}} = \frac{\sigma_{n}^{2}}{M}$$

Step 7: The signal variance remains $\sigma_{s}^{2}$ (signal is unchanged).

Step 8: Therefore, the SNR of the averaged signal is:

$$SNR_{out} = \frac{\sigma_{s}^{2}}{\sigma_{\bar{n}}^{2}} = \frac{\sigma_{s}^{2}}{\sigma_{n}^{2} / M} = M \cdot SNR_{in}$$

**Conclusion:**

$$SNR_{out} = M \cdot SNR_{in} \quad \text{or in dB:} \quad SNR_{out,dB} = SNR_{in,dB} + 10 \log_{10}(M)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Averaging **100 trials** improves SNR by a factor of **100** (or **20 dB**). This is the principle behind **Evoked Potential (EP) extraction** in EEG.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Convolution of an ECG Signal with a Filter Impulse Response

**Problem Setup:**
Given an input signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and an impulse response $h[n] = \{1, -1\}$, compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1: Write the convolution formula:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{3} x[k] \, h[n-k]$$

Step 2: The output length is $N_y = N_x + N_h - 1 = 4 + 2 - 1 = 5$. So $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

Step 3: Compute $y[0]$ (only $k=0$ contributes since $h[0-k]$ is defined for $k=0$):

$$y[0] = x[0] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$$

Step 4: Compute $y[1]$ (k = 0, 1 contribute):

$$y[1] = x[0] h[1] + x[1] h[0] = 1(-1) + 2(1) = 1$$

Step 5: Compute $y[2]$ (k = 0, 1, 2 contribute):

$$y[2] = x[0] h[2] + x[1] h[1] + x[2] h[0] = 0 + 2(-1) + 3(1) = 1$$

Step 6: Compute $y[3]$ (k = 1, 2, 3 contribute):

$$y[3] = x[1] h[2] + x[2] h[1] + x[3] h[0] = 0 + 3(-1) + 4(1) = 1$$

Step 7: Compute $y[4]$ (k = 3 contributes):

$$y[4] = x[3] h[1] = 4(-1) = -4$$

Step 8: Final result:

$$y[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1, -4\}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Autocorrelation of a Sinusoidal Signal

**Problem Setup:**
Given $x[n] = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$, derive the autocorrelation $R_{xx}[\tau]$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1: Write the autocorrelation definition:

$$R_{xx}[\tau] = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} x[n] \, x[n+\tau]$$

Step 2: Substitute $x[n]$:

$$R_{xx}[\tau] = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi) \cdot A \cos(\omega_0 (n+\tau) + \phi)$$

Step 3: Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(\alpha-\beta) + \cos(\alpha+\beta)]$:

$$R_{xx}[\tau] = \frac{A^{2}}{2} \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \big[ \cos(\omega_0 \tau) + \cos(2\omega_0 n + \omega_0 \tau + 2\phi) \big]$$

Step 4: The second term averages to zero over a full period (assuming $\omega_0$ is a rational multiple of $2\pi$):

$$R_{xx}[\tau] = \frac{A^{2}}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau)$$

**Conclusion:**

$$R_{xx}[\tau] = \frac{A^{2}}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The autocorrelation of a sinusoid is **another sinusoid** of the **same frequency** with amplitude $\frac{A^{2}}{2}$ — a key result for detecting periodicity in biomedical signals (e.g., respiratory cycles, heart rate variability).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Operation

| Clinical Goal | Recommended Operation | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Detect QRS complex in ECG | **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz) + Thresholding** | Isolates QRS energy, suppresses baseline wander and HF noise |
| Extract auditory evoked potentials (AEP) | **Ensemble Averaging (M = 1000)** | SNR too low for single trial; averaging boosts SNR by 30 dB |
| Find dominant EEG rhythms (alpha, beta) | **FFT + Power Spectral Density** | Frequency-domain features of EEG are diagnostically meaningful |
| Reduce 50 Hz powerline interference | **Notch Filter (BSF at 50 Hz)** | Targeted removal of narrowband interference |
| Measure heart rate variability (HRV) | **Autocorrelation / PSD of R-R intervals** | Periodicity analysis of R-peaks |
| Detect motion artifacts | **High-pass Filter (cutoff > 0.5 Hz)** | Removes low-frequency baseline drift |
| Compress ECG for storage | **Linear Prediction / Wavelet Transform** | Removes redundancy in waveform |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define signal averaging. How does it improve the SNR of a noisy biomedical signal?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Signal averaging (also called ensemble averaging) is a time-domain signal processing technique in which multiple repetitions (trials) of a time-locked, noisy bioelectric signal are summed and divided by the number of trials to obtain an estimate of the underlying true signal.

**Mathematical Expression (1 mark):**

$$s_{avg}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} x_m[n]$$

**SNR Improvement (1 mark):** If the noise in each trial is zero-mean and uncorrelated, the SNR of the averaged signal improves by a factor of $M$:

$$SNR_{out} = M \cdot SNR_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad SNR_{out,dB} = SNR_{in,dB} + 10 \log_{10}(M)$$

**Application:** Used in **Evoked Potential (EP) recording**, where single-trial SNR is too low (e.g., auditory brainstem response).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between convolution and correlation of two discrete signals. Give one biomedical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Convolution | Correlation |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical Form | $y[n] = \sum_k x[k] h[n-k]$ | $R_{xy}[\tau] = \sum_n x[n] y[n+\tau]$ |
| Operation | Flips one signal before shifting | Does NOT flip; just shifts |
| Output Meaning | Output of an LTI system | Measure of similarity |
| Biomedical App | Modeling **ECG through the body** (system response) | Detecting **R-R intervals** for HRV analysis |

**Key Difference (1 mark):** Convolution involves time-reversal of one signal ($h[n-k]$), while correlation is a non-flipped shift operation ($y[n+\tau]$).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

#### **Part (a)** [7 Marks]
**With a neat block diagram, explain the typical operations performed in the processing of a biomedical signal. Mention at least six operations in sequence.**

**Model Answer:**

A typical biomedical signal (e.g., ECG) undergoes the following sequential operations:

1. **Acquisition** — Bioelectric signal picked up by surface electrodes (e.g., Ag/AgCl).
2. **Pre-amplification** — Boosted by an **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** with high CMRR (> 80 dB) and high input impedance (> 10 MΩ).
3. **Analog Filtering** — Bandpass filtering (e.g., **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** for ECG) removes baseline wander and high-frequency noise.
4. **Sampling and Quantization (A/D Conversion)** — Signal is sampled at $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ (Nyquist) and quantized to **12-bit** levels.
5. **Digital Filtering** — Further noise removal using **FIR/IIR** filters.
6. **Feature Extraction** — Detection of R-peaks, QRS complex, T-wave boundaries using algorithms like the **Pan-Tompkins algorithm**.
7. **Classification / Diagnosis** — Beat classification (Normal, PVC, APC) using decision rules or neural networks.
8. **Display / Storage** — Waveform displayed on monitor; data archived in HL7/FHIR format.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
Electrodes → Pre-Amp → Analog BPF → ADC → Digital Filter → Feature Extraction → Classification → Display
```

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Each operation refines the signal progressively, transforming raw bioelectric activity into clinically actionable information.

---

#### **Part (b)** [7 Marks]
**Explain the concept of ensemble averaging with a derivation of its SNR improvement property. How is it applied in extracting Auditory Evoked Potentials (AEP)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Ensemble averaging is the time-locked summation of M noisy trials of a stimulus-evoked signal. Since the true signal $s[n]$ is deterministic and identical in each trial, while the noise $n_m[n]$ is random and zero-mean, averaging preserves the signal and suppresses the noise.

**Mathematical Model:**

$$x_m[n] = s[n] + n_m[n], \quad m = 1, 2, \ldots, M$$

$$s_{avg}[n] = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} x_m[n] = s[n] + \frac{1}{M} \sum_{m=1}^{M} n_m[n]$$

**SNR Derivation (3 marks):** Since noise samples are uncorrelated with variance $\sigma_n^2$:

$$\sigma_{\bar{n}}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_n^2}{M} \quad \Rightarrow \quad SNR_{out} = M \cdot SNR_{in}$$

In decibels:

$$SNR_{out,dB} = SNR_{in,dB} + 10 \log_{10}(M)$$

**Application to AEP (2 marks):** AEPs are tiny (1 µV or less) and buried in ongoing EEG noise. By presenting M = 1000 auditory clicks and averaging the EEG epochs time-locked to each click, the SNR improves by **30 dB**, revealing the characteristic AEP waveform (Wave I–V).

**Conclusion:** Ensemble averaging is the gold-standard technique for extracting periodic, stimulus-locked bioelectric events from noise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

#### **Part (a)** [7 Marks]
**Explain the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT). Derive the N-point DFT equation and explain its significance in biomedical signal analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The DFT converts a finite-length discrete-time signal $x[n]$ of length $N$ into its discrete frequency-domain representation $X[k]$, evaluated at $N$ equally spaced frequencies.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Starting from the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) and sampling the frequency variable at $\omega_k = 2\pi k / N$:

$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j 2\pi kn / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

The inverse transform is:

$$x[n] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, e^{j 2\pi kn / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

**Properties (1 mark):**
- Linearity, periodicity ($X[k+N] = X[k]$), symmetry.
- Computational complexity: $O(N^2)$ for direct DFT, reduced to $O(N \log_2 N)$ using the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**.

**Significance in Biomedical Signal Analysis (1 mark):** Used in **ECG rhythm analysis** (detecting dominant frequencies), **EEG spectral bands** (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma), and **PCG heart sound analysis**.

---

#### **Part (b)** [7 Marks]
**With necessary equations, explain the concept of windowing in signal processing. Why is the Hamming window preferred over the rectangular window for FFT analysis of biomedical signals?**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Windowing is the multiplication of a finite-length signal $x[n]$ with a window function $w[n]$ to taper the signal edges, thereby reducing **spectral leakage** during FFT analysis.

$$x_w[n] = x[n] \cdot w[n]$$

**Common Window Functions (2 marks):**

| Window | Equation | Main Lobe Width | Side Lobe Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $w[n] = 1$ | $4\pi / N$ | −13 dB |
| Hanning | $w[n] = 0.5(1 - \cos(2\pi n / N))$ | $8\pi / N$ | −31 dB |
| **Hamming** | $w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi n / N)$ | $8\pi / N$ | **−41 dB** |

**Why Hamming over Rectangular (2 marks):** The rectangular window has abrupt discontinuities at the edges, which produce **high spectral leakage** (−13 dB side lobes), masking weak frequency components (e.g., beta rhythms in EEG). The **Hamming window** provides a much lower side-lobe level (**−41 dB**) at the cost of a slightly wider main lobe, making it the preferred choice for biomedical spectral analysis.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Proper windowing ensures accurate frequency-domain representation, critical for clinical diagnostics based on spectral content.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing convolution with correlation** — students often write the correlation formula but label it as convolution. Always check whether the second signal is **time-reversed** ($h[n-k]$) or not ($y[n+\tau]$).
> 2. **Forgetting to justify the SNR improvement** in averaging — simply writing "$M$ times" without the **variance derivation** costs 1–2 marks. Always show the $\sigma_{\bar{n}}^{2} = \sigma_n^2 / M$ step.
> 3. **Mixing up Z-transform and Fourier transform** — the Z-transform is the **generalized** form; Fourier is obtained by setting $z = e^{j\omega}$ on the unit circle.
> 4. **Ignoring the Nyquist criterion** — failing to state $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ in sampling questions is a frequent deduction.
> 5. **Writing the IDFT without the $\frac{1}{N}$ normalization factor** — KTU examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 6. **Not stating units** for sampling frequency (Hz) or amplitudes (µV/mV) — always include units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 1 — Typical Signal Processing Operations: Quick Recall List**

- **Signal Amplification** uses INAs; gain: $A_v = 1 + 2R_1 / R_{gain}$.
- **Filtering**: LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF — analog (RC/active) and digital (FIR/IIR).
- **Sampling Theorem (Nyquist)**: $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Quantization step**: $\Delta = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$; resolution improves with bits.
- **DFT** converts time-domain to frequency-domain; FFT is the fast algorithm in $O(N \log_2 N)$.
- **IDFT** has $\frac{1}{N}$ normalization — never omit it.
- **Convolution** ($x[n] * h[n]$) models an LTI system output — one signal is **flipped**.
- **Correlation** ($R_{xy}[\tau]$) measures similarity — **no flipping** of the second signal.
- **Autocorrelation** of a sinusoid: $R_{xx}[\tau] = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(\omega_0 \tau)$ — same frequency, halved amplitude-squared.
- **Ensemble Averaging** improves SNR by factor $M$: $SNR_{out} = M \cdot SNR_{in}$ (or $10 \log_{10} M$ dB).
- **Windowing** reduces spectral leakage; **Hamming** window is preferred for biomedical FFT.
- **PSD**: $P[f] = \frac{\vert X[f] \vert^2}{N}$ — power distribution across frequencies.
- **Z-Transform**: $X(z) = \sum x[n] z^{-n}$ — general form; Fourier = Z on unit circle.
- **Typical ECG bandwidth**: 0.05 – 150 Hz; **EEG bandwidth**: 0.5 – 100 Hz; **EMG bandwidth**: 20 – 500 Hz.
- **CMRR** of biomedical amplifiers must be > 80 dB.
- **Signal chain order**: Acquire → Amplify → Filter → Sample → Quantize → Process → Extract → Classify → Display.
- **Ensemble averaging is essential** for evoked potentials (AEP, VEP, SEP) extraction.
- **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** is the standard for real-time QRS detection in ECG.
- **FIR vs IIR**: FIR is always stable and linear-phase; IIR is more efficient but can be unstable.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Typical Signal Processing Operations**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Biomedical Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of Biomedical Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Biomedical Signal

A **biomedical signal** is a time-varying or spatially-varying measurable physical quantity that originates from a living biological system (human, animal, or plant) and carries diagnostic, therapeutic, or physiological information about that system. These signals are typically low-frequency, low-amplitude, and contaminated by noise, making them ideal candidates for advanced digital signal processing techniques.

Mathematically, a continuous-time biomedical signal can be represented as a function of time and space:
$$x(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$

where $s(t)$ is the true underlying physiological signal of interest and $n(t)$ represents the corrupting noise (e.g., 50/60 Hz powerline interference, baseline wander, motion artefacts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):** Biomedical signals are recordings of physiological activities of living organisms, characterized by their non-stationary nature, low amplitude (often in the $\mu V$ to $mV$ range), and low frequency content (typically $0.1 \text{ Hz}$ to $10 \text{ kHz}$).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Body as a Symphony Orchestra"

Think of the human body as a **grand symphony orchestra**. Each instrument (heart, brain, muscles, lungs) plays its own unique "melody" — the biomedical signal. A cardiologist, like a conductor, listens to the heart's rhythm (the **ECG**), while a neurologist tracks the brain's electrical symphony (the **EEG**). Just as microphones are needed to record an orchestra, electrodes, transducers, and sensors act as the "microphones" of the human body. However, unlike a clean studio recording, these biological "performances" are full of background noise — muscle tremors, electrical hum, patient movement — which is exactly why **Biomedical Signal Processing** is such a vital engineering discipline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU examinations:** The examiner expects you to associate each signal with **(i) its origin, (ii) its typical frequency range, (iii) its amplitude range, and (iv) its clinical use**. Memorizing these four parameters for each signal is a guaranteed way to score full marks.

## 1.3 The Two Fundamental Classes of Biomedical Signals

| Class | Description | Examples |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| **Deterministic / Quasi-deterministic** | Signals that can be described by explicit mathematical functions; repeated patterns across cycles | ECG, repetitive EEG rhythms, pulse waveforms |
| **Stochastic / Random** | Signals characterized by statistical properties (mean, variance, PSD) | EEG during deep sleep, EMG during sustained contraction, speech |

A further critical sub-classification used in KTU Module 1 is:

- **Stationary signals:** Statistical properties invariant with time (e.g., steady-state ECG).
- **Non-stationary signals:** Statistical properties change with time (e.g., EEG during a seizure, ECG under stress).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Classify the signal," always state *both* the deterministic/stochastic nature *and* the stationary/non-stationary nature — this is a classic two-step answer pattern worth **2 + 2 = 4 marks** in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Biomedical Signals

Biomedical signals are classified by their **physical origin** into six primary families. Each family represents a different energy domain.

### 2.1.1 Bioelectric Signals
These arise from the **electrochemical activity of excitable cells** (neurons, muscle fibers, cardiac pacemaker cells). They are recorded using **surface or needle electrodes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operational Principle — The Nernst Equation Foundation:** The basis of all bioelectric signals is the differential ion concentration ($\text{Na}^+$, $\text{K}^+$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$, $\text{Cl}^-$) across a semi-permeable cell membrane, generating a transmembrane potential given by the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz voltage equation**.

**Examples:**
- **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** — Heart's electrical activity
- **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** — Brain's electrical activity
- **EMG (Electromyogram)** — Skeletal muscle electrical activity
- **EOG (Electrooculogram)** — Eye movement potential
- **ERG (Electroretinogram)** — Retinal response to light

### 2.1.2 Biochemical Signals
Concentrations or partial pressures of chemical species in body fluids.
- **pH of blood:** Normal range **7.35 – 7.45**
- **$pO_2$ (Partial pressure of oxygen):** Normal arterial **80 – 100 mmHg**
- **$pCO_2$ (Partial pressure of carbon dioxide):** Normal arterial **35 – 45 mmHg**
- **Glucose concentration:** Fasting **70 – 110 mg/dL**

### 2.1.3 Biomechanical Signals
Related to mechanical functions — pressure, force, displacement, flow, velocity.
- **Blood Pressure:** Systolic / Diastolic (Normal: **120/80 mmHg**)
- **Heart Rate (HR):** Normal resting **60 – 100 bpm**
- **Respiratory Flow Rate:** Tidal volume **~500 mL** per breath

### 2.1.4 Bioacoustic Signals
Sounds produced by the body's mechanical systems.
- **Heart Sounds (Phonocardiogram — PCG):** S1 and S2 lub-dub
- **Lung Sounds (Respiratory sounds):** Vesicular, bronchial, wheezes, crackles
- **Fetal Heart Sounds**

### 2.1.5 Bio-optical Signals
Optical properties of tissues — absorption, reflection, scattering.
- **Pulse Oximetry ($SpO_2$):** Normal **95 – 100%**
- **Photoplethysmogram (PPG):** Blood volume pulse

### 2.1.6 Biomagnetic Signals
Magnetic fields generated by ionic currents in the body.
- **MCG (Magnetocardiogram):** Heart's magnetic field (~**$10^{-10}$ Tesla**)
- **MEG (Magnetoencephalogram):** Brain's magnetic field (~**$10^{-13}$ Tesla**)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Signal Parameter Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~60% of Part A marks for Module 1.**

| Signal | Origin | Frequency Range | Amplitude Range | Electrodes / Sensors | Clinical Use |
|--------|--------|-----------------|-----------------|----------------------|--------------|
| **ECG** | Cardiac depolarization/repolarization | 0.05 – 150 Hz | 0.5 – 5 mV | 10 surface electrodes (12-lead) | Arrhythmia, MI detection |
| **EEG** | Cortical neuronal postsynaptic potentials | 0.5 – 100 Hz | 5 – 300 $\mu V$ | Scalp electrodes (10-20 system) | Epilepsy, sleep disorders |
| **EMG** | Motor unit action potentials | 20 – 5000 Hz | 50 $\mu V$ – 5 mV | Surface or needle electrodes | Neuromuscular disorders |
| **EOG** | Corneal-retinal standing potential | DC – 100 Hz | 0.05 – 3.5 mV | Surface electrodes around eyes | Eye movement, sleep studies |
| **ERG** | Retinal photoreceptor activity | 0.2 – 200 Hz | 5 $\mu V$ – 1 mV | Contact lens electrodes | Retinal disease diagnosis |
| **PCG** | Heart valve closure vibrations | 20 – 2000 Hz | Variable | Microphone/stethoscope | Valve disorders (stenosis, regurgitation) |
| **PPG** | Blood volume changes in tissue | 0.5 – 20 Hz | Perfusion-dependent | Optical LED + photodetector | $SpO_2$ monitoring, HR |
| **MCG** | Cardiac ionic currents | DC – 100 Hz | $\sim 10^{-10}$ T | SQUID magnetometer | Fetal MCG, ischemia |
| **MEG** | Cortical pyramidal cell currents | DC – 100 Hz | $\sim 10^{-13}$ T | SQUID magnetometer | Epilepsy focus localization |
| **Blood Pressure (BP)** | Arterial wall tension | 0 – 60 Hz (pulsatile) | 0 – 200 mmHg | Sphygmomanometer / catheter | Hypertension, shock |
| **Respiratory Signal** | Chest wall movement | 0.1 – 10 Hz | Tidal volume 500 mL | Strain gauge / thermistor | Sleep apnea, COPD |
| **Body Temperature** | Metabolic heat production | DC – 0.1 Hz | 35 – 42 °C | Thermistor / thermocouple | Fever, hypothermia |

### 2.3 Fundamental Governing Equations

**1. The Action Potential (Hodgkin-Huxley Model — Conceptual):**
$$C_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} = -g_{Na} m^3 h (V_m - V_{Na}) - g_K n^4 (V_m - V_K) - g_L (V_m - V_L) + I_{ext}$$

where $C_m$ is membrane capacitance, $g$ are conductances, and $V_m$ is the membrane potential.

**2. The Nernst Equation (Equilibrium potential for an ion):**
$$E_{ion} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$$

where $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$, $T$ is absolute temperature, $z$ is ion valence, $F = 96485 \text{ C/mol}$ is Faraday's constant.

**3. The SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) — Universal biomedical figure of merit:**
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}\right)$$

**4. Heart Rate from ECG R-R interval:**
$$\text{HR (bpm)} = \frac{60}{T_{R-R} \text{ (seconds)}}$$

**5. Sample Rate Selection (Nyquist-Shannon Theorem):**
$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

Practical KTU rule for biomedical signals:
$$f_s = (2.5 \text{ to } 5) \cdot f_{max}$$

For example, for ECG with $f_{max} = 150$ Hz, the standard clinical sampling rate is **360 Hz** or **500 Hz**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the examples of biomedical signals forms the **foundation for designing**:
1. **Patient monitoring systems** (ICU monitors tracking ECG, $SpO_2$, BP, respiration)
2. **Wearable health devices** (smartwatches with PPG, single-lead ECG)
3. **Implantable devices** (pacemakers sense intracardiac ECG)
4. **Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI)** using EEG
5. **Diagnostic AI models** for arrhythmia, seizure detection
6. **Biomedical filter design** — knowing the signal bandwidth allows design of optimal Butterworth, Chebyshev, or FIR filters

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind the Numbers:** ECG has a small bandwidth (0.05 – 150 Hz) but high amplitude (mV) — so noise sources like 50 Hz powerline interference are *in-band* and require **notch filters**. Conversely, EEG has tiny amplitude ($\mu V$) — so **amplification and shielding** are the primary engineering challenges, not filtering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Sampling Rate for an ECG Signal

**Problem Statement:** A 12-lead ECG system has an effective maximum frequency of interest $f_{max} = 150$ Hz. Determine the minimum sampling rate required by the Nyquist criterion, and recommend a practical sampling rate used in clinical ECG systems.

### Step 1 — State the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem
The Nyquist criterion states that to reconstruct a bandlimited signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency component present in the signal.

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Given Value
The given maximum frequency component of the ECG signal is:
$$f_{max} = 150 \text{ Hz}$$

Substituting into the Nyquist inequality:
$$f_s \geq 2 \times 150$$
$$f_s \geq 300 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Practical Engineering Margin
In clinical practice, a safety factor of 20% – 67% above the Nyquist rate is applied to accommodate:
- Anti-aliasing filter roll-off
- Patient-specific high-frequency components (e.g., pacemaker spikes)
- Filter transition bands

Using a factor of 2.4 (a common clinical choice):
$$f_{s,practical} = 2.4 \times 150 = 360 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 4 — Cross-Check with Real Clinical Standards
Standard clinical sampling rates used worldwide are:
- **360 Hz** (AHA / MIT-BIH database legacy)
- **500 Hz** (modern digital ECG machines)
- **1000 – 5000 Hz** (high-resolution research ECG, intracardiac electrograms)

### Final Result
$$\boxed{f_{s,min} = 300 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_{s,practical} = 360 \text{ Hz or 500 Hz}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 12-lead ECG system should sample at a **minimum of 300 Hz** to avoid aliasing, with **360 Hz or 500 Hz** being the clinical standard. This is a classic **2-mark sub-part** frequently asked in KTU Module 1.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Signal Characteristics and Engineering Implications

| Signal | Dominant Frequency Band | Primary Noise Source | Key Engineering Challenge | Typical Filter Needed |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **ECG** | 0.5 – 40 Hz (QRS dominant) | 50/60 Hz powerline, baseline wander | QRS detection accuracy | Notch (50 Hz) + Highpass (0.5 Hz) |
| **EEG** | 0.5 – 30 Hz (delta, theta, alpha, beta bands) | EMG artefact, eye blinks | Low SNR ($\mu V$-level) | Low-noise preamp + bandpass |
| **EMG** | 20 – 500 Hz | Motion artefact, ECG crosstalk | Wide dynamic range | Highpass (20 Hz) + lowpass (500 Hz) |
| **EOG** | DC – 10 Hz | Blink artefacts, EMG | DC drift removal | Highpass (0.05 Hz) |
| **PCG** | 20 – 2000 Hz | Lung sounds, ambient noise | S1/S2 segmentation | Bandpass (20 – 2000 Hz) |
| **PPG** | 0.5 – 8 Hz (cardiac) | Motion artefact | Motion artefact removal | Adaptive filtering |
| **MCG/MEG** | DC – 100 Hz | Earth's magnetic field | Extreme magnetic shielding | SQUID + gradiometer |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Deriving Heart Rate from ECG R-R Interval

**Problem:** An ECG recording shows consecutive R-peaks at $t_1 = 1.20$ s and $t_2 = 2.00$ s. Calculate the heart rate in beats per minute (bpm).

### Step 1 — Compute the R-R Interval
The R-R interval is the time difference between two consecutive R-wave peaks:
$$T_{R-R} = t_2 - t_1$$
$$T_{R-R} = 2.00 - 1.20 = 0.80 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Heart Rate Formula
Heart rate in bpm is the number of beats in 60 seconds. If one beat takes $T_{R-R}$ seconds, then in 60 seconds, the number of beats is:
$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{T_{R-R}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute and Evaluate
$$\text{HR} = \frac{60}{0.80}$$
$$\text{HR} = 75 \text{ bpm}$$

### Step 4 — Clinical Interpretation
$$\text{HR} = 75 \text{ bpm} \in [60, 100] \text{ bpm} \implies \text{Normal sinus rhythm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** A 14-mark question may ask: *"Given an ECG signal with R-R intervals of 0.8 s and 1.2 s alternating, comment on the rhythm."* The answer: **Sinus arrhythmia** — varying R-R intervals within the normal HR range, common in young healthy adults during respiration.

## 3.4 Worked Example: SNR Calculation for an EMG Signal

**Problem:** An EMG signal has RMS amplitude $A_s = 2.5$ mV. The 50 Hz powerline interference has RMS amplitude $A_n = 0.25$ mV. Calculate the SNR in decibels (dB).

### Step 1 — Recall the Amplitude-Based SNR Formula
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{signal}}{A_{noise}}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Values
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2.5 \text{ mV}}{0.25 \text{ mV}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Ratio
$$\frac{2.5}{0.25} = 10$$
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(10)$$
$$\log_{10}(10) = 1$$
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \times 1 = 20 \text{ dB}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{\text{SNR} = 20 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 20 dB SNR means the signal power is **100 times** the noise power — a generally acceptable quality for clinical EMG recordings. Values below 10 dB typically require additional filtering or signal averaging.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Origin and Acquisition Chain of Biomedical Signals

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end pipeline from the **biological source** in the body to the **processed diagnostic output**, covering every major class of biomedical signal introduced in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Biological Source / Living System]
    A1[Excitable Cells: Heart, Brain, Muscle, Eye]
    A2[Chemical Concentrations: Blood Gases, pH, Glucose]
    A3[Mechanical Activity: Heart Valves, Vessels, Lungs]
    A4[Magnetic Fields from Ionic Currents]
    A5[Optical Tissue Properties: Blood Volume, Oxygenation]
    A0 --> A1
    A0 --> A2
    A0 --> A3
    A0 --> A4
    A0 --> A5
    A1 --> B1[Bioelectric Signal: ECG EEG EMG EOG ERG]
    A2 --> B2[Biochemical Signal: pH pO2 pCO2 Glucose]
    A3 --> B3[Biomechanical Signal: BP HR Respiration Flow]
    A4 --> B4[Biomagnetic Signal: MCG MEG]
    A5 --> B5[Bio-optical Signal: PPG SpO2]
    A3 --> B6[Bioacoustic Signal: PCG Lung Sounds]
    B1 --> C1[Transducer: Electrodes]
    B2 --> C2[Transducer: Ion-Selective Electrode, Clark Cell]
    B3 --> C3[Transducer: Pressure Sensor Strain Gauge]
    B4 --> C4[Transducer: SQUID Magnetometer]
    B5 --> C5[Transducer: LED Photodetector Pair]
    B6 --> C6[Transducer: Microphone Accelerometer]
    C1 --> D1[Amplifier with High CMRR]
    C2 --> D2[Chemical-to-Electrical Converter]
    C3 --> D3[Bridge Amplifier Signal Conditioner]
    C4 --> D4[Ultra-Low Noise Preamplifier]
    C5 --> D5[Transimpedance Amplifier]
    C6 --> D6[Charge Amplifier Audio Amplifier]
    D1 --> E[Analog Filtering and Anti-Aliasing]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Digital Signal Processing DSP]
    G --> H[Diagnostic Display and Clinical Decision]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Every biomedical signal, regardless of its energy domain (electrical, chemical, mechanical, magnetic, optical, acoustic), follows the same fundamental signal chain: **Source → Transducer → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → DSP → Diagnosis**. This unifies all examples of biomedical signals under one engineering framework.

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Six Major Families of Biomedical Signals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Biomedical Signals]
    S --> F1[Bioelectric]
    S --> F2[Biochemical]
    S --> F3[Biomechanical]
    S --> F4[Bioacoustic]
    S --> F5[Bio-optical]
    S --> F6[Biomagnetic]
    F1 --> G1[ECG: 0.05-150 Hz, mV range]
    F1 --> G2[EEG: 0.5-100 Hz, microV range]
    F1 --> G3[EMG: 20-5000 Hz, microV-mV range]
    F1 --> G4[EOG: DC-100 Hz, mV range]
    F1 --> G5[ERG: 0.2-200 Hz, microV-mV range]
    F2 --> H1[pH of blood]
    F2 --> H2[Partial pressure pO2 pCO2]
    F2 --> H3[Glucose and electrolytes]
    F3 --> I1[Blood pressure]
    F3 --> I2[Heart rate and respiration]
    F3 --> I3[Gait and motion signals]
    F4 --> J1[Phonocardiogram PCG: Heart sounds]
    F4 --> J2[Respiratory sounds: Lung auscultation]
    F4 --> J3[Fetal heart sounds]
    F5 --> K1[Pulse oximetry SpO2]
    F5 --> K2[Photoplethysmogram PPG]
    F6 --> L1[Magnetocardiogram MCG]
    F6 --> L2[Magnetoencephalogram MEG]
```

## 4.3 Frequency Spectrum Map — Where Each Signal Lives

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DC_Band [DC to 0.5 Hz]
        SG1[EOG drift Temperature Gait tilt]
    end
    subgraph SubHz [0.5 to 40 Hz]
        SG2[ECG dominant EEG dominant PPG HR Respiration]
    end
    subgraph Mid_Band [40 to 500 Hz]
        SG3[EMG low band PCG low band]
    end
    subgraph High_Band [500 to 5000 Hz]
        SG4[EMG high band PCG high band Lung sounds]
    end
    DC_Band --> SubHz
    SubHz --> Mid_Band
    Mid_Band --> High_Band
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this map matters for KTU exams:** When designing a **digital filter** for a specific signal, the student must justify the **cutoff frequencies** based on the signal's frequency band. For instance, an ECG bandpass filter typically uses **0.5 Hz – 40 Hz**, which is well-grounded in this spectrum map.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define biomedical signals. Classify them with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **biomedical signal** is a measurable time-varying quantity that originates from a biological system and conveys physiological information.
