Classification with examples:
1. **Bioelectric signals** – ECG, EEG, EMG (arise from cellular electrical activity)
2. **Biochemical signals** – Blood pH, $pO_2$, glucose concentration
3. **Biomechanical signals** – Blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory flow
4. **Bioacoustic signals** – Heart sounds (PCG), lung sounds
5. **Bio-optical signals** – Pulse oximetry ($SpO_2$), PPG
6. **Biomagnetic signals** – MCG, MEG

*(Award ½ mark for definition + 2½ marks for classification with examples.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**List the typical frequency and amplitude ranges of ECG and EEG signals. Mention two clinical applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Signal | Frequency Range | Amplitude Range | Clinical Applications |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **ECG** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | **0.5 – 5 mV** | (i) Myocardial infarction detection (ii) Arrhythmia diagnosis |
| **EEG** | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | **5 – 300 $\mu V$** | (i) Epilepsy detection (ii) Sleep disorder analysis |

*(Award 1 mark per signal for frequency/amplitude, ½ mark each for applications.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, has internal choice, and typically contains two 7-mark sub-parts.]**

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify biomedical signals based on their physical origin. Explain in detail the origin, frequency range, amplitude range, electrodes used, and clinical applications of **ECG** and **EEG** signals.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Classification of biomedical signals (3 marks):**
Biomedical signals are classified into six families based on the physical energy domain:
1. **Bioelectric** – electrical potentials from excitable tissues (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, ERG)
2. **Biochemical** – concentrations of chemical species (blood pH, $pO_2$, $pCO_2$)
3. **Biomechanical** – pressure, flow, displacement (BP, heart rate, respiration)
4. **Bioacoustic** – sounds produced by the body (PCG, lung sounds)
5. **Bio-optical** – optical absorption/reflection (PPG, $SpO_2$)
6. **Biomagnetic** – magnetic fields from ionic currents (MCG, MEG)

**ECG – Electrocardiogram (2 marks):**
- **Origin:** Electrical activity of cardiac pacemaker cells (SA node) and subsequent depolarization/repolarization of atrial and ventricular myocardium.
- **Frequency range:** 0.05 – 150 Hz (dominant QRS energy 5 – 40 Hz)
- **Amplitude range:** 0.5 – 5 mV
- **Electrodes:** 10 surface Ag/AgCl electrodes placed on limbs and chest (12-lead system)
- **Clinical applications:** Myocardial infarction diagnosis, arrhythmia detection, QT interval analysis, pacemaker function assessment.

**EEG – Electroencephalogram (2 marks):**
- **Origin:** Synchronized postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons in the brain, recorded from the scalp.
- **Frequency range:** 0.5 – 100 Hz, with characteristic sub-bands:
  - **Delta:** 0.5 – 4 Hz (deep sleep)
  - **Theta:** 4 – 8 Hz (drowsiness)
  - **Alpha:** 8 – 13 Hz (relaxed wakefulness)
  - **Beta:** 13 – 30 Hz (active thinking)
  - **Gamma:** >30 Hz (cognitive processing)
- **Amplitude range:** 5 – 300 $\mu V$ (approximately 100× smaller than ECG)
- **Electrodes:** 19–256 scalp electrodes following the **international 10–20 system**
- **Clinical applications:** Epilepsy diagnosis, sleep disorder (sleep staging), brain death confirmation, anesthesia depth monitoring.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **general acquisition chain of a biomedical signal** from the biological source to the diagnostic display. State the purpose of each block.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
```
Biological Source → Transducer/Sensor → Amplifier → Analog Filter → ADC → DSP → Display
```

**Description of Each Block (4 marks):**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Biological Source** | The physiological process producing the signal — e.g., heart, brain, muscle. |
| **Transducer/Sensor** | Converts the physiological energy into a measurable electrical signal. Examples: Ag/AgCl electrodes (ECG/EEG), piezoelectric crystals (PCG), photodiodes (PPG), SQUIDs (MCG/MEG). |
| **Amplifier** | Boosts the low-amplitude signal (e.g., $\mu V$ EEG) to a usable level. Uses **instrumentation amplifiers** with high CMRR ($> 80$ dB) to reject common-mode noise. |
| **Analog Filter** | Performs **anti-aliasing** and **bandlimiting**. Removes DC offset, high-frequency noise, and 50/60 Hz powerline interference. |
| **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** | Samples the analog signal and quantizes it. Resolution typically **12–24 bits**, sampling rate chosen per Nyquist criterion. |
| **DSP (Digital Signal Processing)** | Performs digital filtering, feature extraction (e.g., R-peak detection, QRS duration), and classification (e.g., normal vs. abnormal). |
| **Display** | Presents the processed signal to the clinician (waveform, numeric parameters, alarms). |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** A common mistake is **omitting the anti-aliasing filter** before the ADC. Examiners specifically look for this — sampling without anti-aliasing causes **aliasing distortion**, which is clinically unacceptable. **Always state the anti-aliasing filter explicitly** and justify its cutoff based on the Nyquist criterion. Also, do **not** confuse the **amplifier** with the **filter** — these are two distinct functional blocks worth separate marks.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **origin, frequency range, amplitude, transducer used, and clinical applications** of the following signals: **(i) EMG, (ii) PCG, (iii) PPG**.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) EMG – Electromyogram (2.5 marks):**
- **Origin:** Summed action potentials of motor units (a motor neuron + the muscle fibers it innervates) during voluntary or stimulated muscle contraction.
- **Frequency range:** 20 – 5000 Hz
- **Amplitude range:** 50 $\mu V$ – 5 mV
- **Transducer:** Surface electrodes (non-invasive) or needle/concentric electrodes (invasive, for single motor unit recording)
- **Clinical applications:** Diagnosis of neuromuscular disorders (myopathy, neuropathy), biofeedback, prosthetic control, ergonomics.

**(ii) PCG – Phonocardiogram (2.5 marks):**
- **Origin:** Vibrations produced by closure of heart valves — S1 (mitral/tricuspid closure) and S2 (aortic/pulmonic closure), and pathological murmurs.
- **Frequency range:** 20 – 2000 Hz
- **Amplitude range:** Variable, typically very low (mPa-level sound pressure)
- **Transducer:** Electronic stethoscope with microphone, or piezoelectric accelerometer placed on the chest wall
- **Clinical applications:** Detection of valvular disorders (stenosis, regurgitation), murmurs, gallop rhythms.

**(iii) PPG – Photoplethysmogram (2 marks):**
- **Origin:** Variations in light absorption by blood vessels, caused by cardiac-cycle-driven changes in blood volume in the microvascular bed of tissue (typically fingertip or earlobe).
- **Frequency range:** 0.5 – 20 Hz (cardiac fundamental at ~1 Hz, dicrotic notch visible)
- **Amplitude range:** Perfusion-dependent; typically 0.1 – 5% of total reflected/transmitted light intensity
- **Transducer:** Pair of LEDs (red 660 nm + infrared 940 nm) and a photodetector
- **Clinical applications:** Pulse oximetry ($SpO_2$ measurement), heart rate monitoring, vascular assessment, wearable fitness devices.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **bioelectric and biomagnetic signals**. Discuss the relative advantages of MCG/MEG over ECG/EEG with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Bioelectric (ECG/EEG) | Biomagnetic (MCG/MEG) |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Origin** | Ionic currents across cell membranes | Same ionic currents, but measured via magnetic field |
| **Detector** | Electrodes (surface Ag/AgCl) | SQUID magnetometer |
| **Sensitivity to tangential currents** | Low (volume conduction smears signal) | High (magnetic fields are not smeared by tissue) |
| **Sensitivity to radial currents** | High | Low |
| **Contact with body** | Required (gel electrolyte) | Non-contact (helium-cooled Dewar) |
| **Spatial resolution** | Limited (~cm for EEG) | Excellent (~mm) |
| **Cost** | Low | Very high (cryogenic, shielded room) |
| **Frequency range** | 0.05 – 5000 Hz | DC – 100 Hz |

**Advantages of MCG/MEG (4 marks):**

1. **Non-contact measurement:** MCG and MEG do not require skin-electrode contact, eliminating skin preparation, gel application, and contact-related artefacts.
2. **Higher spatial resolution:** MEG can localize epileptic foci to within **2 – 3 mm**, compared to ~**10 – 20 mm** for scalp EEG, by detecting tangential currents in sulci.
3. **Insensitive to volume conduction smearing:** Magnetic fields are not distorted by the resistive properties of intervening tissue, unlike electrical potentials.
4. **Complementary to ECG/EEG:** Detects tangential current sources that ECG/EEG may miss — used in **fetal MCG** (detecting fetal heart activity without maternal interference) and in **presurgical epilepsy evaluation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:** Students often write *"MEG is better than EEG"* — this is a **conceptual error**. The correct phrasing is *"MEG provides **complementary** information to EEG, especially for tangential sources."* Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for absolute comparisons. Also, students forget to mention that MCG/MEG require **magnetically shielded rooms** (MSR) and **cryogenic cooling** with liquid helium — this is a frequently asked 1-mark follow-up.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Examples of Biomedical Signals"**

### A. Six Families of Biomedical Signals
- **Bioelectric:** ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, ERG (electrode-based, mV–$\mu V$ range)
- **Biochemical:** pH, $pO_2$, $pCO_2$, glucose (chemical sensors)
- **Biomechanical:** BP, HR, respiration, gait (pressure/flow sensors)
- **Bioacoustic:** PCG, lung sounds, fetal heart sounds (microphones)
- **Bio-optical:** PPG, $SpO_2$ (LED + photodetector)
- **Biomagnetic:** MCG, MEG (SQUID magnetometer)

### B. Critical Numerical Values (Memorize These!)
- ECG: **0.05 – 150 Hz, 0.5 – 5 mV**, 360 Hz sampling
- EEG: **0.5 – 100 Hz, 5 – 300 $\mu V$**, 10–20 electrode system
- EMG: **20 – 5000 Hz, 50 $\mu V$ – 5 mV**
- EOG: **DC – 100 Hz, 0.05 – 3.5 mV**
- Heart rate normal: **60 – 100 bpm**
- Blood pressure normal: **120/80 mmHg**
- $SpO_2$ normal: **95 – 100%**
- Blood pH normal: **7.35 – 7.45**
- MCG strength: **~$10^{-10}$ Tesla**
- MEG strength: **~$10^{-13}$ Tesla**

### C. Universal Signal Acquisition Chain (Worth a 7-Mark Question)
**Source → Transducer → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → DSP → Display**

### D. Key Formulas to Master
- Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$
- Heart rate: $\text{HR} = \dfrac{60}{T_{R-R}}$
- SNR: $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{A_s}{A_n}\right)$
- Nernst equation: $E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\right)$

### E. Examiner Pet Topics (Frequently Asked)
1. **Frequency-amplitude table** of all signals (3–7 marks)
2. **Block diagram of acquisition system** (7 marks)
3. **Comparison of bioelectric vs. biomagnetic** (7 marks)
4. **ECG characteristics and clinical use** (3–7 marks)
5. **Application-based questions on EMG/PPG/PCG** (3 marks)
6. **Sample rate / Nyquist calculation** (2–3 marks)
7. **Stationary vs. non-stationary nature** of signals (2 marks)

### F. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- **Don't** confuse EMG frequency (20–5000 Hz) with ECG (0.05–150 Hz) — they overlap minimally.
- **Don't** forget to specify **units** (Hz, mV, $\mu V$, mmHg, %, °C) — unmarked values lose marks.
- **Don't** write *"biomedical signals are random"* — they are **quasi-periodic** in healthy subjects and only become stochastic in pathology.
- **Don't** omit the **anti-aliasing filter** in any acquisition chain block diagram.
- **Don't** call MEG *"better than EEG"* — it is **complementary**, not superior.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog-to-Digital Conversion (Block Diagram Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analog-to-Digital Conversion (Block Diagram Only) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** is the electronic process of converting a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude **analog biomedical signal** (such as ECG, EEG, or EMG) into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude **digital signal** that can be stored, processed, and analyzed by digital computers, microcontrollers, or DSP processors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** For this module, the focus is strictly on the **functional block diagram** of the ADC system and the role of each constituent block in conditioning a real-world biomedical signal. The internal transistor-level design of each block is *out of scope*. In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing**, the ADC is the indispensable bridge between the biological world (which is inherently analog) and the digital domain of modern diagnostic equipment (Patient Monitors, Holter Recorders, MRI consoles, Digital Stethoscopes). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Medical-Grade Metrics (Bolded for Recall):** > - **Resolution (n):** Typically **12-bit to 24-bit** for clinical-grade biomedical ADCs. > - **Sampling Frequency (fs):** Governed by **Nyquist Theorem**. > - **Full-Scale Range (FSR):** Often **±5 mV** for ECG, **±200 µV** for EEG. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing on the banks of a flowing river (the analog signal). The river's water level is continuously changing, representing the infinite amplitude values of the analog waveform. To take a "snapshot" of the river, you would: 1. **Wait at fixed time intervals** (Sampling) – say, every 1 second, you note the water level. 2. **Round to the nearest marked value** (Quantization) – your measuring stick only has markings every 1 cm. 3. **Write down a numerical code** (Encoding) – you transcribe "level 7" into a digital code like "0111". **That is exactly what an ADC does for biomedical signals!** It samples the continuous ECG waveform at fixed intervals, rounds the voltage to the nearest available binary step, and produces a string of 0s and 1s that a computer can understand. | Real World (Analog) | ADC Process | Digital Domain | |---|---|---| | Continuous ECG voltage curve | Time Sampling | Discrete time points | | Infinite amplitude values | Quantization | Finite amplitude levels | | Voltage value | Encoding | Binary code (bits) | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Need for ADC in Biomedical Instrumentation Human physiological signals are **quasi-periodic, low-amplitude, and highly susceptible to noise** (50/60 Hz powerline interference, baseline wander, motion artifacts). Before any digital algorithm (like Pan-Tompkins QRS detection) can run, the signal **must be digitized**. The ADC block diagram is the standardized pipeline for this conversion. ## 2.2 Component-Wise Operational Logic The ADC pipeline is *not* a single chip doing one job; it is a **cascaded system of specialized sub-blocks**. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires understanding the function of each stage: ### Stage 1: Preamplifier / Signal Conditioner - **Why?** Biomedical signals (EEG ≈ 10–100 µV) are too weak to be digitized directly. - **How?** Provides initial gain (typically **1000x** for EEG) and basic impedance buffering. ### Stage 2: Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter - **Why?** To strictly enforce the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$). Frequencies above $f_s/2$ will alias (fold back) into the signal band and corrupt the diagnosis. - **How?** An active low-pass filter (Butterworth, typically 2nd to 8th order) with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$. ### Stage 3: Sample and Hold (S/H) Circuit - **Why?** The quantizer needs a **stable, non-changing voltage** during the conversion time. Without S/H, the input could change mid-conversion, causing **aperture error**. - **How?** A switch (MOSFET) and a holding capacitor ($C_H$). Switch closes → tracks input; Switch opens → holds the value. ### Stage 4: Quantizer - **Why?** Maps the continuous voltage range to a finite set of $2^n$ discrete levels. - **How?** Compares the held voltage against reference levels using a comparator network (e.g., Flash ADC uses $2^n - 1$ comparators). ### Stage 5: Digital Encoder - **Why?** The quantizer's output (e.g., thermometer code) is not a compact digital format. - **How?** Converts the quantization level into standard binary (Natural Binary, Gray code, or Two's Complement). ### Auxiliary Block: Clock Generator - **Why?** Sampling and quantization are **synchronous processes** requiring a precise timing reference. - **How?** Crystal oscillator providing the master clock that drives the S/H switch and the successive-approximation register (if SAR-ADC). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^n$ | $n$ is the ADC resolution (bits) | Levels (integer) | | Quantization Step Size (Resolution in Volts) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L - 1}$ | Smallest voltage change detectable | Volts (V) | | Maximum Quantization Error | $e_q(max) = \pm \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Half of the step size | Volts (V) | | RMS Quantization Noise | $e_{q(rms)} = \dfrac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Statistical average error | Volts (V) | | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) | $SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB | For a full-scale sinusoid | Decibels (dB) | | Nyquist Sampling Frequency | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | Hertz (Hz) | | Dynamic Range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}(2^n) = 6.02n$ dB | Ratio of largest to smallest detectable signal | Decibels (dB) | | Aperture Time Uncertainty Error | $\Delta V = \dfrac{dV}{dt} \cdot t_{ap}$ | Voltage change during conversion time | Volts (V) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In designing a **digital ECG machine** for KTU-style problems, if the maximum ECG amplitude is 5 mV and a **12-bit ADC** is used with a 5V reference, the step size $\Delta = \dfrac{5}{4095} \approx 1.22$ mV — a resolution adequate for QRS detection but requiring higher resolution (16-bit) for ST-segment analysis. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus specifies "Block Diagram Only" for the structural portion, but the *associated quantitative parameters* (resolution, quantization error, SQNR) are mandatory derivations for Part B questions. ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantization Step Size ($\Delta$) and Maximum Error **Problem Context:** A clinical ECG amplifier uses a 12-bit ADC. The analog input range is set to $\pm 5$ mV (typical for diagnostic ECG). Determine the step size and the maximum quantization error. ### Step-by-Step Mathematical Walkthrough **Given Data:** - Resolution, $n = 12$ bits - Full-Scale Range, $V_{FSR} = +5 \text{ mV} - (-5 \text{ mV}) = 10$ mV **Step 1: Calculate the total number of discrete quantization levels.** $$L = 2^n = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}$$ *Explanation:* A 12-bit word can represent $2^{12}$ unique binary combinations, from `000000000000` to `111111111111`. **Step 2: Apply the step size formula.** The fundamental relationship between the input voltage range and the number of levels is: $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1}$$ *Explanation:* We use $2^n - 1$ (not $2^n$) because the range is divided into $2^n - 1$ *intervals* between $2^n$ *levels*. **Step 3: Substitute numerical values.** $$\Delta = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}}{4096 - 1} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{4095} \text{ V}$$ **Step 4: Final numerical evaluation.** $$\Delta \approx 2.442 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} = 2.442 \text{ µV}$$ *Interpretation:* The ADC can distinguish voltage differences as small as **2.44 microvolts** — essential for capturing the low-amplitude P and T waves of the ECG. **Step 5: Calculate Maximum Quantization Error.** $$e_q(max) = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{2.442 \text{ µV}}{2} = \pm 1.221 \text{ µV}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) **Problem Context:** Calculate the SQNR for the 12-bit ADC above when the input is a full-scale sinusoid. **Step 1: Recall the SQNR expression for a full-scale sine wave input.** $$SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$ *Derivation Logic:* The signal power of a sine wave with peak amplitude $A$ is $A^2/2$. The quantization noise power (treating the error as uniformly distributed between $\pm \Delta/2$) is $\Delta^2/12$. Substituting $\Delta = 2A/2^n$ yields: $$\begin{aligned} SQNR &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\text{Signal Power}}{\text{Noise Power}}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A^2/2}{\Delta^2/12}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A^2/2}{(2A/2^n)^2/12}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{12 \cdot 2^n \cdot 2^n}{2 \cdot 4}\right) \\ &= 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2: Substitute $n = 12$.** $$SQNR = 6.02(12) + 1.76$$ **Step 3: Final evaluation.** $$SQNR = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$ *Interpretation:* A 12-bit ADC offers approximately **74 dB** of dynamic range — sufficient for most clinical-grade ECG and EMG acquisition. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right ADC Architecture | ADC Type | Speed | Resolution | Best Biomedical Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Flash ADC** | Very High (GS/s) | Low (6–8 bit) | Ultra-fast action potentials, neural spike recording | | **SAR (Successive Approximation)** | Medium (MS/s) | Medium-High (12–18 bit) | Standard ECG, EMG, heart rate monitors | | **Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\Delta$)** | Low (kS/s) | Very High (16–24 bit) | High-precision EEG, cochlear implants, patient monitors | | **Dual-Slope** | Very Slow | High | DC measurement (blood pressure, temperature) | | **Pipeline** | High | Medium (10–14 bit) | Ultrasound beamforming, MRI receivers | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Canonical ADC Block Diagram (KTU 2024 Mandatory Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram represents the **canonical block diagram of an Analog-to-Digital Conversion system** as specified in the KTU 2024 PCBMT501 syllabus. Note the cascaded signal flow from the analog biomedical source to the digital processor. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[BIO SIGNAL SOURCE ECG EEG EMG] --> A2[PREAMP AND CONDITIONER] A2 --> A3[ANTI ALIASING LOW PASS FILTER] A3 --> A4[SAMPLE AND HOLD S AND H] A4 --> A5[QUANTIZER] A5 --> A6[DIGITAL ENCODER] A6 --> A7[DIGITAL OUTPUT N BIT WORD] A8[CLOCK GENERATOR] --> A4 A8 --> A5 A1:::srcStyle A2:::procStyle A3:::filtStyle A4:::holdStyle A5:::quantStyle A6:::encStyle A7:::outStyle A8:::clkStyle classDef srcStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef procStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef filtStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef holdStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef quantStyle fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef encStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000 classDef outStyle fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000 classDef clkStyle fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Signal Transformation at Each Block (Conceptual Schematic) The following diagram illustrates the **evolution of the signal waveform** as it traverses each block. This is critical for KTU 14-mark answers which often ask, "Explain with a neat block diagram, the function of each block." ```mermaid flowchart TD B1["STAGE 1: ANALOG INPUT
Continuous time
Continuous amplitude
Smooth ECG waveform"] --> B2["STAGE 2: AFTER SAMPLE AND HOLD
Discrete time
Continuous amplitude
Staircase waveform"] B2 --> B3["STAGE 3: AFTER QUANTIZER
Discrete time
Discrete amplitude
Rounded staircase"] B3 --> B4["STAGE 4: AFTER ENCODER
Discrete time
Discrete amplitude
Binary bit stream 11001001"] B1:::analogStyle B2:::sampStyle B3:::quantSigStyle B4:::digitalStyle classDef analogStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef sampStyle fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef quantSigStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef digitalStyle fill:#AFEEEE,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Detailed Engineering View) For KTU students who want to go beyond the basic diagram, this expanded block diagram shows the **internal sub-systems** of a typical Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC — the most common architecture in portable biomedical devices. ```mermaid flowchart TB C1[ANALOG INPUT] --> C2[INPUT BUFFER] C2 --> C3[ANTI ALIASING FILTER] C3 --> C4[SAMPLE AND HOLD SWITCH] C4 --> C5[COMPARATOR] C6[DAC] --> C5 C5 --> C7[SAR LOGIC CONTROL] C7 --> C8[OUTPUT REGISTER] C8 --> C9[DIGITAL OUTPUT] C7 --> C6 C10[CLOCK] --> C4 C10 --> C7 C1:::inStyle C2:::bufStyle C3:::filterStyle C4:::shStyle C5:::compStyle C6:::dacStyle C7:::sarStyle C8:::regStyle C9:::finalStyle C10:::clockStyle classDef inStyle fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef bufStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef filterStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef shStyle fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef compStyle fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef dacStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef sarStyle fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000 classDef regStyle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008080,color:#000 classDef finalStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef clockStyle fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Visual Requirement:** Always draw the **Anti-Aliasing Filter BEFORE** the Sample-and-Hold block. Drawing it afterwards is a very common mistake (3-mark penalty) because filtering *after* sampling is meaningless — aliasing has already corrupted the spectrum. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Analog-to-Digital Conversion. List any four biomedical signals that require ADC for digital processing.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) is the process of converting a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital signal using a sequence of operations including sampling, quantization, and encoding. **Four Biomedical Signals Requiring ADC:** 1. **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** – for heart rate variability and arrhythmia detection. 2. **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** – for brain-computer interfaces and epilepsy monitoring. 3. **EMG (Electromyogram)** – for muscle activity and prosthetic control. 4. **PPG (Photoplethysmogram)** – for pulse oximetry and SpO2 calculation. (Any four from ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, PCG, EGG, blood pressure waveform are acceptable.) --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is the function of the anti-aliasing filter in the ADC block diagram? Why must it be placed *before* the sampler?** **Model Answer:** **Function of Anti-Aliasing Filter:** It is a low-pass filter that removes all frequency components from the input signal that are **above half the sampling frequency ($f_s/2$)**, also known as the **Nyquist frequency**. This prevents the phenomenon of **aliasing**, where high-frequency components fold back into the lower frequency band and become indistinguishable from the true signal. **Why placed *before* the sampler:** Aliasing is an irreversible distortion that occurs at the *instant of sampling*. Once a high-frequency component has been folded into the signal spectrum, it cannot be removed by any filter placed after the ADC. Hence, the anti-aliasing filter must be an **analog low-pass filter** positioned *before* the Sample-and-Hold circuit. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete process of Analog-to-Digital Conversion of a biomedical signal. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ECG signal has a maximum amplitude of $\pm 4$ mV. It is digitized using a 10-bit ADC. Calculate the **(i)** number of quantization levels, **(ii)** step size, and **(iii)** maximum quantization error. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Block Diagram & Function of Each Block (7 marks):** > Refer to the canonical ADC block diagram in Section 4.1. The complete description expected by the KTU examiner is as follows: 1. **Bioelectric Signal Source (1 mark):** The origin of the analog signal — e.g., ECG electrodes on the patient's chest. The signal is continuous in both time and amplitude. 2. **Preamplifier and Signal Conditioner (1 mark):** Biomedical signals are low-amplitude (µV to mV range). The preamplifier provides initial gain (typically 100–10,000×) and performs impedance matching using an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., AD620). 3. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter (1.5 marks):** A Butterworth or Bessel active filter with cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s/2$. It eliminates high-frequency noise and out-of-band components that would otherwise alias upon sampling. For ECG, $f_c$ is typically 40–150 Hz. 4. **Sample and Hold Circuit (1 mark):** Consists of an analog switch (MOSFET) and a holding capacitor. The switch is closed briefly to "sample" the input, then opens to "hold" the voltage constant during the quantization process. This prevents aperture error. 5. **Quantizer (1 mark):** Maps the continuous held voltage to one of $2^n$ discrete levels using a network of comparators and a reference voltage ladder. The step size is $\Delta = V_{FSR}/(2^n - 1)$. 6. **Digital Encoder (1 mark):** Converts the quantization level (e.g., thermometer code) into a standard binary format (Natural Binary or Gray code), producing the final n-bit digital word. 7. **Clock Generator (0.5 marks):** Synchronizes the sampling switch and the SAR logic to ensure uniform time intervals. **(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** $V_{max} = +4$ mV, $V_{min} = -4$ mV, $n = 10$ bits. **(i) Number of Quantization Levels (2 marks):** $$L = 2^n = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ levels}$$ **(ii) Step Size (2.5 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} V_{FSR} &= V_{max} - V_{min} = 4 \text{ mV} - (-4 \text{ mV}) = 8 \text{ mV} \\ \Delta &= \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{1023} \\ \Delta &= 7.82 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} = 7.82 \text{ µV} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Maximum Quantization Error (2.5 marks):** $$e_{q(max)} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{7.82 \text{ µV}}{2} = \pm 3.91 \text{ µV}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Alternative]** **(a)** Differentiate between the Flash ADC, SAR ADC, and Sigma-Delta ADC. State **one specific biomedical application** where each is preferred. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 16-bit ADC with a $\pm 10$ V input range is used to digitize a neural signal. Calculate the **(i)** dynamic range in dB, **(ii)** SQNR in dB, and **(iii)** the minimum detectable voltage change. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Comparison of ADC Architectures (7 marks):** | Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC | Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\Delta$) | |---|---|---|---| | **Speed** | Fastest (GS/s) | Medium (MS/s) | Slow (kS/s) | | **Resolution** | 6–8 bits | 12–18 bits | 16–24 bits | | **Hardware** | $2^n-1$ comparators | 1 comparator + DAC | Modulator + digital filter | | **Power** | High | Medium | Low | | **Cost** | Expensive | Moderate | Moderate | **Biomedical Applications (1 mark each):** - **Flash ADC →** Recording fast neural action potentials (spike detection) in neuroscience research. - **SAR ADC →** Portable ECG machines and Holter monitors (12-bit, MS/s range). - **Sigma-Delta ADC →** High-precision EEG acquisition and cochlear implant sound processors (16–24-bit, kS/s). **(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** $n = 16$ bits, $V_{FSR} = 10 - (-10) = 20$ V. **(i) Dynamic Range (2.5 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} DR &= 20 \log_{10}(2^n) = 20 \log_{10}(2^{16}) = 20 \times 16 \times 0.3010 \\ DR &= 96.32 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (2.5 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} SQNR &= 6.02n + 1.76 \\ SQNR &= 6.02(16) + 1.76 \\ SQNR &= 96.32 + 1.76 \\ SQNR &= 98.08 \text{ dB} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Minimum Detectable Voltage Change (2 marks):** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{20}{65535} = 3.05 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 305 \text{ µV}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Error in V_FSR:** Students often forget that $V_{FSR}$ is the **peak-to-peak** range (i.e., $V_{max} - V_{min}$), not the peak value. For a $\pm 10$ V range, $V_{FSR} = 20$ V, not 10 V. (**Lose 1 mark**) > 2. **$2^n$ vs $2^n - 1$:** Using $2^n$ in the denominator of $\Delta$ instead of $(2^n - 1)$ is technically the "mid-rise" quantizer approximation. KTU generally accepts $2^n$ for simplicity but **strictly correct** answer uses $(2^n - 1)$. (**Lose 0.5 mark**) > 3. **Block Diagram Direction:** Drawing the anti-aliasing filter *after* the S/H. (**Lose 2 marks**) > 4. **Forgetting the Clock:** The clock generator block is often omitted, but KTU considers it a valid block worth **0.5 mark**. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **ADC = Analog → Digital** bridge. Three sub-processes: **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding**. - ✅ **Block Diagram Order (MUST memorize):** Source → Preamplifier → **Anti-Aliasing Filter** → Sample & Hold → Quantizer → Encoder → Digital Output. (Clock is parallel to S/H & Quantizer.) - ✅ **Anti-Aliasing Filter goes BEFORE the Sampler — NOT after.** (Most common mistake.) - ✅ **Sample and Hold** holds the voltage constant during conversion to prevent aperture error. - ✅ **Resolution Formula:** $L = 2^n$ levels; Step size $\Delta = V_{FSR}/(2^n - 1)$. - ✅ **Max Quantization Error:** $\pm \Delta/2$. **RMS Error:** $\Delta/\sqrt{12}$. - ✅ **SQNR Formula:** $6.02n + 1.76$ dB (for full-scale sinusoid). - ✅ **Dynamic Range:** $6.02n$ dB. - ✅ **Nyquist Criterion:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — the foundation of the anti-aliasing filter design. - ✅ **Biomedical ADC Preferences:** ECG/EMG → SAR (12–16 bit); EEG/Neural → Sigma-Delta (16–24 bit); Spike sorting → Flash (8-bit, GS/s). - ✅ **Quantization is a lossy process** — the original analog voltage cannot be perfectly reconstructed due to $\pm \Delta/2$ error. - ✅ **Encoder outputs:** Natural Binary, Gray Code (preferred for ADCs to minimize bit-transition errors), or Two's Complement.

topics.title: Discrete-Time Signals - Typical Sequences and Sequence Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Signals - Typical Sequences and Sequence Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Discrete-Time Signal

A **discrete-time signal** is a mathematical function defined only at discrete instants of time. Unlike continuous-time signals, which exist for every real value of the independent variable $t$, a discrete-time signal $x(n)$ is defined only for integer values of the index $n \in \mathbb{Z} = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$.

Formally, a discrete-time signal is a mapping:

$$x : \mathbb{Z} \rightarrow \mathbb{R} \text{ (or } \mathbb{C}\text{)}$$

$$x(n) \in \mathbb{R} \quad \text{or} \quad x(n) \in \mathbb{C}$$

The independent variable $n$ is dimensionless in DSP theory, but in **biomedical signal processing**, it is conventionally associated with the sampling period $T_s$ (in **seconds**), such that the actual time is $t = nT_s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A discrete-time signal is **not** automatically a digital signal. A discrete-time signal is digital only if its amplitude is also quantized (represented by a finite set of values). Since DSP chips work with quantized values, the two terms are often used interchangeably in practical contexts, but the distinction is critical in board examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are measuring a patient's heart rate using an ECG monitor. The continuous ECG trace is the **continuous-time signal**. However, the monitor samples the voltage only once every few milliseconds to display it on the screen. The resulting sequence of voltage values $\{x(0), x(1), x(2), \ldots\}$ is a **discrete-time signal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of discrete-time signals like the still frames of a movie reel. The movie itself is continuous, but the reel stores only snapshots (frames) at specific instants. If the frames are captured fast enough (satisfying the **Nyquist criterion**), the brain perceives smooth motion. Similarly, if a biomedical signal is sampled fast enough, we can perfectly reconstruct the original.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

- **Sampling Frequency ($F_s$):** Number of samples per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. Common values in biomedical applications: $F_s = 250$ Hz (ECG), $F_s = 10000$ Hz (Neural spikes).
- **Sampling Period ($T_s$):** Time between consecutive samples, $T_s = 1/F_s$.
- **Time Index ($n$):** Integer sample number, dimensionless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Point:** In KTU board examinations, a discrete-time signal is often depicted graphically as a "lollipop" plot — vertical stems with circular heads at the top, drawn only at integer values of $n$. No value exists between two integers.

### 1.4 Sequence Representation — The Four Pillars

Any discrete-time signal can be represented in **four equivalent ways**:

| Representation | Description | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Functional Form** | Closed-form equation $x(n) = f(n)$ | Derivation, manipulation |
| **Tabular Form** | List of $(n, x(n))$ pairs | Numerical examples |
| **Graphical Form** | Stem (lollipop) plot | Visual analysis |
| **Set/Set-builder Form** | $\{x(n)\} = \{x(-1), x(0), x(1), \ldots\}$ | Set-theoretic operations |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Typical (Canonical) Sequences in DSP

KTU 2024 emphasises five **canonical sequences** that form the alphabet of all discrete-time signal processing. Every complex signal is a linear combination of these basic building blocks.

#### 2.1.1 The Unit Impulse Sequence (Kronecker Delta)

The **unit impulse** $\delta(n)$ is the most fundamental sequence in DSP. It is the discrete-time analog of the Dirac delta function.

$$
\delta(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$

- It is **non-zero only at $n = 0$** with value **1**.
- It is the **identity element for convolution** (just as 1 is the identity for multiplication).
- **Sampling Property:** $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n)\,\delta(n - k) = x(k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** $\delta(n)$ has a height of 1, while the continuous-time Dirac delta $\delta(t)$ has infinite height and unit area. They are NOT the same. This is a favourite trick-question topic in KTU exams.

#### 2.1.2 The Unit Step Sequence

The **unit step** $u(n)$ is defined as:

$$
u(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Relation to Impulse:** The unit step is the running sum of the unit impulse:

$$u(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta(k) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \delta(n - k)$$

Conversely, the impulse is the **first difference** of the step:

$$\delta(n) = u(n) - u(n-1)$$

#### 2.1.3 The Unit Ramp Sequence

The **unit ramp** $r(n)$ is defined as:

$$
r(n) = \begin{cases} n, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}
$$

- It can also be written as $r(n) = n \cdot u(n)$.
- **Relation to Step:** $r(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} u(k) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} k = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ for $n \geq 0$.

#### 2.1.4 The Real Exponential Sequence

$$x(n) = a^{n} \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}, \; a \in \mathbb{R}$$

The behaviour depends on $|a|$:

- If $\vert a \vert < 1$: sequence **decays** towards 0 (stable).
- If $\vert a \vert > 1$: sequence **grows** unbounded (unstable).
- If $\vert a \vert = 1$: sequence has constant magnitude (oscillatory if $a = -1$).

#### 2.1.5 The Sinusoidal Sequence

$$x(n) = A \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$$

where $A$ is the **amplitude** (dimensionless or in physical units like mV), $\omega_0$ is the **normalized digital frequency** (in **radians/sample**), and $\phi$ is the **phase** (in **radians**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A discrete-time sinusoid is **always periodic** if and only if $\omega_0 / (2\pi)$ is a rational number. Otherwise, the sequence is **aperiodic** even though its continuous-time counterpart is periodic. This is a high-weightage question in KTU 2024.

### 2.2 Symmetry Properties of Sequences

KTU frequently tests symmetry, so mastering these is non-negotiable.

- **Even Sequence:** $x(-n) = x(n)$ for all $n$. (Symmetric about vertical axis.)
- **Odd Sequence:** $x(-n) = -x(n)$ for all $n$. (Anti-symmetric; always $x(0) = 0$.)
- **Decomposition Theorem:** Any real sequence can be uniquely decomposed as:

$$x(n) = x_e(n) + x_o(n)$$

where

$$x_e(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(n) + x(-n)] \quad \text{and} \quad x_o(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(n) - x(-n)]$$

### 2.3 Energy and Power of Discrete-Time Signals

KTU 2024 includes this within the introductory module of biomedical signal processing. For a sequence $x(n)$:

**Total Energy:**

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$$

**Average Power:**

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Rule:** A signal is an **Energy Signal** if $0 < E < \infty$ (and $P = 0$). A signal is a **Power Signal** if $0 < P < \infty$ (and $E = \infty$). A signal **cannot be both** energy-type and power-type. Periodic signals are always power signals; finite-duration signals are always energy signals.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Sequence | Functional Form | Notable Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unit Impulse | $\delta(n) = 1$ at $n=0$, else $0$ | Sampling/Identity for convolution |
| 2 | Unit Step | $u(n) = 1$ for $n \geq 0$, else $0$ | Running sum of impulse |
| 3 | Unit Ramp | $r(n) = n \cdot u(n)$ | Sum of step |
| 4 | Exponential | $x(n) = a^{n}$ | Stable if $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
| 5 | Sinusoidal | $A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ | Periodic iff $\omega_0 / 2\pi \in \mathbb{Q}$ |
| 6 | Even part | $x_e(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(n) + x(-n)]$ | Symmetric |
| 7 | Odd part | $x_o(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(n) - x(-n)]$ | Anti-symmetric |
| 8 | Energy | $E = \sum \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$ | Energy signal: $0 < E < \infty$ |
| 9 | Power | $P = \lim \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$ | Power signal: $0 < P < \infty$ |
| 10 | Periodicity | $x(n+N) = x(n)$ for smallest $N \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ | Fundamental period $N$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

In PCBMT501, these sequences are not abstract — they are the everyday tools used in:

- **ECG Signal Analysis:** A heartbeat can be modelled as a sum of decaying exponentials; the QRS complex is approximated as an impulse train convolved with a wavelet.
- **EEG Compression:** Energy and power metrics determine compression thresholds.
- **Evoked Potential Detection:** Sinusoidal sequences (with specific $\omega_0$) model stimulus artefacts; they are subtracted (using symmetry) to isolate the biological response.
- **Neural Spike Sorting:** Unit impulses model the times at which neurons fire.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relation Between Unit Impulse and Unit Step

We will prove that the unit step is the cumulative sum of the unit impulse.

**Step 1: Start with the impulse response definition.**

By definition, $\delta(k) = 1$ when $k = 0$ and $\delta(k) = 0$ otherwise. So the running sum from $-\infty$ to $n$ only includes the impulse at $k=0$ when $n \geq 0$:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta(k)$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the sum in two cases.**

$$
\sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta(k) = \begin{cases} 0, & n < 0 \quad (\text{sum does not include } k=0) \\ 1, & n \geq 0 \quad (\text{sum includes } k=0) \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3: Recognise the result as the unit step.**

Comparing with the definition of $u(n)$, we obtain:

$$\boxed{\,u(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta(k)\,}$$

**Step 4: Prove the inverse relation (impulse as first difference of step).**

By the property of discrete-time difference:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(n) - u(n-1) &= \begin{cases} 1 - 0, & n = 0 \\ 1 - 1, & n > 0 \\ 0 - 0, & n < 0 \end{cases} \\[4pt]
&= \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases} \\[4pt]
&= \delta(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $\delta(n) = u(n) - u(n-1)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Form for the Unit Ramp

**Step 1: Express the ramp as a sum of unit steps.**

By definition, $r(n) = n \cdot u(n)$. The number of ones in $\{u(0), u(1), \ldots, u(n)\}$ is exactly $n+1$ for $n \geq 0$. Therefore:

$$r(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} u(k) = \underbrace{1 + 1 + \cdots + 1}_{(n+1) \text{ terms}} = n+1 \quad ?$$

**Step 2: Recheck the definition carefully.**

A common mistake! The ramp $r(n)$ is the running sum of the **step function itself** shifted in definition. The correct interpretation:

$$r(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} k = 0 + 1 + 2 + \cdots + n = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \quad \text{for } n \geq 0$$

**Step 3: Apply the Gaussian summation formula.**

Using the identity $\sum_{k=1}^{n} k = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$:

$$
\boxed{\,r(n) = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\, u(n)\,}
$$

This closed form is extremely useful in KTU problems involving computation of energy and power of polynomial signals. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Even-Odd Decomposition

**Step 1: Define the even and odd parts.**

$$
x_e(n) = \frac{1}{2}[x(n) + x(-n)] \qquad x_o(n) = \frac{1}{2}[x(n) - x(-n)]
$$

**Step 2: Verify that adding them reconstructs $x(n)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(n) + x_o(n) &= \frac{1}{2}[x(n) + x(-n)] + \frac{1}{2}[x(n) - x(-n)] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}[2x(n)] = x(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verify that $x_e(n)$ is even.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(-n) &= \frac{1}{2}[x(-n) + x(-(-n))] = \frac{1}{2}[x(-n) + x(n)] = x_e(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify that $x_o(n)$ is odd.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_o(-n) &= \frac{1}{2}[x(-n) - x(-(-n))] = \frac{1}{2}[x(-n) - x(n)] = -x_o(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

The decomposition is **unique** because we constructed it by adding and subtracting $x(n)$ with its time-reversed version. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Periodicity of a Discrete-Time Sinusoid

**Step 1: Condition for periodicity.**

A sequence $x(n)$ is periodic with fundamental period $N$ if there exists a positive integer $N$ such that:

$$x(n + N) = x(n) \quad \text{for all } n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**Step 2: Apply to sinusoid.**

Let $x(n) = \cos(\omega_0 n)$. Then:

$$x(n + N) = \cos(\omega_0(n + N)) = \cos(\omega_0 n + \omega_0 N)$$

For this to equal $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ for all $n$, we need:

$$\omega_0 N = 2\pi k \quad \text{for some integer } k$$

**Step 3: Solve for $N$.**

$$
N = \frac{2\pi k}{\omega_0}
$$

For $N$ to be a **positive integer**, the ratio $\omega_0 / 2\pi$ must be a rational number. If it is irrational, no such $N$ exists, and the sinusoid is **aperiodic**.

**Example (KTU-style):** If $\omega_0 = \pi/3$, then $N = 2\pi k / (\pi/3) = 6k$. The smallest positive integer is $N = 6$ (when $k=1$). Hence the sequence is periodic with fundamental period $N=6$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is a major difference from continuous-time signals. A continuous-time sinusoid $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)$ is always periodic. In discrete-time, periodicity depends on the digital frequency $\omega_0$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Quick Computation Table

The following table summarises energy and power computations for canonical sequences — frequently asked in KTU Part A questions.

| Sequence | Energy $E$ | Power $P$ | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta(n)$ | $\sum \vert \delta(n) \vert^{2} = 1$ | $0$ | Energy signal |
| $u(n)$ | $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 1 = \infty$ | $\lim \frac{N+1}{2N+1} = \tfrac{1}{2}$ | Power signal |
| $r(n) = n\,u(n)$ | $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n^{2} = \infty$ | $\lim \frac{\sum_{n=0}^{N} n^{2}}{2N+1} = \infty$ | Neither |
| $a^{n}u(n)$, $\vert a \vert < 1$ | $\sum a^{2n} = \tfrac{1}{1-a^{2}}$ | $0$ | Energy signal |
| $\cos(\omega_0 n)$ | $\infty$ (periodic) | $\tfrac{1}{2}$ | Power signal |
| $A\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ | $\infty$ | $A^{2}/2$ | Power signal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For energy calculation, $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^{n} = \frac{1}{1-r}$ only if $\vert r \vert < 1$. If $\vert r \vert \geq 1$, the sum diverges and energy is infinite. Many students lose marks by forgetting this condition.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sequence Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discrete Time Signal x of n] --> B{Defined over what domain}
    B --> C[Finite Duration Sequence]
    B --> D[Infinite Duration Sequence]
    D --> E{Causal or Non causal}
    E --> F[Causal x of n is 0 for n less than 0]
    E --> G[Non causal x of n is nonzero for n less than 0]
    D --> H{Energy or Power}
    H --> I[Energy Signal 0 less than E less than infinity]
    H --> J[Power Signal 0 less than P less than infinity]
    A --> K{Canonical Building Blocks}
    K --> L[Unit Impulse delta of n]
    K --> M[Unit Step u of n]
    K --> N[Unit Ramp r of n]
    K --> O[Exponential a power n]
    K --> P[Sinusoidal cos omega sub 0 n]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Four Representations of $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Functional Form x of n equals 4 minus absolute n for minus 2 less than or equal to n less than or equal to 2] --> B[Tabular Form]
    A --> C[Sequence Set Form]
    A --> D[Graphical Stem Plot]
    B --> B1[n equals minus 2 x equals 1]
    B --> B2[n equals minus 1 x equals 2]
    B --> B3[n equals 0 x equals 3]
    B --> B4[n equals 1 x equals 2]
    B --> B5[n equals 2 x equals 1]
    C --> C1[Set x of n equals 1 comma 2 comma 3 comma 2 comma 1 with arrow over 1 comma 2 comma 3 comma 2 comma 1]
    D --> D1[Even Symmetric Stem Plot centered at n equals 0]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: How to Test Periodicity (KTU Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Given x of n equals cos of omega sub 0 n] --> B[Set x of n plus N equals x of n]
    B --> C[Derive condition omega sub 0 N equals 2 pi k]
    C --> D{Is omega sub 0 divided by 2 pi rational}
    D --> E[Yes Fundamental period N equals 2 pi k divided by omega sub 0]
    D --> F[No Aperiodic no integer N satisfies condition]
    E --> G[Choose smallest positive integer k such that N is integer]
    G --> H[Output Fundamental period N]
    F --> I[Output Sequence is aperiodic]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Even-Odd Decomposition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x of n] --> B[Time Reverse x to get x of minus n]
    A --> C[Add x of n and x of minus n]
    B --> C
    C --> D[Divide by 2]
    D --> E[Even Part x sub e of n]
    A --> F[Subtract x of minus n from x of n]
    B --> F
    F --> G[Divide by 2]
    G --> H[Odd Part x sub o of n]
    E --> I[Sum x sub e plus x sub o equals x of n]
    H --> I
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Architecture: Biomedical Signal Acquisition Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Physiological Signal] --> B[Transducer ECG EEG EMG]
    B --> C[Continuous Time Signal x of t]
    C --> D[Anti aliasing Filter]
    D --> E[ADC Sampling at F sub s]
    E --> F[Discrete Time Signal x of n]
    F --> G[Quantization]
    G --> H[Digital Signal x sub q of n]
    H --> I[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    I --> J[Display or Storage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In the acquisition chain, the signal transitions from continuous-time (analog) to discrete-time (sampled) to digital (quantized). The first arrow $F$ is where **typical sequences** first appear — this is the gateway to all further DSP operations in PCBMT501.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define a discrete-time signal. List any four typical (basic) sequences used in digital signal processing.

**Model Answer:**

A **discrete-time signal** is a function $x(n)$ defined only at discrete integer values of the independent variable $n \in \mathbb{Z} = \{\ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots\}$.

Four typical sequences are:

1. **Unit impulse sequence** $\delta(n)$: equals 1 at $n=0$ and 0 elsewhere.
2. **Unit step sequence** $u(n)$: equals 1 for $n \geq 0$ and 0 for $n < 0$.
3. **Unit ramp sequence** $r(n)$: equals $n$ for $n \geq 0$ and 0 for $n < 0$.
4. **Real exponential sequence** $x(n) = a^{n}$.

(Other acceptable: sinusoidal, complex exponential.)

**[Marks Distribution: 1 (definition) + 2 (list with brief description)]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Determine whether the discrete-time signal $x(n) = 5\cos(0.2\pi n + \pi/4)$ is periodic. If so, find the fundamental period.

**Model Answer:**

For periodicity, we need $x(n+N) = x(n)$ for the smallest positive integer $N$.

$$
x(n + N) = 5\cos(0.2\pi(n+N) + \pi/4) = 5\cos(0.2\pi n + 0.2\pi N + \pi/4)
$$

For this to equal $x(n)$ for all $n$:

$$0.2\pi N = 2\pi k \implies N = \frac{2\pi k}{0.2\pi} = 10k$$

The smallest positive integer value of $N$ occurs when $k=1$, giving $N = 10$.

**Therefore, the sequence is periodic with fundamental period $N = 10$ samples.** $\blacksquare$

**[Marks Distribution: 2 (derivation) + 1 (conclusion)]**

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the four different representations of a discrete-time signal with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the even and odd parts of the signal $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with the arrow pointing to $x(0) = 3$. Hence verify that $x(n) = x_e(n) + x_o(n)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

A discrete-time signal can be represented in four equivalent ways:

**1. Functional Representation:** A closed-form mathematical expression $x(n) = f(n)$.
- *Example:* $x(n) = \cos(\pi n / 4)$ for $n \geq 0$.

**2. Tabular Representation:** A list of $(n, x(n))$ pairs.
- *Example:*

| $n$ | -1 | 0 | 1 | 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x(n)$ | 0.7 | 1 | 0.7 | 0 |

**3. Graphical Representation:** A stem (lollipop) plot with the horizontal axis showing the index $n$ and vertical stems showing the amplitude $x(n)$.
- *Example:* A stem plot of $x(n) = 2\delta(n) + 3\delta(n-1)$ has a stem of height 2 at $n=0$ and a stem of height 3 at $n=1$.

**4. Sequence (Set) Representation:** A list of values with a sample index arrow above the value at $n=0$.
- *Example:* $x(n) = \{\underset{\uparrow}{1}, 2, 3, 4\}$ — the arrow marks $x(0) = 1$, so $x(-1)$ is undefined or zero, and $x(1) = 2$, $x(2) = 3$, $x(3) = 4$.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

The sequence is $x(n) = \{1, 2, \underset{\uparrow}{3}, 4, 5\}$ where the arrow indicates $x(0) = 3$. Therefore:

$$x(-2) = 1, \quad x(-1) = 2, \quad x(0) = 3, \quad x(1) = 4, \quad x(2) = 5$$

**Step 1: Compute the time-reversed sequence $x(-n)$.**

For $x(-n)$: replace $n$ with $-n$.

$$x(-(-2)) = x(2) = 5, \quad x(-(-1)) = x(1) = 4, \quad x(-0) = x(0) = 3$$
$$x(-(1)) = x(-1) = 2, \quad x(-(2)) = x(-2) = 1$$

So $x(-n) = \{5, 4, \underset{\uparrow}{3}, 2, 1\}$.

**Step 2: Compute the even part $x_e(n) = \frac{1}{2}[x(n) + x(-n)]$.**

$$
x_e(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}\{1+5, \; 2+4, \; 3+3, \; 4+2, \; 5+1\} = \tfrac{1}{2}\{6, 6, 6, 6, 6\} = \{3, 3, \underset{\uparrow}{3}, 3, 3\}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the odd part $x_o(n) = \frac{1}{2}[x(n) - x(-n)]$.**

$$
x_o(n) = \tfrac{1}{2}\{1-5, \; 2-4, \; 3-3, \; 4-2, \; 5-1\} = \tfrac{1}{2}\{-4, -2, 0, 2, 4\} = \{-2, -1, \underset{\uparrow}{0}, 1, 2\}
$$

**Step 4: Verify the decomposition $x(n) = x_e(n) + x_o(n)$.**

$$
x_e(n) + x_o(n) = \{3-2, \; 3-1, \; 3+0, \; 3+1, \; 3+2\} = \{1, 2, \underset{\uparrow}{3}, 4, 5\} = x(n) \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Define and sketch the following sequences for $-3 \leq n \leq 3$:
   (i) $x_1(n) = \delta(n)$  (ii) $x_2(n) = u(n) - u(n-3)$  (iii) $x_3(n) = n \cdot u(n)$  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the energy and power of the following signals and classify each as an energy signal, power signal, or neither:
   (i) $x_1(n) = (0.5)^{n} u(n)$  (ii) $x_2(n) = \cos(0.25\pi n)$  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**(i) $x_1(n) = \delta(n)$:**

By definition, $\delta(n) = 1$ at $n = 0$ and 0 elsewhere. So for $-3 \leq n \leq 3$:

$$\{x_1(-3), x_1(-2), x_1(-1), x_1(0), x_1(1), x_1(2), x_1(3)\} = \{0, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0\}$$

Graph: A single stem of height 1 at $n = 0$.

**(ii) $x_2(n) = u(n) - u(n-3)$:**

$u(n) = 1$ for $n \geq 0$, so $u(n-3) = 1$ for $n \geq 3$. Therefore $x_2(n) = 1$ for $0 \leq n \leq 2$ and 0 elsewhere.

$$\{x_2(-3), x_2(-2), x_2(-1), x_2(0), x_2(1), x_2(2), x_2(3)\} = \{0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0\}$$

Graph: Three unit-height stems from $n=0$ to $n=2$.

**(iii) $x_3(n) = n \cdot u(n)$:**

$x_3(n) = n$ for $n \geq 0$ and 0 for $n < 0$.

$$\{x_3(-3), x_3(-2), x_3(-1), x_3(0), x_3(1), x_3(2), x_3(3)\} = \{0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 2, 3\}$$

Graph: A linearly increasing stem plot starting from $n=0$.

**Sketch (textual):**
```
 3 |           |
 2 |       |   |
 1 |   |   |   |
 0 |___|___|___|___
    -3 -2 -1  0  1  2  3   (n)
```

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**(i) $x_1(n) = (0.5)^n u(n)$:**

Since $x_1(n)$ is non-zero only for $n \geq 0$, $x_1(n) = (0.5)^n$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

**Energy:**

$$
E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x_1(n) \vert^{2} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^{2n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^{n}
$$

Using the geometric series formula $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^{n} = \frac{1}{1-r}$ for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$
E = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{1}{0.75} = \frac{4}{3}
$$

**Power:**

$$
P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x_1(n) \vert^{2} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = 0
$$

**Classification:** Since $0 < E = 4/3 < \infty$ and $P = 0$, $x_1(n)$ is an **energy signal** (and not a power signal).

---

**(ii) $x_2(n) = \cos(0.25\pi n)$:**

**Energy:** The sequence is periodic, so the energy sum diverges:

$$
E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \cos^{2}(0.25\pi n) = \infty
$$

**Power:** Average over one period. The fundamental period is:

$$N = \frac{2\pi}{0.25\pi} = 8$$

Using the identity $\cos^{2}(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$ and the fact that the average of $\cos$ over an integer number of periods is 0:

$$
P = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \cos^{2}(0.25\pi n) = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{n=0}^{7} \frac{1 + \cos(0.5\pi n)}{2}
$$

$$
P = \frac{1}{16} \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{7} 1 + \sum_{n=0}^{7} \cos(0.5\pi n) \right]
$$

The first sum is 8. The second sum: $\cos$ values at $n=0,\ldots,7$ for $0.5\pi n$ are $\{1, 0, -1, 0, 1, 0, -1, 0\}$; sum = 0.

$$
P = \frac{1}{16}[8 + 0] = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Classification:** Since $E = \infty$ and $0 < P = 1/2 < \infty$, $x_2(n)$ is a **power signal** (and not an energy signal). $\blacksquare$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $u(0)$ values:** Some textbooks define $u(0) = 1$, others $u(0) = 0.5$. Use the **KTU standard**: $u(0) = 1$, since discrete-time step is right-continuous in a sense.
> 2. **Forgetting to mark $n=0$ in sequence notation:** The arrow $\uparrow$ above the value of $x(0)$ is **mandatory** in KTU board exams. Skipping it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Periodicity without verifying integer $N$:** Just writing $N = 2\pi/\omega_0$ is incomplete. You must explicitly check that $N$ is a positive integer and find the smallest such $N$.
> 4. **Conflating $\delta(t)$ and $\delta(n)$:** In continuous-time, $\delta(t)$ has unit area; in discrete-time, $\delta(n)$ has unit **value** (height). This is worth 2–3 marks in Part A.
> 5. **Energy/Power classification errors:** A signal cannot be both energy and power. Always state $E = \infty$ for power signals and $P = 0$ for energy signals to be safe.
> 6. **Direction of sum for ramp:** When asked for $r(n)$ via summation, students often write $\sum u(k) = n$ instead of $\sum k = n(n+1)/2$. This is a 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Discrete-Time Signals & Typical Sequences**

- [x] A **discrete-time signal** $x(n)$ is defined for integer $n$ only. A **digital signal** is a discrete-time signal whose amplitude is also quantized.
- [x] Five **canonical sequences**: unit impulse $\delta(n)$, unit step $u(n)$, unit ramp $r(n)$, exponential $a^{n}$, sinusoidal $\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi)$.
- [x] **Key relations**: $u(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} \delta(k)$ and $\delta(n) = u(n) - u(n-1)$.
- [x] **Ramp closed form**: $r(n) = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ for $n \geq 0$.
- [x] **Even-Odd decomposition**: $x(n) = x_e(n) + x_o(n)$, where $x_e(n) = \frac{1}{2}[x(n) + x(-n)]$ and $x_o(n) = \frac{1}{2}[x(n) - x(-n)]$.
- [x] **Four representations**: functional, tabular, graphical (stem plot), sequence set form.
- [x] **Periodicity condition** for sinusoids: $N = \frac{2\pi k}{\omega_0}$ must be a positive integer; smallest such $N$ is the fundamental period.
- [x] **Energy**: $E = \sum \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$; **Power**: $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$.
- [x] **Classification rule**: $0 < E < \infty \Rightarrow$ energy signal; $0 < P < \infty \Rightarrow$ power signal. Signals cannot be both.
- [x] Geometric series identity: $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} r^{n} = \frac{1}{1-r}$ only if $\vert r \vert < 1$.
- [x] **Biomedical relevance**: ECG sampled at ~250 Hz, EEG at ~250 Hz, EMG at ~2 kHz, neural spikes at ~10 kHz.
- [x] **Notation convention**: $n$ is the index (dimensionless), $t = nT_s$ is the physical time (seconds).
- [x] Always specify the **range of $n$** explicitly in board answers; undefined values are implicitly zero only if the sequence is causal.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete-Time Systems - Classification of Discrete-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Systems - Classification of Discrete-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Discrete-Time System

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Rigorous)**
> A **discrete-time system** is a mathematical operator $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ to an output discrete-time sequence $y[n]$, where $n$ is an integer-valued time index. Formally:
> $$y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$$

In biomedical signal processing, the input $x[n]$ could be a sampled ECG, EEG, or EMG signal, and the system performs operations like **filtering**, **denoising**, or **feature extraction** to produce the processed output $y[n]$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Black Box Doctor" Analogy

Imagine a doctor's diagnostic machine (a black box). You feed it a patient's vital signs at specific time intervals (discrete samples), and the machine produces a health assessment (output).

- **If the machine only looks at the current reading** → it is **memoryless (static)**.
- **If it remembers previous readings** → it has **memory (dynamic)**.
- **If shifting when you arrive doesn't change the diagnosis** → it is **time-invariant**.
- **If doubling the input always doubles the response** → it is **linear**.
- **If it never asks about tomorrow's readings** → it is **causal**.
- **If wild input swings never crash the readings** → it is **stable**.

The classification of discrete-time systems is essentially a list of *behavioural properties* that help us predict how a system will behave in practical applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Classification of discrete-time systems is a fundamental topic. Students must be able to:
> 1. Identify the **six primary classifications**.
> 2. Apply the **mathematical tests** for each property.
> 3. Determine the classification of any given system from its input-output relationship.

### 1.3 The Six Primary Classifications (Overview)

The six properties every KTU student must know are:

1. **Static (Memoryless) vs Dynamic (with Memory)**
2. **Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant**
3. **Linear vs Non-Linear**
4. **Causal vs Non-Causal**
5. **Stable vs Unstable**
6. **Invertible vs Non-Invertible**

A given system may simultaneously be "Linear, Time-Invariant, Causal, Stable, Static, and Invertible" — or any combination of these properties.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification 1: Static (Memoryless) vs Dynamic (With Memory)

**Static (Memoryless) System:** The output at time $n$ depends *only* on the input at the *same* time $n$.

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = f(x[n])$$

The system has **no memory** — it forgets all past samples instantly.

**Dynamic (With Memory) System:** The output depends on *past and/or future* input samples.

$$y[n] = f(x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots, x[n-N])$$

It has memory of length $N$ samples (for an FIR-type system) or infinite memory (for an IIR-type system).

**Biomedical Examples:**
- *Memoryless:* A simple **scaler** $y[n] = 2x[n]$ — used in basic signal amplification.
- *Dynamic:* The **moving average filter** $y[n] = \tfrac{1}{3}(x[n] + x[n-1] + x[n-2])$ — used to smooth noisy ECG signals.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Test:** If you see $x[n-k]$ for any $k \neq 0$ in the equation, the system is **dynamic**.

### 2.2 Classification 2: Linear vs Non-Linear

A system is **linear** if and only if it satisfies the **principle of superposition**, which combines **additivity** and **homogeneity (scaling)**.

$$T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a\,T\{x_1[n]\} + b\,T\{x_2[n]\}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. Apply a weighted sum of two inputs to the system.
2. Compute the output of the combined input.
3. Compute the weighted sum of individual outputs.
4. **If both results are equal → LINEAR; otherwise → NON-LINEAR.**

**Biomedical Examples:**
- *Linear:* The accumulator $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ — used in integration-based feature extraction.
- *Non-Linear:* $y[n] = x^2[n]$ — used in **squared-amplitude EEG** analysis for power computations.

### 2.3 Classification 3: Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant

A system is **time-invariant** if a shift in the input signal produces an *identical shift* in the output signal.

**Test:**
1. Find $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$.
2. Find $y_1[n] = T\{x[n-n_0]\}$.
3. Compute $y[n-n_0]$.
4. **If $y_1[n] = y[n-n_0]$ for all $n$ and all $n_0$ → TIME-INVARIANT.**

$$T\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0] \quad \text{(Time-Invariant Condition)}$$

**Biomedical Examples:**
- *Time-Invariant:* $y[n] = 0.5\,x[n] - 0.3\,x[n-1]$ — a fixed-coefficient filter.
- *Time-Variant:* $y[n] = n\,x[n]$ — a system whose behaviour changes with time; useful in **adaptive noise cancellation**.

### 2.4 Classification 4: Causal vs Non-Causal

A system is **causal** if the output at time $n$ depends *only* on present and past input values (i.e., $x[k]$ for $k \leq n$).

$$y[n] = f(x[n], x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots) \quad \text{(Causal — No future dependence)}$$

If the system uses any **future** input sample $x[n+k]$ where $k > 0$, it is **non-causal**.

**Biomedical Examples:**
- *Causal:* $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ — a first-difference operator used in ECG baseline wander removal.
- *Non-Causal:* $y[n] = \tfrac{1}{2}(x[n+1] + x[n-1])$ — requires future samples; used in **offline signal smoothing** for diagnostic review.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Time Constraint:** All real-time biomedical monitoring systems **must be causal**. Non-causal systems can only be used in offline/recorded data analysis.

### 2.5 Classification 5: Stable vs Unstable (BIBO Stability)

A system is **BIBO Stable (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Stable)** if every bounded input produces a bounded output.

**Formal Test:**
If $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $n$, then there must exist some finite $M_y$ such that $\vert y[n] \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ for all $n$.

**Necessary and Sufficient Condition (LTI Systems):** A causal LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$ is BIBO stable if and only if:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

**Biomedical Examples:**
- *Stable:* $y[n] = 0.5\,x[n]$ — output can never exceed 0.5 times the input.
- *Unstable:* $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} x[k]$ (the accumulator) — bounded input can produce a linearly growing unbounded output.

### 2.6 Classification 6: Invertible vs Non-Invertible

A system is **invertible** if distinct inputs produce distinct outputs, allowing **unique recovery** of $x[n]$ from $y[n]$ using an inverse system $T^{-1}\{\cdot\}$.

**Test:** Find $T^{-1}\{\cdot\}$ such that:

$$T^{-1}\{T\{x[n]\}\} = x[n]$$

**Biomedical Examples:**
- *Invertible:* $y[n] = 2x[n]$ — inverse is $x[n] = \tfrac{1}{2}y[n]$.
- *Non-Invertible:* $y[n] = x^2[n]$ — multiple inputs ($\pm x[n]$) give the same output.

---

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Property** | **Mathematical Test Condition** | **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Special Form** | **Biomedical Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static (Memoryless)** | $y[n] = f(x[n])$ only | $y[n] = a\,x[n] + b$ | Signal amplification/scaling |
| **Dynamic (With Memory)** | $y[n]$ depends on $x[n-k]$ for $k \neq 0$ | $y[n] = \sum h[k]\,x[n-k]$ | Filtering, smoothing |
| **Linear** | $T\{ax_1 + bx_2\} = aT\{x_1\} + bT\{x_2\}$ | Convolution-sum structure | Most DSP filters |
| **Time-Invariant** | $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0]$ | Constant coefficients | Standard fixed filters |
| **Causal** | $y[n]$ depends only on $x[k]$ for $k \leq n$ | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Real-time patient monitors |
| **Stable (BIBO)** | Bounded input $\Rightarrow$ Bounded output | $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Reliable diagnostic systems |
| **Invertible** | $T^{-1}\{T\{x[n]\}\} = x[n]$ exists uniquely | $\text{ROC includes unit circle}$ & no zero on it | Lossless signal recovery |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification framework is not just academic — it determines **what a system can and cannot do** in biomedical applications:
- **LTI systems** dominate real-time ECG/EEG filtering because they are mathematically tractable and predictable.
- **Causality** is non-negotiable for ICU monitors.
- **Stability** is essential for implanted devices like pacemakers (a single unstable computation can be life-threatening).
- **Invertibility** is critical in compressed-sensing MRI reconstruction.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: BIBO Stability Test for LTI Systems

**Starting Assumption:** Consider a causal LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$. The output is the convolution sum:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} h[k]\,x[n-k]$$

**Step 1 — Take absolute value on both sides:** Apply the triangle inequality to bound the output magnitude.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert y[n] \vert &= \left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} h[k]\,x[n-k] \right\vert \\
&\leq \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert \cdot \vert x[n-k] \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The magnitude of a sum is at most the sum of magnitudes (triangle inequality).

**Step 2 — Apply the bounded-input assumption:** Suppose $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x$ for all $n$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert y[n] \vert &\leq \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert \cdot M_x \\
&= M_x \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Since $\vert x[n-k] \vert \leq M_x$, it can be factored out as a constant.

**Step 3 — Define the output bound:** If the impulse response is absolutely summable, define:

$$S = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert < \infty$$

Then $\vert y[n] \vert \leq M_x \cdot S < \infty$, which is a finite bound. Hence the system is BIBO stable.

**Final Result:** A causal LTI discrete-time system is BIBO stable **if and only if**:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Test for Time-Invariance

**System:** $y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = n\,x[n]$ (a candidate time-variant system).

**Step 1 — Compute the response to the shifted input $x[n-n_0]$:**

$$y_1[n] = T\{x[n-n_0]\} = n\,x[n-n_0]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the shifted version of the original output:**

$$y[n-n_0] = (n-n_0)\,x[n-n_0]$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_1[n] - y[n-n_0] &= n\,x[n-n_0] - (n-n_0)\,x[n-n_0] \\
&= n_0\,x[n-n_0]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The difference is $n_0\,x[n-n_0]$, which is non-zero for $n_0 \neq 0$ and any non-zero input. Hence $y_1[n] \neq y[n-n_0]$, and the system is **time-variant**.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Linearity Test

**System:** $y[n] = 2x[n] + 3$

**Step 1 — Apply two inputs separately:**

$$y_1[n] = 2x_1[n] + 3$$
$$y_2[n] = 2x_2[n] + 3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the combined input $a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} &= 2(a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) + 3 \\
&= 2a\,x_1[n] + 2b\,x_2[n] + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linear combination of individual outputs:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a\,y_1[n] + b\,y_2[n] &= a(2x_1[n] + 3) + b(2x_2[n] + 3) \\
&= 2a\,x_1[n] + 2b\,x_2[n] + 3(a+b)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare the two results:**

$$T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = 2a x_1 + 2b x_2 + 3$$
$$a y_1 + b y_2 = 2a x_1 + 2b x_2 + 3(a+b)$$

These are **not equal** (unless $a+b=1$), so the system is **non-linear**. The constant term 3 violates additivity.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Memory and Causality Test

**System:** $y[n] = x[n] - x[n+2]$

- **Memory:** The output uses $x[n+2]$ (a *future* sample) → system is **dynamic** (has memory).
- **Causality:** It uses $x[n+2]$ with $+2$ shift, which is a future sample → system is **non-causal**.
- **Invertibility:** The system can be inverted by rearranging: $x[n+2] = x[n] - y[n]$, so shifting by $-2$ recovers $x[n]$ → system is **invertible**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Property Test Summary

| **System Equation** | **Static/Dynamic** | **Linear/Non-Linear** | **Time-Inv/Var** | **Causal/Non-Causal** | **Stable (BIBO)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y[n] = 2x[n]$ | Static | Linear | Time-Invariant | Causal | Stable |
| $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ | Dynamic | Linear | Time-Invariant | Causal | Stable |
| $y[n] = x^2[n]$ | Static | Non-Linear | Time-Invariant | Causal | Stable |
| $y[n] = n\,x[n]$ | Static | Linear | Time-Variant | Causal | Stable (if $x[n]$ bounded) |
| $y[n] = x[n+1]$ | Dynamic | Linear | Time-Invariant | Non-Causal | Stable |
| $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} x[k]$ | Dynamic | Linear | Time-Invariant | Causal | **Unstable** |
| $y[n] = \cos(\pi n/3)\,x[n]$ | Static | Linear | Time-Variant | Causal | Stable |
| $y[n] = x[n] - x[n+2]$ | Dynamic | Linear | Time-Invariant | Non-Causal | Stable |

```mermaid
graph TD

    E --> E2[Non Causal Uses Future Samples]

    F --> F2[Unstable Bounded In Unbounded Out]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Decision Flow for System Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given System Equation] --> Q1{Does y depend on x n minus k for k not zero}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Dyn[Dynamic]
    Q1 -- No --> Stat[Static]

    Start --> Q2{Does T of a x1 plus b x2 equal a T of x1 plus b T of x2}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Lin[Linear]
    Q2 -- No --> NL[Non Linear]

    Start --> Q3{Does T of x of n minus n0 equal y of n minus n0 for all n0}
    Q3 -- Yes --> TI[Time Invariant]
    Q3 -- No --> TV[Time Variant]

    Start --> Q4{Does y of n depend on x of n plus k for any k greater than zero}
    Q4 -- No --> Cau[Causal]
    Q4 -- Yes --> NCau[Non Causal]

    Start --> Q5{Is sum of absolute h of n less than infinity}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Stab[Stable BIBO]
    Q5 -- No --> Unstab[Unstable]

    Start --> Q6{Does a unique inverse system exist}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Inv[Invertible]
    Q6 -- No --> NInv[Non Invertible]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Biosignal Source ECG EEG EMG] --> S2[Analog Front End Amplification]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** In real-time biomedical systems (e.g., patient monitors), the discrete-time system block $T\{\cdot\}$ *must* be **Causal**, **Stable**, and ideally **Linear and Time-Invariant (LTI)** to guarantee predictable, reliable, and mathematically tractable behaviour. Non-causal and non-linear operations are reserved for offline diagnostic post-processing.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a discrete-time system. With an example, explain the difference between a static and a dynamic discrete-time system.*

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time system is a mathematical operator $T\{\cdot\}$ that transforms an input sequence $x[n]$ into an output sequence $y[n]$, where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

- **Static (Memoryless):** Output depends only on the present input — example: $y[n] = 3x[n]$.
- **Dynamic (With Memory):** Output depends on past and/or future inputs — example: $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$.

If the system equation contains $x[n-k]$ for any $k \neq 0$, the system has memory (dynamic); otherwise, it is static. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the BIBO stability criterion for a discrete-time LTI system.*

**Model Answer:**

A discrete-time LTI system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output. The necessary and sufficient condition is that its impulse response $h[n]$ be **absolutely summable**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

If this sum is finite, the system is stable; if infinite, the system is unstable. Example: $h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$ is stable because $\sum (0.5)^n = 2 < \infty$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write the **linearity equation** as $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\}$ only (forgetting the scaling property $a, b$). This is incomplete — you will lose 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** For **time-invariance**, students forget to check the condition for **all** $n_0$, not just one value. The system must satisfy the test for every possible shift.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** For **causality**, students confuse "static" with "causal". A static system is always causal, but a causal system need not be static.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In **BIBO stability** derivations, students forget to apply the **triangle inequality** step explicitly, leading to a "skip" that costs marks.
