> Compute the 4-point DIT-FFT of the sequence $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. Sketch the butterfly flow graph and verify the result.

**Model Solution:**

**Bit-reversal of input** ($N=4$, indices in 2-bit binary): $\{00, 01, 10, 11\} \to \{00, 10, 01, 11\}$, so bit-reversed order is $\{x(0), x(2), x(1), x(3)\} = \{1, 3, 2, 4\}$.

**Stage 1 — Two 2-point DFTs** (twiddle $W_2^0 = 1$):
- $G(0) = 1 + 3 = 4$
- $G(1) = 1 - 3 = -2$
- $H(0) = 2 + 4 = 6$
- $H(1) = 2 - 4 = -2$

**Stage 2 — Final butterflies** (twiddles $W_4^0 = 1$ and $W_4^1 = -j$):
- $X(0) = G(0) + W_4^0 H(0) = 4 + 6 = 10$
- $X(2) = G(0) - W_4^0 H(0) = 4 - 6 = -2$
- $X(1) = G(1) + W_4^1 H(1) = -2 + (-j)(-2) = -2 + 2j$
- $X(3) = G(1) - W_4^1 H(1) = -2 - (-j)(-2) = -2 - 2j$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{X(k) = \{10,\; -2+2j,\; -2,\; -2-2j\}}$$

**Verification (direct DFT):** $X(0) = 1+2+3+4 = 10$ ✓; $X(1) = 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2+2j$ ✓; $X(2) = 1-2+3-4 = -2$ ✓; $X(3) = 1+2j-3-4j = -2-2j$ ✓.

**Computational saving:** Direct DFT requires $4^2 = 16$ complex multiplications, while DIT-FFT requires $(4/2)\log_2 4 = 4$ — a **4× reduction**. **(7 marks — bit reversal 1, Stage 1 2, Stage 2 2, verification 2)**

---

#### **Question 2 (a)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] (7 marks)

> Derive the radix-2 Decimation-in-Frequency (DIF) FFT algorithm by splitting the output sequence into even and odd indexed frequency components. Clearly state the basic butterfly.

**Model Solution:**

Starting with the N-point DFT and splitting the sum over $n$ into two halves (each of length $M = N/2$):

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} x(n)\, W_N^{nk} + \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} x(n+M)\, W_N^{(n+M)k}$$

Factor out $W_N^{nk}$ and use $W_N^{Mk} = (-1)^k$:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \bigl[x(n) + (-1)^k\, x(n+M)\bigr]\, W_N^{nk}$$

**Even-indexed frequencies** ($k = 2r$): $(-1)^{2r} = 1$:

$$X(2r) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \bigl[x(n) + x(n+M)\bigr]\, W_M^{nr} = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} y(n)\, W_M^{nr}$$

where $y(n) = x(n) + x(n+M)$.

**Odd-indexed frequencies** ($k = 2r+1$): $(-1)^{2r+1} = -1$:

$$X(2r+1) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \bigl[x(n) - x(n+M)\bigr]\, W_N^{n}\, W_M^{nr} = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} z(n)\, W_M^{nr}$$

where $z(n) = [x(n) - x(n+M)]\, W_N^{n}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **basic DIF butterfly** is:
> - $y(n) = x(n) + x(n+M)$  → feeds the even-frequency bins
> - $z(n) = [x(n) - x(n+M)]\, W_N^{n}$  → feeds the odd-frequency bins
> The twiddle multiplication happens **before** the smaller DFT — opposite of DIT.

Recursion proceeds for $\log_2 N$ stages. **(7 marks — splitting 2, even/odd split 3, butterfly definition 2)**

---

#### **Question 2 (b)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper] (7 marks)

> Compute the 4-point DIF-FFT of the sequence $x(n) = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. Draw the flow graph and verify your answer using the direct DFT.

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Pre-butterfly** (input in natural order, twiddles $W_4^0 = 1$, $W_4^1 = -j$):
- $y(0) = x(0) + x(2) = 1 + 3 = 4$
- $y(1) = x(1) + x(3) = 2 + 4 = 6$
- $z(0) = [x(0) - x(2)]\, W_4^0 = (-2)(1) = -2$
- $z(1) = [x(1) - x(3)]\, W_4^1 = (-2)(-j) = 2j$

**Stage 2 — Two 2-point DFTs** (twiddle $W_2^0 = 1$):
- $X(0) = y(0) + y(1) = 4 + 6 = 10$  *(even-indexed output)*
- $X(2) = y(0) - y(1) = 4 - 6 = -2$  *(even-indexed output)*
- $X(1) = z(0) + z(1) = -2 + 2j$    *(odd-indexed output)*
- $X(3) = z(0) - z(1) = -2 - 2j$    *(odd-indexed output)*

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{X(k) = \{10,\; -2+2j,\; -2,\; -2-2j\}}$$

This is **bit-reversed in the index ordering** of the *intermediate* computation, but the final output matches the DIT result exactly. Verification: $X(0) = 1+2+3+4 = 10$ ✓; $X(1) = 1 + 2(-j) + 3(-1) + 4(j) = -2+2j$ ✓; $X(2) = 1-2+3-4 = -2$ ✓; $X(3) = 1+2j-3-4j = -2-2j$ ✓. **(7 marks — Stage 1 2, Stage 2 2, verification 1.5, flow graph 1.5)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **FFT is an algorithm, not a new transform** — it computes the *same* DFT in $O(N \log_2 N)$ operations.
- **Radix-2 condition:** $N$ must be a power of 2 ($N = 2, 4, 8, 16, \dots$).
- **Twiddle factor:** $W_N = e^{-j2\pi/N}$, magnitude 1, with **symmetry** $W_N^{k+N/2} = -W_N^k$ and **periodicity** $W_N^{k+N} = W_N^k$.
- **Squaring identity:** $W_N^{2nk} = W_{N/2}^{nk}$ — the *single most important* property for DIT-FFT.
- **DIT-FFT:** Input is **bit-reversed**, output is **natural**; twiddle is on the **lower branch** *after* the smaller DFT; $X(k) = G(k) \pm W_N^k H(k)$.
- **DIF-FFT:** Input is **natural**, output is **bit-reversed**; twiddle is on the **lower (difference) branch** *before* the smaller DFT; $y(n) = x(n) + x(n+M)$ and $z(n) = [x(n) - x(n+M)] W_N^n$.
- **Number of stages:** $\log_2 N$.
- **Number of butterflies per stage:** $N/2$.
- **Total butterflies:** $(N/2)\log_2 N$.
- **Total complex multiplications:** $(N/2)\log_2 N$ (twiddle multiplications only).
- **Total complex additions:** $N \log_2 N$.
- **Speedup factor vs direct DFT:** $\dfrac{2N}{\log_2 N}$ — grows rapidly with $N$.
- **In-place computation:** ✓ — only $N$ complex memory locations required.
- **DIT and DIF are transposes of each other** — same numerical answer.
- **Biomedical relevance:** ECG/EEG/EMG spectral analysis, real-time HRV, hearing-aid DSP, Doppler ultrasound.
- **Verification trick:** $X(0) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)$ (DC bin equals the sum of all samples) — a 5-second sanity check that catches arithmetic errors.
- **For $N = 8$ FFT:** 3 stages, 12 butterflies, 8 unique twiddle constants — perfect for KTU's 14-mark derivations.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exam hall:** **"DIT = Decimate Input, Time-reversed input goes in, Natural comes out"** and **"DIF = Decimate output Frequency, Natural input goes in, output is jumbled (bit-reversed)"**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: z-Transform - Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** z-Transform - Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2: z-Transform - Properties

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

The **z-transform** of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is formally defined as the bilateral summation:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a **complex variable** (typically expressed in polar form as $z = r e^{j\omega}$). The *properties* of the z-transform are a set of mathematical theorems that govern how algebraic operations performed on the time-domain signal $x[n]$ translate into equivalent, often simpler, operations on the z-domain representation $X(z)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Properties of the z-transform are the operational rules (linearity, shifting, scaling, convolution, etc.) that allow a biomedical signal processing engineer to manipulate and analyze discrete-time bio-signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) efficiently in the z-domain without repeatedly evaluating the summation definition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Biomedical Translator's Rule Book"**
>
> Imagine the z-transform as a **universal language translator** for biomedical signals. An ECG signal $x[n]$ is "spoken" in time-domain English. Its z-transform $X(z)$ is the equivalent "mathematical French." The **properties** are like the *grammar rules* of this translation:
>
> - When you **delay** a heart-beat signal by 3 samples (a time shift), it's equivalent to multiplying its z-transform by $z^{-3}$ — like a delay stamp on a medical report.
> - When you **add** two bio-signals, you simply add their z-transforms — just like mixing two drugs gives the sum of their individual effects (Linearity).
> - When you **convolve** (filter) a noisy ECG with a smoothing filter, the z-transforms simply **multiply** — turning a difficult integration into a trivial multiplication.

These rules are the backbone of digital filter design and spectral analysis in every modern biomedical device — from Holter monitors to MRI image reconstruction.

### 1.3 Region of Convergence (ROC) — A Critical Companion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every z-transform property is valid **only when the resulting z-transform and the original z-transform share a common Region of Convergence (ROC)**. The ROC is the set of all $z$ values in the complex plane for which the summation $X(z)$ converges (i.e., $\vert X(z) \vert < \infty$).

| Signal Type | ROC Shape | Causal LTI System Example |
|---|---|---|
| Causal $x[n] = 0, n < 0$ | Outside a circle: $\vert z \vert > R_1$ | ECG signal (starts at $n=0$) |
| Anti-causal $x[n] = 0, n > 0$ | Inside a circle: $\vert z \vert < R_2$ | Time-reversed EMG record |
| Two-sided $x[n] \neq 0, \forall n$ | Annular: $R_1 < \vert z \vert < R_2$ | Windowed EEG epoch |

### 1.4 Unit Circle & Frequency Interpretation

When evaluated on the **unit circle** (i.e., $\vert z \vert = 1$, so $z = e^{j\omega}$), the z-transform reduces to the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)**:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, e^{-j\omega n} = X(z) \Big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$$

This is the bridge that connects z-domain analysis to the **frequency spectrum** of biomedical signals, making z-transform properties indispensable in **QRS detection, spectral analysis of heart-rate variability, and EEG band-power computation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The properties of the z-transform are derived directly from the definition $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$. Each property shows how a time-domain operation alters $X(z)$.

## 2.1 Property 1 — Linearity (Superposition)

The z-transform is a **linear operator**. If $x_1[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z)$ and $x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_2(z)$ with ROC $R_1$ and $R_2$ respectively, then:

$$a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; a_1 X_1(z) + a_2 X_2(z)$$

**ROC:** At least the intersection $R_1 \cap R_2$; it may be larger if pole-zero cancellation occurs.

**Biomedical Utility:** Combining multiple biosignal sources (e.g., maternal + fetal ECG) is achieved by simply adding their z-transforms.

## 2.2 Property 2 — Time Shifting (Delay / Advance)

A delay of $n_0$ samples in time corresponds to multiplication by $z^{-n_0}$ in the z-domain.

$$x[n - n_0] \;\longleftrightarrow\; z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

**ROC:** Identical to that of $X(z)$ (except possibly $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Time shifting is the foundation of **difference equations** for digital filters. A causal LTI system described by $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ becomes a rational polynomial $H(z) = \frac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$ in the z-domain.

**Biomedical Utility:** Modeling propagation delay in neural signal pathways; designing digital FIR filters for noise removal in ECG.

## 2.3 Property 3 — Time Reversal (Folding)

$$x[-n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(1/z)$$

**ROC:** Inverted — if original ROC is $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$, the new ROC is $\frac{1}{r_2} < \vert z \vert < \frac{1}{r_1}$.

**Biomedical Utility:** Backward playback analysis of phonocardiogram (heart sound) signals for murmur detection.

## 2.4 Property 4 — Time Scaling (Multiplication by $a^n$)

Multiplying a sequence by an exponential $a^n$ (where $a$ is a constant, possibly complex) scales the z-plane:

$$a^n \, x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X(z/a)$$

**ROC:** Scaled — $r_1 \vert a \vert < \vert z \vert < r_2 \vert a \vert$.

**Biomedical Utility:** Resampling (decimation/interpolation) of digitized biosignals.

## 2.5 Property 5 — Differentiation in the z-Domain

Differentiating $X(z)$ with respect to $z$ (or equivalently, multiplying by $-z \frac{d}{dz}$) corresponds to multiplying $x[n]$ by $n$ in the time domain:

$$n \, x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; -z \frac{d X(z)}{dz}$$

**ROC:** Same as that of $X(z)$.

**Biomedical Utility:** Computing moment-generating functions (mean, variance) of stochastic bio-signals; analyzing signal trends.

## 2.6 Property 6 — Convolution Theorem

The convolution of two sequences in the time domain becomes **multiplication** in the z-domain:

$$x_1[n] * x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$$

**ROC:** At least the intersection $R_1 \cap R_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** This is the **single most important property** in digital filter design. For an LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$ and input $x[n]$, the output is $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$, which in the z-domain becomes $Y(z) = X(z) H(z)$, where $H(z)$ is the **transfer function**.

**Biomedical Utility:** ECG filtering (low-pass Butterworth, high-pass baseline wander removal), EEG band-pass filtering.

## 2.7 Property 7 — Multiplication (Complex Convolution)

The dual of the convolution theorem — multiplication in time equals complex convolution in z:

$$x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X_1(v) \, X_2\!\left(\frac{z}{v}\right) v^{-1} dv$$

**ROC:** At least $R_1 \cdot R_2$ (multiplicative combination).

**Biomedical Utility:** Modulation theory, windowing, multiplying an ECG by a Hamming window before spectral analysis.

## 2.8 Property 8 — Conjugation

If $x[n]$ is real, $X(z) = X^*(z^*)$.

$$x^*[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; X^*(z^*)$$

**ROC:** Same as that of $X(z)$.

**Biomedical Utility:** Verifying the conjugate symmetry of spectra of real-valued bio-signals (a key property that guarantees real-valued outputs).

## 2.9 Property 9 — Initial Value Theorem (IVT)

If $x[n]$ is **causal** (i.e., $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$), the initial sample can be computed directly from $X(z)$:

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

> [!WARNING]
> The IVT applies **only for causal signals**, and **only if $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ exists finitely** (which is true for proper rational functions).

**Biomedical Utility:** Quick verification of filter response at $n = 0$ without inverse transform.

## 2.10 Property 10 — Final Value Theorem (FVT)

If the poles of $(1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ lie **strictly inside the unit circle** (i.e., the system is stable), then:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} (z - 1) \frac{X(z)}{z}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The FVT requires that the ROC of $X(z)$ includes the unit circle, except possibly $z = 1$. If there are poles on or outside the unit circle, the FVT is **not applicable**.

**Biomedical Utility:** Steady-state analysis of bio-signal filters; computing the DC gain of a low-pass filter applied to an EMG signal.

## 2.11 Property 11 — Parseval's Theorem (Energy Conservation)

For sequences in $\ell^2$ (finite-energy biosignals):

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_{C} X(z) X^*(1/z^*) \frac{dz}{z}$$

For real sequences, evaluated on the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 \, d\omega$$

**Biomedical Utility:** Computing the **total energy of an ECG complex**, **power spectral density** of EEG, and verifying filter energy conservation.

## 2.12 Property 12 — Real & Imaginary Part Decomposition

For a complex signal $x[n] = x_r[n] + j x_i[n]$:

$$x_r[n] = \frac{x[n] + x^*[n]}{2}, \quad x_i[n] = \frac{x[n] - x^*[n]}{2j}$$

$$x_r[n] \longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}\big[X(z) + X^*(z^*)\big]$$
$$j x_i[n] \longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}\big[X(z) - X^*(z^*)\big]$$

**Biomedical Utility:** Separating the real (in-phase) and imaginary (quadrature) components in complex demodulation of bio-signals.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Time-Domain Operation | z-Domain Representation | ROC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linearity** | $a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]$ | $a_1 X_1(z) + a_2 X_2(z)$ | $\supseteq R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| 2 | **Time Shift** | $x[n - n_0]$ | $z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | $= R$, except $\{0, \infty\}$ |
| 3 | **Time Reversal** | $x[-n]$ | $X(1/z)$ | Inverted: $1/r_2 < \vert z \vert < 1/r_1$ |
| 4 | **Scaling** | $a^n x[n]$ | $X(z/a)$ | $r_1 \vert a \vert < \vert z \vert < r_2 \vert a \vert$ |
| 5 | **Differentiation** | $n \, x[n]$ | $-z \frac{d X(z)}{dz}$ | $= R$ |
| 6 | **Convolution** | $x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ | $X_1(z) X_2(z)$ | $\supseteq R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| 7 | **Multiplication** | $x_1[n] x_2[n]$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint X_1(v) X_2(z/v) v^{-1} dv$ | $\supseteq R_1 \cdot R_2$ |
| 8 | **Conjugation** | $x^*[n]$ | $X^*(z^*)$ | $= R$ |
| 9 | **Initial Value** | $x[0]$ | $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Causal $x[n]$ required |
| 10 | **Final Value** | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$ | $\lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ | Stable, poles inside unit circle |
| 11 | **Parseval** | $\sum \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint X(z) X^*(1/z^*) z^{-1} dz$ | ROC includes unit circle |
| 12 | **Re/Im Split** | $x_r[n] = \text{Re}\{x[n]\}$ | $\frac{1}{2}[X(z) + X^*(z^*)]$ | $= R$ |

---

## 2.13 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where z-transform properties are used in real biomedical engineering:**
> 1. **Digital Filter Design (IIR/FIR):** Difference equation → rational $H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$ using time-shifting and linearity.
> 2. **ECG QRS Detection (Pan-Tompkins Algorithm):** Uses band-pass filtering (convolution) and derivative squaring (differentiation property + multiplication).
> 3. **EEG Spectral Analysis:** DTFT is the z-transform on the unit circle; Parseval's theorem computes total band power.
> 4. **Speech/Heart-Sound Processing:** Linear prediction uses inverse filtering, governed by time-shift and convolution properties.
> 5. **Stability Analysis:** FVT and ROC analysis tell engineers if a digital filter applied to bio-signals will be stable.
> 6. **Biomedical Imaging (CT/MRI reconstruction):** z-transform is the discrete analog of the Laplace transform, used in 2-D image processing.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Each derivation below is a *complete, exhaustive* walkthrough. No step is skipped. Master these derivations — they appear frequently in KTU University Exams (Module 2 carries ~20% weightage in the End Semester Exam).

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linearity Property

**Statement:** If $x_1[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z)$ and $x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_2(z)$, then $a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n] \leftrightarrow a_1 X_1(z) + a_2 X_2(z)$.

**Proof:**

By definition, the z-transform of the combined sequence is:

$$Z\{a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \big(a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]\big) z^{-n}$$

**Step 1:** Distribute the summation (valid for absolutely summable sequences):

$$= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_1 x_1[n] z^{-n} + \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_2 x_2[n] z^{-n}$$

**Step 2:** Pull the constants $a_1$ and $a_2$ outside the summations:

$$= a_1 \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[n] z^{-n} + a_2 \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_2[n] z^{-n}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize each summation as the respective z-transform:

$$= a_1 X_1(z) + a_2 X_2(z)$$

**Step 4 (ROC):** Each individual sum converges for $z \in R_1$ and $z \in R_2$ respectively. The combined sum converges wherever **both** sums converge, i.e., $R_1 \cap R_2$. The ROC may be larger if pole-zero cancellation occurs in $a_1 X_1(z) + a_2 X_2(z)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Time Shifting Property

**Statement:** $x[n - n_0] \leftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$.

**Proof:**

By definition:

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0] z^{-n}$$

**Step 1:** Introduce a change of variable. Let $m = n - n_0$, so $n = m + n_0$ and $dz$ terms transform as $dn = dm$:

$$= \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, z^{-(m + n_0)}$$

**Step 2:** Separate the exponent:

$$= \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] \, z^{-m} \cdot z^{-n_0}$$

**Step 3:** Pull the constant $z^{-n_0}$ outside the summation:

$$= z^{-n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] z^{-m} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

**Step 4 (ROC):** The summation $\sum x[m] z^{-m}$ converges on the same ROC as $X(z)$. Multiplication by $z^{-n_0}$ does not change the convergence region except that:
- If $n_0 > 0$, the point $z = 0$ is excluded (delayed sequences).
- If $n_0 < 0$, the point $z = \infty$ is excluded (advanced sequences). $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Time Reversal Property

**Statement:** $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(1/z) = X(z^{-1})$.

**Proof:**

By definition:

$$Z\{x[-n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[-n] z^{-n}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $m = -n$, so $n = -m$, and when $n: -\infty \to \infty$, then $m: \infty \to -\infty$:

$$= \sum_{m=\infty}^{-\infty} x[m] z^{m}$$

**Step 2:** Reverse the limits (flipping them introduces a sign):

$$= -\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] z^{m} = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] (z^{-1})^{-m}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize this as the standard z-transform evaluated at $z^{-1}$:

$$= X(z^{-1}) = X(1/z)$$

**Step 4 (ROC):** If $X(z)$ converges for $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$, then $X(1/z)$ converges for $r_1 < \vert 1/z \vert < r_2$, which simplifies to:

$$\frac{1}{r_2} < \vert z \vert < \frac{1}{r_1}$$

The ROC is **inverted (reflected)** with respect to the original. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Scaling Property ($a^n x[n]$)

**Statement:** $a^n x[n] \leftrightarrow X(z/a)$, where $a$ is a constant (real or complex).

**Proof:**

By definition:

$$Z\{a^n x[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n x[n] z^{-n}$$

**Step 1:** Combine the powers of $a$ and $z$:

$$= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] (a z^{-1})^n = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \left(\frac{z}{a}\right)^{-n}$$

**Step 2:** Recognize the summation as $X(z)$ evaluated at $z/a$:

$$= X\!\left(\frac{z}{a}\right) = X(z/a)$$

**Step 3 (ROC):** $X(z/a)$ converges for $r_1 < \vert z/a \vert < r_2$, which gives $r_1 \vert a \vert < \vert z \vert < r_2 \vert a \vert$. The ROC is **scaled** by $\vert a \vert$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Differentiation Property

**Statement:** $n \, x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of $X(z)$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \frac{d}{dz} z^{-n}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the power rule $\frac{d}{dz} z^{-n} = -n z^{-n-1}$:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] (-n) z^{-n-1}$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $-z^{-1}$:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = -z^{-1} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} (n x[n]) z^{-n} = -\frac{1}{z} Z\{n x[n]\}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply both sides by $-z$:

$$-z \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = Z\{n x[n]\}$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.6 Derivation of the Convolution Theorem

**Statement:** $x_1[n] * x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z) X_2(z)$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Recall the discrete-time convolution definition:

$$y[n] = x_1[n] * x_2[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k] \, x_2[n - k]$$

**Step 2:** Take the z-transform of $y[n]$:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \left( \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k] x_2[n - k] \right) z^{-n}$$

**Step 3:** Swap the order of summation (justified by absolute convergence):

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k] \left( \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_2[n - k] z^{-n} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the time-shifting property to the inner sum. Let $m = n - k$, so $n = m + k$ and $z^{-n} = z^{-(m+k)}$:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x_2[n - k] z^{-n} = z^{-k} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x_2[m] z^{-m} = z^{-k} X_2(z)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k] \, z^{-k} X_2(z) = X_2(z) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x_1[k] z^{-k}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the final summation as $X_1(z)$:

$$Y(z) = X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.7 Derivation of the Initial Value Theorem

**Statement:** For a causal signal $x[n]$ (i.e., $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$):

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Write the z-transform of the causal signal:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n} = x[0] + x[1] z^{-1} + x[2] z^{-2} + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Take the limit as $z \to \infty$ (equivalently $z^{-1} \to 0$):

$$\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = \lim_{z \to \infty} \left( x[0] + \frac{x[1]}{z} + \frac{x[2]}{z^2} + \cdots \right)$$

**Step 3:** Every term except the first vanishes as $z \to \infty$:

$$= x[0] + 0 + 0 + \cdots = x[0]$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.8 Derivation of the Final Value Theorem

**Statement:** If all poles of $(1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle, then:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Consider the time-shifted sequence $x[n] - x[n-1]$ (using time-shifting, its z-transform is $(1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$):

$$Z\{x[n] - x[n-1]\} = X(z) - z^{-1} X(z) = (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$$

**Step 2:** Take the limit as $z \to 1$ on both sides:

$$\lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z) = \lim_{z \to 1} Z\{x[n] - x[n-1]\} = \lim_{z \to 1} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} (x[n] - x[n-1]) z^{-n}$$

**Step 3:** Since all poles are strictly inside the unit circle, the function is analytic at $z = 1$ and the limit can be moved into the sum:

$$= \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} (x[n] - x[n-1]) \cdot 1^{-n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} (x[n] - x[n-1])$$

**Step 4:** This is a telescoping sum:

$$= \cdots + (x[-1] - x[-2]) + (x[0] - x[-1]) + (x[1] - x[0]) + (x[2] - x[1]) + \cdots$$

**Step 5:** All intermediate terms cancel pairwise, leaving:

$$= \lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] - \lim_{n \to -\infty} x[n] = \lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] - x[-\infty]$$

**Step 6:** For a stable causal signal, $x[-\infty] = 0$ (the signal was zero in the distant past), so:

$$= \lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$$

Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.9 Worked Example: KTU-Style Property Application

**Problem:** Let $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ (causal exponential). Apply properties to find $n a^n u[n]$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** From standard z-transform table: $Z\{a^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z - a}$, with ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

**Step 2:** Apply the differentiation property $n x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the derivative:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\left[\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}\right] = \frac{-a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{-a/z^2}{((z - a)/z)^2} = \frac{-a z}{(z - a)^2}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $-z$:

$$-z \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = -z \cdot \frac{-a z}{(z - a)^2} = \frac{a z^2}{(z - a)^2}$$

**Step 5:** Verify by long division (z-transform of $n a^n u[n]$): Final answer:

$$Z\{n a^n u[n]\} = \frac{a z^2}{(z - a)^2} = \frac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

ROC: $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$. $\blacksquare$

## 4.4 Schematic Table — Time-Domain vs z-Domain Operations

| Time-Domain Operation $x[n]$ | z-Domain Operation $X(z)$ | Property Name | ROC Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| $a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]$ | $a_1 X_1(z) + a_2 X_2(z)$ | Linearity | $R_1 \cap R_2$ (or larger) |
| $x[n - n_0]$ | $z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | Time Shifting | Same (excl. $0, \infty$) |
| $x[-n]$ | $X(1/z)$ | Time Reversal | Inverted |
| $a^n x[n]$ | $X(z/a)$ | Scaling | Scaled by $\vert a \vert$ |
| $n \, x[n]$ | $-z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | Differentiation | Same |
| $x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ | $X_1(z) X_2(z)$ | Convolution | $R_1 \cap R_2$ |
| $x_1[n] x_2[n]$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi j}\oint X_1(v) X_2(z/v) v^{-1} dv$ | Multiplication | $R_1 R_2$ |
| $x^*[n]$ | $X^*(z^*)$ | Conjugation | Same |
| $x[0]$ (causal) | $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Initial Value | Requires causality |
| $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n]$ (stable) | $\lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ | Final Value | Requires stability |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]**
**State and prove the time-shifting property of the z-transform.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x[n] \leftrightarrow X(z)$ with ROC $R$, then the time-shifted sequence $x[n - n_0]$ has the z-transform:

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

**Proof:** Starting from the z-transform definition and substituting $m = n - n_0$:

$$Z\{x[n - n_0]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0] z^{-n} = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] z^{-(m+n_0)} = z^{-n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m] z^{-m} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

The ROC remains the same as that of $X(z)$, except $z = 0$ is excluded if $n_0 > 0$ (causal delay) and $z = \infty$ is excluded if $n_0 < 0$ (advance). $\blacksquare$

**Significance (1 mark):** It is the foundation of difference equation representation of digital filters, where delayed samples $x[n-1], x[n-2]$ become polynomial terms in $z^{-1}$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**What is the convolution property of the z-transform? State its significance in LTI system analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** If $x_1[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z)$ and $x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_2(z)$, then:

$$Z\{x_1[n] * x_2[n]\} = X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$$

**Proof Sketch:** Apply the convolution definition $y[n] = \sum_k x_1[k] x_2[n-k]$, take the z-transform, interchange summations (justified by absolute convergence), and apply the time-shifting property to obtain $Y(z) = X_1(z) X_2(z)$. The ROC is the intersection $R_1 \cap R_2$.

**Significance in LTI Systems (1 mark):** For a causal LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$ and input $x[n]$, the output is $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$, and in the z-domain, $Y(z) = X(z) H(z)$, where $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} h[n] z^{-n}$ is the **transfer function**. This converts a difficult convolution sum into simple algebraic multiplication, forming the mathematical basis of all digital filtering in biomedical signal processing (e.g., ECG noise removal, EEG band-power extraction).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks] State and prove the differentiation property of the z-transform. Using this property, find the z-transform of $n a^n u[n]$, given that the z-transform of $a^n u[n]$ is $\frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$.**

**(b) [7 Marks] State and prove the initial and final value theorems of the z-transform. State the conditions under which each theorem is valid, and explain what happens if the conditions are violated.**

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Differentiation Property Statement:**
If $x[n] \leftrightarrow X(z)$ with ROC $R$, then:

$$n \, x[n] \;\longleftrightarrow\; -z \frac{d X(z)}{dz}$$

The ROC is unchanged.

**Proof (4 marks):**

Starting from $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$, differentiate both sides w.r.t. $z$:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \frac{d}{dz} z^{-n} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] (-n) z^{-n-1}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = -z^{-1} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} n x[n] z^{-n} = -\frac{1}{z} Z\{n x[n]\}$$

Multiplying both sides by $-z$:

$$-z \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = Z\{n x[n]\}$$

$\blacksquare$

**Application — Z-transform of $n a^n u[n]$ (3 marks):**

Given $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$, compute the derivative:

$$\frac{dX}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}(1 - a z^{-1})^{-1} = -(1 - a z^{-1})^{-2} \cdot (a z^{-2}) = \frac{-a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

Multiply by $-z$:

$$-z \frac{dX}{dz} = -z \cdot \frac{-a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{Z\{n a^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}}$, with ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Initial Value Theorem (IVT) — Statement (1 mark):**
For a causal sequence ($x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$):

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

**Proof (1.5 marks):** Writing the causal z-transform as $X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n} = x[0] + x[1] z^{-1} + x[2] z^{-2} + \cdots$, as $z \to \infty$, all terms with $z^{-k}$ for $k \geq 1$ vanish, leaving $x[0]$.

**Condition:** The signal must be **causal**, and the limit $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ must exist finitely (true for proper rational $X(z)$).

**Final Value Theorem (FVT) — Statement (1 mark):**
If the poles of $(1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ all lie strictly inside the unit circle:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$$

**Proof (2 marks):** Consider $Z\{x[n] - x[n-1]\} = (1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$. Since poles are inside the unit circle, the function is analytic at $z = 1$, and the limit can be moved inside the sum. Evaluating at $z = 1$ gives a telescoping series $\sum_n (x[n] - x[n-1])$, which collapses to $\lim_{n\to\infty} x[n] - x[-\infty] = \lim_{n\to\infty} x[n]$ for causal signals.

**Conditions (1 mark):**
- ROC of $X(z)$ must include the unit circle.
- Poles of $(1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$ must lie **strictly inside** the unit circle (no pole at $z = 1$ or beyond).
- If violated, FVT is **not applicable** — the limit may diverge, oscillate, or be undefined.

**Violation Example:** For $x[n] = u[n]$, $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$, with a pole at $z = 1$ (on the unit circle). The FVT gives $\lim_{z \to 1}(1 - z^{-1}) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = 1$, but the actual final value is $\lim_{n \to \infty} u[n] = 1$ — works here only by accident because the pole is exactly at $z=1$, not beyond. For $x[n] = 2^n u[n]$ (unstable), FVT fails because poles are outside the unit circle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a) [7 Marks] State and prove the time-shifting and time-reversal properties of the z-transform. Discuss the effect on the Region of Convergence in each case.**

**(b) [7 Marks] State the convolution theorem of the z-transform and prove it rigorously. Using this theorem, derive the transfer function of a causal LTI system described by the difference equation $y[n] - \frac{3}{4} y[n-1] + \frac{1}{8} y[n-2] = x[n]$. Find the impulse response of the system.**

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Time-Shifting Property (2 marks):**
If $x[n] \leftrightarrow X(z)$ with ROC $R$, then $x[n - n_0] \leftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$, ROC $= R$ (except possibly $z = 0$ or $z = \infty$).

**Proof (1.5 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.2 above.

**Time-Reversal Property (1.5 marks):**
$x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(1/z)$ with ROC inverted: $1/r_2 < \vert z \vert < 1/r_1$.

**Proof (1.5 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.3 above.

**Effect on ROC (0.5 marks):**
- Time shifting does not change the ROC magnitude region; only the endpoints (0 or $\infty$) may be added/removed.
- Time reversal **reflects** the ROC: an external ROC becomes internal and vice versa.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Convolution Theorem Statement (1 mark):** $x_1[n] * x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z) X_2(z)$.

**Proof (2 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.6 above.

**Application — Transfer Function of LTI System (2 marks):**

The difference equation is $y[n] - \frac{3}{4} y[n-1] + \frac{1}{8} y[n-2] = x[n]$.

Take the z-transform of both sides, using the time-shifting property $y[n-k] \leftrightarrow z^{-k} Y(z)$:

$$Y(z) - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} Y(z) + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z)$$

$$Y(z)\left[1 - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2}\right] = X(z)$$

**Transfer function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2}} = \frac{z^2}{z^2 - \frac{3}{4} z + \frac{1}{8}}$$

**Impulse Response (2 marks):**

Factor the denominator: $z^2 - \frac{3}{4} z + \frac{1}{8} = (z - 0.5)(z - 0.25)$.

Using partial fractions, the impulse response is:

$$h[n] = \left[ 4 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n - 3 \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^n \right] u[n]$$

**Verification (0 marks — skip if time is short):** Both poles ($0.5$ and $0.25$) are inside the unit circle, so the system is **stable** and **causal**. ROC: $\vert z \vert > 0.5$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in z-Transform Property Questions:**
>
> 1. **ROC Mismatch (Loss: 1-2 marks):** Students often forget to state the ROC after applying a property. The ROC is **mandatory** in KTU valuation. Even if the transform is correct, missing ROC costs marks.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Time-Shift and Time-Reversal (Loss: 2-3 marks):** Time-shift is $x[n-n_0] \leftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$. Time-reversal is $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(1/z)$. These are NOT the same.
>
> 3. **FVT used on unstable systems (Loss: 1-2 marks):** Applying the Final Value Theorem to a system with poles on or outside the unit circle is a frequent mistake. Always check stability first.
>
> 4. **Forgetting causality for IVT (Loss: 1 mark):** The Initial Value Theorem applies only to **causal** signals. For non-causal signals, the limit as $z \to \infty$ gives $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ evaluated at infinity, which is more complex.
>
> 5. **Skipping steps in convolution proof (Loss: 2-3 marks):** In the convolution proof, you MUST show the interchange of summation order, the change of variable $m = n - k$, and the application of the time-shifting property. KTU examiners reward complete derivations.
>
> 6. **Wrong sign in the differentiation property (Loss: 1-2 marks):** The property is $n x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$. The negative sign is often dropped, leading to wrong answers.
>
> 7. **Confusing convolution (time) with multiplication (z):** The convolution theorem says $*$ in time $\Rightarrow$ $\times$ in z. Some students write the opposite.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for z-Transform Properties:**

- 🔹 **Linearity** — Addition in time → Addition in z; ROC = $R_1 \cap R_2$ (or larger after pole-zero cancellation).
- 🔹 **Time Shifting** — $x[n-n_0] \leftrightarrow z^{-n_0} X(z)$; **delays** add poles/zeros at origin, ROC unchanged.
- 🔹 **Time Reversal** — $x[-n] \leftrightarrow X(1/z)$; ROC is **reflected (inverted)**.
- 🔹 **Scaling** — $a^n x[n] \leftrightarrow X(z/a)$; ROC is **scaled** by $\vert a \vert$.
- 🔹 **Differentiation** — $n x[n] \leftrightarrow -z \frac{dX(z)}{dz}$; ROC unchanged. **Watch the minus sign.**
- 🔹 **Convolution** — $x_1[n] * x_2[n] \leftrightarrow X_1(z) X_2(z)$; **most important property** — foundation of LTI system theory.
- 🔹 **Multiplication (Complex Convolution)** — $x_1[n] x_2[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint X_1(v) X_2(z/v) v^{-1} dv$.
- 🔹 **Conjugation** — $x^*[n] \leftrightarrow X^*(z^*)$; for real signals, spectrum is conjugate-symmetric.
- 🔹 **Initial Value Theorem** — $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$; **causal** signals only.
- 🔹 **Final Value Theorem** — $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1}(1 - z^{-1}) X(z)$; **stable** systems only (poles strictly inside unit circle).
- 🔹 **Parseval's Theorem** — Energy in time = Energy in z (modulo $1/2\pi$ on unit circle); essential for **PSD computation** in EEG/ECG.
- 🔹 **Real-Imaginary Split** — $\text{Re}\{x[n]\} \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}[X(z) + X^*(z^*)]$; used in quadrature demodulation.
- 🔹 **ROC is ALWAYS a ring (annulus)** $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$ in the bilateral case; bounded by poles.
- 🔹 **Causal ROC** is the **exterior** of the outermost pole.
- 🔹 **Stable ROC** must **include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- 🔹 **z-transform on the unit circle** ($z = e^{j\omega}$) is the **DTFT** — the bridge to frequency analysis.
- 🔹 **Biomedical application anchor points**: ECG filtering (convolution), QRS detection (differentiation + squaring), EEG PSD (Parseval), stability of digital filters (FVT/ROC).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Poles and Zeros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Poles and Zeros

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the domain of **Biomedical Signal Processing (BSP)**, every Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time system used to filter physiological signals (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and PCG) can be completely characterized by a mathematical entity known as the **Transfer Function**, typically expressed in the **Z-domain** as $H(z)$.

The Transfer Function $H(z)$ is, in general, a **rational function** (a ratio of two polynomials in $z$):

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}}{a_0 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}}$$

When the numerator and denominator are factored into their root forms, we get the **Pole-Zero Form**:

$$H(z) = \frac{b_0}{a_0} \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(z - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(z - p_k)}$$

Where:
- **Zeros ($z_k$)**: The values of $z$ that make the numerator polynomial equal to zero. They cause the transfer function magnitude $\vert H(z) \vert$ to become **zero**.
- **Poles ($p_k$)**: The values of $z$ that make the denominator polynomial equal to zero. They cause $\vert H(z) \vert$ to become **infinite** (or undefined).
- **$M$**: Order of the numerator (number of zeros).
- **$N$**: Order of the denominator (number of poles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pole-zero analysis forms the foundation for filter design (IIR/FIR), stability analysis, and frequency response evaluation of biomedical filters. The location of poles and zeros in the Z-plane directly dictates how a filter will modify a biomedical signal.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **physical audio equalizer** in a hospital ICU monitoring room. The incoming ECG signal is "dirty" — it contains 50 Hz powerline interference, baseline wander, and high-frequency muscle noise. You need an "audio engineer" to clean it up.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mixing Console Analogy:**
>
> - **Zeros = "Mute Buttons"**: Think of each zero as a frequency that the engineer completely **mutes (silences)**. If you place a zero on the unit circle at angle corresponding to 50 Hz, the system will output **zero amplitude** at 50 Hz, effectively erasing the powerline hum from your ECG.
>
> - **Poles = "Volume Boosters"**: Each pole acts like a **volume slider pushed to maximum**. If you place a pole near a certain frequency on the Z-plane, the system **amplifies** the energy around that frequency. By carefully placing poles close to the unit circle (but inside it) at, say, 0.5 Hz, the filter can amplify low-frequency heart rate components.
>
> - **Pole-Zero Plot = The Engineer's Mixing Board**: The complex Z-plane is the engineer's control panel. By strategically placing **mute buttons (zeros)** at noise frequencies and **volume boosters (poles)** near desired signal frequencies, the engineer sculpts the clean biomedical signal.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Frequency ($F_s$)** for typical ECG: **360 Hz** (MIT-BIH standard) or **500 Hz**.
- **Nyquist Frequency**: $F_N = F_s / 2$.
- For Z-plane mapping: $z = e^{j\Omega} = e^{j2\pi f / F_s}$, where $\Omega$ is the **normalized digital frequency** in radians.
- **Unit Circle**: The circle of radius **1** centered at origin in Z-plane; represents the boundary of stability for causal systems.
- **Conjugate Symmetry**: For real-coefficient filters, poles and zeros occur in **complex conjugate pairs** $(a + jb, a - jb)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown: Why Poles and Zeros Matter

The pole-zero description is not merely a mathematical curiosity — it is the **most compact and insightful** way to understand a discrete-time system. Here is the structured logic:

### Step 1: Factoring the Transfer Function
- Any linear constant-coefficient difference equation with input $x[n]$ and output $y[n]$:
$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$$
- After taking the Z-transform (assuming zero initial conditions), we obtain a **rational function** in $z$ whose numerator and denominator are polynomials.

### Step 2: Identifying Zeros
- The roots of the numerator polynomial $B(z) = b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}$ are called **zeros**.
- At a zero, $H(z_k) = 0$ — the system **completely rejects** the corresponding frequency component.

### Step 3: Identifying Poles
- The roots of the denominator polynomial $A(z) = a_0 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}$ are called **poles**.
- At a pole, $H(p_k) = \infty$ — the system **resonates** at the corresponding frequency.

### Step 4: Stability Determination (KTU Critical Concept)
- A causal LTI system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$).
- If any pole lies on the unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert = 1$), the system is **marginally stable** (oscillatory).
- If any pole lies outside the unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert > 1$), the system is **unstable** (output grows unbounded).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zeros do NOT affect stability.** Zeros can be placed anywhere in the Z-plane (inside, on, or outside the unit circle) without affecting the BIBO stability of the system.

### Step 5: Frequency Response from Pole-Zero Plot
- The frequency response of the system is obtained by evaluating $H(z)$ on the unit circle $z = e^{j\Omega}$:
$$H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{b_0}{a_0} \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(e^{j\Omega} - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(e^{j\Omega} - p_k)}$$
- The magnitude response is the **product of distances from frequency point to zeros divided by product of distances to poles**.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Biomedical Application | Pole-Zero Role |
|------------------------|----------------|
| **ECG Notch Filter (50/60 Hz removal)** | Zeros placed exactly on unit circle at $\Omega = \pm 2\pi(50/F_s)$ to nullify powerline interference. |
| **ECG Low-Pass Filter (anti-aliasing)** | Poles placed inside unit circle, zeros at $\Omega = \pi$ to suppress high frequencies. |
| **ECG High-Pass Filter (baseline wander removal)** | Pole near $z = 1$ (DC) to block low-frequency drift; zero at $z = 1$ to enforce zero DC gain. |
| **Pacemaker Pulse Detection** | Poles placed near specific frequencies to resonate with pacemaker spike spectra. |
| **EEG Band-Pass Filter** | Combination of poles for gain and zeros for rejection outside 0.5–30 Hz band. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Transfer Function (Pole-Zero Form) | $H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(z - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(z - p_k)}$ | $K = b_0/a_0$ is the gain constant |
| 2 | Z-domain Frequency Mapping | $z = e^{j\Omega} = e^{j2\pi f/F_s}$ | $\Omega \in [0, 2\pi]$ or $[-\pi, \pi]$ |
| 3 | BIBO Stability Condition | $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for **all** poles | Causal system; strict inequality |
| 4 | Marginal Stability | $\vert p_k \vert \leq 1$ with simple poles on unit circle | Sustained oscillation |
| 5 | Unstable System | Any $\vert p_k \vert > 1$ | Output diverges |
| 6 | Frequency Response | $H(e^{j\Omega}) = K \frac{\prod \vert e^{j\Omega} - z_k \vert}{\prod \vert e^{j\Omega} - p_k \vert}$ | Magnitude and phase |
| 7 | Magnitude in dB | $\vert H(e^{j\Omega}) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert H(e^{j\Omega}) \vert$ | Standard dB scale |
| 8 | Geometric Distance | Distance from point $e^{j\Omega}$ to pole $p_k$: $\sqrt{(\cos\Omega - \text{Re}(p_k))^2 + (\sin\Omega - \text{Im}(p_k))^2}$ | Used in vector method |
| 9 | Maximum Phase System | All zeros **outside** unit circle | Non-minimum phase |
| 10 | Minimum Phase System | All poles and zeros **inside** unit circle | Minimum group delay |
| 11 | All-Pass System | $\vert H(e^{j\Omega}) \vert = 1$ for all $\Omega$ | Poles and zeros are mirror images |
| 12 | Linear Phase (FIR only) | Symmetric impulse response | No poles except at origin |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Difference Equation to Pole-Zero Form

**Problem**: Given the difference equation representing a 2nd-order biomedical filter (used for ECG smoothing):

$$y[n] - 1.5\,y[n-1] + 0.7\,y[n-2] = x[n] + 0.5\,x[n-1]$$

Find the transfer function $H(z)$ in pole-zero form and determine the stability.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Apply the Z-Transform to both sides.**

Using the time-shift property: $y[n-k] \xleftrightarrow{Z} z^{-k} Y(z)$.

$$Y(z) - 1.5 z^{-1} Y(z) + 0.7 z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z) + 0.5 z^{-1} X(z)$$

**Step 2: Group the $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$ terms.**

$$Y(z)\left[1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2}\right] = X(z)\left[1 + 0.5 z^{-1}\right]$$

**Step 3: Solve for the Transfer Function $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$.**

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 1.5 z^{-1} + 0.7 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4: Convert to positive powers of $z$ by multiplying numerator and denominator by $z^2$.**

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 + 0.5 z}{z^2 - 1.5 z + 0.7} = \frac{z(z + 0.5)}{z^2 - 1.5 z + 0.7}$$

**Step 5: Find the Zeros (roots of numerator).**

$$z(z + 0.5) = 0 \implies z_1 = 0, \quad z_2 = -0.5$$

**Step 6: Find the Poles (roots of denominator) using the quadratic formula.**

$$z^2 - 1.5 z + 0.7 = 0$$

$$z = \frac{1.5 \pm \sqrt{(-1.5)^2 - 4(1)(0.7)}}{2(1)} = \frac{1.5 \pm \sqrt{2.25 - 2.8}}{2}$$

$$z = \frac{1.5 \pm \sqrt{-0.55}}{2} = \frac{1.5 \pm j\,0.7416}{2}$$

$$p_1 = 0.75 + j\,0.3708, \quad p_2 = 0.75 - j\,0.3708$$

**Step 7: Check the magnitude of each pole for stability.**

$$\vert p_1 \vert = \sqrt{(0.75)^2 + (0.3708)^2} = \sqrt{0.5625 + 0.1375} = \sqrt{0.7000} \approx 0.8367$$

$$\vert p_2 \vert = \vert p_1 \vert \approx 0.8367$$

Since both $\vert p_1 \vert < 1$ and $\vert p_2 \vert < 1$, **the system is BIBO STABLE**.

**Step 8: Final Pole-Zero Form.**

$$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{(z - 0)(z + 0.5)}{(z - 0.75 - j\,0.3708)(z - 0.75 + j\,0.3708)} = \frac{z(z + 0.5)}{(z - p_1)(z - p_2)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency Response from Geometric Vector Method

**Problem**: For the filter above, compute the magnitude response $\vert H(e^{j\Omega}) \vert$ at $\Omega = \pi/4$.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Substitute $z = e^{j\pi/4}$ into $H(z)$.**

$$e^{j\pi/4} = \cos(\pi/4) + j\sin(\pi/4) = 0.7071 + j\,0.7071$$

**Step 2: Compute the numerator (vector from $z$ to each zero).**

Distance to $z_1 = 0$:
$$\vert e^{j\pi/4} - 0 \vert = \vert 0.7071 + j\,0.7071 \vert = \sqrt{0.5 + 0.5} = 1.0$$

Distance to $z_2 = -0.5$:
$$\vert e^{j\pi/4} - (-0.5) \vert = \vert 1.2071 + j\,0.7071 \vert = \sqrt{1.4571 + 0.5} = \sqrt{1.9571} \approx 1.3990$$

**Step 3: Compute the denominator (vector from $z$ to each pole).**

Distance to $p_1 = 0.75 + j\,0.3708$:
$$\vert e^{j\pi/4} - p_1 \vert = \vert (0.7071 - 0.75) + j(0.7071 - 0.3708) \vert$$
$$= \vert -0.0429 + j\,0.3363 \vert = \sqrt{0.00184 + 0.11310} = \sqrt{0.11494} \approx 0.3390$$

By conjugate symmetry, $\vert e^{j\pi/4} - p_2 \vert = \vert e^{j\pi/4} - p_1^\ast \vert \approx 0.3390$ (since $p_1, p_2$ are conjugates and $z$ is on the unit circle).

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude response.**

$$\vert H(e^{j\pi/4}) \vert = \frac{\text{Product of zero distances}}{\text{Product of pole distances}} = \frac{1.0 \times 1.3990}{0.3390 \times 0.3390}$$

$$\vert H(e^{j\pi/4}) \vert = \frac{1.3990}{0.11492} \approx 12.17$$

**Step 5: Convert to dB.**

$$\vert H(e^{j\pi/4}) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(12.17) \approx 21.71 \text{ dB}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Pole-Zero Configurations

| Pole-Zero Configuration | Time-Domain Behavior | Frequency-Domain Behavior | Application in BSP |
|------------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| **Single pole at origin** ($p = 0$) | Pure delay of 1 sample | All-pass, $\vert H \vert = 1$ | Signal synchronization in ECG pipelines |
| **Pole on positive real axis (inside UC)** | Exponentially decaying response | Low-pass behavior (more at low $f$) | Smoothing of high-freq EMG noise |
| **Pole on negative real axis (inside UC)** | Alternating sign decay | High-pass behavior (more at high $f$) | DC removal / baseline wander |
| **Complex conjugate poles near UC** | Decaying sinusoidal oscillation | Resonant peak at $f_0 = \angle p \cdot F_s / 2\pi$ | Tuned notch / band-pass filters |
| **Zero on unit circle at $z = 1$** | — | Perfect null at DC ($f = 0$) | Removing ECG baseline drift |
| **Zero on unit circle at $z = -1$** | — | Perfect null at Nyquist ($f = F_s/2$) | Anti-aliasing low-pass filter |
| **Complex conjugate zeros on UC** | — | Perfect null at $f_0$ | Notch filter for 50/60 Hz line noise |
| **Multiple poles near one location** | Sharper resonance, longer ringing | Narrower, taller peak | High-Q bandpass for pacemaker detection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pole-Zero Analysis Pipeline for Biomedical Signals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Biomedical Signal] --> B[ADC Sampling at Fs]
    B --> C[Z-Transform H(z) Representation]
    C --> D[Pole-Zero Form Factorization]
    D --> E1{Z-plane Pole-Zero Plot}
    D --> E2[Region of Convergence Analysis]
    D --> E3[Stability Check: |pk| 1]
    E1 --> F1[Frequency Response H e^jO]
    E2 --> F2[Impulse Response hn]
    E3 --> F3[BIBO Stability Decision]
    F1 --> G1[Filter Type Classification]
    F2 --> G2[System Causality Check]
    F3 --> G3[Design Validation]
    G1 --> H[Clean Processed Signal Output]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style H fill:#d4f5d4
    style E3 fill:#fff4c2
    style F3 fill:#fff4c2
    style D fill:#ffd6d6
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Tree for System Stability from Pole Locations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pole Location Evaluation] --> Q{Compute |pk|}
    Q --> R1[|pk| 1: All poles inside unit circle]
    Q --> R2[|pk| = 1: Some poles on unit circle]
    Q --> R3[|pk| 1: Some poles outside unit circle]
    R1 --> S1[STABLE System]
    R1 --> S1a[Impulse response decays to zero]
    R1 --> S1b[BIBO stable for bounded input]
    R2 --> S2[MARGINALLY STABLE]
    R2 --> S2a[Sustained oscillation if simple pole]
    R2 --> S2b[Unstable if repeated pole on UC]
    R3 --> S3[UNSTABLE System]
    R3 --> S3a[Impulse response grows without bound]
    R3 --> S3b[NEVER use in medical devices]
    S1 --> OUT1[Filter is safe for clinical use]
    S2 --> OUT2[Use with caution or redesign]
    S3 --> OUT3[Redesign filter immediately]
    style S1 fill:#90ee90
    style S2 fill:#ffffe0
    style S3 fill:#ff6b6b
    style OUT1 fill:#90ee90
    style OUT2 fill:#ffffe0
    style OUT3 fill:#ff6b6b
```
