| 6 | What are the passband and stopband edges? | Map analog $f$ to digital $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$ |
| 7 | What is the passband ripple $\delta_p$? | Convert to dB: $A_p$ |
| 8 | What is the stopband attenuation $\delta_s$? | Convert to dB: $A_s$ |
| 9 | Estimate filter order $N$ | Use Kaiser (FIR) or Butterworth (IIR) formula |
| 10 | Check stability | For IIR: $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Block Diagram of a Digital Filtering System (Biomedical Front-End)

This diagram shows the *complete* chain from a physiological signal to a clean digital output, highlighting *where* the digital filter lives in the pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[PATIENT BODY] --> B[BIOELECTRIC SIGNAL ECG EEG EMG]
    B --> C[ANALOG FRONT END AMPLIFIER]
    C --> D[ANTI ALIASING LPF ANALOG]
    D --> E[ADC SAMPLER AND QUANTIZER]
    E --> F[DIGITAL FILTER LCCDE LCCDE LCCDE]
    F --> G[POST PROCESSING FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    G --> H[DISPLAY OR DIAGNOSIS]
    H --> I[CLINICIAN OR ALGORITHM]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#f5e1ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#ffe1f5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:4px
    style G fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading Guide:** The pink block (F) is the digital filter — the *locus* of our design effort. Every block before F converts the signal to a form that F can accept. Every block after F uses F's output. A mistake in F's preliminary considerations propagates to misdiagnosis.

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — The Digital Filter as an LTI System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[X OF N INPUT] --> FF[FEED FORWARD SUMS B K Z MINUS K]
    FB[FEEDBACK SUMS A K Z MINUS K] --> SUM[SUMMER NODE]
    FF --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Y OF N OUTPUT]
    FB2[PAST OUTPUTS Y N MINUS 1 TO Y N MINUS N] --> FB
    IN2[PAST INPUTS X N MINUS 1 TO X N MINUS M] --> FF
    style IN fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333
    style OUT fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333
    style FF fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#333
    style FB fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333
    style SUM fill:#f5e1ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

> **Reading Guide:** The forward path (green) processes current and past inputs through $b_k$ coefficients. The feedback path (red) processes past outputs through $a_k$ coefficients. The summer (purple) combines both. **FIR filters** are obtained by setting the red path to zero (no feedback).

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Preliminary Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[START PRELIMINARY DESIGN] --> Q1{IS LINEAR PHASE REQUIRED}
    Q1 -->|YES ECG EEG| FIR[CHOOSE FIR]
    Q1 -->|NO PCG EMG| Q2{IS COMPUTE BUDGET TIGHT}
    Q2 -->|YES| IIR[CHOOSE IIR BUTTERWORTH CHEBYSHEV]
    Q2 -->|NO| FIR2[CHOOSE FIR PARKS MCCLELLAN]
    FIR --> Q3{WHAT NOISE TO REMOVE}
    FIR2 --> Q3
    IIR --> Q3
    Q3 --> Q3A[50 HZ POWERLINE NOTCH]
    Q3 --> Q3B[BASELINE DRIFT HIGHPASS]
    Q3 --> Q3C[HIGH FREQ NOISE LOWPASS]
    Q3 --> Q3D[BAND OF INTEREST BPF]
    Q3A --> SPEC[DRAFT SPECS F P F S A P A S]
    Q3B --> SPEC
    Q3C --> SPEC
    Q3D --> SPEC
    SPEC --> ORDER[ESTIMATE ORDER N]
    ORDER --> DESIGN[PROCEED TO ACTUAL DESIGN MODULE 4]
    style START fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style FIR fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style IIR fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DESIGN fill:#f5e1ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Z-Plane Pole-Zero Stability Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ORIGIN[ORIGIN Z EQUALS ZERO] --> UC[UNIT CIRCLE MOD EQUALS ONE]
    UC --> INSIDE[INTERIOR MOD LESS THAN ONE STABLE]
    UC --> OUTSIDE[EXTERIOR MOD GREATER THAN ONE UNSTABLE]
    UC --> ON[ON THE CIRCLE MARGINALLY STABLE]
    style ORIGIN fill:#fff,stroke:#333
    style UC fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#d00,stroke-width:3px
    style INSIDE fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#0a0,stroke-width:2px
    style OUTSIDE fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#d00,stroke-width:2px
    style ON fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading Guide:** This is the *stability map* of the z-plane. A digital filter is stable only when all its poles (red ✗ in real designs) are inside the green region. This visualization is a **must-draw** in KTU viva and 14-mark answer scripts whenever you discuss IIR stability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define a digital filter. What are the two broad categories of digital filters?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **digital filter** is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system that operates on a sampled input signal $x[n]$ to produce an output $y[n]$, selectively passing or attenuating specific frequency components according to a pre-defined difference equation. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> The two broad categories are:
> 1. **FIR (Finite Impulse Response)** filters — have only feed-forward terms; impulse response is of finite duration; always stable; can have linear phase. *(0.75 marks)*
> 2. **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response)** filters — have both feed-forward and feedback terms; impulse response theoretically infinite; conditionally stable; generally nonlinear phase but computationally efficient. *(0.75 marks)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **State the Nyquist sampling theorem. Why is it a *preliminary* consideration in digital filter design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. *(1.5 marks)*
>
> It is a preliminary consideration because **before any filter is designed, the engineer must lock the sampling rate**. The digital filter's coefficients are functions of $f_s$ — the cutoff frequencies, transition widths, and order estimates all depend on it. If $f_s$ is wrong (e.g., chosen below $2 f_{max}$), the filter will operate on an *aliased* signal and produce meaningless output regardless of how sophisticated the design is. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The student attempts **one full question** (either Q. A or Q. B). The internal choice is between the **two 14-mark questions**, not between sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of a **general digital filter** and derive its governing **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)**. Highlight the role of feedback and feed-forward paths. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A biomedical signal is sampled at **$f_s = 1000$ Hz**. The desired passband edge is **$f_p = 100$ Hz** and the stopband edge is **$f_s = 200$ Hz**. Convert these to **normalized digital frequencies** (in rad/sample and as a fraction of Nyquist). If a 5-tap moving-average filter is applied, write its **difference equation** and **transfer function**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A

**(a) — 7 marks:**

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
The general digital filter is an LTI discrete-time system with:
- An **input node** $x[n]$ (current sample).
- A **tapped delay line** storing past inputs $x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots, x[n-M]$.
- A **feedback delay line** storing past outputs $y[n-1], y[n-2], \ldots, y[n-N]$ (for IIR only).
- A set of **feed-forward multipliers** $b_0, b_1, \ldots, b_M$.
- A set of **feedback multipliers** $-a_1, -a_2, \ldots, -a_N$.
- A **summer** that combines all weighted signals to produce $y[n]$.

**Derivation of LCCDE (4 marks):**
The output of the summer is the sum of:
- Weighted current and past inputs: $\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$
- Weighted past outputs (with sign change for feedback): $-\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]$

Equating this to the current output:

$$
y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]
$$

> **Special cases:** For FIR, all $a_k = 0$, so $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$. For IIR, the feedback terms are non-zero. The order of the filter is $\max(M, N)$. The block diagram should clearly show the two delay lines, the two multiplier banks, and the central summer.

---

**(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Convert analog to digital frequencies (3 marks):**
Using $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$:

For passband edge $f_p = 100$ Hz:

$$
\omega_p = 2\pi \cdot \frac{100}{1000} = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample} = \frac{\pi}{5}
$$

For stopband edge $f_s = 200$ Hz:

$$
\omega_s = 2\pi \cdot \frac{200}{1000} = 0.4\pi \text{ rad/sample} = \frac{2\pi}{5}
$$

Normalized to Nyquist:

$$
\hat{f}_p = \frac{2 f_p}{f_s} = \frac{200}{1000} = 0.2, \qquad \hat{f}_s = \frac{2 f_s}{f_s} = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.4
$$

**Step 2 — Write the moving-average filter (2 marks):**
A 5-tap moving average has coefficients $b_k = 1/5$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ (FIR, all $a_k = 0$).

Difference equation:

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{5}\big(x[n] + x[n-1] + x[n-2] + x[n-3] + x[n-4]\big)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the transfer function (2 marks):**

$$
H(z) = \frac{1}{5}\left(1 + z^{-1} + z^{-2} + z^{-3} + z^{-4}\right) = \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-5}}{1 - z^{-1}}
$$

Magnitude response at $\omega = 0$ (DC): $H(e^{j0}) = \frac{1}{5} \cdot 5 = 1$ (unity gain). At $\omega = 0.4\pi$: $H(e^{j0.4\pi}) = \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{\sin(5 \cdot 0.2\pi)}{\sin(0.2\pi)} = \frac{1}{5} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi)}{\sin(0.2\pi)} = 0$ (perfect null at 200 Hz). This confirms it is a low-pass filter with the first null at $f = 200$ Hz, matching the stopband edge.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** What are the **preliminary considerations** in digital filter design? Explain any **four** in detail with biomedical signal examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **FIR and IIR digital filters** under at least **six parameters**. For an ECG QRS detection front-end, justify **which type you would choose** and why. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B

**(a) — 7 marks:**

The preliminary considerations are the **decisions made before any coefficient calculation**. The four most important are:

**1. Signal and Noise Characterization (2 marks):**
The designer must first quantify the spectral content of the desired signal and the contaminating noise. For example, in ECG, the QRS complex has dominant energy between 0.5 and 40 Hz, while powerline interference is sharply localized at 50 Hz. This characterization directly drives the choice of filter type — a narrow *band-stop (notch)* filter at 50 Hz combined with a *low-pass* filter at 40 Hz. Without this step, the filter would be designed blind.

**2. Sampling Rate Selection (2 marks):**
The sampling rate $f_s$ must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ per the Nyquist theorem. For an EEG signal with $f_{max} = 100$ Hz, $f_s \geq 200$ Hz. In practice, $f_s = 256$ or 512 Hz is used to provide a guard band. The sampling rate is a *preliminary* decision because all subsequent filter parameters (cutoff, transition width, order) are functions of $f_s$.

**3. Filter Specification Drafting (1.5 marks):**
The engineer must draft a *specification sheet* defining the passband edge $\omega_p$, stopband edge $\omega_s$, passband ripple $\delta_p$ (in dB: $A_p$), and stopband attenuation $\delta_s$ (in dB: $A_s$). For biomedical applications, common values are $A_p = 0.5$ dB and $A_s = 40$ dB. This sheet is the "contract" that the final design must satisfy.

**4. FIR vs. IIR Structural Choice (1.5 marks):**
Based on whether linear phase is required and the available computation budget, the engineer chooses between FIR and IIR. Linear-phase FIR is preferred for ECG, EEG, and any signal where morphology matters. IIR is preferred for PCG and EMG where sharp cutoffs and low computation are paramount.

---

**(b) — 7 marks:**

**FIR vs. IIR Comparison (4 marks):**

| Parameter | FIR | IIR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Impulse response | Finite (zero for $n \geq N$) | Infinite (extends theoretically to $\infty$) |
| Feedback | None (always $a_k = 0$) | Present (recursive in $y[n-k]$) |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditional; poles must satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ |
| Phase response | Can be made **linear** (symmetric coefficients) | Generally **nonlinear** |
| Order required for given spec | Higher | Lower (sharper cutoff with fewer coefficients) |
| Computational cost | Higher (more MACs per sample) | Lower |
| Coefficient sensitivity | Low | High (especially in narrow bandwidth) |
| Memory requirement | Past inputs only | Past inputs **and** past outputs |
| Design methods | Window, frequency sampling, Parks–McClellan | Bilinear transform, impulse invariance |

**ECG Justification (3 marks):**
For an ECG QRS detection front-end, **FIR with linear phase** is the **only acceptable choice**, for the following reasons:

1. **Morphology preservation:** The shape of the QRS complex is the primary diagnostic feature (e.g., for detecting R-on-T, ST elevation, Q-wave pathology). A nonlinear phase filter would *shift* different frequency components of the QRS by different time delays, **distorting the waveform** and potentially masking pathology.

2. **Constant group delay:** A linear-phase FIR has group delay $\tau_g = (N-1)/2$, which is a *fixed* number of samples. This delay is easily compensated by simply shifting the output by $\tau_g$ samples, restoring the temporal alignment with other leads.

3. **Stability guarantee:** An ECG monitor may run for 24–72 hours continuously. Any IIR filter (even a well-designed one) can exhibit *limit cycle oscillations* or instability under overflow conditions. FIR, having no feedback, is **inherently BIBO stable**, making it the safer clinical choice.

4. **Auditable and reproducible:** FIR coefficients can be verified independently against specifications without depending on past output values, simplifying regulatory certification (FDA, CE marking).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $f$ and $\omega$:** Students often write $\omega_p = 100$ Hz instead of $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad/sample. Always write the *unit* explicitly. **1 mark deduction** is common.
> 2. **Forgetting to state FIR or IIR explicitly** in the preliminary design. The examiner looks for this early in the answer — without it, the design is incomplete. **1–2 marks deduction.**
> 3. **Using $p < 1$ instead of $\vert p \vert < 1$** for stability. Poles can be complex. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**. **1 mark deduction.**
> 4. **Not justifying the choice of FIR/IIR** with a biomedical reason in long answers. Just stating "FIR is better" without a reason (e.g., "linear phase preserves QRS morphology") will fetch only **partial marks** for the comparison.
> 5. **Skipping the Nyquist check:** If the problem gives you $f_s$ and a signal frequency, always verify $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ as a preliminary step. Examiners reward this with a free **0.5–1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing passband ripple and stopband attenuation signs:** Passband ripple is usually a *small* number like 0.05, while stopband attenuation is a *large* number like 40 dB. Mix them up and the filter will be unusable.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Print or Photograph Before Exam:**

- A **digital filter** is a discrete LTI system described by the **LCCDE**: $y[n] = -\sum a_k y[n-k] + \sum b_k x[n-k]$.
- **FIR** = only feed-forward ($a_k = 0$); **IIR** = feed-forward + feedback.
- **Linear phase** is possible **only** in FIR (with symmetric/antisymmetric $h[n]$).
- **Stability rule** (IIR): $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for all poles (modulus, not real value).
- **Nyquist** sampling: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$; add 10–25% guard band in practice.
- **Digital frequency**: $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$ (rad/sample); ranges $0$ to $\pi$.
- **Normalized frequency** to Nyquist: $\hat{f} = 2f / f_s$; ranges $0$ to $1$.
- **Passband ripple (dB)**: $A_p = -20\log_{10}(1 - \delta_p)$.
- **Stopband attenuation (dB)**: $A_s = -20\log_{10}(\delta_s)$.
- **Group delay of linear-phase FIR**: $\tau_g = (N-1)/2$ samples (constant).
- **ECG / EEG** → choose **FIR** (linear phase, morphology preservation).
- **PCG / EMG** → choose **IIR** (sharp cutoff, low compute).
- **50 Hz / 60 Hz** interference → choose **notch (band-stop) filter**.
- **Baseline wander (< 0.5 Hz)** → choose **high-pass filter**.
- **High-frequency noise** → choose **low-pass filter**.
- **Filter design steps**: signal analysis → sampling → spec draft → FIR/IIR choice → type (LPF/HPF/BPF/BSF) → order estimate → actual design.
- **Causality** is implicit in real-time digital filters.
- The **preliminary phase decides the structure**; the design phase (Module 4) computes the coefficients.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Linear-Phase FIR Filter Using Windows (Bartlett) and Frequency Sampling Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Linear-Phase FIR Filter Using Windows (Bartlett) and Frequency Sampling Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filter** is a discrete-time filter whose impulse response $h(n)$ is of finite duration, i.e., it settles to zero in a finite number of samples. When the filter exhibits **linear phase**, the output signal is merely a delayed, undistorted replica of the input — preserving the **morphology of biomedical signals** such as ECG, EEG, and EMG waveforms.

The **design of a Linear-Phase FIR filter** is the process of determining the finite-length impulse response $h(n)$ that approximates a desired magnitude response $H_d(\omega)$ while guaranteeing a constant group delay across all frequencies. Two principal techniques specified in the KTU 2024 PCBMT501 Module 3 syllabus are:

1. **Window Method (using Bartlett Window)** — A time-domain truncation technique.
2. **Frequency Sampling Method (FSM)** — A frequency-domain interpolation technique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> *"Design of linear phase FIR filters using windows (Rectangular, Bartlett, Hanning, Hamming, Blackman) and Frequency Sampling Method — design of Low pass and High pass filters."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are photographing a moving object (your desired filter response). The camera's shutter corresponds to a **window** that lets you capture only a finite slice of reality. If the shutter is too narrow (short window), the photo becomes blurry (ripples/gibbs phenomenon). If you shape the shutter edges (tapered window like Bartlett), the photo's edges become softer but cleaner.

**Analogy 1 — The Bartlett Window as a Triangular Stencil:**
Suppose you want to cut a perfect shape (ideal filter) from a piece of paper. The ideal shape extends infinitely. You overlay a triangular cardboard stencil (Bartlett window) on the paper and trace it. The triangle naturally fades to zero at its edges, so no sharp cut-off artifacts appear. The filter coefficients you obtain are simply the **multiplication** of the ideal infinite-duration impulse response with this triangular stencil.

**Analogy 2 — Frequency Sampling as Connecting the Dots:**
Instead of describing the entire filter curve, the **Frequency Sampling Method** asks you to specify the filter's response only at a discrete set of equally spaced frequency points $(N$ samples on the unit circle). The actual filter is then obtained by connecting these points with a mathematical "spline" (the Dirichlet kernel interpolation). It is the DSP equivalent of a child drawing a curve by sampling a few points.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Parameters in FIR Filter Design:**
> - **Filter Length:** $N$ (always an integer; for linear phase FIR, $N$ is odd or even depending on filter type).
> - **Group Delay:** $\alpha = \dfrac{N-1}{2}$ samples (constant for linear phase).
> - **Transition Width:** $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ (bandwidth between passband and stopband).
> - **Sampling Frequency:** $f_s$ in Hz.
> - **Normalized Cutoff:** $\omega_c = \dfrac{2\pi f_c}{f_s}$ rad/sample.
> - **Bartlett Window Peak Value:** $w(0) = 1$ (always normalized to unity at center).
> - **Main Lobe Width (Bartlett):** $\dfrac{8\pi}{N}$ rad/sample (largest among common windows).

### 1.4 Why Linear Phase Matters in Biomedical Signal Processing

Biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) carry diagnostic information primarily in their **waveform shape and relative timing of frequency components**. A non-linear phase filter distorts this shape (phase dispersion), potentially masking features like the **QRS complex in ECG** or **spike-wave discharges in EEG**. Hence, the KTU 2024 PCBMT501 syllabus places strong emphasis on linear-phase FIR design for biomedical applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions for Linear Phase in an FIR Filter

An FIR filter of length $N$ with impulse response $h(n)$, $0 \le n \le N-1$, has **linear phase** if and only if its impulse response satisfies one of the following symmetry conditions:

**Type-I: Symmetric, N odd**
$$h(n) = h(N-1-n), \quad \alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$$

**Type-II: Symmetric, N even**
$$h(n) = h(N-1-n), \quad \alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$$

**Type-III: Antisymmetric, N odd**
$$h(n) = -h(N-1-n), \quad \alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$$

**Type-IV: Antisymmetric, N even**
$$h(n) = -h(N-1-n), \quad \alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why These Constraints?**
> Symmetry (or antisymmetry) in $h(n)$ guarantees that the **phase response** $\theta(\omega) = -\alpha\omega$, a pure linear function of $\omega$. The constant slope $-\alpha$ is the **group delay**, meaning all frequency components are delayed by the same number of samples.

### 2.2 The Window Method — Conceptual Pipeline

The window method involves three logical steps:

1. **Specify the Ideal Filter** $H_d(\omega)$ — typically a brick-wall lowpass or highpass response.
2. **Compute the Inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (IDTFT)** to obtain the infinite-duration impulse response $h_d(n)$.
3. **Truncate and Taper** the ideal impulse response by multiplying it with a finite-length window $w(n)$.

**Mathematical Pipeline:**

$$H_d(\omega) \xrightarrow{\text{IDTFT}} h_d(n) \xrightarrow{\times w(n)} h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$$

**Convolution Theorem Interpretation:**

$$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n) \xLeftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}} H(\omega) = \frac{1}{2\pi} H_d(\omega) * W(\omega)$$

The actual response $H(\omega)$ is the **periodic convolution** of the ideal brick-wall $H_d(\omega)$ with the window's frequency response $W(\omega)$. This convolution is what produces the **Gibbs phenomenon (ripples)** and the **transition band**.

### 2.3 Detailed Analysis of the Bartlett (Triangular) Window

The **Bartlett window** is the simplest tapered (non-rectangular) window. It is essentially the **linear convolution of two rectangular windows**.

**Time-Domain Definition:**

$$w(n) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2n}{N-1}, & 0 \le n \le \dfrac{N-1}{2} \\[6pt] 2 - \dfrac{2n}{N-1}, & \dfrac{N-1}{2} \le n \le N-1 \end{cases}$$

**Frequency-Domain Expression:**

$$W(\omega) = \frac{2}{N} \left[ \frac{\sin\left(\dfrac{\omega N}{4}\right)}{\sin\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)} \right]^2 e^{-j\omega\left(\dfrac{N-1}{2}\right)}$$

**Symmetry Property:** The Bartlett window is **symmetric**, so when applied to a symmetric $h_d(n)$, the resulting FIR filter preserves linear phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distinguishing Bartlett from Triangular Windows:**
> In some textbooks, "Bartlett" denotes the triangular window with zeros at both ends, while "Triangular" can include the modified version that touches 0.5 at endpoints. For KTU board exams, treat them as the **same triangular window** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### 2.4 Detailed Analysis of the Frequency Sampling Method (FSM)

In the **Frequency Sampling Method**, we directly specify the desired frequency response $H(k)$ at $N$ equally spaced points on the unit circle:

$$H(k) = H(\omega) \Big\vert_{\omega = \frac{2\pi k}{N}}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

The impulse response is then obtained via the **Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (IDFT)**:

$$h(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} H(k) \, e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

**Linear-Phase Constraint in FSM:**

For a linear-phase FIR filter of length $N$, the frequency samples must satisfy the **conjugate symmetry** condition:

$$H(k) = H^*(N-k), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

This guarantees real-valued $h(n)$ with the required symmetry. The frequency response between samples is obtained by **Dirichlet kernel interpolation**:

$$H(\omega) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} H(k) \cdot D\left(\omega - \frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)$$

where $D(\theta) = \dfrac{\sin(N\theta/2)}{N\sin(\theta/2)} e^{-j\theta(N-1)/2}$ is the Dirichlet kernel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Steps for a Lowpass FIR via FSM:**
> 1. Choose $N$ and cutoff frequency $\omega_c$.
> 2. Determine the **passband index** $k_p = \lfloor \omega_c N / (2\pi) \rfloor$.
> 3. Set $H(k) = 1$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, k_p$ and $k = N-k_p, \ldots, N-1$ (passband).
> 4. Set $H(k) = 0$ for all other $k$ (stopband).
> 5. Apply linear-phase constraint: $H(k) = H^*(N-k)$.
> 6. Compute $h(n)$ via IDFT.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the master formula set for KTU 2024 PCBMT501 Module 3 problems.**

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbol Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal LP impulse response | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c(n-\alpha))}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$, $n \ne \alpha$ | $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ |
| Ideal LP at center sample | $h_d(\alpha) = \omega_c / \pi$ | Direct value |
| Ideal HP impulse response | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi(n-\alpha)) - \sin(\omega_c(n-\alpha))}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$ | Apply subtraction |
| Ideal BP impulse response | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_2(n-\alpha)) - \sin(\omega_1(n-\alpha))}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$ | With $\omega_2 > \omega_1$ |
| Windowed FIR | $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$ | $0 \le n \le N-1$ |
| Bartlett Window | $w(n) = 1 - \dfrac{2\vert n - \alpha \vert}{N-1}$ | Symmetric triangle |
| Rectangular Window | $w(n) = 1$, $0 \le n \le N-1$ | Sharpest cutoff |
| FSM IDFT | $h(n) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} H(k) e^{j2\pi kn/N}$ | Exact recovery |
| FSM LP Passband | $k_p = \lfloor \omega_c N / (2\pi) \rfloor$ | Integer truncation |
| Transition Width (Bartlett) | $\Delta\omega = 8\pi / N$ | Wider than Hamming |
| Main Lobe Width (Bartlett) | $8\pi / N$ rad/sample | Double of Rectangular |
| Peak Side Lobe (Bartlett) | $-26$ dB | Moderate attenuation |
| Linear Phase Group Delay | $\tau_g = (N-1)/2$ samples | Constant |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear-Phase Condition from Symmetry of $h(n)$

**Objective:** Show that a symmetric $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ implies linear phase.

**Starting Point:** The frequency response of an FIR filter is

$$H(\omega) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Substitute $m = N-1-n$** (so $n = N-1-m$ and the summation index flips):

$$H(\omega) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} h(N-1-m) e^{-j\omega(N-1-m)}$$

**Apply the symmetry condition** $h(N-1-m) = h(m)$:

$$H(\omega) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} h(m) e^{-j\omega(N-1)} e^{j\omega m}$$

**Factor out the constant phase term** $e^{-j\omega(N-1)}$:

$$H(\omega) = e^{-j\omega \frac{N-1}{2}} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} h(m) e^{j\omega\left(m - \frac{N-1}{2}\right)}$$

**Recognize the sum as a real-valued function** $H_r(\omega)$ when $h(m)$ is real and the index shift yields a cosine series:

$$H(\omega) = e^{-j\omega \frac{N-1}{2}} \cdot H_r(\omega)$$

where $H_r(\omega)$ is the **real amplitude response** (a sum of cosines). Therefore:

$$\angle H(\omega) = -\omega \cdot \frac{N-1}{2} \quad (\text{mod } \pi)$$

This is **linear in $\omega$** with slope $-(N-1)/2$, confirming linear phase. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Bartlett Lowpass FIR Design (KTU-style 14-mark)

**Problem:** Design a linear-phase lowpass FIR filter of length $N = 7$ with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \pi/2$ rad/sample using the **Bartlett window**. Plot or list the filter coefficients and verify linear phase.

#### Step 1: Compute the Group Delay

$$\alpha = \frac{N-1}{2} = \frac{7-1}{2} = 3$$

#### Step 2: Compute Ideal Impulse Response $h_d(n)$ for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, 6$

For $n \ne \alpha = 3$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c(n - \alpha))}{\pi(n - \alpha)} = \frac{\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}(n-3)\right)}{\pi(n-3)}$$

For $n = \alpha = 3$:

$$h_d(3) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} = \frac{\pi/2}{\pi} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Tabulating $h_d(n)$ for $n = 0$ to $6$:**

| $n$ | $n - \alpha$ | $\omega_c(n-\alpha) = \tfrac{\pi}{2}(n-3)$ | $\sin(\cdot)$ | $h_d(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $-3$ | $-3\pi/2$ | $+1$ | $1/(3\pi) = 0.1061$ |
| 1 | $-2$ | $-\pi$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 2 | $-1$ | $-\pi/2$ | $-1$ | $1/\pi = 0.3183$ |
| 3 | $0$ | $0$ | — | $0.5$ |
| 4 | $+1$ | $+\pi/2$ | $+1$ | $1/\pi = 0.3183$ |
| 5 | $+2$ | $+\pi$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 6 | $+3$ | $+3\pi/2$ | $-1$ | $-1/(3\pi) = -0.1061$ |

#### Step 3: Compute the Bartlett Window $w(n)$ for $N = 7$

The Bartlett window for $N = 7$ has values:

$$w(n) = 1 - \frac{2 \vert n - 3 \vert}{6} = 1 - \frac{\vert n - 3 \vert}{3}$$

| $n$ | $\vert n - 3 \vert$ | $w(n)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 3 | $1 - 1 = 0$ |
| 1 | 2 | $1 - 2/3 = 1/3$ |
| 2 | 1 | $1 - 1/3 = 2/3$ |
| 3 | 0 | $1 - 0 = 1$ |
| 4 | 1 | $2/3$ |
| 5 | 2 | $1/3$ |
| 6 | 3 | $0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The Bartlett window goes to **zero at both ends**, unlike the rectangular window (which is 1 everywhere). This is what makes it tapered.

#### Step 4: Multiply $h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$ to Obtain $h(n)$

| $n$ | $h_d(n)$ | $w(n)$ | $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $1/(3\pi) = 0.1061$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 1 | $0$ | $1/3$ | $0$ |
| 2 | $1/\pi = 0.3183$ | $2/3$ | $0.2122$ |
| 3 | $0.5$ | $1$ | $0.5$ |
| 4 | $1/\pi = 0.3183$ | $2/3$ | $0.2122$ |
| 5 | $0$ | $1/3$ | $0$ |
| 6 | $-1/(3\pi) = -0.1061$ | $0$ | $0$ |

#### Step 5: Verify Linear Phase (Symmetry Check)

$$h(0) = 0 = h(6), \quad h(1) = 0 = h(5), \quad h(2) = 0.2122 = h(4), \quad h(3) = 0.5$$

The condition $h(n) = h(N-1-n) = h(6-n)$ is **satisfied** for all $n$. Therefore, the filter has **linear phase** with group delay $\tau_g = 3$ samples. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Frequency Sampling Method (Lowpass)

**Problem:** Design a linear-phase lowpass FIR filter of length $N = 7$ with cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/2$ using the **Frequency Sampling Method**.

#### Step 1: Determine the Number of Passband Samples

$$k_p = \left\lfloor \frac{\omega_c N}{2\pi} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{(\pi/2)(7)}{2\pi} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 1.75 \right\rfloor = 1$$

So $H(k) = 1$ for $k = 0$ and $k = 1$ (positive-frequency passband), and by symmetry $H(k) = 1$ for $k = N-1 = 6$ and $k = N-2 = 5$.

#### Step 2: Assign Frequency Samples

| $k$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $H(k)$ | $1$ | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $1$ | $1$ |

#### Step 3: Apply Linear-Phase Constraint

For a Type-I symmetric real filter, $H(k) = H^*(N-k)$. Since $H(k)$ is real here, we need $H(k) = H(N-k)$:

- $H(0) = H(7) = H(0)$ ✓
- $H(1) = H(6)$ ✓
- $H(2) = H(5)$ — but $H(2) = 0$ and $H(5) = 1$ — this **violates** symmetry!

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution for KTU Problems:** For odd $N$ with real coefficients, the constraint is
> $$H(k) = H(N-k) \quad \text{when } H(k) \text{ is real.}$$
> To fix our example, either use $N$ even or use **complex samples** with phase $-j\omega\alpha$ baked in:
> $$H(k) = e^{-j2\pi k \alpha / N} \cdot A(k)$$
> where $A(k)$ is the real amplitude (1 or 0).

#### Step 4: Reassign with Phase Compensation

Let $\alpha = (N-1)/2 = 3$. Then:

$$H(k) = A(k) \cdot e^{-j\frac{2\pi k (3)}{7}}$$

| $k$ | $A(k)$ | $H(k)$ (complex) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | $1$ |
| 1 | 1 | $e^{-j6\pi/7}$ |
| 2 | 0 | $0$ |
| 3 | 0 | $0$ |
| 4 | 0 | $0$ |
| 5 | 1 | $e^{-j30\pi/7} = e^{j12\pi/7}$ |
| 6 | 1 | $e^{-j36\pi/7} = e^{j4\pi/7}$ |

#### Step 5: Compute $h(n)$ via IDFT

$$h(n) = \frac{1}{7} \sum_{k=0}^{6} H(k) e^{j\frac{2\pi kn}{7}}$$

Substituting the four non-zero $H(k)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(n) &= \frac{1}{7} \Big[ 1 \cdot e^{j0} + e^{-j6\pi/7} \cdot e^{j2\pi n/7} + e^{j12\pi/7} \cdot e^{j10\pi n/7} + e^{j4\pi/7} \cdot e^{j12\pi n/7} \Big] \\
&= \frac{1}{7} \Big[ 1 + e^{j\frac{2\pi}{7}(n-3)} + e^{j\frac{2\pi}{7}(5n+6)} + e^{j\frac{2\pi}{7}(6n+2)} \Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

After simplification (and noting that the last two terms are conjugates when $n$ is real), the result for $n = 0$ to $6$ is:

| $n$ | $h(n)$ | Decimal |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $1/7 + 2\cos(6\pi/7)/7$ | $-0.0952$ |
| 1 | $1/7 + 2\cos(4\pi/7)/7$ | $0.1052$ |
| 2 | $1/7 + 2\cos(2\pi/7)/7$ | $0.3614$ |
| 3 | $1/7 + 2\cos(0)/7$ | $0.4286$ |
| 4 | $1/7 + 2\cos(2\pi/7)/7$ | $0.3614$ |
| 5 | $1/7 + 2\cos(4\pi/7)/7$ | $0.1052$ |
| 6 | $1/7 + 2\cos(6\pi/7)/7$ | $-0.0952$ |

#### Step 6: Verify Symmetry

$h(0) = h(6) = -0.0952$, $h(1) = h(5) = 0.1052$, $h(2) = h(4) = 0.3614$, $h(3) = 0.4286$. **Symmetric** → Linear phase confirmed. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Comparison: Window Method vs Frequency Sampling Method

| Parameter | Window Method (Bartlett) | Frequency Sampling Method |
|---|---|---|
| Design Domain | Time-domain (truncation) | Frequency-domain (sampling) |
| Control of $H(\omega)$ | Indirect (via window choice) | Direct (at sampled frequencies) |
| Computational Cost | Very low (closed-form $h_d(n)$) | Moderate (IDFT of $N$ samples) |
| Transition Width | Fixed by window (Bartlett: $8\pi/N$) | Adjustable by transition samples |
| Gibbs Phenomenon | Yes (reduced by tapering) | Reduced if transition samples optimized |
| Stability | Always stable (no feedback) | Always stable (no feedback) |
| Standard for Arbitrary $H(\omega)$ | Not suitable | **Highly suitable** |
| Best Use Case | Standard LP/HP/BP filters | Multi-band, arbitrary magnitude responses |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Very High | High |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FIR Filter Design Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Frequency Response H_d omega] --> B[Compute Ideal Impulse h_d n via IDTFT]
    B --> C[Choose Window Type e.g. Bartlett]
    C --> D[Generate Window Sequence w n]
    D --> E[Multiply h_d n times w n]
    E --> F[Truncate to N samples]
    F --> G[Verify Symmetry h n = h N-1-n]
    G --> H{Symmetric?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Linear Phase FIR Achieved]
    H -- No --> J[Adjust Window or N]
    J --> D

    K[Desired Magnitude at N samples] --> L[Define H k for k=0 to N-1]
    L --> M[Apply Linear Phase Constraint H k = H* N-k]
    M --> N[Compute h n via IDFT]
    N --> O[Verify Real and Symmetric h n]
    O --> P[Linear Phase FIR via FSM]

    style I fill:#90EE90
    style P fill:#90EE90
    style A fill:#FFB6C1
    style K fill:#FFB6C1
    style G fill:#87CEEB
    style O fill:#87CEEB
```

### 4.2 Linear Phase FIR Filter Signal Flow (Direct Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x n] --> B[Delay z^-1]
    B --> C[Delay z^-1]
    C --> D[Delay z^-1]
    D --> E[Delay z^-1]
    E --> F[Delay z^-1]
    F --> G[Delay z^-1]
    G --> H[Output Node]

    A --> A1[x n times h 0]
    B --> A1
    A1 --> SUM1

    C --> A2[x n-2 times h 2]
    A2 --> SUM1

    E --> A3[x n-4 times h 4]
    A3 --> SUM1

    G --> A4[x n-6 times h 6]
    A4 --> SUM1

    SUM1 --> OUT[y n]

    B --> B1[x n-1 times h 1]
    D --> B1
    B1 --> SUM1

    D --> C1[x n-3 times h 3]
    C1 --> SUM1

    F --> D1[x n-5 times h 5]
    D1 --> SUM1

    style A fill:#FFD700
    style OUT fill:#90EE90
    style SUM1 fill:#FFA07A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The signal enters from the left at $x(n)$ and passes through a tapped delay line (each block represents $z^{-1}$). Each tap multiplies the delayed signal by a coefficient $h(k)$ (the FIR coefficients designed above). All products are summed at $\Sigma$ to produce $y(n)$. The symmetry $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ is what makes this structure inherently linear-phase.

### 4.3 Comparison: Time vs Frequency Domain Design Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Linear-Phase FIR Design Problem] --> Q1{Need arbitrary magnitude response?}
    Q1 -- No --> WIN[Use Window Method]
    Q1 -- Yes --> FSM[Use Frequency Sampling Method]

    WIN --> W1[Pick N and omega_c]
    W1 --> W2[Compute h_d n analytically]
    W2 --> W3[Apply Window e.g. Bartlett]
    W3 --> W4[Truncate and verify]
    W4 --> RESULT[h n ready for implementation]

    FSM --> F1[Pick N and omega_c]
    F1 --> F2[Sample H omega at N points]
    F2 --> F3[Add transition band samples if needed]
    F3 --> F4[Apply linear phase constraint]
    F4 --> F5[Compute h n via IDFT]
    F5 --> F4b[Verify real and symmetric]
    F4b --> RESULT

    style START fill:#FFB6C1
    style RESULT fill:#90EE90
    style WIN fill:#87CEEB
    style FSM fill:#FFD700
```
