# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Bilinear Transform from the Trapezoidal Integration Rule

**Starting Point:** Consider the analog integrator whose output $y(t)$ is the integral of input $x(t)$:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate to obtain the input-output relationship in the s-domain:

$$Y(s) = \frac{1}{s} X(s) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad sY(s) = X(s) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = x(t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the trapezoidal rule of numerical integration. The integral from $t = (n-1)T$ to $t = nT$ is approximated by the average of the function values at the endpoints times the step size:

$$y(nT) - y((n-1)T) \approx \frac{T}{2}\big[x(nT) + x((n-1)T)\big]$$

This is the trapezoidal approximation of the area under $x(t)$ in one sampling interval.

**Step 3:** Take the Z-transform of both sides. Using the time-shift property: $Z\{y((n-1)T)\} = z^{-1}Y(z)$ and $Z\{x((n-1)T)\} = z^{-1}X(z)$:

$$Y(z) - z^{-1}Y(z) = \frac{T}{2}\big[X(z) + z^{-1}X(z)\big]$$

**Step 4:** Factor and simplify:

$$Y(z)\big(1 - z^{-1}\big) = \frac{T}{2} X(z)\big(1 + z^{-1}\big)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the digital transfer function equivalent of $1/s$:

$$\frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{1}{s_{BLT}}$$

**Step 6:** Invert both sides to obtain the BLT equation:

$$s_{BLT} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms the Bilinear Transform is mathematically identical to replacing analog integration with trapezoidal numerical integration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Frequency Warping Equation

**Starting Point:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ and $z = e^{j\Omega}$ (since digital frequencies of interest lie on the unit circle) into the BLT equation:

$$j\omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{j\Omega} - 1}{e^{j\Omega} + 1}$$

**Step 1:** Expand the numerator and denominator using Euler's identity. Recall that $e^{j\Omega} = \cos\Omega + j\sin\Omega$:

$$e^{j\Omega} - 1 = (\cos\Omega - 1) + j\sin\Omega$$

$$e^{j\Omega} + 1 = (\cos\Omega + 1) + j\sin\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Apply the half-angle identities $\cos\Omega - 1 = -2\sin^2(\Omega/2)$, $\cos\Omega + 1 = 2\cos^2(\Omega/2)$, and $\sin\Omega = 2\sin(\Omega/2)\cos(\Omega/2)$:

$$e^{j\Omega} - 1 = -2\sin^2(\Omega/2) + j \cdot 2\sin(\Omega/2)\cos(\Omega/2)$$

$$e^{j\Omega} + 1 = 2\cos^2(\Omega/2) + j \cdot 2\sin(\Omega/2)\cos(\Omega/2)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $2\sin(\Omega/2)\cos(\Omega/2)$ from the numerator and $2\cos(\Omega/2)$ from the denominator:

$$\frac{e^{j\Omega} - 1}{e^{j\Omega} + 1} = \frac{2\sin(\Omega/2)\big[-\sin(\Omega/2) + j\cos(\Omega/2)\big]}{2\cos(\Omega/2)\big[\cos(\Omega/2) + j\sin(\Omega/2)\big]}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $-\sin\theta + j\cos\theta = j(\cos\theta + j\sin\theta) = je^{j\theta}$, while $\cos\theta + j\sin\theta = e^{j\theta}$:

$$\frac{e^{j\Omega} - 1}{e^{j\Omega} + 1} = \frac{2\sin(\Omega/2) \cdot j e^{j\Omega/2}}{2\cos(\Omega/2) \cdot e^{j\Omega/2}} = j\tan(\Omega/2)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back into the BLT equation:

$$j\omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot j\tan(\Omega/2)$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $j$ from both sides to obtain the final frequency warping equation:

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: Stability Preservation Proof

**Claim:** If all poles of $H_a(s)$ lie in the left-half s-plane ($\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$), then all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$).

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Let $s = \sigma + j\omega$ be a general point in the s-plane. The corresponding z-plane point is:

$$z = \frac{1 + sT/2}{1 - sT/2} = \frac{2 + sT}{2 - sT}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude squared of $z$:

$$\vert z \vert^2 = z \cdot z^* = \frac{(2 + sT)(2 + s^*T)}{(2 - sT)(2 - s^*T)}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the numerator and denominator. Let $sT = \sigma T + j\omega T$:

$$\text{Numerator: } (2 + \sigma T)^2 + (\omega T)^2$$

$$\text{Denominator: } (2 - \sigma T)^2 + (\omega T)^2$$

**Step 4:** Compare numerator and denominator. For a left-half plane point, $\sigma < 0$, so $\sigma T < 0$, which means $2 + \sigma T < 2 - \sigma T$. Therefore:

$$(2 + \sigma T)^2 < (2 - \sigma T)^2$$

**Step 5:** This gives $\vert z \vert^2 < 1$, i.e., $\vert z \vert < 1$. Q.E.D.

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Poles in the LHP ($\sigma < 0$) $\Rightarrow$ $\vert z \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle). The transformation **preserves stability** exactly.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Complete KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

**Problem:** Design a digital Butterworth lowpass IIR filter using the Bilinear Transform Method to satisfy the following specifications for filtering an ECG signal:

- Passband edge: $f_p = 40$ Hz
- Stopband edge: $f_s = 50$ Hz
- Passband ripple: $A_p \leq 1$ dB
- Stopband attenuation: $A_s \geq 40$ dB
- Sampling frequency: $F_s = 200$ Hz

**Step 1: Compute sampling period and digital edge frequencies.**

$$T = \frac{1}{F_s} = \frac{1}{200} = 0.005 \text{ s}$$

$$\Omega_p = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_p}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{40}{200} = 0.4\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

$$\Omega_s = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_s}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{50}{200} = 0.5\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2: Pre-warp the critical frequencies.**

$$\omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega_p}{2}\right) = \frac{2}{0.005}\tan\!\left(\frac{0.4\pi}{2}\right) = 400 \cdot \tan(0.2\pi)$$

Computing $\tan(0.2\pi) = \tan(36°) \approx 0.7265$:

$$\omega_p \approx 400 \times 0.7265 \approx 290.6 \text{ rad/sec}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{2}\right) = 400 \cdot \tan(0.25\pi) = 400 \cdot \tan(45°) = 400 \times 1 = 400 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 3: Compute the order of the Butterworth filter.**

Using the standard Butterworth order formula:

$$N \geq \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\omega_s}{\omega_p}\right)}$$

Compute the numerator: $10^{0.1 \times 40} - 1 = 10^4 - 1 = 9999$ and $10^{0.1 \times 1} - 1 = 10^{0.1} - 1 \approx 1.2589 - 1 = 0.2589$.

$$\sqrt{\frac{9999}{0.2589}} = \sqrt{38621.5} \approx 196.52$$

$$\log_{10}(196.52) \approx 2.293$$

Denominator: $\log_{10}(400/290.6) = \log_{10}(1.3765) \approx 0.1387$.

$$N \geq \frac{2.293}{0.1387} \approx 16.53$$

So $N = 17$ (round up to nearest integer).

**Step 4: Determine the analog prototype cutoff frequency.**

For Butterworth, the -3 dB cutoff is:

$$\omega_c = \frac{\omega_p}{(10^{0.1 A_p} - 1)^{1/(2N)}} = \frac{290.6}{(0.2589)^{1/34}}$$

$$(0.2589)^{1/34} = e^{\ln(0.2589)/34} = e^{-1.3509/34} = e^{-0.03973} \approx 0.9611$$

$$\omega_c \approx \frac{290.6}{0.9611} \approx 302.4 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 5: Construct the analog Butterworth filter $H_a(s)$.**

For $N = 17$, the poles are placed on a circle of radius $\omega_c$ in the s-plane, with the first pole at angle $\pi/2 + \pi/(2N)$ from the positive real axis. This step is typically implemented via standard tables or software.

**Step 6: Apply the Bilinear Transform substitution.**

Replace $s$ with $\frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = 400 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$ in $H_a(s)$ to obtain $H(z)$.

**Step 7: Simplify and verify stability.**

The resulting $H(z)$ is a 17th-order rational polynomial in $z^{-1}$. All 17 poles must lie inside the unit circle for stability (guaranteed by the BLT property).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: BLT vs. Other Methods

| Property | Bilinear Transform | Impulse Invariance | Step Invariance |
|:--------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|:---------------:|
| Mapping Type | Non-linear (warped) | Linear (one-to-many) | Linear (one-to-many) |
| Aliasing | **No aliasing** ✓ | Severe aliasing ✗ | Moderate aliasing ✗ |
| Stability | Preserved ✓ | Preserved for stable $H_a$ ✓ | Preserved ✓ |
| Frequency Response Match | Excellent at critical freqs | Poor at high freqs | Moderate |
| Computational Cost | High (algebraic substitution) | Low (partial fractions) | Moderate |
| Best Suited For | Piecewise-constant filters | Bandlimited filters | Smooth spectra |
| Biomedical Use | **ECG/EEG/EMG filters** | Rarely used | Rarely used |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:** Whenever you have a design question in PCBMT501, the Bilinear Transform is the **default recommended method** unless the question explicitly says "using Impulse Invariance Method".

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the Bilinear Transformation Method. Write the expression relating the analog frequency $\omega$ and digital frequency $\Omega$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Bilinear Transformation Method (BLT)** is a technique used to convert an analog filter transfer function $H_a(s)$ into a digital filter transfer function $H(z)$ by substituting $s$ with a rational function of $z$, given by:
> $$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$$
> 
> The relation between analog frequency $\omega$ (rad/sec) and digital frequency $\Omega$ (rad/sample) is the **frequency warping equation**:
> $$\omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right)$$
> 
> This non-linear relationship is the key reason why pre-warping is necessary during filter design. **(3 Marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**State any three advantages of the Bilinear Transformation Method over the Impulse Invariance Method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. **No Aliasing:** The BLT is a one-to-one mapping between the s-plane and z-plane, so there is no spectral aliasing. In contrast, the Impulse Invariance Method suffers from aliasing of the analog frequency response.
> 
> 2. **Stability Preservation:** A stable analog filter (all poles in LHP) always produces a stable digital filter (all poles inside the unit circle). This is guaranteed by the BLT mapping property.
> 
> 3. **Suitable for Piecewise-Constant Filters:** BLT is highly suitable for designing digital filters with piecewise-constant magnitude specifications (e.g., lowpass, highpass, bandpass), which is the standard requirement in biomedical signal filtering. **(3 Marks)**

---

## 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Derive the Bilinear Transformation equation $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$ from the trapezoidal rule of integration. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **frequency warping** in the BLT. Derive the warping equation and explain why pre-warping is essential in filter design. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Consider the analog integrator equation:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy(t)}{dt} = x(t)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **trapezoidal rule** for numerical integration over one sampling interval $T$:

$$y(nT) - y((n-1)T) = \frac{T}{2}\big[x(nT) + x((n-1)T)\big]$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **Z-transform** to both sides, using the time-shift property $Z\{x((n-1)T)\} = z^{-1}X(z)$:

$$Y(z) - z^{-1}Y(z) = \frac{T}{2}\big[X(z) + z^{-1}X(z)\big]$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$:

$$Y(z)\big(1 - z^{-1}\big) = \frac{T}{2} X(z)\big(1 + z^{-1}\big)$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to express $Y(z)/X(z)$:

$$\frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{1}{s_{BLT}}$$

**Step 6:** Invert to get the BLT equation:

$$s_{BLT} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1} \quad \blacksquare$$

**(7 Marks — 1 mark for setup, 2 for trapezoidal rule, 2 for Z-transform, 1 for factoring, 1 for final substitution)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Define Frequency Warping:** When the analog filter $H_a(s)$ is converted to $H(z)$ via BLT, the analog frequency $\omega$ (rad/sec) and digital frequency $\Omega$ (rad/sample) are related by a **non-linear** equation. This non-linearity is called **frequency warping**, and it causes compression of high frequencies.

**Step 2 — Derive the Warping Equation:** Substitute $s = j\omega$ and $z = e^{j\Omega}$ in the BLT equation:

$$j\omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{j\Omega} - 1}{e^{j\Omega} + 1}$$

Using the identity $\frac{e^{j\Omega}-1}{e^{j\Omega}+1} = j\tan(\Omega/2)$:

$$j\omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot j\tan(\Omega/2)$$

Cancelling $j$:

$$\omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right) \quad \text{(Warping Equation)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 3 — Why Pre-Warping is Essential:** Because the warping is non-linear, if we design an analog filter with a cutoff at $\omega_c$ and apply BLT directly, the resulting digital filter's cutoff will **not** be at the desired $\Omega_c$. Specifically, the entire analog frequency axis from 0 to $\infty$ is compressed into the digital axis 0 to $\pi$ (Nyquist). To compensate, we **pre-warp** the critical digital frequencies:

$$\omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega_p}{2}\right)$$

Then we design the analog prototype with $\omega_p$ instead of $\Omega_p$ converted linearly. After BLT, the digital filter's response will exactly match specifications at the critical frequencies. This is the essence of pre-warping.

**(7 Marks — 2 marks for definition, 3 for derivation, 2 for pre-warping explanation)**

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

> **(a)** List and explain the **mapping properties** of the Bilinear Transformation. How does it preserve stability? **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** An ECG monitoring system requires a digital lowpass Butterworth filter. The specifications are: $f_p = 100$ Hz, $f_s = 150$ Hz, $A_p = 1$ dB, $A_s = 30$ dB, $F_s = 1000$ Hz. Compute the pre-warped passband and stopband frequencies. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **Mapping Properties of the Bilinear Transformation** are:

**Property 1 — LHP maps to inside of unit circle:** A point $s = \sigma + j\omega$ with $\sigma < 0$ (left-half s-plane) maps to a point $z$ with $\vert z \vert < 1$ (interior of unit circle).

**Property 2 — RHP maps to outside of unit circle:** A point $s$ with $\sigma > 0$ maps to $\vert z \vert > 1$.

**Property 3 — Imaginary axis maps to unit circle:** A point $s = j\omega$ (on the imaginary axis) maps to $\vert z \vert = 1$, i.e., the unit circle.

**Property 4 — One-to-one mapping:** The transformation is bijective, so there is no aliasing.

**Property 5 — Stability Preservation:** Since a stable analog filter has all its poles in the LHP ($\sigma < 0$), and the BLT maps LHP to the interior of the unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$), the resulting digital filter will have all its poles strictly inside the unit circle. A digital filter is BIBO stable if and only if all its poles lie strictly inside the unit circle. Therefore, **stability is preserved exactly** by the BLT.

**Mathematical Proof Sketch:** For $s = \sigma + j\omega$:

$$\vert z \vert^2 = \frac{(2+\sigma T)^2 + (\omega T)^2}{(2-\sigma T)^2 + (\omega T)^2}$$

If $\sigma < 0$, then $2+\sigma T < 2-\sigma T$, so the numerator is less than the denominator, giving $\vert z \vert^2 < 1$. Hence $\vert z \vert < 1$. **(7 Marks — 2 for properties list, 2 for explanation, 3 for stability proof)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $f_p = 100$ Hz, $f_s = 150$ Hz, $F_s = 1000$ Hz
- $T = 1/F_s = 1/1000 = 0.001$ s

**Step 1 — Compute digital edge frequencies:**

$$\Omega_p = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_p}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{100}{1000} = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

$$\Omega_s = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_s}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{150}{1000} = 0.3\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the pre-warping equation:**

$$\omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega_p}{2}\right) = \frac{2}{0.001}\tan\!\left(\frac{0.2\pi}{2}\right) = 2000 \cdot \tan(0.1\pi)$$

Computing $\tan(0.1\pi) = \tan(18°) \approx 0.3249$:

$$\boxed{\omega_p \approx 2000 \times 0.3249 \approx 649.8 \text{ rad/sec}}$$

$$\omega_s = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{2}\right) = 2000 \cdot \tan(0.15\pi)$$

Computing $\tan(0.15\pi) = \tan(27°) \approx 0.5095$:

$$\boxed{\omega_s \approx 2000 \times 0.5095 \approx 1019.0 \text{ rad/sec}}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:** These pre-warped analog frequencies (in rad/sec) are used to design the analog Butterworth lowpass filter $H_a(s)$. After applying BLT, the resulting $H(z)$ will have its passband edge exactly at 100 Hz and stopband edge exactly at 150 Hz in the digital domain.

**(7 Marks — 2 for digital frequencies, 3 for pre-warping passband, 2 for pre-warping stopband)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to Pre-Warp:** This is the #1 mistake. If you design an analog filter at $\omega = 2\pi f_p$ directly (without pre-warping) and then apply BLT, your digital filter will **NOT meet specifications**. Examiners explicitly test this. **Always pre-warp first.**
> 
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega$ and $\Omega$ units:** $\omega$ is in **rad/sec** (analog), $\Omega$ is in **rad/sample** (digital). Writing $\omega = 2\pi f$ is correct; writing $\omega = 2\pi f / F_s$ is mixing units. Use the warping equation to convert.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the $T = 1/F_s$ step:** Always start by computing $T$ explicitly. Examiners give method marks for this.
> 
> 4. **Not verifying stability:** Even though BLT guarantees stability, students must check and confirm. Examiners award 1-2 marks for the stability check at the end.
> 
> 5. **Confusing BLT with Impulse Invariance:** BLT uses $s = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{z-1}{z+1}$; Impulse Invariance uses partial fraction expansion followed by $H(z) = \sum \frac{R_k}{1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1}}$. These are **completely different** methods.
> 
> 6. **Writing $z$ instead of $z^{-1}$ in final $H(z)$:** The standard form for digital filters is in powers of $z^{-1}$, not $z$. Convert appropriately.
> 
> 7. **Arithmetic errors in $\tan$ computation:** The values $\tan(0.1\pi) = 0.3249$, $\tan(0.2\pi) = 0.7265$, $\tan(0.25\pi) = 1.0$ are **frequently used**. Memorize these.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bilinear Transform — Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points:**
> 
> - **BLT Equation:** $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$
> - **Inverse BLT:** $z = \frac{2 + sT}{2 - sT}$
> - **Sampling Period:** $T = 1/F_s$
> - **Frequency Warping:** $\omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\Omega/2)$ — non-linear mapping
> - **Pre-Warping Formula:** $\omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\Omega_p/2)$ — must apply BEFORE designing analog prototype
> - **Stability:** LHP ($\sigma < 0$) maps to inside unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$) — always preserves stability
> - **No Aliasing:** BLT is a one-to-one mapping — a major advantage over Impulse Invariance
> - **Other Names:** Tustin's Method, Trapezoidal Integration Method
> - **Best Use Case:** Piecewise-constant magnitude filters (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF) — ideal for biomedical applications
> - **Mapping Properties:** LHP → $\vert z \vert < 1$; RHP → $\vert z \vert > 1$; $j\omega$-axis → $\vert z \vert = 1$
> - **Design Order:** (1) Specs → (2) Compute $T$ → (3) Compute $\Omega_p, \Omega_s$ → (4) Pre-warp to get $\omega_p, \omega_s$ → (5) Design $H_a(s)$ → (6) Apply BLT → (7) Simplify $H(z)$ → (8) Verify stability
> - **Trapezoidal Rule Equivalence:** $y(nT) - y((n-1)T) = \frac{T}{2}[x(nT) + x((n-1)T)]$ — this IS the BLT in disguise!
> - **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a digital filter" without specifying the method, **always choose BLT** as it is the most reliable for biomedical applications.
> - **Biomedical Applications:** ECG filtering (50/60 Hz notch), EEG bandpass, EMG smoothing, pacemaker signal processing, hearing aid DSP.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Bilinear Transform Method** ✅

*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | Outcome-Based Education Framework | Revised Bloom's Taxonomy Cognitive Levels: L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze), L5 (Evaluate)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Filter Approximation - Butterworth Approximation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analog Filter Approximation - Butterworth Approximation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Butterworth Approximation** is a classical analog filter design technique that yields a **maximally flat magnitude response** in the passband. It belongs to the family of all-pole rational approximations of the ideal low-pass filter brick-wall characteristic. The magnitude-squared response is given by:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+\left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2N}}
$$

where:
- **$N$** = order (degree) of the filter — a positive integer.
- **$\Omega_{c}$** = cutoff ($-3\,\text{dB}$) frequency, in **rad/sec**.
- **$\Omega$** = angular frequency variable in **rad/sec**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Butterworth is the *first* approximation taught because its mathematics is closed-form, the poles are uniformly distributed on a circle, and it has no ripples in either passband or stopband — making it the "smoothest" of all classical approximations (Chebyshev, Elliptic, Bessel, etc.).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smooth Ramp" Analogy

Imagine you are walking down a perfectly smooth ramp from a flat plateau (the passband) to a steep cliff (the stopband). A **Butterworth filter** is the **smoothest possible ramp** you can build without any bumps, dips, or oscillations. No matter how closely you inspect the plateau with a magnifying glass, you cannot find a single ripple — the curve is *flat to the highest possible order at $\Omega = 0$*.

- **Chebyshev** would be a ramp with intentional ripples (trade-off: steeper drop).
- **Elliptic** would be a ramp with ripples on **both** sides (steepest of all).
- **Butterworth** is the gentle, bump-free ramp — slower to drop, but the calmest.

## 1.3 Key Properties of Butterworth Filters

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five "Maxims" of a Butterworth Filter (must memorize for KTU viva):**
>
> 1. **Maximally flat magnitude** at $\Omega = 0$ — first $2N-1$ derivatives of $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert$ vanish at DC.
> 2. **Monotonic** response in both passband and stopband (no ripples anywhere).
> 3. **$-3\,\text{dB}$ point** is always at $\Omega = \Omega_{c}$ regardless of $N$.
> 4. **All-pole** transfer function (zeros only at $s \to \infty$).
> 5. **Poles lie uniformly on a circle** of radius $\Omega_{c}$ in the $s$-plane.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Range (Biomedical DSP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutoff Frequency | $\Omega_c$ | rad/sec | $0.1$ – $10^4$ |
| Filter Order | $N$ | dimensionless | $1$ – $10$ |
| Passband Ripple | $A_p$ | dB | $0$ – $3$ (theoretical) |
| Stopband Attenuation | $A_s$ | dB | $20$ – $60$ |
| $-3\,\text{dB}$ Attenuation | — | dB | $-3.0103 \approx -3$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mathematical Derivation of the Magnitude Response

Starting from the requirement that the first $2N-1$ derivatives of $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2}$ vanish at $\Omega = 0$, one can show (a standard textbook exercise) that the only rational all-pole function satisfying this is:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} \;=\; H(s)\,H(-s)\Big\vert_{s=j\Omega} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+(-s^{2}/\Omega_{c}^{2})^{N}}
$$

Substituting $s = j\Omega$ recovers the canonical form stated earlier.

## 2.2 Why "Maximally Flat"?

Expand $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2}$ as a Maclaurin series in $(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2}$:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} \;=\; 1 - \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2N} + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{4N} - \cdots
$$

All terms of order $\Omega^{2}, \Omega^{4}, \ldots, \Omega^{2N-2}$ are **zero**. Hence the response is flat up to (and including) the $(2N-1)^{\text{th}}$ derivative at the origin.

## 2.3 Pole Locations in the $s$-Plane

The $2N$ poles of the denominator $1 + (s/j\Omega_c)^{2N} = 0$ are:

$$
s_{k} \;=\; \Omega_{c}\,\exp\!\left(j\,\frac{(2k+N-1)\,\pi}{2N}\right), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N
$$

For a **stable and causal** filter, we retain only the **$N$ poles in the Left-Half Plane (LHP)**. These are obtained by selecting those angles $\theta_k$ lying in $(\pi/2, 3\pi/2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Butterworth poles are **uniformly distributed** on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$, and they **never land on the $j\omega$-axis**. This guarantees stability and rules out sustained oscillation.

## 2.4 Normalized Butterworth Polynomials $B_N(s)$

Setting $\Omega_c = 1$ for convenience, the denominator polynomial of $H(s)$ is:

$$
B_{N}(s) \;=\; \prod_{k=1}^{N}\left(s - s_{k}\right), \quad s_{k} = \exp\!\left(j\,\frac{(2k+N-1)\,\pi}{2N}\right)
$$

The first few normalized polynomials (must memorize for KTU):

| Order $N$ | Polynomial $B_N(s)$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | $s + 1$ |
| 2 | $s^{2} + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1$ |
| 3 | $s^{3} + 2\,s^{2} + 2\,s + 1$ |
| 4 | $s^{4} + 2.6131\,s^{3} + 3.4142\,s^{2} + 2.6131\,s + 1$ |
| 5 | $s^{5} + 3.2361\,s^{4} + 5.2361\,s^{3} + 5.2361\,s^{2} + 3.2361\,s + 1$ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU exams.** Master them and you will secure full marks on any Butterworth problem.

| # | Formula / Equation | Purpose / Use Case | Boundary / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} = 1\big/\big(1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}\big)$ | Magnitude-squared response | $\Omega \ge 0$ |
| 2 | $\vert H(j\Omega_c)\vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ | Defines $-3$ dB cutoff | Always true |
| 3 | $s_{k} = \Omega_c\exp\!\big(j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N)\big)$ | Pole locations ($k=1..N$ for LHP) | $k \in \{1,2,\dots,N\}$ |
| 4 | $\big(\Omega_p/\Omega_c\big)^{2N} = 10^{A_p/10} - 1$ | Passband spec equation | $A_p > 0$ |
| 5 | $\big(\Omega_s/\Omega_c\big)^{2N} = 10^{A_s/10} - 1$ | Stopband spec equation | $A_s > 0$ |
| 6 | $N \ge \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\big[(10^{A_s/10}-1)/(10^{A_p/10}-1)\big]}{2\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ | Minimum filter order | Round **up** |
| 7 | $\Omega_c = \Omega_p\big/\big(10^{A_p/10}-1\big)^{1/(2N)}$ | Cutoff from passband spec | Use after $N$ chosen |
| 8 | $\Omega_c = \Omega_s\big/\big(10^{A_s/10}-1\big)^{1/(2N)}$ | Cutoff from stopband spec | Use after $N$ chosen |
| 9 | $\alpha = -20\log_{10}\!\big[1+\big(\Omega/\Omega_c\big)^{2N}\big]^{1/2}$ | Attenuation in dB at freq $\Omega$ | Loss formula |
| 10 | $H(s) = \Omega_c^{N}/B_N(s)$ with $s \to s/\Omega_c$ | Denormalized transfer function | $\Omega_c \ne 1$ |

**Critical LaTeX note:** All absolute-value bars $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside tables have been written using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown parsing.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **biomedical signal processing** (your course!), Butterworth filters are the **workhorse** for:
- **ECG preprocessing** — removing $50/60\,\text{Hz}$ power-line interference with low-order ($N = 2$ or $3$) Butterworth lowpass filters.
- **EMG bandpass design** — extracting the $20$ – $500\,\text{Hz}$ muscle signal band.
- **EEG anti-aliasing** — front-end analog filters ahead of the ADC, where linear phase is not critical but flat passband IS critical (because you do not want to distort signal amplitude near the band edge).
- **Pacemaker input filters** — where a maximally flat response guarantees no spurious peaks that could trigger false detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Pole Equation to Polynomial Form

We start from the requirement that $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} = H(s)H(-s)$ must equal $1/(1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N})$.

**Step 1:** Write the design equation in $s$-domain by noting $\Omega^{2} = -s^{2}$ on $s = j\Omega$:

$$
H(s)\,H(-s) \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{-s^{2}}{\Omega_{c}^{2}}\right)^{N}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + (-1)^{N}\left(\dfrac{s}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2N}}
$$

**Step 2:** For the denominator to vanish (i.e., poles), set:

$$
1 + (-1)^{N}\left(\frac{s}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2N} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \left(\frac{s}{\Omega_{c}}\right)^{2N} \;=\; (-1)^{N+1}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the $2N$-th roots of $(-1)^{N+1}$. Since $(-1)^{N+1} = e^{j\pi(N+1)}$, the roots are:

$$
\frac{s_{k}}{\Omega_{c}} \;=\; e^{\,j\,\pi(N+1)/(2N) \,\cdot\, k'} \quad \text{where } k' \in \{1, 3, 5, \ldots, 4N-1\}
$$

**Step 4:** Index-shifting to compact form (let $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N$):

$$
s_{k} \;=\; \Omega_{c}\,\exp\!\left(j\,\frac{(2k + N - 1)\,\pi}{2N}\right), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N
$$

This yields exactly $2N$ poles, symmetric about both axes. The LHP half gives a stable, causal $H(s)$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Design Equations from Specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU "Textbook-Type" Problem:** Given $A_p$, $\Omega_p$, $A_s$, $\Omega_s$, find $N$ and $\Omega_c$. This is the most frequently asked question (worth 7–14 marks).

**Step 1:** Write the passband constraint at $\Omega = \Omega_p$:

$$
\frac{1}{1+\left(\dfrac{\Omega_p}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}} \;\le\; 10^{-A_p/10}
$$

Rearranging (assuming equality, since we want the tightest design):

$$
\left(\frac{\Omega_p}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} \;=\; 10^{A_p/10} - 1
$$

**Step 2:** Write the stopband constraint at $\Omega = \Omega_s$:

$$
\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} \;=\; 10^{A_s/10} - 1
$$

**Step 3:** Divide stopband equation by passband equation to eliminate $\Omega_c$:

$$
\frac{(\Omega_s/\Omega_c)^{2N}}{(\Omega_p/\Omega_c)^{2N}} \;=\; \left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)^{2N} \;=\; \frac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}
$$

**Step 4:** Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides and solve for $N$:

$$
2N\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right) \;=\; \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}\right]
$$

$$
N \;\ge\; \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}\right]}{2\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}
$$

**Step 5:** Round $N$ **up** to the next integer (this is mandatory — smaller $N$ fails specs).

**Step 6:** Substitute the chosen $N$ back into either constraint to find $\Omega_c$. The standard convention is to use the **passband** equation, ensuring the response JUST meets passband spec:

$$
\Omega_c \;=\; \frac{\Omega_p}{\left(10^{A_p/10} - 1\right)^{1/(2N)}}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style, 14-mark template)

**Problem:** Design a Butterworth lowpass filter with passband edge $\Omega_p = 1000\,\text{rad/s}$ at $A_p = 3\,\text{dB}$, and stopband edge $\Omega_s = 2000\,\text{rad/s}$ at $A_s = 30\,\text{dB}$. Find $N$, $\Omega_c$, and $H(s)$.

**Step 1 — Compute $N$:**

$$
N \;\ge\; \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{3.0} - 1}{10^{0.3} - 1}\right]}{2\,\log_{10}(2.0)}
$$

Intermediate arithmetic:

$$
10^{3.0} - 1 = 999 \quad\text{and}\quad 10^{0.3} - 1 = 0.9953 \approx 1
$$

Therefore numerator $\log_{10}(999/0.9953) \approx \log_{10}(1003.7) \approx 3.0017$.

Denominator $= 2\,\log_{10}(2) = 2 \times 0.3010 = 0.6021$.

$$
N \;\ge\; \frac{3.0017}{0.6021} \;\approx\; 4.985
$$

**Step 2 — Choose $N = 5$** (round up).

**Step 3 — Compute $\Omega_c$ from the passband equation:**

$$
\Omega_c \;=\; \frac{1000}{(10^{0.3} - 1)^{1/10}} \;=\; \frac{1000}{(0.9953)^{0.1}}
$$

$$
(0.9953)^{0.1} \;\approx\; e^{0.1\,\ln(0.9953)} \;\approx\; e^{0.1 \times (-0.00471)} \;\approx\; e^{-0.000471} \;\approx\; 0.99953
$$

$$
\Omega_c \;\approx\; \frac{1000}{0.99953} \;\approx\; 1000.47\,\text{rad/s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Because we chose $A_p = 3\,\text{dB}$ (the natural Butterworth attenuation), the cutoff $\Omega_c$ is essentially equal to $\Omega_p$. This is a coincidence of the Butterworth definition.

**Step 4 — Form the normalized polynomial:**

From the cheat-sheet, $B_5(s) = s^{5} + 3.2361\,s^{4} + 5.2361\,s^{3} + 5.2361\,s^{2} + 3.2361\,s + 1$.

**Step 5 — Denormalize by substituting $s \to s/\Omega_c$:**

$$
H(s) \;=\; \frac{\Omega_c^{5}}{B_5\!\left(\dfrac{s}{\Omega_c}\right)}
$$

$$
H(s) \;=\; \frac{(1000.47)^{5}}{s^{5} + 3.2361\,\Omega_c\,s^{4} + 5.2361\,\Omega_c^{2}\,s^{3} + 5.2361\,\Omega_c^{3}\,s^{2} + 3.2361\,\Omega_c^{4}\,s + \Omega_c^{5}}
$$

With $\Omega_c \approx 1000.47$, the coefficients become very large — confirming that the filter is **practical only after frequency and impedance scaling**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Decision Flow

| Design Stage | Input Parameters | Output / Decision | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Gather specs | $\Omega_p, A_p, \Omega_s, A_s$ | — | Mixing rad/s and Hz |
| 2. Compute $N$ | Specs above | $N \ge \ldots$ (round up) | Forgetting to round up |
| 3. Compute $\Omega_c$ | $N$ + passband or stopband eqn | $\Omega_c$ in rad/s | Using wrong eqn (pass vs. stop) |
| 4. Look up $B_N(s)$ | $N$ | Polynomial coefficients | Wrong polynomial order |
| 5. Denormalize | $\Omega_c$ | $H(s)$ in physical units | Skipping $s \to s/\Omega_c$ |
| 6. Verify specs | $H(j\Omega)$ at $\Omega_p, \Omega_s$ | $A_p, A_s$ must be met | Not re-checking after rounding $N$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Butterworth Filter Design Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT Specifications] --> B[Passband edge Op rad per sec]
    A --> C[Passband ripple Ap dB]
    A --> D[Stopband edge Os rad per sec]
    A --> E[Stopband attenuation As dB]
    B --> F[Compute Order N]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{N integer check}
    G -->|Round up| H[Compute Cutoff Oc]
    H --> I[Look up Normalized B N of s]
    I --> J[Denormalize s to s by Oc]
    J --> K[Transfer Function H of s]
    K --> L[Frequency Scaling]
    L --> M[Impedance Scaling optional]
    M --> N[OUTPUT Final Realizable Filter]
```

## 4.2 Pole Distribution Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Splane["s-PLANE (Left Half Highlighted)"]
        P1((Pole 1))
        P2((Pole 2))
        P3((Pole 3))
        P4((Pole 4))
        P5((Pole 5))
        P6((Pole 6))
        P7((Pole 7))
        P8((Pole 8))
    end
    P1 -.LHP.-> P2
    P2 -.LHP.-> P3
    P3 -.LHP.-> P4
    P5 -.RHP discarded.-> P6
    P6 -.RHP discarded.-> P7
    P7 -.RHP discarded.-> P8
```

## 4.3 Magnitude Response Curve (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Omega = 0: Gain = 1 0 dB"] --> B["Flat maximally flat region passband"]
    B --> C["Omega = Oc: Gain = 1 by sqrt 2 minus 3 dB"]
    C --> D["Monotonic roll off stopband"]
    D --> E["Asymptotic slope minus 20N dB per decade"]
```

## 4.4 Design Algorithm — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InputSpecs
    InputSpecs --> ComputeN: Use design equations
    ComputeN --> RoundN: Round up to next integer
    RoundN --> ComputeCutoff: Pick passband or stopband eqn
    ComputeCutoff --> SelectPoles: LHP poles only on circle of radius Oc
    SelectPoles --> FormPolynomial: Multiply factors s minus sk
    FormPolynomial --> Denormalize: Substitute s by s by Oc
    Denormalize --> Validate: Check Ap and As met
    Validate --> [*]: Design Complete
    Validate --> IncrementN: Reject if specs fail
    IncrementN --> ComputeCutoff
```

## 4.5 Why Block-Diagram View (Not Physical Drawing)

The Mermaid diagrams above deliberately avoid physical circuit schematics because Butterworth approximation is fundamentally a **mathematical design method**, not a hardware topology. The blocks shown map the **information flow** during design — exactly what KTU examiners expect a student to demonstrate in the "design procedure" question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**
*State any three properties of the Butterworth filter approximation.*

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Maximally flat magnitude response** at $\Omega = 0$: the first $2N-1$ derivatives of $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert$ vanish at the origin.
> 2. **Monotonic** response in both passband and stopband, with **no ripples**.
> 3. The **$-3\,\text{dB}$ cutoff frequency $\Omega_c$** is independent of the filter order $N$.
> 4. The transfer function is **all-pole** (no finite zeros), and the poles lie on a **circle of radius $\Omega_c$** uniformly distributed in the $s$-plane.
> 5. As $N \to \infty$, the response approaches the ideal brick-wall characteristic.

*(Any three properties fetch full 3 marks.)*

### Question A2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Define the term "maximally flat magnitude response" with respect to Butterworth filters.*

**Model Answer:**
A magnitude response $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert$ is said to be **maximally flat** at $\Omega = 0$ if its Taylor-series expansion about the origin has the form:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega)\vert \;=\; 1 - a_N\,\Omega^{2N} + a_{N+1}\,\Omega^{2N+2} - \cdots
$$

That is, **all coefficients of $\Omega^{2}, \Omega^{4}, \ldots, \Omega^{2N-2}$ are zero**. For a Butterworth filter of order $N$, the first $2N-1$ derivatives of $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2}$ evaluated at $\Omega = 0$ vanish, ensuring the **smoothest possible** passband without any ripple.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the magnitude-squared response of an $N^{\text{th}}$-order Butterworth lowpass filter starting from the maximally flat condition. Show that the poles lie on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the $s$-plane. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Butterworth lowpass filter satisfies the following specifications:
- Passband edge: $\Omega_p = 2\,\text{k rad/s}$, max ripple $A_p = 1\,\text{dB}$.
- Stopband edge: $\Omega_s = 8\,\text{k rad/s}$, min attenuation $A_s = 40\,\text{dB}$.
Determine the filter order $N$ and the $-3\,\text{dB}$ cutoff $\Omega_c$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Maximally flat condition:**
The most general all-pole magnitude-squared response is:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + a_2\,\Omega^{2} + a_4\,\Omega^{4} + \cdots + a_{2N}\,\Omega^{2N}}
$$

Imposing the maximally flat condition requires $a_2 = a_4 = \cdots = a_{2N-2} = 0$, leaving only $a_{2N}$:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + a_{2N}\,\Omega^{2N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the cutoff:**
At $\Omega = \Omega_c$, the gain must drop by $-3\,\text{dB}$, so $\vert H(j\Omega_c)\vert^{2} = 1/2$. Substituting:

$$
\frac{1}{1 + a_{2N}\,\Omega_c^{2N}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a_{2N} \;=\; \frac{1}{\Omega_c^{2N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Canonical form:**

$$
\boxed{\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Pole locations:**
Pole equation: $1 + (-s^{2}/\Omega_c^{2})^{N} = 0 \Rightarrow s^{2N} = (-1)^{N+1}\Omega_c^{2N}$.

Taking $2N$-th roots:

$$
s_{k} \;=\; \Omega_c\,\exp\!\left(j\,\frac{(2k+N-1)\,\pi}{2N}\right), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, 2N
$$

**Step 5 — Magnitudes:**
Since $\vert \exp(j\theta)\vert = 1$, every pole lies at distance $\vert s_k\vert = \Omega_c$ from the origin, confirming **all $2N$ poles lie on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$**. The LHP poles (those with $\Re(s_k) < 0$) are selected for stability.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $N$:**

$$
N \;\ge\; \frac{\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{10^{40/10} - 1}{10^{1/10} - 1}\right]}{2\,\log_{10}(8/2)}
$$

Numerator: $10^{4} - 1 = 9999$ and $10^{0.1} - 1 = 0.25893$.

Ratio $= 9999/0.25893 = 38{,}619.6$.

$\log_{10}(38{,}619.6) = 4.5870$.

Denominator: $2\,\log_{10}(4) = 2 \times 0.6021 = 1.2041$.

$$
N \;\ge\; \frac{4.5870}{1.2041} \;\approx\; 3.810
$$

**Step 2 — Choose $N = 4$.**

**Step 3 — Compute $\Omega_c$ from the passband equation:**

$$
\Omega_c \;=\; \frac{\Omega_p}{(10^{A_p/10} - 1)^{1/(2N)}} \;=\; \frac{2000}{(0.25893)^{1/8}}
$$

Compute $(0.25893)^{1/8}$:

$$
\ln(0.25893) = -1.3511 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{-1.3511}{8} = -0.1689 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{-0.1689} = 0.8448
$$

$$
\Omega_c \;\approx\; \frac{2000}{0.8448} \;\approx\; 2367.4\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Final answers:** $\boxed{N = 4}$, $\boxed{\Omega_c \approx 2367\,\text{rad/s}}$.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of the $-3\,\text{dB}$ point in a Butterworth filter. Why does this point occur at $\Omega = \Omega_c$ independent of the order $N$? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 3rd-order normalized Butterworth filter ($N = 3$, $\Omega_c = 1\,\text{rad/s}$), determine the transfer function $H(s)$. Hence compute the magnitude response at $\Omega = 1$, $2$, and $5\,\text{rad/s}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The $-3\,\text{dB}$ point is the frequency at which the power gain drops to **half** its DC value:

$$
10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \;=\; -3.0103\,\text{dB} \;\approx\; -3\,\text{dB}
$$

For a Butterworth filter:

$$
\vert H(j\Omega_c)\vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + (\Omega_c/\Omega_c)^{2N}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1+1} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}
$$

Notice that **$N$ cancels out** — the $-3\,\text{dB}$ point is **always** at $\Omega = \Omega_c$ for any $N$. This is the defining feature that distinguishes Butterworth from Chebyshev (where the cutoff depends on the ripple spec).

**Significance:**
1. It serves as a **universal reference** for comparing filters of different orders.
2. It allows the designer to specify $\Omega_c$ directly from the desired signal bandwidth.
3. It is the **point of half-power**, important in biomedical instrumentation where signal energy retention matters.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Poles for $N = 3$, $\Omega_c = 1$:**

$$
s_k = \exp\!\left(j\,\frac{(2k+2)\,\pi}{6}\right), \quad k = 1, 2, 3
$$

Compute angles:

$$
\theta_1 = \frac{3\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2}, \quad \theta_2 = \frac{4\pi}{6} = \frac{2\pi}{3}, \quad \theta_3 = \frac{5\pi}{6}
$$

Wait — for $k=1$: $(2(1)+3-1)\pi/(2 \cdot 3) = 4\pi/6 = 2\pi/3$, $(2(2)+2)\pi/6 = 6\pi/6 = \pi$, $(2(3)+2)\pi/6 = 8\pi/6 = 4\pi/3$. Poles:

$$
s_1 = e^{\,j\,2\pi/3} = -0.5 + j\,0.866, \quad s_2 = e^{\,j\,\pi} = -1, \quad s_3 = e^{\,j\,4\pi/3} = -0.5 - j\,0.866
$$

All three are in the LHP. ✓

**Step 2 — Form the polynomial:**

$$
B_3(s) = (s+1)(s^{2} + s + 1) = s^{3} + 2\,s^{2} + 2\,s + 1
$$

(matches the cheat-sheet)

**Step 3 — Transfer function:**

$$
H(s) \;=\; \frac{1}{s^{3} + 2\,s^{2} + 2\,s + 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude at requested frequencies:**

General formula: $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert = 1\big/\sqrt{1 + \Omega^{6}}$.

- At $\Omega = 1$: $\vert H\vert = 1/\sqrt{2} = 0.7071$ — i.e. $-3\,\text{dB}$. ✓
- At $\Omega = 2$: $\vert H\vert = 1/\sqrt{1 + 64} = 1/\sqrt{65} = 0.1240$ — i.e. $-18.13\,\text{dB}$.
- At $\Omega = 5$: $\vert H\vert = 1/\sqrt{1 + 15625} = 1/\sqrt{15626} = 0.00800$ — i.e. $-41.94\,\text{dB}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (where students lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to round $N$ UP** — using $N = 3$ when $N = 3.8$ was computed leads to failed specs. Always round up.
> 2. **Mixing $\Omega$ (rad/s) with $f$ (Hz)** — KTU specs are usually in rad/s; using $f$ without converting ($\Omega = 2\pi f$) is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Using the wrong cutoff equation** — passband equation vs. stopband equation give different $\Omega_c$. Convention: choose whichever yields a higher $\Omega_c$ (tighter spec).
> 4. **Skipping the denormalization step** — writing $H(s) = 1/B_N(s)$ when the actual cutoff is not $1$ rad/s loses full 7 marks in part (b).
> 5. **Including RHP poles** in the polynomial — Butterworth is stable ONLY with LHP poles.
> 6. **Confusing $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2}$ with $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert$** — the formula uses the SQUARE, and forgetting the square root loses 1 mark on numerical answers.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist before the exam — 60-second revision.**

- ✅ Butterworth = **maximally flat, monotonic, all-pole**.
- ✅ Canonical formula: $\vert H(j\Omega)\vert^{2} = 1/(1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N})$.
- ✅ $\Omega_c$ is always the $-3\,\text{dB}$ point — independent of $N$.
- ✅ $2N$ poles on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$; keep LHP $N$ poles only.
- ✅ Poles: $s_k = \Omega_c \exp(j\pi(2k+N-1)/(2N))$, $k = 1, \ldots, N$.
- ✅ Order formula: $N \ge \log_{10}[(10^{A_s/10}-1)/(10^{A_p/10}-1)] / [2\log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)]$ — round **up**.
- ✅ $\Omega_c$ from passband: $\Omega_p/(10^{A_p/10}-1)^{1/(2N)}$.
- ✅ $\Omega_c$ from stopband: $\Omega_s/(10^{A_s/10}-1)^{1/(2N)}$.
- ✅ Denormalize: replace $s \to s/\Omega_c$ in the normalized $B_N(s)$.
- ✅ Memorize polynomials up to $N = 5$.
- ✅ Beyond $-3\,\text{dB}$, the roll-off is $-20N\,\text{dB/decade}$.
- ✅ Use Butterworth when you need **flat amplitude** (e.g., ECG/EEG pre-filters).
- ✅ **Do NOT** use Butterworth when you need linear phase (then use Bessel).
- ✅ **Do NOT** use Butterworth when you need the steepest roll-off (then use Elliptic).

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — BUTTERWORTH APPROXIMATION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Transformations (Analog and Digital Domain)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Transformations (Analog and Digital Domain)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Transformation** is a systematic mathematical procedure that maps the complex frequency variable of a **normalized lowpass prototype filter** ($H_{LP}(s)$ with cutoff $\Omega = 1$ rad/s) into a new complex frequency variable, producing a **different filter class** (lowpass with new cutoff, highpass, bandpass, or bandstop) without re-computing the entire filter approximation.

Mathematically, a frequency transformation is a **substitution of the form**:

$$s \longrightarrow g(s) \quad \text{or} \quad z^{-1} \longrightarrow G(z^{-1})$$

where $g(\cdot)$ is a **rational function of $s$** (in the analog domain) and $G(\cdot)$ is an **all-pass function of $z^{-1}$** (in the digital domain).

### Classification by Domain

| Domain | Transformation Type | Variable Substitution |
|:------:|:------------------:|:--------------------:|
| Analog (s-plane) | LP → LP, LP → HP, LP → BP, LP → BS | $s \rightarrow g(s)$ |
| Digital (z-plane) | LP → LP, LP → HP, LP → BP, LP → BS | $z^{-1} \rightarrow G(z^{-1})$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 Highlight**: The syllabus explicitly requires both *analog* transformations (used when designing IIR filters via the analog prototype method) and *digital* transformations (used for transforming a digital prototype directly in the z-plane).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Substitution" Intuition

Imagine you have perfected a **vanilla cake recipe** (the normalized lowpass prototype, $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/s). Now you want to make:

- **A lemon cake** (highpass — different frequency flavor)
- **A chocolate–vanilla layered cake** (bandpass — two frequency bands)
- **A cake with no chocolate layer** (bandstop — reject a band)

You do **not** start from scratch. Instead, you **transform the original recipe** by substituting ingredients while preserving the **ratio of sweetness, texture, and structure**. This is exactly what frequency transformation does to a filter: it preserves the **filter shape (ripple, order, roll-off slope)** but changes **where in the frequency axis** the action happens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we need it in Biomedical Signal Processing (BSP)**:
> - **ECG** → requires **bandpass** (0.5–40 Hz) to remove baseline wander and high-frequency noise.
> - **EEG** → requires **bandpass** (0.5–100 Hz) for cortical rhythms (alpha, beta, delta).
> - **EMG** → requires **highpass** (>20 Hz) to suppress motion artifact.
> - **Pacemaker sense amplifier** → requires **bandpass** (typically 30–100 Hz).
>
> All of these can be derived by transforming a single prototype lowpass filter!

---

## 1.3 The Two Foundational Strategies in IIR Filter Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before studying transformations, recall the two strategies used in IIR design:
>
> **Strategy A — Analog Prototype + Bilinear Transformation**:
> $$\text{Analog Prototype } H_{LP}(s) \xrightarrow{\text{Analog Freq. Trans.}} H_{\text{desired}}(s) \xrightarrow{\text{Bilinear}} H(z)$$
>
> **Strategy B — Digital Prototype + Digital Frequency Transformation**:
> $$\text{Digital Prototype } H_{LP}(z) \xrightarrow{\text{Digital Freq. Trans.}} H_{\text{desired}}(z)$$
>
> Both strategies are examinable under KTU 2024 Module 3.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Nyquist Frequency**: $\omega_N = \pi$ rad/sample (digital)
- **Normalized analog cutoff**: $\Omega_0 = 1$ rad/s (prototype)
- **Pre-warping constant** (for bilinear): $\Omega_a = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$
- **3 dB cutoff**: frequency at which magnitude response falls to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of passband
- **Group delay**: derivative of phase w.r.t. frequency, preserved under transformation (in most cases)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analog Frequency Transformations — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting point**: A normalized analog lowpass prototype $H_{LP}(s)$ with passband edge $\Omega_p = 1$ rad/s. We substitute $s$ with a rational function of $s$ to obtain the new filter.

### 2.1.1 Lowpass → Lowpass Transformation (LP → LP)

**Goal**: Change cutoff from $1$ rad/s to $\Omega_u$ (desired upper cutoff).

**Substitution rule**:
$$s \longrightarrow \frac{s}{\Omega_u}$$

**Why it works**: The new filter $H(s) = H_{LP}(s/\Omega_u)$ has magnitude $|H(j\Omega)| = |H_{LP}(j\Omega/\Omega_u)|$. When $\Omega = \Omega_u$, the argument becomes $1$, matching the original prototype's cutoff.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility**: Used to scale a Butterworth/Chebyshev/elliptic design to any cutoff in Hz, e.g., designing a 40 Hz ECG lowpass filter from a 1 rad/s prototype.

---

### 2.1.2 Lowpass → Highpass Transformation (LP → HP)

**Goal**: Create a highpass filter with passband edge $\Omega_l$ (lower cutoff of HP = the boundary where signal starts to pass).

**Substitution rule**:
$$s \longrightarrow \frac{\Omega_l}{s}$$

**Why it works**: At $s = j\Omega_l$, the substitution yields $j\Omega_l / (j\Omega_l) = j1$, matching prototype cutoff. As $\Omega \to 0$, $|s| \to \infty$, so $|H_{LP}| \to 0$ (blocked). As $\Omega \to \infty$, $|s| \to 0$, so $|H_{LP}| \to 1$ (passed). This is the inverse behavior of a highpass filter.

---

### 2.1.3 Lowpass → Bandpass Transformation (LP → BP)

**Goal**: Create a bandpass filter with lower cutoff $\Omega_l$ and upper cutoff $\Omega_u$ (where $0 < \Omega_l < \Omega_u$).

**Substitution rule**:
$$s \longrightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$$

**Why it works**: The substitution converts a single prototype pole/zero into a **pair** of poles/zeros, one above and one below the **geometric center** $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$. At $s = j\Omega_0$, the numerator becomes $-\Omega_0^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u = 0$, giving $s_{new} = 0$ → $|H_{LP}(0)| = 1$ (passband center). At $s = j\Omega_l$ or $s = j\Omega_u$, $s_{new} = \pm j1$ → cutoff.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical application**: Designing the 0.5–40 Hz ECG bandpass amplifier. Here $\Omega_l = 2\pi(0.5)$, $\Omega_u = 2\pi(40)$.

---

### 2.1.4 Lowpass → Bandstop (Band-Reject) Transformation (LP → BS)

**Goal**: Create a bandstop filter that rejects the band $[\Omega_l, \Omega_u]$.

**Substitution rule**:
$$s \longrightarrow \frac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$$

**Why it works**: Reciprocal of the BP transformation. At $s = j\Omega_0$, denominator vanishes → $s_{new} \to \infty$ → $|H_{LP}(\infty)| = 0$ (rejection). At $s = 0$ or $s \to \infty$, $s_{new} \to 0$ → $|H_{LP}(0)| = 1$ (passband).

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical application**: **50/60 Hz notch filter** for powerline interference rejection in ECG/EEG recording systems. The 50 Hz bandstop is essential because mains interference contaminates biopotential recordings.

---

## 2.2 Digital Frequency Transformations — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Digital transformations are **all-pass functions** $G(z^{-1})$ such that $|G(e^{-j\omega})| = 1$ for all $\omega$. This guarantees that the magnitude response shape is preserved.

The general substitution form is:
$$z^{-1} \longrightarrow G(z^{-1})$$

For **every prototype pole at $z = p_k$**, we get $N$ new poles at the roots of $G(z^{-1}) = p_k$, where $N$ is the order of $G$. Similarly for zeros.

### 2.2.1 Digital LP → LP Transformation

$$z^{-1} \longrightarrow \frac{z^{-1} - \alpha}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}}$$

where:
$$\alpha = \frac{\sin\left[\frac{\omega_c - \theta_c}{2}\right]}{\sin\left[\frac{\omega_c + \theta_c}{2}\right]}$$

- $\omega_c$ = original prototype cutoff
- $\theta_c$ = desired new cutoff
- $|\alpha| < 1$ ensures stability

### 2.2.2 Digital LP → HP Transformation

$$z^{-1} \longrightarrow -\frac{z^{-1} + \alpha}{1 + \alpha z^{-1}}$$

where $\alpha$ is the same as above.

### 2.2.3 Digital LP → BP Transformation

$$z^{-1} \longrightarrow -\frac{z^{-2} - \frac{2\alpha\beta}{\beta+1} z^{-1} + \frac{\beta-1}{\beta+1}}{\frac{\beta-1}{\beta+1} z^{-2} - \frac{2\alpha\beta}{\beta+1} z^{-1} + 1}$$

with parameters:
$$\alpha = \frac{\cos\left[\frac{\omega_l + \omega_u}{2}\right]}{\cos\left[\frac{\omega_l - \omega_u}{2}\right]}, \quad \beta = \cot\left[\frac{\omega_u - \omega_l}{2}\right] \tan\left[\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right]$$

### 2.2.4 Digital LP → BS Transformation

$$z^{-1} \longrightarrow \frac{z^{-2} - \frac{2\alpha}{\beta+1} z^{-1} + \frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}}{\frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta} z^{-2} - \frac{2\alpha}{\beta+1} z^{-1} + 1}$$

with:
$$\alpha = \frac{\cos\left[\frac{\omega_l + \omega_u}{2}\right]}{\cos\left[\frac{\omega_l - \omega_u}{2}\right]}, \quad \beta = \tan\left[\frac{\omega_u - \omega_l}{2}\right] \tan\left[\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right]$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete KTU 2024 Module 3 frequency transformation reference** — memorize before attempting any exam problem.

### Analog Domain Transformations

| Transformation | Substitution $s \rightarrow$ | Parameters | Application in BSP |
|:--------------:|:----------------------------:|:----------:|:------------------:|
| **LP $\rightarrow$ LP** | $\dfrac{s}{\Omega_u}$ | $\Omega_u$ = desired cutoff | Anti-alias lowpass |
| **LP $\rightarrow$ HP** | $\dfrac{\Omega_l}{s}$ | $\Omega_l$ = HP cutoff | EMG highpass (motion artifact removal) |
| **LP $\rightarrow$ BP** | $\dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$ | $\Omega_l, \Omega_u$ = band edges | ECG bandpass (0.5–40 Hz) |
| **LP $\rightarrow$ BS** | $\dfrac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$ | $\Omega_l, \Omega_u$ = stop band edges | 50 Hz powerline notch |

### Digital Domain Transformations (applied to $z^{-1}$)

| Transformation | All-Pass Substitution $G(z^{-1})$ | Key Parameter | Notes |
|:--------------:|:----------------------------------:|:-------------:|:-----:|
| **LP $\rightarrow$ LP** | $\dfrac{z^{-1} - \alpha}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}}$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{\sin[(\omega_c - \theta_c)/2]}{\sin[(\omega_c + \theta_c)/2]}$ | Order preserved |
| **LP $\rightarrow$ HP** | $-\dfrac{z^{-1} + \alpha}{1 + \alpha z^{-1}}$ | $\alpha$ as above | Order preserved |
| **LP $\rightarrow$ BP** | Second-order all-pass (see §2.2.3) | $\alpha, \beta$ given | Order **doubles** |
| **LP $\rightarrow$ BS** | Second-order all-pass (see §2.2.4) | $\alpha, \beta$ given | Order **doubles** |

### Order Multiplication Rule (Critical for KTU)

> [!WARNING]
> When applying BP or BS transformation, the **filter order doubles**. If the prototype LP is order $N$, the resulting BP/BS filter will have order $2N$. This is because each prototype pole generates a **conjugate pair** in the new filter.

### Constraints (Boundary Conditions)

| Constraint | Analog | Digital |
|:----------:|:------:|:-------:|
| Stability | $\text{Re}(p) < 0$ for all poles $p$ of $H(s)$ | $\vert z \vert < 1$ for all poles of $H(z)$ |
| Passband | Magnitude ripple in $[1-\delta_p, 1]$ | Same |
| Prototype normalization | $\Omega_p = 1$ rad/s | $\omega_c$ = arbitrary (rad/sample) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility Summary

| Biomedical Signal | Required Filter | Transformation |
|:-----------------:|:---------------:|:--------------:|
| **ECG** | BP (0.5–40 Hz) | LP $\rightarrow$ BP |
| **EEG** | BP (0.5–100 Hz) | LP $\rightarrow$ BP |
| **EMG** | HP (>20 Hz) | LP $\rightarrow$ HP |
| **Pacemaker sense** | BP (30–100 Hz) | LP $\rightarrow$ BP |
| **Mains interference** | BS (49–51 Hz) | LP $\rightarrow$ BS |
| **Anti-aliasing** | LP (≤0.45 $f_s$) | LP $\rightarrow$ LP |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Analog LP → BP Transformation

**Objective**: Show mathematically why $s \rightarrow \dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$ converts a lowpass to a bandpass.

**Starting assumption**: A prototype lowpass with passband $[-1, +1]$ rad/s (normalized).

**Step 1 — Define band edges**: Let the desired bandpass have edges at $\Omega_l$ (lower) and $\Omega_u$ (upper), with $\Omega_l < \Omega_u$.

**Step 2 — Define bandwidth and center**:
$$B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l \quad \text{(bandwidth)}$$
$$\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \cdot \Omega_u} \quad \text{(geometric center frequency)}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency mapping requirement**: We need a transformation such that:
- $\Omega = \Omega_l \longrightarrow$ prototype variable $= -1$
- $\Omega = \Omega_u \longrightarrow$ prototype variable $= +1$
- $\Omega = \Omega_0 \longrightarrow$ prototype variable $= 0$

**Step 4 — The candidate rational function** (must be ratio of quadratics to map $\Omega$ axis correctly):
$$s_{new}(s) = \frac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{s \cdot B}$$

**Step 5 — Verification at $\Omega_l$**:
$$s_{new}(j\Omega_l) = \frac{-\Omega_l^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{j\Omega_l (\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = \frac{\Omega_l(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{j\Omega_l(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = \frac{1}{j} = -j1$$

**Step 6 — Verification at $\Omega_u$**:
$$s_{new}(j\Omega_u) = \frac{-\Omega_u^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{j\Omega_u(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = \frac{-\Omega_u(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{j\Omega_u(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = -\frac{1}{j} = +j1$$

**Step 7 — Verification at $\Omega_0$**:
$$s_{new}(j\Omega_0) = \frac{-\Omega_0^2 + \Omega_0^2}{j\Omega_0(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = 0$$

All three conditions are satisfied. The transformation is therefore:
$$\boxed{\;s \longrightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Analog LP → BS Transformation

**Objective**: Derive the substitution $s \rightarrow \dfrac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$.

**Step 1 — Use the LP-to-BP relationship**: If $g_{BP}(s)$ maps LP to BP, then the inverse map $1/g_{BP}(s)$ maps LP to BS (because reversing the swap of passband and stopband interchanges BP and BS).

**Step 2 — Take the reciprocal of the LP → BP substitution**:
$$g_{BP}(s) = \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$$

$$g_{BS}(s) = \frac{1}{g_{BP}(s)} = \frac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$$

**Step 3 — Verify at $\Omega_0$**:
$$g_{BS}(j\Omega_0) = \frac{j\Omega_0(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{-\Omega_0^2 + \Omega_0^2} = \infty$$

This sends the prototype variable to infinity, corresponding to the **stop band** of the BS filter — which is exactly what we want at the center frequency.

**Step 4 — Verify at $\Omega = 0$**:
$$g_{BS}(0) = \frac{0}{0 + \Omega_l \Omega_u} = 0$$

Maps prototype variable to 0 → prototype's passband center → passes DC. Correct for BS.

**Step 5 — Verify at $\Omega \to \infty$**:
$$g_{BS}(j\infty) = \frac{j\infty \cdot B}{-\infty} \to 0$$

Also passes high frequencies. Correct.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — ECG Bandpass via Analog LP→BP

**Problem**: Design a 2nd-order Butterworth bandpass filter for ECG with lower cutoff $f_l = 0.5$ Hz and upper cutoff $f_u = 40$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Convert to rad/s**:
$$\Omega_l = 2\pi (0.5) = \pi \approx 3.1416 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\Omega_u = 2\pi (40) = 80\pi \approx 251.327 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute bandwidth and center**:
$$B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l = 251.327 - 3.1416 = 248.186 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u} = \sqrt{3.1416 \times 251.327} = \sqrt{789.57} \approx 28.10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Recall 2nd-order Butterworth lowpass prototype**:
$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Apply LP → BP substitution** $s \rightarrow \dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_0^2}{sB}$:

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\frac{s^2 + 789.57}{248.186\,s}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\frac{s^2 + 789.57}{248.186\,s}\right) + 1}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(248.186\,s)^2$**:

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{(248.186)^2 s^2}{(s^2 + 789.57)^2 + \sqrt{2}(248.186)s(s^2 + 789.57) + (248.186)^2 s^2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute constants**:
$$(248.186)^2 = 61596.28$$
$$\sqrt{2} \cdot 248.186 = 351.02$$

**Step 7 — Expand denominator**:
$$(s^2 + 789.57)^2 = s^4 + 1579.14\,s^2 + 623420.5$$
$$351.02\,s(s^2 + 789.57) = 351.02\,s^3 + 277161\,s$$
$$61596.28\,s^2$$

**Step 8 — Total denominator**:
$$s^4 + 351.02\,s^3 + (1579.14 + 61596.28)\,s^2 + 277161\,s + 623420.5$$
$$= s^4 + 351.02\,s^3 + 63175.42\,s^2 + 277161\,s + 623420.5$$

**Step 9 — Final transfer function**:
$$\boxed{\;H_{BP}(s) = \frac{61596.28\,s^2}{s^4 + 351.02\,s^3 + 63175.42\,s^2 + 277161\,s + 623420.5}\;}$$

**Step 10 — Verification**: Order doubled from $N=2$ to $N=4$ ✓ (as predicted by the order-multiplication rule).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Digital LP → LP Transformation

**Problem**: A 1st-order digital lowpass $H_{LP}(z) = \dfrac{0.5}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}}$ has cutoff at $\omega_c = \pi/3$. Transform it to a new cutoff $\theta_c = \pi/6$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$**:
$$\alpha = \frac{\sin\left[\frac{\pi/3 - \pi/6}{2}\right]}{\sin\left[\frac{\pi/3 + \pi/6}{2}\right]} = \frac{\sin(\pi/12)}{\sin(\pi/4)}$$

$$\sin(\pi/12) = \sin(15°) \approx 0.2588$$
$$\sin(\pi/4) = \sin(45°) \approx 0.7071$$

$$\alpha = \frac{0.2588}{0.7071} \approx 0.3660$$

**Step 2 — Apply substitution** $z^{-1} \rightarrow \dfrac{z^{-1} - \alpha}{1 - \alpha z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z^{-1} - 0.3660}{1 - 0.3660\,z^{-1}}$:

$$H_{LP_{new}}(z) = \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.5 \cdot \frac{z^{-1} - 0.3660}{1 - 0.3660\,z^{-1}}}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $1 - 0.3660\,z^{-1}$**:
$$H_{LP_{new}}(z) = \frac{0.5(1 - 0.3660\,z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.3660\,z^{-1}) - 0.5(z^{-1} - 0.3660\,z^{-1}\cdot z^{-1})}$$

Wait — careful expansion. The second term in denominator:
$$0.5 \cdot \frac{z^{-1} - 0.3660}{1 - 0.3660 z^{-1}} = \frac{0.5z^{-1} - 0.1830}{1 - 0.3660 z^{-1}}$$

So:
$$H_{LP_{new}}(z) = \frac{0.5}{1 - \frac{0.5z^{-1} - 0.1830}{1 - 0.3660 z^{-1}}}$$

$$= \frac{0.5(1 - 0.3660 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.3660 z^{-1}) - (0.5z^{-1} - 0.1830)}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify denominator**:
$$1 - 0.3660 z^{-1} - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.1830 = 1.1830 - 0.8660\,z^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Final result**:
$$\boxed{\;H_{LP_{new}}(z) = \frac{0.5(1 - 0.3660 z^{-1})}{1.1830 - 0.8660\,z^{-1}}\;}$$

Stability check: pole at $z = 0.8660/1.1830 \approx 0.7321$. Since $\vert 0.7321 \vert < 1$, the filter is stable ✓.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Transformation Flowchart (Analog Domain)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Normalized LP Prototype H_LP s, Omega equals 1"] --> B{"Choose Target Filter Type?"}
    B -->|"LP with new cutoff"| C["s replaced by s over Omega_u"]
    B -->|"Highpass filter"| D["s replaced by Omega_l over s"]
    B -->|"Bandpass filter"| E["s replaced by s squared plus Omega_l Omega_u over s times Omega_u minus Omega_l"]
    B -->|"Bandstop filter"| F["s replaced by s times Omega_u minus Omega_l over s squared plus Omega_l Omega_u"]
    C --> G["Optional: Apply Bilinear Transform to get H of z"]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Implement in Hardware or Software"]
    F --> H
    E --> H
    D --> H
    C --> H
```

## 4.2 Digital Domain Transformation Strategy Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Digital LP Prototype H_LP z"] --> B{"Compute alpha or alpha beta parameters"}
    B --> C1["LP to LP: z inv replaced by z inv minus alpha over 1 minus alpha z inv"]
    B --> C2["LP to HP: z inv replaced by minus z inv plus alpha over 1 plus alpha z inv"]
    B --> C3["LP to BP: second order all pass with alpha and beta"]
    B --> C4["LP to BS: second order all pass with alpha and beta"]
    C1 --> D["Pole order N preserved"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> E["Pole order doubles to 2N"]
    C4 --> E
    D --> F["Check stability: pole magnitude less than 1"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Biomedical Application: ECG, EEG, EMG, Notch"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: ECG Bandpass Realization

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Raw ECG Signal x of t, contaminated with baseline wander and 50 Hz noise"] --> B["Analog Front End: Instrumentation Amplifier"]
    B --> C["Analog Anti Alias LP at 200 Hz"]
    C --> D["ADC Sampling at 500 Hz"]
    D --> E["Digital LP Prototype 1st Order Butterworth Omega c equals 1 rad per s"]
    E --> F["Apply LP to BP Digital Transformation with alpha and beta parameters for 0.5 to 40 Hz"]
    F --> G["Filtered ECG y of n, clean diagnostic signal"]
    G --> H["Display on Monitor and QRS Detection Algorithm"]
```

## 4.4 Transformation Order Multiplication Visualization (BP Case)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Prototype LP: 1 pole at s equals minus 1"] --> B["Apply LP to BP substitution"]
    B --> C["Result BP: 2 poles, conjugate pair at s equals minus a plus or minus j b"]
    A2["Prototype LP: 2nd order, 2 poles"] --> B2["Apply LP to BP substitution"]
    B2 --> C2["Result BP: 4 poles, two conjugate pairs"]
    C --> D["Order doubled: N becomes 2N"]
    C2 --> D
```
