<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define frequency transformation in the context of IIR filter design. Why is it preferred over independent design for each filter type?**

**Model Answer**:
Frequency transformation is a mathematical substitution applied to a normalized lowpass prototype filter's transfer function to obtain other filter types (highpass, bandpass, bandstop) or lowpass with a different cutoff. The substitution is of the form $s \rightarrow g(s)$ (analog) or $z^{-1} \rightarrow G(z^{-1})$ (digital).

**Why preferred**:
1. Eliminates redundant filter approximation work (Butterworth, Chebyshev, etc., need to be computed only once).
2. Guarantees the new filter inherits the same **passband ripple, stopband attenuation, and order** as the prototype.
3. Reduces design complexity — only one approximation + four transformations.
4. In biomedical DSP, allows a single prototype to generate ECG, EEG, EMG, and notch filters systematically.

*(3 marks — KTU expects definition (1) + at least 2 advantages (1) + biomedical relevance (1))*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is the role of the all-pass property in digital frequency transformations?**

**Model Answer**:
A digital frequency transformation uses the substitution $z^{-1} \rightarrow G(z^{-1})$ where $G(z^{-1})$ is an **all-pass function**, i.e., $\vert G(e^{-j\omega}) \vert = 1$ for all $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$.

**Role**:
1. **Magnitude preservation**: The magnitude response shape of the prototype is preserved exactly (only the frequency axis is rescaled).
2. **Stability preservation**: If the prototype is stable ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$) and $G$ is a stable all-pass, the new filter is also stable.
3. **Phase linearity option**: All-pass functions allow additional control over the phase response, important in biomedical applications where waveform morphology (e.g., QRS complex) must be preserved.

*(3 marks — definition (1) + 2 properties with biomedical tie-in (2))*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Derive the analog frequency transformation that converts a normalized lowpass prototype filter with passband edge at 1 rad/s to a **bandpass filter** with lower cutoff $\Omega_l$ and upper cutoff $\Omega_u$. Show all three frequency-mapping verifications. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Starting from a 2nd-order normalized Butterworth lowpass prototype $H_{LP}(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,s + 1}$, design an analog bandpass filter for ECG acquisition with $f_l = 0.5$ Hz and $f_u = 40$ Hz. Write the final $H_{BP}(s)$. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a)**:

**Step 1 — Prototype and target definitions**:
- Prototype: passband $[-1, +1]$ rad/s.
- Target BP: passband $[\Omega_l, \Omega_u]$.

**Step 2 — Mapping requirements**:
- $\Omega = \Omega_l \rightarrow$ prototype variable $= -1$
- $\Omega = \Omega_u \rightarrow$ prototype variable $= +1$
- $\Omega = \Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u} \rightarrow$ prototype variable $= 0$

**Step 3 — Candidate transformation** (must be ratio of quadratics):
$$s_{new} = \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$$

**Step 4 — Verification at $\Omega_l$**:
$$s_{new}(j\Omega_l) = \frac{-\Omega_l^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{j\Omega_l (\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = \frac{\Omega_l(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{j\Omega_l(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = -j1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Verification at $\Omega_u$**:
$$s_{new}(j\Omega_u) = \frac{-\Omega_u^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{j\Omega_u(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = -\frac{\Omega_u(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{j\Omega_u(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = +j1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Verification at $\Omega_0$**:
$$s_{new}(j\Omega_0) = \frac{-\Omega_0^2 + \Omega_0^2}{j\Omega_0(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

All three verifications pass. The transformation is:
$$\boxed{\;s \longrightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}\;}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to (b)**:

**Step 1 — Convert Hz to rad/s**:
$$\Omega_l = 2\pi \cdot 0.5 = \pi, \quad \Omega_u = 2\pi \cdot 40 = 80\pi$$

**Step 2 — Compute $B$ and $\Omega_0$**:
$$B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l = 80\pi - \pi = 79\pi \approx 248.19 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\Omega_0^2 = \Omega_l \Omega_u = \pi \cdot 80\pi = 80\pi^2 \approx 789.57$$
$$\Omega_0 \approx 28.10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $H_{LP}(s)$**:
$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{1}{\left(\frac{s^2 + 789.57}{248.19\,s}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\frac{s^2 + 789.57}{248.19\,s}\right) + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(248.19\,s)^2 = 61598.2\,s^2$**:
$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{61598.2\,s^2}{(s^2 + 789.57)^2 + \sqrt{2}(248.19)s(s^2 + 789.57) + 61598.2\,s^2}$$

**Step 5 — Expand**:
$$(s^2 + 789.57)^2 = s^4 + 1579.14\,s^2 + 623420.5$$
$$351.0\,s(s^2 + 789.57) = 351.0\,s^3 + 277150\,s$$

**Step 6 — Sum the denominator**:
$$s^4 + 351.0\,s^3 + (1579.14 + 61598.2)\,s^2 + 277150\,s + 623420.5$$
$$= s^4 + 351.0\,s^3 + 63177.3\,s^2 + 277150\,s + 623420.5$$

**Step 7 — Final result**:
$$\boxed{\;H_{BP}(s) = \frac{61598.2\,s^2}{s^4 + 351.0\,s^3 + 63177.3\,s^2 + 277150\,s + 623420.5}\;}$$

**Step 8 — Validation**: Order doubled from $N=2$ to $N=4$ ✓.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, explain the **two strategies** for designing a digital IIR filter using frequency transformations. Compare their pre-warping and stability requirements. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A digital lowpass prototype with transfer function $H_{LP}(z) = \dfrac{0.3 + 0.2 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$ has cutoff at $\omega_c = \pi/4$. Apply the **digital LP → HP transformation** to obtain a new highpass filter with desired cutoff $\theta_c = \pi/6$. Verify the stability of the resulting filter. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to (a)**:

**Strategy A: Analog Prototype + Bilinear Transformation**

```
Normalised LP prototype H_LP(s) 
        ↓ Analog Frequency Transformation (LP→LP, HP, BP, BS)
Desired analog filter H(s)
        ↓ Bilinear Transform with pre-warping: Ω = (2/T) tan(ω/2)
Digital filter H(z)
```

**Strategy B: Digital Prototype + Digital Frequency Transformation**

```
Digital LP prototype H_LP(z)
        ↓ Digital Frequency Transformation: z⁻¹ → G(z⁻¹)
Desired digital filter H(z)
```

**Comparison Table**:

| Aspect | Strategy A (Analog) | Strategy B (Digital) |
|:------:|:-------------------:|:--------------------:|
| Pre-warping | **Required** ($\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$) | **Not required** |
| Order in BP/BS | Doubles (each pole → pair) | Doubles |
| Stability check | s-plane LHP | $\vert z \vert < 1$ |
| Frequency warping | Yes (bilinear) | No (exact) |
| Computational load | Higher (s + bilinear) | Lower (direct z) |
| Best for | Hardware analog + digital hybrid | Pure digital (DSP/microcontroller) |

**Pre-warping formula (Strategy A)**:
$$\Omega_a = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

This ensures that a critical frequency $\omega$ in the digital domain maps to the correct analog $\Omega_a$, eliminating frequency-axis distortion introduced by the bilinear transform.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b)**:

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$ for LP → HP**:
$$\alpha = \frac{\sin\left[\frac{\omega_c - \theta_c}{2}\right]}{\sin\left[\frac{\omega_c + \theta_c}{2}\right]} = \frac{\sin\left[\frac{\pi/4 - \pi/6}{2}\right]}{\sin\left[\frac{\pi/4 + \pi/6}{2}\right]}$$

Numerator angle: $\frac{\pi/4 - \pi/6}{2} = \frac{3\pi/12 - 2\pi/12}{2} = \frac{\pi/12}{2} = \frac{\pi}{24} = 7.5°$

Denominator angle: $\frac{\pi/4 + \pi/6}{2} = \frac{3\pi/12 + 2\pi/12}{2} = \frac{5\pi/12}{2} = \frac{5\pi}{24} = 37.5°$

$$\alpha = \frac{\sin(7.5°)}{\sin(37.5°)} = \frac{0.1305}{0.6088} \approx 0.2143$$

**Step 2 — Apply substitution** $z^{-1} \rightarrow -\dfrac{z^{-1} + \alpha}{1 + \alpha z^{-1}} = -\dfrac{z^{-1} + 0.2143}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}}$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $H_{LP}(z)$**:

$$H_{HP}(z) = \frac{0.3 + 0.2 \cdot \left(-\frac{z^{-1} + 0.2143}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}}\right)}{1 - 0.5 \cdot \left(-\frac{z^{-1} + 0.2143}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator**:
$$\text{Num} = 0.3 + 0.2 \cdot \left(-\frac{z^{-1} + 0.2143}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}}\right) = \frac{0.3(1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}) - 0.2(z^{-1} + 0.2143)}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}}$$

$$= \frac{0.3 + 0.0643 z^{-1} - 0.2 z^{-1} - 0.0429}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}} = \frac{0.2571 - 0.1357 z^{-1}}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Denominator**:
$$\text{Den} = 1 + 0.5 \cdot \frac{z^{-1} + 0.2143}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}} = \frac{(1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}) + 0.5(z^{-1} + 0.2143)}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}}$$

$$= \frac{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1} + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.1072}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}} = \frac{1.1072 + 0.7143 z^{-1}}{1 + 0.2143 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 6 — Final transfer function**:
$$H_{HP}(z) = \frac{0.2571 - 0.1357 z^{-1}}{1.1072 + 0.7143 z^{-1}}$$

Or, normalizing:
$$\boxed{\;H_{HP}(z) = \frac{0.2322 - 0.1225 z^{-1}}{1 + 0.6451 z^{-1}}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Stability check**: Pole at $z = -0.6451$. Since $\vert -0.6451 \vert = 0.6451 < 1$, the filter is **stable** ✓.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to square the substitution for BP/BS**: Some students write $H_{BP}(s) = H_{LP}(g(s))$ where $g(s) = \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$. The squared term (because $H_{LP}$ is in $s^2$ at the lowest order) must be expanded. **Mark loss: −2 to −3**.
>
> 2. **Not pre-warping analog frequencies before bilinear transform**: KTU specifically tests this. If you write "design at 40 Hz" without pre-warping, you **lose 4 marks** on a 14-mark question.
>
> 3. **Sign error in LP → HP digital transformation**: The minus sign in $G_{HP}(z^{-1}) = -\frac{z^{-1}+\alpha}{1+\alpha z^{-1}}$ is **critical**. Dropping it gives a lowpass, not a highpass. **Mark loss: −3**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting order doubling in BP/BS**: Writing "Butterworth order 2 gives a bandpass of order 2" is **wrong**. Order must be 4. Examiners check this. **Mark loss: −2**.
>
> 5. **Mixing analog and digital notations**: Writing $s$ inside $H(z)$ or using $H(s)$ after bilinear transform. **Mark loss: −2**.
>
> 6. **Not verifying stability**: A filter with pole magnitude $\geq 1$ receives **zero credit** for the final result in KTU 2024.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — KTU 2024 Module 3**

- ✅ Frequency transformation is **substitution of $s$ or $z^{-1}$** in a prototype filter.
- ✅ **Analog LP→LP**: $s \rightarrow s/\Omega_u$.
- ✅ **Analog LP→HP**: $s \rightarrow \Omega_l/s$.
- ✅ **Analog LP→BP**: $s \rightarrow (s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u)/[s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)]$.
- ✅ **Analog LP→BS**: $s \rightarrow s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)/(s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u)$.
- ✅ **Digital transformations use all-pass functions** $G(z^{-1})$ with $\vert G(e^{-j\omega}) \vert = 1$.
- ✅ **Digital LP→LP**: $\alpha = \sin[(\omega_c - \theta_c)/2]/\sin[(\omega_c + \theta_c)/2]$.
- ✅ **Digital LP→HP**: $G(z^{-1}) = -(z^{-1} + \alpha)/(1 + \alpha z^{-1})$.
- ✅ **Order rule**: BP and BS transformations **double the filter order** ($N \rightarrow 2N$).
- ✅ **Stability**: For analog, all poles in LHP; for digital, all poles inside unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$).
- ✅ **Biomedical applications**:
  - **ECG** → BP 0.5–40 Hz
  - **EEG** → BP 0.5–100 Hz
  - **EMG** → HP > 20 Hz
  - **Mains notch** → BS 49–51 Hz
- ✅ **Bilinear transform requires pre-warping**: $\Omega_a = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$ to avoid frequency-axis distortion.
- ✅ **Two design strategies** exist: Analog prototype + bilinear (Strategy A) and Digital prototype + digital transformation (Strategy B).
- ✅ **Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic** approximations all transform identically — only the prototype changes.
- ✅ **Q factor of bandpass**: $Q = \Omega_0 / B = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}/(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)$ — used to characterize selectivity.
- ✅ **Prototype normalization**: $\Omega_p = 1$ rad/s for analog prototype; arbitrary $\omega_c$ for digital prototype.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Periodogram - Averaged Periodogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Periodogram - Averaged Periodogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Periodogram

In the context of **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** estimation of deterministic, non-stationary, and random biomedical signals (such as ECG, EEG, and EMG), the **Periodogram** is formally defined as a *non-parametric spectral estimation technique* that computes the squared magnitude of the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of a finite-length signal segment, normalized by the number of samples.

Mathematically, for a finite-length discrete-time signal $x(n)$ with $N$ samples truncated by a rectangular window $w(n)$, the periodogram is expressed as:

$$
P_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j\omega n} \right|^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCBMT501 - Module 4):**
> The Periodogram forms the foundational block for *non-parametric spectral estimation*. KTU examiners frequently test its role as the unbiased estimator of the true PSD — but with a high variance trade-off. The **Averaged Periodogram** (Bartlett & Welch methods) is introduced to mitigate this variance.

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition of Averaged Periodogram

The **Averaged Periodogram** is an enhanced non-parametric spectral estimation method that addresses the **high variance** limitation of a single raw periodogram. It works on the principle of *ensemble averaging* of multiple periodograms computed from disjoint (Bartlett) or overlapping (Welch) signal segments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Averaged Periodogram:**
> It is the arithmetic mean of $K$ independent periodograms computed from $K$ signal sub-segments, each of length $L$, such that the variance of the PSD estimate is reduced by a factor of $K$.

$$
\bar{P}_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} P_{x_i}(\omega)
$$

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to **estimate the average rainfall of Kerala during July**. If you measure the rainfall in just one specific year (2023), your estimate will have huge variance — it could be an unusually wet or dry year. To get a more reliable estimate, you measure the rainfall across 30 different years and then **average** them. The "averaged measurement" gives you a far more trustworthy value.

- The **single 2023 measurement** is analogous to the **raw Periodogram** (high variance, single observation).
- The **30-year average rainfall** is analogous to the **Averaged Periodogram** (reduced variance, multiple observations).
- In Welch's method, instead of using completely different years, you use **overlapping windows** (e.g., years that share 50% of their data), which preserves more information — analogous to sliding-window averaging in signals.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Important)

The following metrics are **bolded** for board exam relevance:

- **N**: Total number of signal samples.
- **L**: Length of each sub-segment (segment length).
- **K**: Number of segments used for averaging.
- **D**: Overlap factor between consecutive segments.
- **Bias**: $\text{Bias} = E[\hat{P}_x(\omega)] - P_x(\omega)$.
- **Variance**: $\text{Var}[\hat{P}_x(\omega)]$ — reduced by factor $K$ in averaged periodogram.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Periodogram (Step-by-Step)

The raw periodogram is computed using the following structured logic:

1. **Acquisition:** Collect a finite discrete-time biomedical signal $x(n)$ of length $N$.
2. **Windowing:** Apply a data-tapering window $w(n)$ (Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning) to minimize spectral leakage.
3. **Fourier Transform:** Compute the $N$-point Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of the windowed signal $X(\omega)$.
4. **Magnitude Squared:** Square the magnitude $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$.
5. **Normalization:** Divide by $N$ to obtain the raw periodogram $P_x(\omega)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by $N$?**
> Dividing by $N$ ensures that the periodogram represents an *asymptotically unbiased* estimate of the true Power Spectral Density. Without this normalization, the magnitude would scale with signal length, giving unphysical PSD values.

## 2.2 The Variance Problem — The "Why" Behind Averaging

The raw periodogram is an **asymptotically unbiased** estimator, but it is **not consistent**. As $N \to \infty$, the bias tends to zero, but the variance does **not** tend to zero — it remains constant and equal to the square of the true PSD.

$$
\text{Var}[P_x(\omega)] \approx P_x^2(\omega)
$$

This is unacceptable for analyzing low-amplitude biomedical features (e.g., EEG sub-bands, EMG fatigue indicators). Hence, the **Averaged Periodogram** is required.

## 2.3 Bartlett's Method (Non-Overlapping Segments)

**Algorithm Logic:**

1. Divide the $N$-sample signal into $K$ non-overlapping segments, each of length $L = N/K$.
2. For each segment $x_i(n)$, compute the periodogram $P_{x_i}(\omega)$.
3. Average the $K$ periodograms to obtain the Bartlett estimate.

$$
P_{\text{Bartlett}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} P_{x_i}(\omega) = \frac{1}{KL} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n) \, e^{-j\omega n} \right|^2
$$

**Key Property:** Variance is reduced by factor $K$, but resolution degrades because segment length $L$ is smaller than $N$.

## 2.4 Welch's Method (Overlapping Segments with Windowing)

**Algorithm Logic:**

1. Divide the $N$-sample signal into $K$ overlapping segments with overlap ratio $D$ (typically $50\%$ or $75\%$), each of length $L$.
2. Apply a non-rectangular window (Hamming/Hanning) to each segment.
3. Compute the periodogram of each windowed segment.
4. Average all $K$ periodograms to obtain the Welch estimate.

$$
P_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K \cdot L \cdot U} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \left| \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) \, x_i(n) \, e^{-j\omega n} \right|^2
$$

where $U$ is the window energy normalization factor:

$$
U = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)
$$

**Key Properties:**
- **Reduced variance** by factor approaching $K$.
- **Better resolution** preserved (longer segments possible due to overlap).
- **Reduced spectral leakage** due to non-rectangular windowing.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| $P_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(\omega) \right\vert^2$ | Raw Periodogram | Squared magnitude DFT divided by $N$ |
| $L = N / K$ | Segment Length | Bartlett's non-overlapping division |
| $K = \frac{N - L}{L - D} + 1$ | Number of Segments | Welch's overlapping formula |
| $P_{\text{avg}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum P_{x_i}(\omega)$ | Averaged Periodogram | Mean of $K$ periodograms |
| $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum w^2(n)$ | Window Energy | Normalization factor for Welch |
| $\text{Var}[\bar{P}_x] \approx \frac{1}{K} P_x^2(\omega)$ | Reduced Variance | Variance reduction by factor $K$ |
| $\text{Bias} \approx \frac{1}{L}$ | Resolution Bias | Inversely proportional to segment length |
| $\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{L}$ | Frequency Resolution | Resolution in Hz for segment length $L$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In clinical and biomedical applications, the averaged periodogram is **indispensable** for:

- **ECG HRV Analysis:** Detecting low-frequency (LF) and high-frequency (HF) power components for autonomic nervous system assessment.
- **EEG Spectral Band Analysis:** Estimating power in Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta, and Gamma bands for sleep staging and seizure detection.
- **EMG Fatigue Analysis:** Tracking median frequency shifts in surface EMG signals.
- **Auditory Evoked Potentials:** Estimating SNR via averaged periodograms across multiple stimulus trials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Periodogram from the Autocorrelation Function

**Starting Point:** The Wiener-Khinchin Theorem states that the Power Spectral Density $P_x(\omega)$ of a Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) random process $x(n)$ is the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of its autocorrelation sequence $r_{xx}(m)$.

$$
P_x(\omega) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) \, e^{-j\omega m}
$$

**Step 1:** Estimate the autocorrelation function from a finite-length realization $x(n)$ of length $N$. The biased autocorrelation estimate is:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n) \, x(n+m), \quad \text{for } \vert m \vert < N
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the estimated autocorrelation into the Wiener-Khinchin relation.

$$
\hat{P}_x(\omega) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) \, e^{-j\omega m}
$$

**Step 3:** Expand the summation explicitly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}_x(\omega) &= \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n) \, x(n+m) \right] e^{-j\omega m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Parseval-type rearrangement by interchanging the order of summation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}_x(\omega) &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \sum_{m=-(N-1-n)}^{N-1-n} x(n+m) \, e^{-j\omega m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $k = n + m$ to convert the inner sum into a complete summation over $k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}_x(\omega) &= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(k) \, e^{-j\omega (k-n)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Factor out the exponential terms to expose the DFT structure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}_x(\omega) &= \frac{1}{N} \left( \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(k) \, e^{-j\omega k} \right) \left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{+j\omega n} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Recognize that the first sum is the forward DFT $X(\omega)$ and the second sum is its complex conjugate $X^*(\omega)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{P}_x(\omega) &= \frac{1}{N} \, X(\omega) \, X^*(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(\omega) \right\vert^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result — The Periodogram:**

$$
\boxed{\hat{P}_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Variance Reduction in Averaged Periodogram

**Step 1:** Let the $K$ individual periodograms be $P_1(\omega), P_2(\omega), \dots, P_K(\omega)$, each computed from independent segments of length $L$.

**Step 2:** Define the averaged periodogram as:

$$
\bar{P}_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} P_i(\omega)
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the expected value (mean) of the averaged periodogram.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\bar{P}_x(\omega)] &= E\left[ \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} P_i(\omega) \right] = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} E[P_i(\omega)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Since each segment is identically distributed, $E[P_i(\omega)] = E[P_1(\omega)]$ for all $i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\bar{P}_x(\omega)] &= \frac{1}{K} \cdot K \cdot E[P_1(\omega)] = E[P_1(\omega)]
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the averaged periodogram is **unbiased** if each individual periodogram is unbiased.

**Step 5:** Compute the variance of the averaged periodogram assuming statistical independence of segments.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}[\bar{P}_x(\omega)] &= \text{Var}\left[ \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} P_i(\omega) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** For independent random variables, the variance of the sum is the sum of variances.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}[\bar{P}_x(\omega)] &= \frac{1}{K^2} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \text{Var}[P_i(\omega)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Since all $K$ periodograms are identically distributed, $\text{Var}[P_i(\omega)] = \text{Var}[P_1(\omega)]$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}[\bar{P}_x(\omega)] &= \frac{1}{K^2} \cdot K \cdot \text{Var}[P_1(\omega)] = \frac{1}{K} \text{Var}[P_1(\omega)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result — Variance Reduction:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Var}[\bar{P}_x(\omega)] = \frac{1}{K} \text{Var}[P_1(\omega)] = \frac{1}{K} P_x^2(\omega)}
$$

This proves that averaging $K$ periodograms reduces variance by a factor of $K$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Welch's Segment Count

Given a signal of length $N$, segment length $L$, and overlap of $D$ samples:

**Step 1:** With overlap $D$, the segments slide forward by $(L - D)$ samples each step.

**Step 2:** The number of complete segments $K$ is derived by counting how many segments fit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \left\lfloor \frac{N - L}{L - D} \right\rfloor + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** For $50\%$ overlap, $D = L/2$, simplifying to:

$$
K \approx \frac{2N}{L} - 1
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Trade-Off Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| **Parameter** | **Increase Effect on Variance** | **Increase Effect on Resolution** | **Increase Effect on Bias** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Segment Length $L$ | Variance increases | Resolution improves | Bias decreases |
| Number of Segments $K$ | Variance decreases | Resolution degrades | Bias increases |
| Overlap $D$ | Variance decreases (with window) | Resolution preserved | Slight bias increase |
| Window Smoothness | Variance decreases | Resolution degrades | Bias increases |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Computation of the Raw Periodogram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomedical Signal xn of length N] --> B[Apply Data Window wn]
    B --> C[Compute N-point DFT X omega]
    C --> D[Compute Magnitude Squared]
    D --> E[Normalize by dividing by N]
    E --> F[Output Periodogram Px omega]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Computation of the Averaged Periodogram (Bartlett Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal xn of length N] --> B[Segment into K non-overlapping blocks]
    B --> C1[Segment 1 length L]
    B --> C2[Segment 2 length L]
    B --> C3[Segment K length L]
    C1 --> D1[Apply Window]
    C2 --> D2[Apply Window]
    C3 --> D3[Apply Window]
    D1 --> E1[Compute Periodogram P1]
    D2 --> E2[Compute Periodogram P2]
    D3 --> E3[Compute Periodogram PK]
    E1 --> F[Sum and Divide by K]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Output Averaged Periodogram]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Computation of Welch's Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal xn of length N] --> B[Sliding Window with Overlap D]
    B --> C1[Windowed Segment 1]
    B --> C2[Windowed Segment 2]
    B --> C3[Windowed Segment K]
    C1 --> D1[Compute DFT]
    C2 --> D2[Compute DFT]
    C3 --> D3[Compute DFT]
    D1 --> E1[Compute Periodogram Pi1]
    D2 --> E2[Compute Periodogram Pi2]
    D3 --> E3[Compute Periodogram PiK]
    E1 --> F[Apply Window Energy Normalization U]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G[Average across K segments]
    G --> H[Output Welch PSD Estimate]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Comparison — Periodogram vs Averaged Periodogram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RAW[Raw Periodogram]
        R1[Single DFT] --> R2[Single Squared Magnitude]
        R2 --> R3[High Variance Output]
    end
    subgraph AVG[Averaged Periodogram]
        A1[Multiple DFTs] --> A2[Multiple Squared Magnitudes]
        A2 --> A3[Mean Across Segments]
        A3 --> A4[Reduced Variance Output]
    end
    RAW --- COMPARE{Trade-off Analysis}
    AVG --- COMPARE
    COMPARE --> T1[Bias: Same]
    COMPARE --> T2[Variance: Reduced by 1 over K]
    COMPARE --> T3[Resolution: Reduced for same N]
```

## 4.5 Workflow Diagram — KTU Exam Problem Solving Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Read Problem Statement] --> S2[Identify N, L, K, D]
    S2 --> S3[Choose Method: Bartlett or Welch]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Periodogram Formula per Segment]
    S4 --> S5[Average Across K Segments]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Window Normalization if Welch]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Variance and Bias]
    S7 --> S8[Final PSD Estimate]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Periodogram. Mention its key limitation.
**Model Answer:** The Periodogram is a non-parametric PSD estimator defined as $P_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(\omega) \right\vert^2$, where $X(\omega)$ is the DFT of the $N$-point signal $x(n)$. Its key limitation is **high variance** — the variance does not decrease as $N \to \infty$, making it an inconsistent estimator.

### Question 2: What is the Averaged Periodogram? State the variance reduction achieved.
**Model Answer:** The Averaged Periodogram is the mean of $K$ individual periodograms computed from signal sub-segments. It reduces the variance of the PSD estimate by a factor of $K$ compared to the raw periodogram, achieving the relation $\text{Var}[\bar{P}_x(\omega)] = \frac{1}{K} P_x^2(\omega)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for the periodogram starting from the Wiener-Khinchin theorem. Show that the periodogram equals the squared magnitude of the DFT divided by $N$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a biomedical signal $x(n)$ of length $N = 1024$ samples sampled at $f_s = 256$ Hz, compute the PSD using Bartlett's method with $K = 8$ non-overlapping segments. Determine the segment length, frequency resolution, and expected variance reduction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Given:** The Wiener-Khinchin theorem states $P_x(\omega) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) e^{-j\omega m}$.

**Step 1:** Estimate autocorrelation for finite signal of length $N$:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n) \, x(n+m)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the Wiener-Khinchin relation:

$$
\hat{P}_x(\omega) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) \, e^{-j\omega m}
$$

**Step 3:** Interchange the order of summation:

$$
\hat{P}_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(k) \, e^{-j\omega (k-n)}
$$

**Step 4:** Separate into two DFT-like sums:

$$
\hat{P}_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left( \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(k) e^{-j\omega k} \right) \left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{+j\omega n} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the conjugate pair $X(\omega) \cdot X^*(\omega) = \vert X(\omega) \vert^2$:

$$
\hat{P}_x(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(\omega) \right\vert^2
$$

**Conclusion:** This proves the periodogram is the squared magnitude of the DFT scaled by $1/N$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $N = 1024$, $f_s = 256$ Hz, $K = 8$.

**Step 1:** Compute segment length:

$$
L = \frac{N}{K} = \frac{1024}{8} = 128 \text{ samples}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute frequency resolution:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{L} = \frac{256}{128} = 2 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute variance reduction factor:

$$
\text{Variance Reduction} = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125 \text{ or } 12.5\%
$$

**Step 4:** PSD estimate formula for Bartlett's method:

$$
P_{\text{Bartlett}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{8 \cdot 128} \sum_{i=1}^{8} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{127} x_i(n) \, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2
$$

**Step 5:** For a Hann window, normalization factor $U = 0.375$ (for unit-energy Hann). The windowed Welch improvement gives smoother spectrum.

**Conclusion:** The Bartlett estimator provides 8× variance reduction at the cost of coarser 2 Hz resolution.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain Welch's method for power spectral density estimation. Compare it with Bartlett's method in terms of variance, resolution, and bias. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-second EEG signal sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz is processed using Welch's method with segment length $L = 500$ samples and 50% overlap. Compute the number of segments, the frequency resolution, and the expected variance reduction. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Welch's Method — Procedure:**

1. Segment the signal $x(n)$ into $K$ overlapping windows of length $L$ with overlap $D$ (typically 50%).
2. Apply a tapered window $w(n)$ (Hann/Hamming) to each segment.
3. Compute the periodogram of each windowed segment.
4. Normalize each periodogram by the window energy $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum w^2(n)$.
5. Average all normalized periodograms.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
P_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K \cdot L \cdot U} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) \, x_i(n) \, e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| **Property** | **Bartlett** | **Welch** |
|---|---|---|
| Overlap | None (0%) | 50% or 75% |
| Windowing | Usually Rectangular | Hann/Hamming |
| Variance Reduction | $1/K$ | $1/K$ (approximate) |
| Frequency Resolution | $f_s / L$ | $f_s / L$ (same) |
| Spectral Leakage | Higher | Lower |
| Number of Segments | $N/L$ | $(2N/L) - 1$ (for 50%) |
| Bias | Higher | Lower (tapered window) |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** Duration $T = 4$ s, $f_s = 500$ Hz, $L = 500$ samples, 50% overlap.

**Step 1:** Compute total number of samples:

$$
N = T \cdot f_s = 4 \times 500 = 2000 \text{ samples}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the number of segments with 50% overlap ($D = 250$):

$$
K = \left\lfloor \frac{N - L}{L - D} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{2000 - 500}{500 - 250} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor 6 \right\rfloor + 1 = 7
$$

**Step 3:** Frequency resolution:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{L} = \frac{500}{500} = 1 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4:** Variance reduction factor:

$$
\text{Variance Reduction} = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 14.29\%
$$

**Step 5:** Window energy for Hann window (length 500):

$$
U_{\text{Hann}} = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} \left( 0.5 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{L-1}\right) \right)^2 \approx 0.375
$$

**Conclusion:** With 7 segments and 1 Hz resolution, the EEG spectral analysis achieves approximately 7× variance reduction with sharp frequency precision ideal for delta/theta/alpha/beta band separation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/N$ normalization factor** — Marks are deducted if the periodogram is written as $\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ without the $1/N$ scaling. KTU examiners strictly check this.
> 2. **Confusing Bias and Variance concepts** — Students often state the periodogram is "unbiased" without specifying it is *asymptotically unbiased*. The variance does *not* vanish — this is the critical reason for averaging.
> 3. **Mixing up Bartlett and Welch segment counts** — The number of segments formula differs: $K = N/L$ for Bartlett, $K = \frac{N-L}{L-D} + 1$ for Welch. Examiners test this distinction.
> 4. **Ignoring window energy normalization $U$ in Welch** — Failing to divide by $U$ in the Welch formula leads to biased amplitude estimates.
> 5. **Not stating the overlap percentage explicitly** — Always specify whether overlap is 0%, 50%, or 75% in numerical problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Periodogram** is defined as $\frac{1}{N} \left\vert X(\omega) \right\vert^2$ — non-parametric, asymptotically unbiased, but inconsistent.
- **Averaged Periodogram** = mean of $K$ periodograms from sub-segments — reduces variance by factor $K$.
- **Bartlett's Method** uses **non-overlapping** rectangular-windowed segments of length $L = N/K$.
- **Welch's Method** uses **overlapping** tapered-windowed segments with energy normalization $U$.
- **Variance Reduction:** $\text{Var}[\bar{P}_x(\omega)] = \frac{1}{K} P_x^2(\omega)$.
- **Resolution:** $\Delta f = f_s / L$ — degrades as segment length decreases.
- **Bias Trade-off:** Smaller $L$ → Higher bias, Lower resolution; Larger $K$ → Lower variance, Higher bias.
- **Welch Formula:** $P_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K L U} \sum_{i} \left\vert \sum_{n} w(n) x_i(n) e^{-j\omega n} \right\vert^2$.
- **Welch Segments with 50% Overlap:** $K \approx 2N/L - 1$.
- **Window Energy:** $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ — must normalize each periodogram by this factor.
- **Biomedical Applications:** ECG HRV (LF/HF ratio), EEG band power, EMG median frequency, evoked potential SNR estimation.
- **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the Wiener-Khinchin derivation, mention the bias-variance trade-off explicitly, and state the variance reduction factor $1/K$ in numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# QRS Detection in ECG — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Detection of QRS complex in ECG — algorithmic framework, preprocessing, feature extraction, and decision logic. This is a *high-weightage* topic for ESE and is frequently tested in Part B questions.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **QRS complex** is the most prominent waveform in an Electrocardiogram (ECG) signal, representing the **ventricular depolarization** of the cardiac muscle. The QRS complex consists of three deflections:

- **Q wave:** A small downward deflection preceding the R wave.
- **R wave:** The large upward deflection (highest amplitude component).
- **S wave:** The downward deflection following the R wave.

**QRS Detection** is the algorithmic process of identifying the temporal location (time index) of each QRS complex within a continuous ECG signal stream. It is the **fundamental first step** in virtually every ECG analysis pipeline, including heart rate computation, arrhythmia classification, and heart rate variability (HRV) analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physiological Duration:** A normal QRS complex lasts **80–120 ms** (i.e., **2–3 small boxes** on a standard ECG paper at 25 mm/s). Anything above 120 ms is considered an abnormally wide QRS, often indicative of bundle branch block.

## 1.2 The ECG Signal — A Composite Picture

A standard ECG Lead-II waveform (P-QRS-T) spans approximately **0.8 seconds per cardiac cycle** at a normal resting heart rate of 75 bpm. The constituent waves and their typical parameters are:

| Wave | Physiological Event | Typical Amplitude | Typical Duration |
|------|---------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| P wave | Atrial depolarization | 0.25 mV | 80–100 ms |
| **QRS** | **Ventricular depolarization** | **1.0–1.5 mV (R peak)** | **80–120 ms** |
| T wave | Ventricular repolarization | 0.1–0.5 mV | 160 ms |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Why QRS Detection is the "Heart" of ECG Processing

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Heart as a Drum and the QRS as the Loudest Beat**
>
> Imagine listening to a **live musical performance** through a noisy wall. The drummer plays steadily, but there is a guitarist strumming softly, a singer humming faintly, and a child occasionally tapping a glass. **The drummer's beats are the QRS complexes — the loudest, sharpest, and most rhythmic events in the signal.**
>
> To measure the "tempo" of the song (i.e., the heart rate), you don't need to analyze the entire orchestra in detail. You only need to **reliably count when the drum hits**. This is exactly what QRS detection does: it ignores the small P waves, T waves, baseline wander, muscle noise, and 50/60 Hz powerline interference, and **isolates the strongest, sharpest feature** in the ECG.
>
> Just as a sound engineer would use filters to remove low-frequency rumble and high-frequency hiss before counting beats, an ECG processing algorithm applies a **band-pass filter** to emphasize the QRS energy band (typically 5–30 Hz) and suppress everything else.

## 1.4 Why is QRS Detection Challenging?

The QRS complex, despite being the most energetic component, is still susceptible to corruption. The principal sources of contamination are:

1. **Baseline Wander (0.15 – 0.5 Hz):** Caused by patient respiration, electrode polarization, and body movement. This shifts the ECG baseline up and down.
2. **Powerline Interference (50/60 Hz):** Electromagnetic coupling from the AC mains supply.
3. **Muscle Artifact (EMG noise):** High-frequency random noise from skeletal muscle contractions (DC to >500 Hz).
4. **Patient Motion Artifacts:** Abrupt baseline shifts due to electrode movement.
5. **Pathological Morphologies:** In some diseases, the QRS can be inverted, biphasic, or unusually wide — making detection by simple thresholding difficult.
6. **Tall T-waves:** A peaked T-wave can sometimes be falsely detected as an R-wave, leading to a doubled heart rate (a critical error in patient monitoring).

## 1.5 The Standard QRS Detection Pipeline — A Bird's-Eye View

A canonical QRS detection algorithm operates in **five sequential stages**:

1. **Preprocessing (Band-Pass Filtering):** Removes baseline wander, powerline interference, and high-frequency muscle noise. Output: A clean, QRS-emphasized signal.
2. **Differentiation:** Emphasizes the **steep slope** of the QRS complex. Output: A signal dominated by the R-wave transitions.
3. **Squaring (Nonlinear Transformation):** Makes all values positive and **amplifies larger slopes** disproportionately. Output: A high-energy, unipolar signal.
4. **Moving-Window Integration (Smoothing):** Spreads the QRS energy over time to produce a smooth envelope. Output: A unimodal pulse for each QRS.
5. **Adaptive Thresholding & Decision Logic:** Compares the integrated output to a moving threshold to declare "QRS detected" or "no QRS."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The most famous implementation of this pipeline is the Pan-Tompkins Algorithm (1985)**, which is the de-facto standard taught in KTU syllabi and is the most likely 14-mark question topic from this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Mathematical Treatment

### Stage 1: Band-Pass Filtering (5–30 Hz)

The band-pass filter is a **cascade of a Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** and a **High-Pass Filter (HPF)**. In Pan-Tompkins, both filters are realized as **second-order IIR filters** using integer arithmetic for real-time hardware deployment.

**Low-Pass Filter (LPF) — Cutoff ≈ 11 Hz:**
The transfer function of the Pan-Tompkins LPF is:

$$
H_{LP}(z) = \frac{(1 - z^{-6})^2}{(1 - z^{-1})^2}
$$

The LPF eliminates high-frequency noise above ~11 Hz. The difference equation is:

$$
y[n] = 2y[n-1] - y[n-2] + x[n] - 2x[n-6] + x[n-12]
$$

**High-Pass Filter (HPF) — Cutoff ≈ 5 Hz:**
The HPF is implemented as an **all-pass minus low-pass** cascade:

$$
H_{HP}(z) = \frac{(-1 + 32z^{-16} + z^{-32})}{(1 - z^{-1})}
$$

The difference equation is:

$$
y[n] = x[n-16] - \frac{1}{32}\bigl(y[n-1] + x[n] - x[n-32]\bigr)
$$

The HPF removes baseline wander and DC offset below ~5 Hz.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Rate Convention (KTU Standard):** All formulas assume an ECG sampled at **$F_s = 200$ Hz** (Pan-Tompkins original) or **$F_s = 360$ Hz** (MIT-BIH database standard). Always state the assumed $F_s$ in your answer.

---

### Stage 2: Differentiation

The differentiator is a **five-point derivative** that approximates the first derivative $\frac{dx}{dt}$. Its transfer function is:

$$
H_{diff}(z) = \frac{1}{8T}\bigl(2 + z^{-1} - z^{-3} - 2z^{-4}\bigr)
$$

The difference equation is:

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{8}\bigl(2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4]\bigr)
$$

The output is proportional to the **slope** of the ECG. Since the QRS has the steepest slope of all ECG waves, the differentiator suppresses P and T waves dramatically.

---

### Stage 3: Squaring (Point-by-Point)

$$
y[n] = \bigl(x[n]\bigr)^2
$$

Squaring has three powerful effects:
1. **Unipolarity:** All values become $\ge 0$.
2. **Nonlinear Amplification:** Large slopes (QRS) are amplified more than small slopes (P, T).
3. **Energy Compression:** The output emphasizes high-energy regions of the signal.

---

### Stage 4: Moving-Window Integration

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]
$$

where $N$ is the window length. The choice of $N$ is critical:

$$
N \approx 0.15 \times F_s
$$

For $F_s = 200$ Hz, $N = 30$ samples (i.e., a 150 ms window — slightly longer than the widest expected QRS). This produces a **smooth, unimodal pulse** for each QRS detection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why N = 150 ms?** Because a normal QRS lasts 80–120 ms, choosing a window of ~150 ms ensures that the entire QRS energy is captured within a single integration pulse, preventing double-counting of the R and S waves as separate events.

---

### Stage 5: Adaptive Thresholding

The threshold $THR$ is **not fixed** — it is continuously updated based on the running estimates of the **signal level** ($SPKI$ for QRS peaks) and the **noise level** ($NPKI$ for non-QRS peaks):

$$
THR[n] = NPKI[n] + 0.25 \cdot \bigl(SPKI[n] - NPKI[n]\bigr)
$$

**Update Rules:**

- When a QRS is detected (peak $\ge THR$):
$$
SPKI[n] = 0.125 \cdot PEAK + 0.875 \cdot SPKI[n-1]
$$
- When no QRS is detected (peak $< THR$):
$$
NPKI[n] = 0.125 \cdot PEAK + 0.875 \cdot NPKI[n-1]
$$

Additionally, two **back-up thresholds** ($THR_{min}$ and the lower threshold) provide robustness against missed detections:

$$
THR_{min} = 0.4 \cdot THR \quad \text{(minimum allowable threshold)}
$$

A QRS is declared if the signal exceeds $THR$ for a sustained period **and** no QRS was detected in the previous **200 ms refractory period** (to avoid T-wave double-counting).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Stage** | **Operation** | **Equation** | **Parameter / Note** |
|---|---|---|---|
| LPF | Cascade integrator-comb | $y[n] = 2y[n-1] - y[n-2] + x[n] - 2x[n-6] + x[n-12]$ | $F_c \approx 11$ Hz, $F_s = 200$ Hz |
| HPF | All-pass minus LPF | $y[n] = x[n-16] - \frac{1}{32}(y[n-1] + x[n] - x[n-32])$ | $F_c \approx 5$ Hz |
| Differentiator | 5-point derivative | $y[n] = \frac{1}{8}(2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4])$ | Approximates $\frac{dx}{dt}$ |
| Squaring | Nonlinear | $y[n] = (x[n])^2$ | Unipolar; emphasizes QRS |
| Moving Average | MA filter | $y[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x[n-k]$ | $N = 0.15 \cdot F_s$ |
| Adaptive Threshold | Midpoint rule | $THR[n] = NPKI[n] + 0.25(SPKI[n] - NPKI[n])$ | $SPKI, NPKI$ updated by gain $0.125$ |
| Min Threshold | Floor rule | $THR_{min} = 0.4 \cdot THR$ | Prevents false negatives |
| Refractory Period | Lockout | $T_{refr} = 200$ ms | Prevents T-wave double-counting |
| RR Interval | Inter-beat | $RR[k] = t_{R}[k] - t_{R}[k-1]$ | Output of detector |
| Instantaneous HR | Reciprocal | $HR = \frac{60}{RR}$ in seconds | bpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Pan-Tompkins algorithm is embedded in **Holter monitors, ICU patient monitors (Philips, GE), implantable pacemakers, and wearable fitness ECG patches (Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile)**. It is the baseline against which all modern deep-learning QRS detectors (e.g., 1D-CNN, LSTM) are benchmarked using the **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** (48 records, ~30 min each, 360 Hz).

---

## 2.3 Alternative QRS Detection Techniques (KTU Frequently Tested)

While Pan-Tompkins is dominant, examiners may ask about these alternatives:

### A. Derivative-Based Method (Simple & KTU-Favorite)

1. Compute $y[n] = \frac{1}{8}(2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4])$.
2. Square: $y[n] = y[n]^2$.
3. Apply MA filter: $z[n] = \frac{1}{N}\sum y$.
4. Threshold: QRS at $z[n] > THR$ with 200 ms refractory.

### B. Hilbert Transform-Based Detection

The QRS energy is detected using the **envelope** of the analytic signal:
$$
x_a(t) = x(t) + j \cdot H\{x(t)\}
$$
where $H\{.\}$ is the Hilbert transform. The envelope is:
$$
E(t) = \sqrt{x^2(t) + H\{x(t)\}^2}
$$
QRS peaks correspond to maxima of $E(t)$.

### C. Wavelet-Transform Detection

The ECG is **decomposed** using the **Modulated Mexican Hat wavelet** (or Daubechies-4) at scales corresponding to the QRS frequency band (scales 3–5 at $F_s = 360$ Hz). The QRS is located at the **zero-crossings of the wavelet coefficients** between pairs of modulus maxima.

### D. Matched Filtering

A **template QRS** is correlated with the incoming signal. A peak in the cross-correlation output indicates a QRS match. Effective when the QRS morphology is consistent but fails on highly variable arrhythmias.

### E. Digital Filter-Bank + Neural Network

Modern deep-learning approach: ECG is passed through a bank of band-pass filters, the outputs are fed to a **1D-CNN or LSTM**, which outputs a binary QRS / non-QRS label per sample.

---

## 2.4 Performance Metrics for QRS Detectors

| **Metric** | **Definition** | **Formula** | **Typical Value (Pan-Tompkins on MIT-BIH)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity ($Se$) | True detection rate | $Se = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | **99.75%** |
| Positive Predictivity ($+P$) | Reliability of detections | $+P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | **99.54%** |
| Detection Error Rate ($DER$) | Overall error | $DER = \frac{FP + FN}{Total\ Beats}$ | **0.71%** |

Where $TP$ = true positives (correct QRS detections), $FP$ = false positives (false alarms), $FN$ = false negatives (missed QRS).

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: Pan-Tompkins Differentiation Stage

**Problem:** Derive the five-point derivative filter used in the Pan-Tompkins algorithm and show its frequency response. Assume $F_s = 200$ Hz.

### Step 1: Continuous-Time Derivative Approximation

The first derivative $\frac{dx}{t}$ is approximated by the **central difference** over a span of 4 sampling intervals:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} \approx \frac{1}{8T}\bigl[2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4]\bigr]
$$

The coefficients are obtained by fitting a polynomial and sampling at $n, n-1, n-3, n-4$ with weights corresponding to the derivative of the Lagrange interpolation polynomial.

### Step 2: Justification of Coefficients

Consider the Taylor series expansion of $x[n-1], x[n-3], x[n-4]$ around $x[n]$:

$$
x[n-1] = x[n] - T x'[n] + \frac{T^2}{2}x''[n] - \frac{T^3}{6}x'''[n] + \dots
$$

$$
x[n-3] = x[n] - 3Tx'[n] + \frac{9T^2}{2}x''[n] - \frac{27T^3}{6}x'''[n] + \dots
$$

$$
x[n-4] = x[n] - 4Tx'[n] + 8T^2 x''[n] - \frac{64T^3}{6}x'''[n] + \dots
$$

### Step 3: Form the Linear Combination

Construct $y[n] = a_0 x[n] + a_1 x[n-1] + a_3 x[n-3] + a_4 x[n-4]$. The goal is to make $y[n] \approx T \cdot x'[n]$, i.e., the coefficients of $x[n], x[n-1], x[n-3], x[n-4]$ in the expansion of $y$ should be $0, 0, 0, 0$ for the constant, quadratic, and cubic terms, and the linear coefficient should be $T$.

Solving the system yields:

$$
a_0 = \frac{2}{8T}, \quad a_1 = \frac{1}{8T}, \quad a_3 = -\frac{1}{8T}, \quad a_4 = -\frac{2}{8T}
$$

Thus:

$$
y[n] = \frac{1}{8T}\bigl[2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4]\bigr]
$$

### Step 4: Transfer Function

Taking the z-transform:

$$
H_{diff}(z) = \frac{1}{8T}\bigl[2 + z^{-1} - z^{-3} - 2z^{-4}\bigr]
$$

### Step 5: Frequency Response

Substitute $z = e^{j\omega T}$:

$$
H_{diff}(e^{j\omega T}) = \frac{1}{8T}\bigl[2 + e^{-j\omega T} - e^{-j3\omega T} - 2e^{-j4\omega T}\bigr]
$$

This is a **high-pass** filter that attenuates DC and very low frequencies (P and T waves) and amplifies mid-band frequencies (QRS).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Threshold Computation on Sample Data

**Problem:** A Pan-Tompkins detector has the following running statistics:
- $SPKI$ (last QRS peak) = 1.0
- $NPKI$ (last non-QRS peak) = 0.2
- Current integrated signal peak = 0.85
- No QRS detected in the last 200 ms

Determine whether a QRS is declared. If yes, update $SPKI$.

### Step 1: Compute the Threshold

$$
THR = NPKI + 0.25 \cdot (SPKI - NPKI) = 0.2 + 0.25 \cdot (1.0 - 0.2)
$$

$$
THR = 0.2 + 0.25 \cdot 0.8 = 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4
$$

### Step 2: Compare Peak to Threshold

$$
PEAK = 0.85 \quad \text{and} \quad THR = 0.4
$$

Since $0.85 > 0.4$ and no QRS was in the refractory window, **a QRS is declared**.

### Step 3: Update $SPKI$

$$
SPKI_{new} = 0.125 \cdot 0.85 + 0.875 \cdot 1.0 = 0.10625 + 0.875 = 0.98125
$$

### Step 4: Check Minimum Threshold

$$
THR_{min} = 0.4 \cdot THR = 0.4 \cdot 0.4 = 0.16
$$

Since $0.98125 > 0.16$, the threshold is valid for the next iteration.

**Conclusion:** A QRS is correctly detected, and the signal-level estimate is updated to **$SPKI \approx 0.981$**, which will gradually adapt to the true QRS peak amplitude.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Comparison

| **Algorithm** | **Complexity** | **Sensitivity on MIT-BIH** | **Real-Time?** | **Robustness to Noise** | **KTU Likelihood** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pan-Tompkins (1985) | Medium (IIR + MA) | 99.75% | Yes | High (Adaptive) | ★★★★★ |
| Derivative + Threshold | Low | 95–97% | Yes | Low (fixed THR) | ★★★★ |
| Hilbert Envelope | Medium | 97% | Yes | Medium | ★★ |
| Wavelet (Modulated MH) | High (multi-scale) | 99.5% | Slower | Very High | ★★★ |
| Matched Filter | Medium | 98% (variable) | Yes | Low (template-dependent) | ★★ |
| 1D-CNN / LSTM | Very High (GPU) | 99.9%+ | Inference only | Very High | ★★ (Modern) |

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pan-Tompkins Algorithm — Full Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw ECG Signal x of n] --> B[Band Pass Filter 5 to 30 Hz]
    B --> C[Differentiator 5 point]
    C --> D[Squaring y equals x squared]
    D --> E[Moving Window MA Filter N equals 30]
    E --> F[Adaptive Thresholding Logic]
    F --> G{QRS Detected Check Refractory 200 ms}
    G -->|Yes| H[Output QRS Peak Index]
    G -->|No| I[Update NPKI Noise Estimate]
    I --> F
    H --> J[Update SPKI Signal Estimate]
    J --> F
```

## 4.2 ECG Waveform with QRS Annotations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[P WAVE Atrial Depol] --> PQ[PQ Segment]
    PQ --> QRS[QRS COMPLEX Ventricular Depol]
    QRS --> ST[ST Segment]
    ST --> T[T WAVE Ventricular Repol]
    T --> TP[TP Segment Baseline]
    TP --> P
```

## 4.3 Adaptive Thresholding — Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Integrated Signal z of n] --> T{Is z greater than THR}
    T -->|No| N1[Update NPKI with gain 0.125]
    N1 --> W[Wait for next sample]
    T -->|Yes| R{Is Refractory Period 200 ms over}
    R -->|No| W
    R -->|Yes| DEC[Declare QRS Detected]
    DEC --> U[Update SPKI with gain 0.125]
    U --> RT[Reset Refractory Timer]
    RT --> W
    W --> S
```

## 4.4 Noise Sources in ECG — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Raw ECG Acquisition] --> BW[Baseline Wander 0.15 to 0.5 Hz]
    RAW --> PL[Powerline Interference 50 or 60 Hz]
    RAW --> MA[Muscle Artifact EMG 20 to 500 Hz]
    RAW --> MOT[Motion Artifact Abrupt DC shifts]
    BW --> BPF[Band Pass Filter 5 to 30 Hz]
    PL --> BPF
    MA --> BPF
    MOT --> BPF
    BPF --> PROC[Pan Tompkins Processing Pipeline]
    PROC --> OUT[Clean QRS Detection Output]
```

## 4.5 Detection Performance — Confusion Matrix View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Actual QRS Present] -->|Detected Correctly| TP[True Positive TP]
    A -->|Missed by Algorithm| FN[False Negative FN]
    B[No Actual QRS] -->|False Alarm| FP[False Positive FP]
    B -->|Correctly Ignored| TN[True Negative TN]
    TP --> SE[Sensitivity equals TP by TP plus FN]
    FP --> PP[Positive Predictivity equals TP by TP plus FP]
```
