# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]

**Define the QRS complex. Why is its detection considered the most critical step in ECG signal processing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The QRS complex is the portion of the ECG signal that represents **ventricular depolarization**, characterized by the Q, R, and S waves, with a normal duration of **80–120 ms** and an R-wave amplitude of typically 1.0–1.5 mV.

It is the most critical step because:
1. (1 mark) The R-peak is the **most prominent and easily identifiable feature** in the ECG, serving as the fiducial point for all subsequent analysis.
2. (1 mark) It is required to compute **instantaneous heart rate, RR intervals, and HRV** — the foundations of all cardiac diagnostics.
3. (1 mark) Most arrhythmias (tachycardia, bradycardia, premature beats) are defined by abnormalities in the QRS rhythm, so accurate QRS detection is the prerequisite for **arrhythmia classification**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**List the five processing stages of the Pan-Tompkins QRS detection algorithm in order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. (1 mark) **Band-pass filtering** (5–30 Hz) — removes baseline wander and high-frequency noise.
2. (1 mark) **Differentiation** — emphasizes the steep QRS slope.
3. (1 mark) **Squaring** — nonlinear amplification of the QRS energy.
4. (Bonus/Mention) **Moving-window integration** — produces a smooth QRS envelope.
5. (Bonus/Mention) **Adaptive thresholding with refractory period** — makes the final detection decision.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Similar]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for QRS detection in ECG. Write the difference equations for the band-pass filter stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of adaptive thresholding in Pan-Tompkins algorithm. How are the signal and noise levels updated? Justify the choice of the 200 ms refractory period. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Raw ECG → Band-Pass Filter (5-30 Hz)
         → Differentiator (5-point)
         → Squaring
         → Moving Average (N = 30)
         → Adaptive Thresholding
         → QRS Detection Output
```

**Band-Pass Filter Equations (4 marks):**

The band-pass filter is a cascade of LPF (cutoff 11 Hz) and HPF (cutoff 5 Hz).

**LPF Difference Equation:**
$$
y_{LP}[n] = 2y_{LP}[n-1] - y_{LP}[n-2] + x[n] - 2x[n-6] + x[n-12]
$$
This is a cascaded integrator-comb structure. It removes high-frequency noise and EMG artifacts.

**HPF Difference Equation:**
$$
y_{HP}[n] = x[n-16] - \frac{1}{32}\bigl(y_{HP}[n-1] + x[n] - x[n-32]\bigr)
$$
This removes baseline wander and DC offset caused by respiration.

**Combined Effect:** The cascade produces a clean ECG with the QRS frequency band (5–30 Hz) preserved and all other bands suppressed.

**Subsequent Stages (Brief Mention):** Differentiation, squaring, and moving-average integration further enhance the QRS energy before thresholding.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Role of Adaptive Thresholding (2 marks):**
A fixed threshold fails in real-world ECG because the QRS amplitude varies with patient, electrode placement, and pathology. Adaptive thresholding **automatically tracks the changing QRS amplitude** and the noise floor, providing robust detection across diverse conditions.

**Signal and Noise Update Rules (3 marks):**
The algorithm maintains two running estimates:
- $SPKI$ — running estimate of the **QRS signal peak level**.
- $NPKI$ — running estimate of the **noise peak level**.

The threshold is computed as the **mid-point biased toward noise**:
$$
THR[n] = NPKI[n] + 0.25 \cdot (SPKI[n] - NPKI[n])
$$

**Update Law (with gain = 0.125):**
- On QRS detection: $SPKI_{new} = 0.125 \cdot PEAK + 0.875 \cdot SPKI_{old}$
- On non-QRS peak: $NPKI_{new} = 0.125 \cdot PEAK + 0.875 \cdot NPKI_{old}$

The gain 0.125 corresponds to a **time constant of ~8 beats**, allowing the threshold to adapt smoothly.

**Justification of 200 ms Refractory Period (2 marks):**
The **physiological upper bound of normal heart rate is ~300 bpm**, which corresponds to an inter-beat interval of 200 ms. A refractory period of 200 ms ensures that:
1. The **T-wave following a QRS** (which peaks ~200–300 ms after R) is **not double-counted** as a separate QRS.
2. The detector enforces a **physiologically plausible maximum heart rate**.
3. False positives due to high-amplitude T-waves (a major clinical error source) are eliminated.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Similar]

**(a)** Describe the need for preprocessing of ECG signals before QRS detection. Explain the role of band-pass filtering with relevant frequency ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the Wavelet Transform-based QRS detection method. Compare its performance with the Pan-Tompkins algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Need for Preprocessing (3 marks):**
The raw ECG acquired from surface electrodes is corrupted by multiple noise sources:
1. **Baseline Wander (0.15–0.5 Hz):** Caused by respiration and electrode movement. Can cause the QRS to drift above/below the threshold, leading to false positives/negatives.
2. **Powerline Interference (50 Hz in India, 60 Hz in USA):** Sinusoidal noise from AC mains.
3. **High-Frequency Muscle Artifact (EMG):** Random noise from skeletal muscles, can mimic QRS spikes.
4. **Patient Motion Artifacts:** Abrupt baseline shifts.

Without preprocessing, the **QRS detector would have a high false-alarm rate** and poor sensitivity.

**Role of Band-Pass Filtering (4 marks):**
A band-pass filter (5–30 Hz) is applied to isolate the QRS frequency band:
- **HPF (5 Hz cutoff):** Removes baseline wander, DC offset, and slow drift.
- **LPF (30 Hz cutoff):** Removes high-frequency EMG noise and 50 Hz powerline interference.

The QRS complex has its dominant spectral energy in the **5–30 Hz** range, while P and T waves lie below 5 Hz and EMG noise lies above 30 Hz. Hence, band-pass filtering simultaneously **enhances the QRS** and **suppresses competing noise sources**.

The Pan-Tompkins implementation uses **integer-arithmetic IIR filters** for real-time hardware deployment.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks

**Wavelet-Transform Based Detection (4 marks):**

The ECG is decomposed using the **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** with a mother wavelet matched to the QRS shape — typically the **Modulated Mexican Hat (ModMH) wavelet** or **Daubechies-4 (db4)**.

The CWT is defined as:
$$
C(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi^*\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt
$$

where $a$ is the scale and $b$ is the translation.

For an ECG sampled at $F_s = 360$ Hz, the QRS energy is concentrated at **scales 3–5**. The detection algorithm:
1. Compute wavelet coefficients at scales 1–8.
2. Identify **modulus maxima pairs** (positive and negative peaks).
3. Locate **zero-crossings** between these pairs — each zero-crossing corresponds to an R-peak.
4. Apply a threshold to the modulus maxima amplitude to reject noise-induced crossings.

**Comparison with Pan-Tompkins (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Pan-Tompkins** | **Wavelet** |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | 99.75% | 99.5–99.8% |
| Robustness to Baseline | Good (HPF) | Excellent (multi-scale) |
| Computational Cost | Low (real-time) | High (multi-scale CWT) |
| Real-Time Feasibility | Yes | Difficult (often offline) |
| Performance on Noisy ECG | Good | Very Good |

**Conclusion:** Wavelet methods offer superior robustness to baseline wander and morphology variation but at higher computational cost. Pan-Tompkins remains the standard for real-time embedded applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in QRS Detection Questions:**
>
> 1. **(–2 marks)** Writing "**Pan-Tompkins uses a band-pass filter**" without specifying the **frequency range (5–30 Hz)** or the **cutoffs (LPF=11 Hz, HPF=5 Hz)**.
> 2. **(–2 marks)** Stating the differentiator equation as a simple first-difference $(x[n] - x[n-1])$ instead of the **five-point derivative** with coefficients $\frac{1}{8}(2, 1, 0, -1, -2)$.
> 3. **(–2 marks)** Forgetting to mention the **moving-window integration** with $N = 0.15 \cdot F_s$ — this is a mandatory fourth stage.
> 4. **(–2 marks)** Writing **fixed threshold** instead of **adaptive thresholding** with $SPKI$ and $NPKI$ updates. Adaptive thresholding is the *defining feature* of Pan-Tompkins.
> 5. **(–2 marks)** Not justifying the **200 ms refractory period** — examiners expect a physiological link to the **maximum heart rate of 300 bpm** and **T-wave rejection**.
> 6. **(–1 mark)** Forgetting to specify the **assumed sampling rate $F_s$** (typically 200 Hz or 360 Hz) in your numerical answers.
> 7. **(–2 marks)** Drawing a block diagram without arrow labels or stage names. Always **label every block and arrow direction**.
> 8. **(–1 mark)** Spelling "Pan-Tompkins" as "Pan-Tomkins" or "Pantompkins" — spelling matters in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — QRS Detection in ECG**

- **Definition:** QRS complex = ventricular depolarization, duration **80–120 ms**, R-peak amplitude **1.0–1.5 mV**.
- **Why detect?** QRS is the fiducial point for HR, HRV, and arrhythmia analysis.
- **Main Algorithm:** **Pan-Tompkins (1985)** — five-stage pipeline.
- **Five Stages:** Band-pass (5–30 Hz) → Differentiator → Squaring → MA Integration → Adaptive Thresholding.
- **LPF Equation:** $y[n] = 2y[n-1] - y[n-2] + x[n] - 2x[n-6] + x[n-12]$ (cutoff 11 Hz).
- **HPF Equation:** $y[n] = x[n-16] - \frac{1}{32}(y[n-1] + x[n] - x[n-32])$ (cutoff 5 Hz).
- **Differentiator:** 5-point, $y[n] = \frac{1}{8}(2x[n] + x[n-1] - x[n-3] - 2x[n-4])$.
- **Squaring:** $y[n] = x[n]^2$ — unipolar, nonlinear amplification.
- **MA Window:** $N = 0.15 \cdot F_s$ (e.g., $N = 30$ at 200 Hz, $N = 54$ at 360 Hz).
- **Threshold:** $THR = NPKI + 0.25(SPKI - NPKI)$, with update gain **0.125**.
- **Min Threshold:** $THR_{min} = 0.4 \cdot THR$ — prevents false negatives.
- **Refractory Period:** **200 ms** — prevents T-wave double-counting, enforces max HR of 300 bpm.
- **Performance:** $Se \approx 99.75\%$, $+P \approx 99.54\%$, $DER \approx 0.71\%$ on MIT-BIH.
- **Alternatives:** Hilbert envelope, Wavelet (ModMH, db4), Matched filter, 1D-CNN.
- **Noise Sources:** Baseline wander (0.15–0.5 Hz), 50/60 Hz PLI, EMG, motion artifacts.
- **Sampling Rate:** Always state $F_s$ (typically **200 Hz** for Pan-Tompkins original, **360 Hz** for MIT-BIH).
- **Clinical Pitfall:** Tall T-waves can cause false positives — handled by 200 ms refractory.
- **Standard Database:** MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database — 48 records, 360 Hz, 11-bit resolution, 30 min each.
- **Real-World Use:** Holter monitors, ICU monitors, Apple Watch, AliveCor KardiaMobile, implantable pacemakers.
- **High-Yield Exam Keywords:** *Pan-Tompkins, adaptive thresholding, 5–30 Hz band-pass, 200 ms refractory, MIT-BIH, sensitivity, positive predictivity, ventricular depolarization*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Heart Rate Variability Using Periodogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Heart Rate Variability Using Periodogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heart Rate Variability (HRV) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heart Rate Variability (HRV)** is the physiological phenomenon of variation in the time interval between successive heartbeats, quantified from the **R-R intervals** of the electrocardiogram (ECG). It is a **non-invasive marker of autonomic nervous system (ANS) regulation** of the cardiovascular system.

Mathematically, if the $n$-th R-peak occurs at time $t_n$, the **instantaneous R-R interval** is:

$$
RR_n = t_{n+1} - t_n
$$

The instantaneous heart rate is then:

$$
HR_n = \frac{60}{RR_n} \;\; \text{[beats per minute]}
$$

The sequence of R-R intervals (or instantaneous HR) plotted as a function of time is called the **tachogram** or **R-R interval time series** — this is the primary input to spectral HRV analysis.

## 1.2 The Periodogram — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> The **Periodogram** is a **non-parametric estimator of the Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of a stationary random process. It is computed as the squared magnitude of the normalized Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) of the signal.

$$
P_{xx}(\omega_k) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \vert X(\omega_k) \vert^2
$$

where:
- $X(\omega_k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j \omega_k n}$ is the DFT of the signal
- $N$ is the total number of samples
- $\omega_k = \frac{2 \pi k}{N}$ is the normalized angular frequency

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: A Musician's Rhythm Variability**
>
> Imagine a drummer tapping a steady 60 BPM beat. A healthy, well-rested drummer keeps near-perfect timing between taps — the *variability* in those intervals is subtle but informative. Now imagine a tired, stressed drummer — the intervals become irregular, sometimes fast, sometimes slow.
>
> The human heart is exactly like that drummer. The **"taps"** are the R-peaks of the ECG, and the **"tempo wobble"** is HRV. A *healthy* heart is not a metronome — it constantly adapts via the **Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)**: the *sympathetic* (fight/flight, speeds up) and *parasympathetic* (rest/recover, slows down) branches.
>
> The **Periodogram** is like passing the drummer's tap-interval log through a *spectrum analyzer* — it tells you **how much wobble happens at each tempo-frequency**. A "wobble" oscillating at 0.25 Hz (every 4 seconds) corresponds to breathing-driven (respiratory sinus arrhythmia) variability — the parasympathetic signature. A slower "wobble" at 0.1 Hz corresponds to blood-pressure regulation (Mayer waves) — the sympathetic-vagal balance.

## 1.4 Why Spectral Analysis of HRV?

Time-domain HRV metrics (SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50) are useful but **collapse all variability into single numbers**, losing frequency information. Spectral analysis (via the Periodogram) **decomposes HRV into physiological frequency bands**, each linked to a specific autonomic mechanism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Frequency Bands of HRV Power Spectrum:**
>
> | Band | Frequency Range | Physiological Correlate |
> |---|---|---|
> | **VLF** (Very Low Frequency) | **0.003 – 0.04 Hz** | Thermoregulation, renin–angiotensin, slow humoral mechanisms |
> | **LF** (Low Frequency) | **0.04 – 0.15 Hz** | Baroreflex, sympathetic + parasympathetic (mixed) — "Mayer waves" |
> | **HF** (High Frequency) | **0.15 – 0.4 Hz** | Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA) — **pure parasympathetic** |
> | **LF/HF Ratio** | — | Sympathovagal balance index |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of HRV Spectral Analysis

The end-to-end processing chain is:

1. **ECG Acquisition** — Record $N$ seconds of clean ECG at sampling rate $F_s$ (typically ≥ 256 Hz, ideally 500–1000 Hz for R-peak fidelity).
2. **Pre-processing** — Band-pass filter (0.5–40 Hz) to remove baseline wander, mains interference, and high-frequency noise.
3. **R-peak Detection** — Apply Pan-Tompkins (or similar) algorithm to locate R-peak indices $n_i$.
4. **R-R Interval Extraction** — Compute $RR_i = (n_{i+1} - n_i)/F_s$ in seconds.
5. **Ectopic / Artifact Rejection** — Remove physiologically impossible intervals (e.g., $RR < 0.3\,\text{s}$ or $RR > 2.0\,\text{s}$); apply interpolation if needed.
6. **Resampling** — Resample the irregularly-sampled tachogram to an equispaced series at $F_s^{\text{HRV}} = 1$ to $4\,\text{Hz}$ (typically **4 Hz**).
7. **Detrending & Windowing** — Remove mean and apply a window (Hanning/Hamming) to reduce spectral leakage.
8. **Periodogram Computation** — Compute $P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2$.
9. **Band Power Integration** — Integrate $P_{xx}(\omega)$ over the VLF, LF, HF ranges.
10. **Feature Extraction** — Compute LF, HF (in ms² or normalized units), LF/HF ratio.

## 2.2 The Direct Periodogram — Why It Is Biased Yet Inconsistent

The direct periodogram is computed over the *entire* signal as a single block:

$$
P_{\text{direct}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j \omega n} \right\vert^2
$$

**Properties:**
- **Asymptotically unbiased:** $E\{P_{\text{direct}}(\omega)\} \to P_{\text{true}}(\omega)$ as $N \to \infty$ (spectral resolution improves).
- **Variance does NOT decrease with $N$** — it remains of order $O\{P_{\text{true}}^2(\omega)\}$ regardless of record length. Hence the periodogram is **inconsistent**.

This is a fundamental limitation: longer recordings give better *resolution* but **no smoother** estimate.

## 2.3 Welch's Method (Averaged / Modified Periodogram) — Variance Reduction

To overcome the high-variance problem, **Welch (1967)** proposed:

1. Segment the data $x(n)$ of length $N$ into $K$ overlapping (or non-overlapping) sub-segments $x_i(n)$ of length $L$, with $50\%$ overlap being standard.
2. Window each segment with a taper $w(n)$ (Hanning is common).
3. Compute the periodogram of each windowed segment.
4. Average the periodograms.

$$
P_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K \cdot L \cdot U} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) \, x_i(n) \, e^{-j \omega n} \right\vert^2
$$

where $U = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ is the window-energy normalization constant.

**Variance reduction** (Bartlett's formula):

$$
\text{Var}\{P_{\text{Welch}}\} \approx \frac{1}{K} \cdot P_{\text{true}}^2(\omega)
$$

Thus, **averaging $K$ segments reduces variance by a factor of $K$**, at the cost of reduced frequency resolution (segment length $L$ determines the resolution: $\Delta f = F_s / L$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme — HRV Periodogram Analysis**

| # | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RR_n = (n_{n+1} - n_n) / F_s$ | R-R interval extraction, units: seconds |
| 2 | $HR_n = 60 / RR_n$ | Instantaneous heart rate, units: bpm |
| 3 | $P_{\text{periodogram}}(\omega_k) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \vert X(\omega_k) \vert^2$ | Direct periodogram, units: power |
| 4 | $P_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K L U} \sum_{i} \vert X_i(\omega) \vert^2$ | Welch averaged periodogram |
| 5 | $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ | Window-energy normalization |
| 6 | $\Delta f = F_s / L$ | Frequency resolution, units: Hz |
| 7 | $\text{Var}\{P_{\text{Welch}}\} \approx P^2_{\text{true}} / K$ | Variance of Welch PSD |
| 8 | $\text{LF power} = \int_{0.04}^{0.15} P_{xx}(f) \, df$ | LF band power, units: $\text{ms}^2$ |
| 9 | $\text{HF power} = \int_{0.15}^{0.4} P_{xx}(f) \, df$ | HF band power, units: $\text{ms}^2$ |
| 10 | $\text{LFnu} = \text{LF}/(\text{LF} + \text{HF}) \times 100$ | LF in normalized units |
| 11 | $\text{HFnu} = \text{HF}/(\text{LF} + \text{HF}) \times 100$ | HF in normalized units |
| 12 | $\text{LF}/\text{HF} = \int_{0.04}^{0.15} P_{xx}(f) df \,\big/\, \int_{0.15}^{0.4} P_{xx}(f) df$ | Sympathovagal balance index |
| 13 | $S_x(\omega) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) e^{-j \omega m}$ | Wiener-Khinchin relation |
| 14 | $\text{SDNN} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(RR_i - \overline{RR})^2}$ | Time-domain HRV (ms) |
| 15 | $\text{RMSSD} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N-1}(RR_{i+1} - RR_i)^2}$ | Time-domain HRV (ms) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on $P_{xx}(f) df$ integration:** The trapezoidal rule on the discrete PSD samples approximates these integrals. Units of the integrated power are $\text{ms}^2$ when the tachogram is in ms.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical Cardiology** — Reduced HRV predicts post-myocardial-infarction mortality, diabetic autonomic neuropathy, and sudden cardiac death.
- **Stress & Mental Health Research** — HF component tracks parasympathetic withdrawal under acute stress.
- **Sports Physiology** — Trained endurance athletes exhibit elevated HRV (high HF, high total power).
- **Wearable Devices & mHealth** — Smartwatches and fitness bands use Welch-type periodogram variants for real-time HRV monitoring.
- **Sleep Stage Classification** — HF power rises during deep (NREM) sleep and falls during REM.

## 2.6 Why a Periodogram (Non-Parametric) for HRV?

- **Physiological signals are short and non-stationary** — parametric AR models risk model-order mis-specification.
- The **periodogram is model-free, robust, and computationally simple** (FFT-based).
- The **Welch variant balances variance and resolution** appropriately for typical 5-minute HRV recordings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Periodogram from the Wiener-Khinchin Theorem

### Step 1 — Start with the autocorrelation sequence

For a wide-sense stationary (WSS) random process $x(n)$, the autocorrelation is:

$$
r_{xx}(m) = E\{x(n) \, x(n+m)\}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Wiener-Khinchin theorem

The PSD is the Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT) of $r_{xx}(m)$:

$$
P_{xx}(\omega) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) \, e^{-j \omega m}
$$

### Step 3 — Estimate $r_{xx}(m)$ from a finite record

For a finite record $x(n)$, $n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, the biased autocorrelation estimate is:

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1 - \vert m \vert} x(n) \, x(n + \vert m \vert), \quad \vert m \vert < N
$$

The factor $1/N$ (not $1/(N-\vert m \vert)$) is the **biased** estimator — it is preferred because it guarantees that $\hat{P}_{xx}(\omega) \ge 0$ for all $\omega$.

### Step 4 — Substitute the estimate into Wiener-Khinchin

$$
\hat{P}_{xx}(\omega) = \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \hat{r}_{xx}(m) \, e^{-j \omega m}
$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange the double sum

$$
\hat{P}_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n) x(n + \vert m \vert) \, e^{-j \omega m}
$$

### Step 6 — Change variables and apply the Bartlett convolution trick

Let $k = n + m$, so $m = k - n$. The double sum becomes:

$$
\hat{P}_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{j \omega n} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(k) e^{-j \omega k}
$$

### Step 7 — Recognize the DFT

$$
\hat{P}_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \left( \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{j \omega n} \right) \left( \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(k) e^{-j \omega k} \right) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2
$$

This is the **Periodogram** $P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$. **Derivation complete.** ∎

## 3.2 Derivation of Welch's Variance Reduction

### Step 1 — Express the variance of the direct periodogram

It is a known result that the variance of the direct periodogram equals the square of the true PSD (for Gaussian processes, the periodogram at each frequency is exponentially distributed):

$$
\text{Var}\{P_{\text{direct}}(\omega_k)\} = P_{xx}^2(\omega_k) \quad \text{(independent of } N \text{)}
$$

### Step 2 — Segment the data into $K$ non-overlapping sub-records

Each segment $x_i(n)$ has length $L = N/K$. Compute the periodogram of each:

$$
P_i(\omega) = \frac{1}{L} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) x_i(n) e^{-j \omega n} \right\vert^2
$$

If segments are independent (no overlap, segment length $\ge$ correlation time), $P_i(\omega)$ are i.i.d. with mean $P_{xx}(\omega)$ and variance $P_{xx}^2(\omega)$.

### Step 3 — Average the periodograms

$$
P_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} P_i(\omega)
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the variance of a sum of i.i.d. variables

$$
\text{Var}\{P_{\text{Welch}}(\omega)\} = \text{Var}\left\{\frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} P_i(\omega)\right\} = \frac{1}{K^2} \sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \text{Var}\{P_i(\omega)\}
$$

$$
= \frac{1}{K^2} \cdot K \cdot P_{xx}^2(\omega) = \frac{P_{xx}^2(\omega)}{K}
$$

This is **Bartlett's formula**. The variance is reduced by a factor of $K$. **Derivation complete.** ∎

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — HRV Periodogram Bands

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A 5-minute ECG is recorded. After R-peak detection, the R-R intervals (in ms) for $N=20$ beats are (assumed pre-processed):
>
> $RR = [800, 820, 790, 810, 800, 815, 805, 795, 810, 800, 820, 805, 795, 815, 805, 800, 810, 800, 815, 805]$ ms

### Step 1 — Compute the mean and detrend

$$
\overline{RR} = \frac{1}{20}\sum_{i=1}^{20} RR_i = \frac{16060}{20} = 803\,\text{ms}
$$

### Step 2 — Detrend (remove DC)

$$
x(i) = RR_i - \overline{RR}
$$

Resulting (ms): $[-3, +17, -13, +7, -3, +12, +2, -8, +7, -3, +17, +2, -8, +12, +2, -3, +7, -3, +12, +2]$

### Step 3 — Apply Hanning window

$$
w(n) = 0.5 - 0.5 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, 19
$$

### Step 4 — Compute FFT of windowed series

After FFT, $|X(k)|^2$ yields the periodogram samples. The frequency resolution is:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{F_s^{\text{HRV}}}{N} = \frac{4\,\text{Hz}}{20} = 0.2\,\text{Hz}
$$

(Too coarse for this example; in practice, $N \ge 256$ samples are used.)

### Step 5 — Illustrative band-power integration (assumed PSD values)

Suppose the periodogram yields a two-bin approximation:

- LF band ($0.04$–$0.15\,\text{Hz}$): bin centered at $0.1\,\text{Hz}$, $P(0.1) = 2500\,\text{ms}^2/\text{Hz}$
- HF band ($0.15$–$0.4\,\text{Hz}$): bin centered at $0.3\,\text{Hz}$, $P(0.3) = 1600\,\text{ms}^2/\text{Hz}$

### Step 6 — Integrate (rectangular rule, single bin each)

$$
\text{LF} = 2500 \times 0.11 = 275\,\text{ms}^2
$$

$$
\text{HF} = 1600 \times 0.25 = 400\,\text{ms}^2
$$

### Step 7 — Compute the LF/HF ratio

$$
\text{LF}/\text{HF} = \frac{275}{400} = 0.6875
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** LF/HF < 1 indicates parasympathetic (vagal) dominance, consistent with a relaxed, resting state. A value > 2 would suggest sympathetic dominance (stress, exercise, pathology).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Computation Pipeline

| Step | Operation | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Band-pass filter | Raw ECG (Fs = 500 Hz) | Cleaned ECG (0.5–40 Hz) |
| 2 | Pan-Tompkins R-peak detection | Cleaned ECG | R-peak indices $\{n_i\}$ |
| 3 | R-R extraction | $\{n_i\}$ | Tachogram $RR_i$ (irregular) |
| 4 | Artifact rejection | Tachogram | Filtered tachogram |
| 5 | Cubic-spline resampling | Irregular tachogram | Equispaced $x(n)$ at 4 Hz |
| 6 | Detrend & window | $x(n)$ | $x_w(n)$ |
| 7 | FFT | $x_w(n)$ | $X(k)$ |
| 8 | Periodogram | $X(k)$ | $P_{xx}(f_k) = \vert X(k)\vert^2 / N$ |
| 9 | Band integration | $P_{xx}(f_k)$ | VLF, LF, HF (ms²) |
| 10 | Compute ratios | LF, HF | LF/HF, LFnu, HFnu |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of HRV Periodogram Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ECG Signal Acquisition Fs 500 Hz] --> B[Band Pass Filter 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    B --> C[Pan Tompkins R Peak Detector]
    C --> D[RR Interval Extraction]
    D --> E[Artifact and Ectopic Rejection]
    E --> F[Cubic Spline Resampling at 4 Hz]
    F --> G[Detrend Mean Removal]
    G --> H[Apply Hanning Window]
    H --> I[FFT Computation N point]
    I --> J[Periodogram Magnitude Squared Divided by N]
    J --> K[PSD Estimate Pxx of f]
    K --> L[Band Power Integration VLF LF HF]
    L --> M[HRV Features LF HF LF over HF Ratio]
    M --> N[Clinical Interpretation]
    N1[Sympathovagal Balance] --> N
    N2[Parasympathetic Tone] --> N
    N3[Cardiovascular Risk Stratification] --> N
```

## 4.2 Frequency-Band Decomposition Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSD[HRV Power Spectral Density] --> B1[VLF 0.003 to 0.04 Hz]
    PSD --> B2[LF 0.04 to 0.15 Hz]
    PSD --> B3[HF 0.15 to 0.4 Hz]
    B1 --> I1[Thermoregulation]
    B1 --> I2[Renin Angiotensin System]
    B2 --> I3[Baroreflex Activity]
    B2 --> I4[Sympathovagal Mix]
    B3 --> I5[Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia]
    B3 --> I6[Parasympathetic Vagal]
    B2 --> R[LF over HF Ratio]
    B3 --> R
    R --> I7[Sympathovagal Balance Index]
```

## 4.3 Periodogram Variant Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Choose HRV Spectral Method] --> Q1{Signal Length N}
    Q1 -->|Short 1 to 5 min| P1[Direct Periodogram]
    Q1 -->|Medium 5 to 30 min| P2[Welch Averaged Periodogram]
    Q1 -->|Long more than 30 min| P3[Welch with Narrow Segments]
    Q1 -->|Non Stationary| P4[Short Time Fourier Transform]
    P2 --> P2A[Segment Length 256 samples]
    P2 --> P2B[50 Percent Overlap]
    P2 --> P2C[Average K Periodograms]
    P2A --> P2C
    P2B --> P2C
    P2C --> P2D[Reduced Variance PSD]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow from R-Peak to Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[R Peak at t1] --> S1[RR1 t2 minus t1]
    R2[R Peak at t2] --> S1
    S1 --> T[Tachogram Series]
    R2 --> S2[RR2 t3 minus t2]
    R3[R Peak at t3] --> S2
    S2 --> T
    T --> RS[Resample at 4 Hz]
    RS --> WT[Windowed Tachogram]
    WT --> FT[FFT]
    FT --> PG[Periodogram]
    PG --> SPEC[HRV Power Spectrum]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
> Define Heart Rate Variability (HRV). List the standard frequency bands used in its spectral analysis and state the physiological correlate of each band.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Heart Rate Variability (HRV) is the variation in the time interval between successive R-R peaks of the QRS complex in the ECG, reflecting the continuous modulation of the sinoatrial node by the autonomic nervous system (ANS).

**Standard Frequency Bands (2 marks):**

| Band | Range | Physiological Correlate |
|---|---|---|
| VLF (Very Low Frequency) | 0.003 – 0.04 Hz | Thermoregulation, renin–angiotensin system, humoral factors |
| LF (Low Frequency) | 0.04 – 0.15 Hz | Baroreflex control; mixed sympathetic + parasympathetic activity (Mayer waves) |
| HF (High Frequency) | 0.15 – 0.4 Hz | Respiratory Sinus Arrhythmia (RSA) — pure parasympathetic (vagal) tone |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> What is a Periodogram? State its formula and explain why Welch's averaged periodogram is preferred over the direct periodogram.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Periodogram is a non-parametric estimate of the Power Spectral Density (PSD) of a signal, computed as the squared magnitude of the normalized Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT).

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
P_{xx}(\omega_k) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \vert X(\omega_k) \vert^2
$$

**Why Welch's method is preferred (1 mark):** The direct periodogram has a **variance that does not decrease** with increasing record length $N$ (it is asymptotically unbiased but not consistent). Welch's method segments the data into $K$ overlapping sub-records, computes the periodogram of each, and **averages** them — reducing the variance by a factor of $K$ at the cost of slightly reduced frequency resolution.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Direct Periodogram** from the Wiener-Khinchin theorem, clearly showing each algebraic step. State the two key statistical properties of the direct periodogram estimator. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete procedure for **spectral analysis of HRV using Welch's averaged periodogram** method. Define LF/HF ratio and explain its physiological significance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Autocorrelation definition (1 mark):**

For a WSS process, $r_{xx}(m) = E\{x(n) x(n+m)\}$.

**Step 2 — Wiener-Khinchin theorem (1 mark):**

$$
P_{xx}(\omega) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(m) e^{-j \omega m}
$$

**Step 3 — Biased autocorrelation estimate from finite data (1 mark):**

$$
\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n) x(n + \vert m \vert)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitution (1 mark):**

$$
\hat{P}_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=-(N-1)}^{N-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-\vert m \vert} x(n) x(n + \vert m \vert) e^{-j \omega m}
$$

**Step 5 — Change of variables and Bartlett's identity (1 mark):**

Let $k = n + m$:

$$
\hat{P}_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{j \omega n} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(k) e^{-j \omega k}
$$

**Step 6 — Identification (1 mark):**

$$
\hat{P}_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X(\omega) \vert^2 = P_{\text{periodogram}}(\omega)
$$

**Step 7 — Key statistical properties (1 mark):**

1. **Asymptotically unbiased:** $E\{P_{\text{direct}}(\omega)\} \to P_{\text{true}}(\omega)$ as $N \to \infty$ (resolution $\Delta f = F_s/N \to 0$).
2. **Inconsistent / high variance:** $\text{Var}\{P_{\text{direct}}(\omega)\} \approx P_{\text{true}}^2(\omega)$ — independent of $N$, hence a poor estimator for finite records.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Block diagram description (2 marks):**

Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram. The pipeline: ECG → Band-pass filter → Pan-Tompkins R-peak detection → RR-interval tachogram → Artifact rejection → Cubic-spline resampling at 4 Hz → Detrend & Hanning window → Welch periodogram (FFT-based, averaged over $K$ overlapping segments) → Band-power integration → LF, HF, LF/HF.

**Step 2 — Welch's averaging formula (2 marks):**

$$
P_{\text{Welch}}(\omega) = \frac{1}{K L U} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n) x_i(n) e^{-j \omega n} \right\vert^2
$$

with $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum w^2(n)$ and typical $50\%$ segment overlap. Variance reduces by factor $K$.

**Step 3 — LF/HF ratio definition (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{LF/HF} = \frac{\int_{0.04}^{0.15} P_{xx}(f) df}{\int_{0.15}^{0.4} P_{xx}(f) df}
$$

**Step 4 — Physiological significance (1.5 marks):**

- LF/HF **low (< 1)** → parasympathetic dominance (relaxed, resting, deep sleep, athletes).
- LF/HF **high (> 2)** → sympathetic dominance (stress, exercise, heart failure, anxiety).
- It is the **sympathovagal balance index** — a high value is associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the concept of Heart Rate Variability (HRV). Discuss in detail the various frequency-domain parameters used in HRV analysis along with their physiological significance. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the **Direct Periodogram** and **Welch's Averaged Periodogram** methods in terms of (i) mathematical formulation, (ii) statistical properties (bias and variance), (iii) frequency resolution, and (iv) computational complexity. State two clinical applications of HRV spectral analysis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Concept of HRV (1.5 marks):**

HRV is the beat-to-beat variation in cardiac cycle length, measured from successive R-R intervals of the ECG. It is a non-invasive quantitative marker of autonomic cardiovascular regulation. HRV arises from continuous interplay between sympathetic (accelerator) and parasympathetic (brake) branches of the ANS acting on the SA node.

**Step 2 — Tachogram generation (1 mark):**

Tachogram: $RR_n = (n_{n+1} - n_n) / F_s$, plotted vs. beat index. Even with constant resting HR, the intervals fluctuate.

**Step 3 — Frequency-domain parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Definition | Physiological Significance |
|---|---|---|
| VLF power | $\int_{0.003}^{0.04} P_{xx}(f) df$ | Thermoregulation, humoral, renin–angiotensin |
| LF power | $\int_{0.04}^{0.15} P_{xx}(f) df$ | Baroreflex; mixed sympathetic + parasympathetic; "Mayer waves" |
| HF power | $\int_{0.15}^{0.4} P_{xx}(f) df$ | Respiratory sinus arrhythmia; pure vagal/parasympathetic |
| LFnu | $\frac{\text{LF}}{\text{LF}+\text{HF}} \times 100$ | Sympathetic index (normalized) |
| HFnu | $\frac{\text{HF}}{\text{LF}+\text{HF}} \times 100$ | Parasympathetic index (normalized) |
| LF/HF ratio | LF/HF | Sympathovagal balance |

**Step 4 — Physiological significance summary (1.5 marks):**

- High HF → strong parasympathetic tone (rest, sleep, fitness).
- High LF/HF → sympathetic dominance (stress, pathology).
- Reduced total power → poor autonomic regulation; predictor of mortality post-MI.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Direct Periodogram | Welch's Averaged Periodogram |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Formulation** | $P(\omega) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ on whole record | $P_W(\omega) = \frac{1}{KLU}\sum_{i=1}^{K}\vert X_i(\omega) \vert^2$ over $K$ windowed overlapping segments |
| **(ii) Bias** | Asymptotically unbiased, but bias $\to 0$ only as $N \to \infty$ | Slightly biased (window smoothing) — bias-variance trade-off |
| **(ii) Variance** | $\text{Var} \approx P^2_{\text{true}}$ — **independent of $N$** (inconsistent) | $\text{Var} \approx P^2_{\text{true}}/K$ — **reduced by factor $K$** (consistent) |
| **(iii) Frequency resolution** | $\Delta f = F_s / N$ — best possible | $\Delta f = F_s / L$ — coarser, $L < N$ |
| **(iv) Computational cost** | Single FFT of length $N$ — cheap | $K$ FFTs of length $L$ — $K$× more computation, but parallelizable |

**Two Clinical Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Post-Myocardial Infarction Risk Stratification:** Reduced SDNN and LF/HF imbalance are independent predictors of mortality and arrhythmic events after MI.
2. **Diabetic Autonomic Neuropathy:** Depressed HRV (especially loss of HF power) is an early marker of parasympathetic denervation in long-standing diabetes.
3. *(Optional extra)* Heart-failure severity grading, sudden infant death syndrome screening, athlete overtraining detection.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/N$ normalization** in the periodogram formula — KTU examiners explicitly test this. Marks lost: **2 marks**.
> 2. **Conflating VLF lower bound (0.003 Hz)** with 0.04 Hz — VLF starts at 0.003, not 0.04. Mixing LF and VLF ranges is a frequent error.
> 3. **Stating that the direct periodogram is biased** — it is **asymptotically unbiased** but with high variance. Many students say "unbiased" and lose a mark.
> 4. **Writing "Welch's periodogram increases resolution"** — **FALSE**. Welch *trades* resolution for variance reduction. Be precise.
> 5. **Skipping units** — Band powers must be reported in $\text{ms}^2$ (or $\text{s}^2$), frequencies in Hz.
> 6. **Confusing instantaneous HR with HRV** — $HR = 60/RR$ is a single point; HRV is the *variability* of the sequence.
> 7. **Forgetting to detrend** the tachogram before FFT — DC offset leaks into the VLF band and inflates it.
> 8. **Not mentioning resampling** when the R-R tachogram is irregular — examiners specifically test this in Module 4.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — HRV Periodogram Analysis**

- **HRV** = variability of R-R intervals; reflects autonomic modulation of the heart.
- **Tachogram** = sequence of R-R intervals (in ms) plotted vs. beat number; the input to spectral analysis.
- **Periodogram** = $\frac{1}{N}\vert X(\omega) \vert^2$ — non-parametric PSD estimate via squared DFT magnitude.
- **Statistical flaw of direct periodogram:** variance does **not** decrease with $N$ → inconsistent.
- **Welch's method:** segment → window → FFT → average → variance reduced by $K$, resolution reduced.
- **Window energy:** $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum w^2(n)$ normalizes the periodogram.
- **Resolution:** $\Delta f = F_s/L$ for Welch, $F_s/N$ for direct.
- **Standard HRV bands:** VLF (0.003–0.04 Hz), LF (0.04–0.15 Hz), HF (0.15–0.4 Hz).
- **HF ↔ parasympathetic (vagal, respiration-coupled RSA).**
- **LF ↔ baroreflex (sympathetic + parasympathetic).**
- **LF/HF ratio ↔ sympathovagal balance.**
- **Pipeline:** ECG → filter → R-peak (Pan-Tompkins) → RR → reject artifacts → resample (4 Hz) → window → FFT → integrate bands → compute features.
- **Bias-variance trade-off:** Welch *increases* bias slightly but *reduces* variance dramatically.
- **Why non-parametric for HRV?** Short, non-stationary recordings + no model-order selection needed.
- **Clinical uses:** post-MI mortality prediction, diabetic autonomic neuropathy, heart-failure grading, stress/athletic-performance monitoring.
- **Key numbers to memorize:** $F_s^{\text{HRV}} = 4\,\text{Hz}$, segment length $L = 256$, 50% overlap, Hanning window.
- **Wiener-Khinchin:** PSD = DTFT of autocorrelation — the foundation linking time-domain to frequency-domain HRV.
- **Knee-deep fact:** The Periodogram is always **non-negative** — a defining property of a true PSD estimate.
- **Pitfall:** Do NOT apply the periodogram directly to the ECG — always extract and resample the R-R tachogram first.
- **Tip for KTU numericals:** Always state the frequency band limits explicitly before integrating the PSD; examiners award method marks even if integration arithmetic is partially wrong.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive Filters - Principle of Wiener Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adaptive Filters - Principle of Wiener Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Adaptive Filter (KTU 2024 Definition):** An adaptive filter is a time-varying filter whose coefficients (tap weights) are *self-adjusting* in response to changes in the statistical properties of the input signal and the operating environment. Unlike fixed (FIR/IIR) filters designed for a *known* spectrum, adaptive filters continuously minimize a pre-defined error criterion (typically the Mean Square Error) by updating their weights using recursive algorithms such as **LMS (Least Mean Squares)** or **RLS (Recursive Least Squares)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wiener Filter (KTU 2024 Definition):** The Wiener filter is the *optimum linear filter* that minimizes the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** between its output and a desired/reference signal, assuming the input signals are **wide-sense stationary (WSS)** and the second-order statistics (mean, autocorrelation, cross-correlation) are known. The filter is called *optimum in the mean-square sense*, and its solution is governed by the **Wiener-Hopf Equation**.

## 1.2 Key Constituents of an Adaptive System

A canonical adaptive filter system in biomedical signal processing (e.g., **50/60 Hz mains interference removal** from ECG) consists of four building blocks:

1. **Input Signal, $x(n)$** – The reference noise correlated with the contaminating noise.
2. **Desired Signal, $d(n)$** – The clean primary signal (e.g., the noise-free ECG).
3. **Adaptive Filter, $w(n)$** – A linear combiner (FIR structure) with adjustable weights $w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{M-1}$.
4. **Error Signal, $e(n)$** – The difference $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$, used as feedback to update the weights.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Noisy Hospital Room"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **patient monitoring room** in an ICU. A cardiac patient is connected to an ECG machine, but the trace is contaminated by a **constant 50 Hz hum** from the AC power lines and the noise from nearby ventilators. The hospital maintenance engineer installs a **secondary microphone** near the AC source to pick up a *reference* noise signal $x(n)$ (the sound of the hum) — but it is *uncorrelated* with the patient's heart activity. An **adaptive noise canceller** is then used.

> The "engineer" inside the filter continuously **listens** to the hum through the reference microphone and **subtracts** a *modeled* version of that hum from the corrupted ECG. When the room conditions change (a new device is switched on, the patient moves), the filter *automatically re-tunes* its internal coefficients to match the new hum profile. This *self-adjusting* mechanism is exactly what **Wiener filtering** achieves mathematically, while **LMS/RLS** are the *real-time algorithms* that converge toward that Wiener solution.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need Adaptive Filters in Biomedical Applications?

- The statistics of biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG) are **non-stationary** — they change with time, patient state, and artifacts.
- A fixed FIR/IIR filter cannot track these changes.
- Adaptive filters provide **optimum tracking** by continuously re-optimizing their weights.
- Applications include: **mains-line interference removal, baseline wander suppression, motion artifact cancellation, fetal ECG extraction from maternal ECG, evoked potential estimation, and adaptive pacemakers.**

## 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics

- **Mean Square Error (MSE):** $\xi = E[e^2(n)]$
- **Minimum MSE (MMSE):** $\xi_{min}$ — achieved when the filter reaches its Wiener optimum.
- **Convergence Rate:** How fast the algorithm reaches the optimum.
- **Misadjustment:** Steady-state excess MSE (in %).
- **Computational Complexity:** Measured in **multiplications and additions per iteration (MIPS)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of the Wiener Filter — Logical Steps

The Wiener filter is derived under the following standard assumptions:

1. The input $x(n)$ and the desired signal $d(n)$ are **jointly wide-sense stationary** discrete-time random processes.
2. The filter is **linear** and of the **FIR (Finite Impulse Response)** type with $M$ taps.
3. The error criterion is the **mean-square value** of the error signal $e(n)$.
4. The filter is **optimum in the mean-square sense**, i.e., the error $e(n)$ is **orthogonal** to every input sample that influences the filter output. This is the celebrated **Principle of Orthogonality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Principle of Orthogonality (Core Theorem):** The mean-square error $\xi = E[e^2(n)]$ is minimized **if and only if** the error $e(n)$ is orthogonal to each input sample used in filtering, i.e.,  
> $$E[x(n-k) \cdot e(n)] = 0, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, M-1$$

## 2.2 Mathematical Setup of the FIR Wiener Filter

Let the filter have tap weights $w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{M-1}$ and let the input vector at instant $n$ be:

$$\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n),\; x(n-1),\; x(n-2),\; \ldots,\; x(n-M+1)]^{T}$$

The filter output is the **linear convolution**:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k \, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}(n)$$

The error signal is defined as:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$$

The mean-square error is:

$$\xi(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^2(n)] = E\left[\left(d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)\right)^2\right]$$

Expanding:

$$\xi(\mathbf{w}) = E[d^2(n)] - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)]$ is the **M × M autocorrelation matrix** of the input.
- $\mathbf{r}_{dx} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)]$ is the **M × 1 cross-correlation vector** between input and desired signal.

## 2.3 The Wiener-Hopf Equation (Core Result)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Setting the gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \xi = 0$ yields the fundamental **Wiener-Hopf Equation** in matrix form:
> $$\mathbf{R}_{xx}\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx}$$
> The optimum Wiener filter weights are therefore:
> $$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\,\mathbf{r}_{dx}$$
> This is the closed-form *offline* solution of the Wiener filtering problem.

## 2.4 Minimum Mean-Square Error (MMSE)

Substituting $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ back into the MSE expression gives the **minimum achievable MSE**:

$$\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}_{dx}^{T}\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}_{dx}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$$

where $\sigma_d^2 = E[d^2(n)]$ is the variance (power) of the desired signal.

## 2.5 Properties of the Autocorrelation Matrix $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$

- **Symmetric (Hermitian):** $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{T}$ for real signals.
- **Positive Semi-Definite (and Positive Definite for persistently exciting inputs):** $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{x} \ge 0$.
- **Toeplitz:** All elements along any diagonal are equal, i.e., $[\mathbf{R}_{xx}]_{ij} = r_{xx}(i-j)$.
- **Eigenstructure:** Has real, non-negative eigenvalues and a complete set of orthonormal eigenvectors.

## 2.6 The Error Performance Surface

The MSE $\xi(\mathbf{w})$ is a **quadratic function of the weights**. For $M = 2$, it forms a **paraboloid (bowl-shaped surface)**; for general $M$, it is a **hyperparaboloid**. The bottom of the bowl corresponds to $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$, and the bowl's shape is governed by the eigenstructure of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU favourite** question: *"Why is the performance surface of the Wiener filter quadratic?"*  
> **Answer:** Because $\xi(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}$ is a second-order polynomial in the weight vector. The Hessian (matrix of second derivatives) is $2\mathbf{R}_{xx}$, which is constant — hence the surface is a perfect (unrotated) quadratic paraboloid in the transformed principal-axis basis.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Physical / Engineering Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| Filter Output | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Estimated clean signal |
| Error Signal | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Residual noise / distortion |
| Mean-Square Error (MSE) | $\xi(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^{2}(n)]$ | Cost function to be minimized |
| Autocorrelation Matrix | $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)]$ | $M \times M$ Toeplitz matrix of input lags |
| Cross-Correlation Vector | $\mathbf{r}_{dx} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)]$ | $M \times 1$ cross-statistics |
| **Wiener-Hopf Equation** | $\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx}$ | **Core governing equation** |
| Optimum Weights | $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$ | Closed-form solution |
| Minimum MSE (MMSE) | $\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{r}_{dx}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$ | Best achievable error power |
| Gradient of MSE | $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\xi = 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{r}_{dx}$ | First derivative w.r.t. weights |
| Orthogonality Condition | $E[\mathbf{x}(n)e_{opt}(n)] = 0$ | Optimum error uncorrelated with input |
| Eigen-decomposition | $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^{T}$ | Principal-axis rotation of bowl |
| Optimum weights (EVD form) | $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \dfrac{q_{k}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx}}{\lambda_{k}}\,q_{k}$ | Modal expansion of solution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The Wiener filter is the theoretical *gold standard* for optimal linear estimation used in **system identification, channel equalization, speech enhancement, image restoration, denoising of fMRI/EEG data, and predictive coding in pacemakers and cochlear implants**.

## 2.8 Conditions for Existence and Uniqueness

- $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ must be **invertible** (i.e., positive definite). This requires the input to be *persistently exciting* and have **linearly independent samples**.
- If the input is a sum of $L$ sinusoids, the filter must have **at least $M \ge 2L$ taps** for proper statistical estimation.
- The WSS assumption must hold over the observation window; otherwise the solution is time-varying.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: From MSE Cost Function to the Wiener-Hopf Equation

We start with the mean-square error:

$$\xi(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^2(n)] = E\left[\left(d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)\right)^{2}\right]$$

**Step 1 — Expand the squared term.** Using the identity $(a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$:

$$\xi(\mathbf{w}) = E[d^{2}(n)] - 2E[d(n)\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)] + E[(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n))^{2}]$$

**Step 2 — Take $E[\cdot]$ inside, treating $\mathbf{w}$ as deterministic.**

$$\xi(\mathbf{w}) = E[d^{2}(n)] - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}E[\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)] + \mathbf{w}^{T}E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)]\mathbf{w}$$

*Explanation:* For the second term we used the fact that $d(n)$ is a scalar and $E[\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)] = \mathbf{w}^{T}E[\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)]$ since $\mathbf{w}$ is non-random. For the third term, $E[(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x})(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x})^{T}] = \mathbf{w}^{T}E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^{T}]\mathbf{w}$.

**Step 3 — Define the statistical quantities.**

$$\sigma_d^{2} \triangleq E[d^{2}(n)],\quad \mathbf{r}_{dx} \triangleq E[\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)],\quad \mathbf{R}_{xx} \triangleq E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)]$$

Therefore:

$$\xi(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^{2} - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate $\xi(\mathbf{w})$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ (gradient vector).** Using matrix calculus identities:

$$\frac{\partial (\sigma_d^{2})}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{0},\quad \frac{\partial (\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx})}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{r}_{dx},\quad \frac{\partial (\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w})}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}$$

Hence:

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\xi = -2\mathbf{r}_{dx} + 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 5 — Set the gradient to zero for the minimum.**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\xi = \mathbf{0} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = 2\mathbf{r}_{dx}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Wiener-Hopf Equation** in canonical matrix form:
> $$\boxed{\;\mathbf{R}_{xx}\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx}\;}$$
> Solving (assuming $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ is non-singular):
> $$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the minimum (not maximum).** The Hessian is $2\mathbf{R}_{xx}$, which is positive definite. Therefore the stationary point is a **global minimum**.

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Minimum Mean-Square Error $\xi_{min}$

We substitute the optimum weight vector back into $\xi(\mathbf{w})$.

**Step 1 — Start from the expanded MSE:**

$$\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^{2} - 2\mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx} + \mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Wiener-Hopf result $\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx}$.**

The third term becomes:

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^{2} - 2\mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx} + \mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the explicit optimum weights.**

$$\boxed{\;\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{r}_{dx}^{T}\,\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\,\mathbf{r}_{dx}\;}$$

This is a **scalar**, $\ge 0$, with equality only when the desired signal $d(n)$ is entirely predictable from the input $x(n)$.

## 3.3 Derivation #3: The Orthogonality Principle

At the optimum, the error must satisfy:

$$E[\mathbf{x}(n)\,e_{opt}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 1 — Recall the error expression:**

$$e_{opt}(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cross-correlation:**

$$E[\mathbf{x}(n)e_{opt}(n)] = E[\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)] - E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)]\mathbf{w}_{opt}$$

**Step 3 — Use the definitions and substitute the Wiener-Hopf equation:**

$$E[\mathbf{x}(n)e_{opt}(n)] = \mathbf{r}_{dx} - \mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx} - \mathbf{r}_{dx} = \mathbf{0}$$

This is the **geometric interpretation** of the Wiener filter: the projection of $d(n)$ onto the subspace spanned by $\{x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M+1)\}$ is $y(n)$, and the *residual* $e_{opt}(n)$ is orthogonal to the subspace.

## 3.4 Derivation #4: Eigenvalue Expansion of the Optimum Solution

Let $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^{T}$ where $\mathbf{Q}$ contains orthonormal eigenvectors $q_k$ and $\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_0, \ldots, \lambda_{M-1})$.

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Wiener-Hopf equation:**

$$\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^{T}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Pre-multiply by $\mathbf{Q}^{T}$:**

$$\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^{T}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{Q}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Define rotated weight vector $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{Q}^{T}\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ and rotated cross-correlation $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{Q}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$.** The system decouples into $M$ scalar equations:

$$\lambda_k\, v_k = p_k \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v_k = \frac{p_k}{\lambda_k}$$

**Step 4 — Transform back:**

$$\boxed{\;\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} \frac{q_k^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx}}{\lambda_k}\, q_k\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Each eigenmode $q_k$ contributes to $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ in proportion to the cross-correlation with the desired signal ($q_k^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$) and inversely proportional to the input's power along that mode ($\lambda_k$). Small eigenvalues correspond to *ill-conditioned* directions where noise dominates and weight growth is large — this directly motivates the **eigenvalue spread** controlling LMS convergence speed.

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem:** A 2-tap Wiener filter is required. The input autocorrelation matrix and cross-correlation vector are:

$$\mathbf{R}_{xx} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{r}_{dx} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \sigma_d^{2} = 5$$

Find the optimum weights and the MMSE.

**Step 1 — Compute the determinant of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$:**

$$\det(\mathbf{R}_{xx}) = (2)(2) - (1)(1) = 4 - 1 = 3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inverse:**

$$\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$:**

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 - 2 \\ -1 + 4 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\xi_{min}$:**

$$\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{r}_{dx}^{T}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = 5 - [1,\;2]\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = 5 - 2 = 3$$

**Step 5 — Verify using the full formula:**

$$\mathbf{r}_{dx}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx} = [1, 2]\,\frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}[1, 2]\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}(6) = 2$$

$$\xi_{min} = 5 - 2 = 3 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Comment:** Always show the determinant, the inverse (or the use of Cramer's rule for $2\times 2$ systems), and end with a *verification* using the alternative $\xi_{min}$ formula. This guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

- No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone node names.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Adaptive Filter with Wiener Solution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput["INPUT SIGNAL X N"] --> nodeTaps["ADAPTIVE FILTER W0 ... WM-1"]
    nodeTaps --> nodeOutput["FILTER OUTPUT Y N"]
    nodeDesired["DESIRED SIGNAL D N"] --> nodeSum["SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    nodeOutput --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeError["ERROR SIGNAL E N"]
    nodeError --> nodeAlgo["ADAPTIVE ALGORITHM LMS OR RLS"]
    nodeInput --> nodeAlgo
    nodeAlgo --> nodeUpdate["WEIGHT UPDATE W N+1"]
    nodeUpdate --> nodeTaps
    nodeError --> nodeMSE["MEAN SQUARE ERROR XI W"]
    nodeMSE --> nodeOpt["WIENER HOPF EQUATION R W OPT EQUALS R DX"]
    nodeOpt --> nodeMMSE["MINIMUM MSE XI MIN"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Wiener Filter Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStep1["STEP 1 DEFINE MSE XI W E E SQUARED N"] --> nodeStep2["STEP 2 EXPAND QUADRATIC FORM"]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3["STEP 3 COMPUTE GRADIENT NABLA W XI"]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4["STEP 4 SET GRADIENT TO ZERO"]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5["STEP 5 WIENER HOPF R W OPT EQUALS R DX"]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6["STEP 6 SOLVE W OPT EQUALS R INV R DX"]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7["STEP 7 SUBSTITUTE BACK FOR XI MIN"]
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep8["STEP 8 VERIFY ORTHOGONALITY"]
    nodeStep8 --> nodeResult["OUTPUT OPTIMUM FILTER AND MMSE"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Error Performance Surface (M = 2 Taps)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisW0["W0 AXIS HORIZONTAL"] --> bowl["QUADRATIC BOWL PARABOLOID"]
    axisW1["W1 AXIS VERTICAL"] --> bowl
    bowl --> bottom["BOTTOM AT W OPT GIVES XI MIN"]
    bowl --> topEdge["RIM AT LARGE WEIGHTS GIVES HIGH MSE"]
    bowl --> slope["GRADIENT VECTOR POINTS UP THE SLOPE"]
    bottom --> eigen["EIGENVALUES LAMBDA 0 LAMBDA 1 CONTROL CURVATURE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture Description (for the in-text understanding):** In a typical biomedical noise-cancellation setup, the reference noise $x(n)$ (acquired from a noise-only sensor) is fed into the adaptive filter's tap-delay line. The tap outputs $x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M+1)$ are linearly combined using weights $w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{M-1}$ to produce $y(n)$. The corrupted biomedical signal (e.g., ECG with mains hum) is the desired signal $d(n)$. The error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ is fed back to the adaptive algorithm (LMS or RLS), which adjusts $\mathbf{w}(n)$ such that the MSE $E[e^2(n)]$ is minimized. At convergence, the weights $\mathbf{w}$ approach the **Wiener optimum** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$ and the error power reaches $\xi_{min}$. The shape of the convergence trajectory on the error performance surface is governed by the **eigenvalue spread** $\lambda_{max}/\lambda_{min}$ of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$.
