# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and explain the Principle of Orthogonality in Wiener filter theory.**

**Model Answer:**
The Principle of Orthogonality states that the mean-square error $\xi = E[e^2(n)]$ is minimized **if and only if** the error signal $e_{opt}(n)$ is orthogonal to every input sample that influences the filter output. Mathematically:

$$E[x(n-k)\,e_{opt}(n)] = 0, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$$

In vector form, $E[\mathbf{x}(n)\,e_{opt}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$. This implies that the optimum error contains no component that is linearly predictable from the input; all linearly extractable information has been captured by the filter output $y(n)$.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three applications of adaptive filters in biomedical signal processing.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Cancellation of 50/60 Hz mains interference** from ECG and EMG signals.
2. **Fetal ECG extraction** from maternal abdominal recordings using maternal ECG as reference.
3. **Motion artifact removal** in ambulatory ECG/EEG Holter monitoring.
4. **Adaptive averaging** for evoked potential (EP) estimation.
5. **Adaptive pacemakers** for atrial/ventricular event detection.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** For each 14-mark question, the standard pattern is *Part (a) — 7 marks* and *Part (b) — 7 marks*. Students must attempt *one full question* of their choice.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the Wiener-Hopf equation for an $M$-tap FIR filter using the principle of minimum mean-square error. Clearly state all assumptions and the orthogonality condition. (7 marks)

**(b)** The autocorrelation matrix and cross-correlation vector of a 2-tap Wiener filter problem are:

$$\mathbf{R}_{xx} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{r}_{dx} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \sigma_d^{2} = 4$$

Find (i) the optimum weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$, (ii) the minimum mean-square error $\xi_{min}$, and (iii) the output $y(n)$ if the input is $x(n) = [2, -1, 0, 1]^T$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Define the filter output, error, and MSE.**

Filter output: $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k x(n-k)$

Error: $e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$

MSE: $\xi(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^{2}(n)]$

**Step 2 — Expand the MSE.**

$$\xi(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^{2} - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero.**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\xi = -2\mathbf{r}_{dx} + 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Wiener-Hopf Equation.**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}}$$

**Step 5 — State the Orthogonality Condition.**

$$E[\mathbf{x}(n)e_{opt}(n)] = \mathbf{0} \;\;\text{i.e., the optimum error is uncorrelated with every input sample.}$$

**Assumptions:** jointly WSS processes, linear FIR filter, deterministic weights, real-valued signals.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Optimum weights.**

Determinant: $\det(\mathbf{R}_{xx}) = (3)(2) - (1)(1) = 5$

Inverse: $\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 4 - 1 \\ -2 + 3 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(ii) Minimum MSE.**

$$\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{r}_{dx}^{T}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = 4 - [2, 1]\begin{bmatrix} 0.6 \\ 0.2 \end{bmatrix} = 4 - 1.4 = 2.6$$

**(iii) Filter output for given input.**

Using $\mathbf{w}_{opt}^{T} = [0.6,\;0.2]$ and the input sequence $x(n) = [2, -1, 0, 1]^T$:

For an FIR filter, the output convolution gives (per the convolution $y(n) = w_0 x(n) + w_1 x(n-1)$):

- $y(0) = 0.6 \cdot x(0) = 0.6(2) = 1.2$
- $y(1) = 0.6 \cdot x(1) + 0.2 \cdot x(0) = 0.6(-1) + 0.2(2) = -0.6 + 0.4 = -0.2$
- $y(2) = 0.6 \cdot x(2) + 0.2 \cdot x(1) = 0.6(0) + 0.2(-1) = -0.2$
- $y(3) = 0.6 \cdot x(3) + 0.2 \cdot x(2) = 0.6(1) + 0.2(0) = 0.6$

So $y(n) = [1.2, -0.2, -0.2, 0.6]^T$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Common Mistakes):**
> 1. **Sign error in $\xi_{min}$ formula** — many students write $\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^2 + \mathbf{r}_{dx}^T\mathbf{w}_{opt}$. The correct sign is **minus**, not plus.
> 2. **Forgetting the determinant** when computing the inverse of a $2 \times 2$ matrix — KTU awards a *dedicated 1 mark* for showing the determinant calculation.
> 3. **Confusing the convolution direction** — for an FIR filter $y(n) = \sum_k w_k x(n-k)$, the impulse response coefficients are $w_0, w_1, \ldots$. Many students incorrectly reverse the order.
> 4. **Not stating the WSS assumption** explicitly — 1 mark is reserved for this in the derivation.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of an **adaptive filter** with a neat block diagram. Discuss why the **Wiener filter** forms the *theoretical foundation* of adaptive filtering. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a 2-tap FIR adaptive filter with input $x(n)$, derive the **error performance surface** $\xi(w_0, w_1)$. Explain how its shape is influenced by the **eigenvalues** of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition.** An adaptive filter is a self-designing filter that adjusts its coefficients automatically to minimize a chosen error criterion (typically MSE) in response to changes in the signal environment.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram Description (textual).** The system consists of:
- A tap-delay line $\{x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M+1)\}$.
- An adaptive linear combiner with weights $\{w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{M-1}\}$.
- A summing junction that forms the error $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- An adaptive algorithm block (LMS / RLS) that updates weights.

**Step 3 — Why Wiener is the Foundation.** The Wiener filter gives the *closed-form optimum* of the MSE cost function. Adaptive algorithms like LMS and RLS are essentially *iterative, real-time methods* that *converge* to the Wiener solution:

- **LMS** uses stochastic gradient descent: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- **RLS** directly minimizes the *exponentially weighted* least-squares error.
- Both algorithms, when converged, yield $\mathbf{w} \to \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$.

**Step 4 — Limitations of Wiener.** It requires stationary statistics and matrix inversion; this is why *adaptive* algorithms (LMS/RLS) were developed to track *non-stationary* biomedical signals.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Quadratic Form.** For $M = 2$:

$$\xi(w_0, w_1) = E[d^{2}(n)] - 2w_0 r_{dx}(0) - 2w_1 r_{dx}(1) + w_0^{2} r_{xx}(0) + 2w_0 w_1 r_{xx}(1) + w_1^{2} r_{xx}(0)$$

Using $\mathbf{R}_{xx} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(1) \\ r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) \end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{dx} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{dx}(0) \\ r_{dx}(1) \end{bmatrix}$:

$$\xi(w_0, w_1) = \sigma_d^{2} - 2[w_0,\,w_1]\begin{bmatrix} r_{dx}(0) \\ r_{dx}(1) \end{bmatrix} + [w_0,\,w_1]\begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(1) \\ r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} w_0 \\ w_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Hessian Analysis.** The Hessian (second derivative matrix) is:

$$\mathbf{H} = 2\mathbf{R}_{xx} = 2\begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(1) \\ r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) \end{bmatrix}$$

Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$:

$$\lambda_{max} = r_{xx}(0) + \vert r_{xx}(1) \vert, \quad \lambda_{min} = r_{xx}(0) - \vert r_{xx}(1) \vert$$

**Step 3 — Shape of the Bowl.**

- $\lambda_{min}$ governs the **shallowest curvature**; $\lambda_{max}$ governs the **steepest curvature**.
- The **eigenvalue spread** $\chi = \lambda_{max}/\lambda_{min}$ dictates how *elongated* the bowl is.
- A **large $\chi$** → highly elongated bowl → **slow LMS convergence**.
- A **small $\chi \approx 1$** → circular bowl → **fast LMS convergence**.
- For biomedical signals (e.g., colored EEG), $\chi$ is typically large (10–1000), making LMS slow and motivating **Normalized LMS (NLMS)** or **RLS**.

**Step 4 — Minimum Location.** The minimum lies at $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$, where $\xi = \xi_{min}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Many students forget to compute the *eigenvalue spread* $\chi$ explicitly. KTU asks: *"Discuss the relationship between eigenvalue spread and convergence speed."* Always compute $\chi = \lambda_{max}/\lambda_{min}$ and link it to the LMS step-size stability bound $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$ for a *complete answer*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Principle of Wiener Filters**

- ☐ **Adaptive Filter** = time-varying filter whose weights self-adjust to minimize an error criterion.
- ☐ **Wiener Filter** = optimum *linear* filter minimizing $E[e^2(n)]$ for WSS signals.
- ☐ **FIR Wiener Filter Output:** $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- ☐ **Error:** $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- ☐ **MSE Cost Function:** $\xi(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{dx} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}$.
- ☐ **Wiener-Hopf Equation:** $\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx}$.
- ☐ **Optimum Weights:** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$ (requires $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ to be positive definite).
- ☐ **Minimum MSE:** $\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}_{dx}^{T}\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{dx}$.
- ☐ **Principle of Orthogonality:** $E[\mathbf{x}(n) e_{opt}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$.
- ☐ **Error Performance Surface:** a *quadratic paraboloid* (hyperparaboloid in general), bowl-shaped, with unique global minimum at $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$.
- ☐ **Eigenvalue spread** $\chi = \lambda_{max}/\lambda_{min}$ governs LMS convergence speed.
- ☐ **Hessian** $= 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ (positive definite, confirms minimum).
- ☐ **Biomedical Applications:** 50/60 Hz notch (adaptive), fetal ECG extraction, motion artifact removal, evoked potential enhancement, adaptive pacemakers.
- ☐ **Adaptive algorithms (LMS, RLS, NLMS)** are *real-time* methods that *iteratively* approach the Wiener solution.
- ☐ **LMS Update Rule:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ with stability bound $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$.
- ☐ **Stability / Existence:** $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$ must be non-singular; persistency of excitation required.
- ☐ **Limitations of Wiener:** offline, requires stationary statistics, $O(M^3)$ inversion cost — motivates adaptive methods.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Mnemonic — "W O R D S H A P E":**
> **W**iener-Hopf Equation: $\mathbf{R}_{xx}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{r}_{dx}$
> **O**rthogonality: $E[\mathbf{x}\,e_{opt}] = 0$
> **R**esidual MMSE: $\xi_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{r}_{dx}^T\mathbf{w}_{opt}$
> **D**eterministic weights, stochastic signals
> **S**ymmetric & Toeplitz $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$
> **H**essian $= 2\mathbf{R}_{xx}$
> **A**daptive algorithms (LMS/RLS) approach it
> **P**erformance surface is **quadratic**
> **E**igen-spread controls convergence
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Adaptive Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle of Adaptive Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **Adaptive Filter** is a time-varying, self-designing digital filter whose coefficients (tap weights) are continuously updated by an adaptive algorithm in response to changes in the input signal statistics and the operating environment. The objective of the adaptive filter is to iteratively minimize a predefined error criterion (most commonly the **Mean Square Error (MSE)**) between a desired reference signal and the actual filter output.

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing**, adaptive filters are extensively employed because physiological signals such as the **Electrocardiogram (ECG)**, **Electroencephalogram (EEG)**, **Electromyogram (EMG)**, and **Fetal ECG (FECG)** are inherently non-stationary — meaning their statistical properties (mean, variance, spectral content) drift over time due to respiration, motion artifacts, electrode drift, and physiological state changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** An adaptive filter is characterized by three essential blocks:
> 1. A **Digital Filter Structure** (typically FIR or IIR) with adjustable coefficients $\mathbf{w}(n)$.
> 2. An **Error Measurement Unit** that produces the error signal $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
> 3. An **Adaptive Algorithm** (e.g., LMS, NLMS, RLS) that recursively updates the filter weights to minimize a cost function.

### 1.2 Adaptive Filter vs. Conventional Fixed Filter

| Parameter | Fixed (Wiener) Filter | Adaptive Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Coefficients** | Computed once from statistics | Updated continuously |
| **Signal Stationarity** | Requires **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** input | Works for **non-stationary** inputs |
| **Prior Statistics** | Needs $R_{xx}$ and $R_{xd}$ a priori | Learns statistics online |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (offline computation) | Moderate to high (recursive updates) |
| **Tracking Ability** | None (static solution) | Excellent (tracks slow variations) |
| **KTU Exam Keyword** | "Optimal but rigid" | "Suboptimal but flexible" |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hearing Aid in a Noisy Restaurant"

Imagine you are wearing a smart hearing aid inside a noisy restaurant. The device has a tiny microphone near your ear that picks up the **noise** (other people's chatter), and another microphone near the speaker's mouth that picks up the **desired voice mixed with noise**. The hearing aid must somehow remove the noise from the desired signal.

- The **noise microphone** acts as the **reference input** $x(n)$.
- The **microphone near the speaker** produces the **primary (desired) signal** $d(n)$ — contaminated with the same noise.
- The hearing aid's internal processor **learns** the acoustic path of the noise (by trial and error), produces an *anti-noise* $y(n)$, and subtracts it from $d(n)$.
- The difference — the **error signal** $e(n)$ — is the cleaned speech.

This *trial-and-error self-learning* behavior is exactly the **principle of adaptive filtering**. The "trial" is generating an anti-noise, and the "error" is fed back to adjust the internal weights so that the next trial produces a better anti-noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Recap:** A fixed filter is like a pair of prescription glasses made for one specific vision — it cannot adjust to changing light. An adaptive filter is like **auto-focus spectacles** that continuously re-adjust their lens power based on the changing scene. In biomedical signals, the "scene" is the patient's physiology — it never stays still.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

The following bolded terms appear repeatedly in KTU examination papers and must be memorized:

- **Mean Square Error (MSE):** $J(n) = E[\,e^2(n)\,]$ — the cost function to be minimized.
- **Convergence Rate:** Number of iterations required for the algorithm to reach the steady-state optimum.
- **Misadjustment:** Ratio of excess MSE (due to gradient noise) to the minimum achievable MSE.
- **Steady-State Error:** Residual error after the algorithm has converged.
- **Tracking:** Ability of the filter to follow slow statistical variations in the input.
- **Stability Bound:** Critical range of the step-size parameter $\mu$ that guarantees convergence.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Block-Level Architecture

Every adaptive filter, irrespective of its application, is described by **four canonical signals**:

- **Input vector:** $\mathbf{x}(n) = [\,x(n),\, x(n-1),\, \ldots,\, x(n-N+1)\,]^T$ (length $N$).
- **Desired (reference) signal:** $d(n)$.
- **Filter output:** $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- **Error signal:** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> Here, $\mathbf{w}(n) = [w_0(n), w_1(n), \ldots, w_{N-1}(n)]^T$ is the **weight vector** at discrete time $n$, and $N$ is the **filter order** (number of taps).

### 2.2 The Cost (Objective) Function

The mean square error is expanded as:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\,e^2(n)\,] = E[\,d^2(n)\,] - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T\,\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{R} = E[\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\,]$ — **Autocorrelation Matrix** (symmetric, positive definite, size $N \times N$).
- $\mathbf{p} = E[\,d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,]$ — **Cross-Correlation Vector** (length $N$).

This quadratic function defines the **Error Performance Surface**, which is a *paraboloid* in the $N$-dimensional weight space.

### 2.3 Optimal Solution — The Wiener-Hopf Equation

Setting the gradient $\nabla J = 0$ yields the **Wiener-Hopf Equation**, whose solution is the **Optimal Wiener Weight Vector**:

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}$$

The corresponding **Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE)** is:

$$J_{min} = E[\,d^2(n)\,] - \mathbf{p}^T\,\mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Wiener solution is the *theoretical optimum* but requires offline knowledge of $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$ — which is why we need adaptive algorithms that estimate these statistics *iteratively*.

### 2.4 Steepest Descent Update (Theoretical Foundation)

The gradient of $J$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ is:

$$\nabla J(n) = -2\,\mathbf{p} + 2\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}(n) = 2\,\mathbf{R}\bigl(\mathbf{w}(n) - \mathbf{w}_{opt}\bigr)$$

Steepest descent updates the weights in the direction of the negative gradient:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla J(n)$$

where $\mu$ is the **step-size (learning-rate) parameter**, governing convergence speed and stability.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Filter output (inner product of weights and inputs). Used in **all** adaptive applications. |
| 2 | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Error signal that drives adaptation. |
| 3 | $J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\,e^2(n)\,]$ | Mean Square Error cost function. |
| 4 | $J = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ | Quadratic expansion of MSE (defines paraboloid). |
| 5 | $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | **Wiener-Hopf solution** (optimum but non-causal in practice). |
| 6 | $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | Minimum achievable MSE. |
| 7 | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | **LMS algorithm** (Widrow-Hoff rule) — most widely used. |
| 8 | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ | Stability bound on step size ($\lambda_{max}$ = largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$). |
| 9 | $\tau \approx \dfrac{1}{2\mu\lambda_k}$ | Time constant of convergence along the $k$-th eigen-mode. |
| 10 | $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T]$, $\mathbf{p} = E[d\mathbf{x}]$ | Statistical matrices required by the Wiener filter. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The LMS algorithm is the *workhorse* of biomedical adaptive filters — used in **ECG noise cancellers, hearing aids, pacemakers, and fetal ECG extraction**. RLS converges faster but at higher computational cost (used in high-end defibrillators and patient monitoring systems).

### 2.6 The LMS Algorithm — Detailed Logic Steps

1. **Initialize** the weight vector $\mathbf{w}(0) = \mathbf{0}$ (zero vector) and choose a small step-size $\mu$.
2. At each time instant $n$, **acquire** the input sample $x(n)$ and the desired sample $d(n)$.
3. **Compute** the filter output: $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$.
4. **Compute** the error: $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
5. **Update** the weight vector: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$.
6. **Increment** $n \to n+1$ and **return to step 2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does LMS work?** It replaces the *true* gradient $\nabla J = 2\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{w}-\mathbf{w}_{opt})$ with its *instantaneous* (noisy) estimate $\hat{\nabla} J = 2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$. The expectation of this estimate equals the true gradient, so LMS performs **stochastic gradient descent** — converging "on average" toward the Wiener optimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Expansion of the Mean Square Error (MSE) Function

**Objective:** To derive the quadratic form $J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ starting from the definition $J(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^2(n)]$.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of the error signal:**

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 2 — Square the error signal:**

$$e^2(n) = \bigl[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\bigr]\bigl[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Expand the product using the distributive law:**

$$e^2(n) = d^2(n) - 2\,d(n)\,\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n) + \bigl[\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\bigr]\bigl[\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the last term is a scalar and equals $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\mathbf{w}$:**

$$e^2(n) = d^2(n) - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T\bigl[d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\bigr] + \mathbf{w}^T\bigl[\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\bigr]\mathbf{w}$$

> *Note: This step uses the scalar identity $a^T b = b^T a$ to reorder the middle term as a $\mathbf{w}^T(\cdot)$ product.*

**Step 5 — Take the expectation operator $E[\cdot]$ on both sides:**

$$E[e^2(n)] = E[d^2(n)] - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T\,E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] + \mathbf{w}^T\,E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)]\,\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the standard definitions:**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T\,\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The MSE is a *quadratic* function of $\mathbf{w}$, with the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ controlling the curvature and the cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p}$ controlling the displacement from the origin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimal Wiener Weight Vector

**Objective:** To find $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ that minimizes $J(\mathbf{w})$ by setting $\nabla J = 0$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate the quadratic form with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}}\bigl[\sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the matrix calculus identity** $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{a}) = \mathbf{a}$ **and** $\frac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ (since $\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric):

$$\nabla J = 0 - 2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$$

> *Justification: For a symmetric $\mathbf{R}$, the derivative of the scalar $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ is $(\mathbf{R} + \mathbf{R}^T)\mathbf{w} = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$.*

**Step 3 — Set the gradient to zero at the optimum:**

$$-2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange:**

$$2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = 2\mathbf{p}$$

**Step 5 — Pre-multiply by $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ (assuming $\mathbf{R}$ is positive definite and hence invertible):**

$$\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Wiener-Hopf equation**, the cornerstone result that the entire theory of adaptive filtering is built upon. Direct evaluation requires $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$, which is computationally expensive (and often impossible in real-time biomedical applications) — hence adaptive algorithms estimate it iteratively.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: The LMS Update Rule (from Instantaneous Gradient Approximation)

**Objective:** To derive the Widrow-Hoff LMS update $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.

**Step 1 — Start from the steepest-descent update:**

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla J(n)$$

**Step 2 — Use the true gradient from Section 3.2:**

$$\nabla J(n) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n) - 2\mathbf{p} = -2\,E[\,e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\,]$$

**Step 3 — Replace the *expectation* by its *instantaneous (sample) estimate* $e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$:**

$$\hat{\nabla}J(n) = -2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

> *This is the crucial LMS approximation — gradient noise is introduced, but expectations are preserved.*

**Step 4 — Substitute into the steepest-descent update:**

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\bigl[-2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Each tap weight is updated by an amount proportional to the *local error* and the *corresponding input sample*. This is why LMS converges fastest when input samples are large (large $x(n)$ ⇒ large correction) — and why normalization (NLMS) is often needed in practice.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Stability Bound on Step-Size $\mu$

**Objective:** To derive $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ for guaranteed convergence.

**Step 1 — Express the weight-error vector** $\mathbf{v}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mathbf{w}_{opt}$.

**Step 2 — Subtract $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ from both sides of the LMS update and substitute the optimality condition $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}$:**

$$E[\mathbf{v}(n+1)] = \bigl(\mathbf{I} - 2\mu\mathbf{R}\bigr)\,E[\mathbf{v}(n)]$$

**Step 3 — Diagonalize $\mathbf{R}$ using its eigenvector matrix $\mathbf{Q}$:** $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^T$, where $\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_N)$.

**Step 4 — The convergence along each eigen-direction is governed by the geometric series $E[v_k(n)] = (1 - 2\mu\lambda_k)^n E[v_k(0)]$, which converges to zero iff:**

$$\vert 1 - 2\mu\lambda_k \vert < 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_k}$$

**Step 5 — For convergence in *all* directions, the bound must hold for the worst case (largest eigenvalue):**

$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip:** Since $\lambda_{max} \le \text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$, a *conservative* bound is $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})}$, which is often used in real-time biomedical implementations where $\lambda_{max}$ is unknown.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Adaptive Algorithms

| Property | LMS | NLMS | RLS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Update Complexity** | $O(N)$ | $O(N)$ | $O(N^2)$ |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow (eigenvalue-dependent) | Faster (normalized) | Very fast |
| **Step-Size** | Fixed $\mu$ | Normalized $\mu$ | Implicit |
| **Stability Risk** | Sensitive to $\mu$ | More robust | Risk of divergence |
| **KTU Use Case** | ECG noise cancellation | Speech enhancement | High-end patient monitors |
| **Misadjustment** | High (noisy gradient) | Moderate | Low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Adaptive Filter — Top-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x of n] --> B[Adaptive FIR Filter W of n]
    D[Desired Signal d of n] --> F[Error Computation]
    B --> E[Filter Output y of n]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Error Signal e of n]
    G --> H[Adaptive Algorithm LMS NLM RLS]
    H --> B
    F --> J[Minimized MSE Output]
    G --> J
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Explanation:**
> - **A** is the primary input (e.g., noisy ECG from electrode).
> - **B** is the digital filter with adaptive tap weights.
> - **D** is the reference/clean signal (or a delayed copy).
> - **E** is the estimated output.
> - **F** performs subtraction to produce the error $e(n)$.
> - **H** processes the error and updates $\mathbf{w}(n)$ for the next iteration.

---

### 4.2 Four Canonical Adaptive Filter Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Config1[Configuration 1 System Identification]
        S1[Unknown Plant h of n] --> Y1[Output y of n]
        X1[Input x of n] --> S1
        X1 --> AF1[Adaptive Filter w of n]
        AF1 --> Y1h[Estimate y_hat of n]
        Y1 --> SUB1[Subtractor]
        Y1h --> SUB1
        SUB1 --> E1[Error e of n drives adaptation]
    end

    subgraph Config2[Configuration 2 Noise Cancellation]
        S2[Correlated Noise n of n] --> D2[Primary d of n Signal Plus Noise]
        X2[Noise Reference x of n] --> AF2[Adaptive Filter w of n]
        AF2 --> YH2[Anti Noise y of n]
        D2 --> SUB2[Subtractor]
        YH2 --> SUB2
        SUB2 --> E2[Clean Signal e of n]
    end

    subgraph Config3[Configuration 3 Prediction]
        XS3[Input x of n] --> AF3[Adaptive Filter w of n]
        AF3 --> YP3[Predicted x of n plus 1]
        XS3 --> DLY3[Delay Block z to minus 1]
        DLY3 --> D3[Desired d of n equals x of n]
    end

    subgraph Config4[Configuration 4 Adaptive Line Enhancer]
        XS4[Input x of n] --> DLY4[Delay Delta]
        DLY4 --> AF4[Adaptive Filter w of n]
        AF4 --> YL4[Output y of n]
        DLY4 --> DS4[Desired d of n]
        YL4 --> SUB4[Subtractor]
        DS4 --> SUB4
        SUB4 --> E4[Error e of n]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic — Mapping Biomedical Applications to Configurations:**
> | Configuration | Biomedical Application |
> |---|---|
> | System Identification | Modelling the electrode-skin interface |
> | Noise Cancellation | Removing 50/60 Hz mains interference from ECG |
> | Prediction | Fetal ECG extraction using maternal abdominal ECG |
> | Adaptive Line Enhancer | Detecting repetitive QRS complexes buried in noise |

---

### 4.3 LMS Algorithm — Sequential Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start at n equals 0] --> A1[Initialize w of 0 equals zero vector]
    A1 --> A2[Choose step size mu within stability bound]
    A2 --> B1[Acquire input sample x of n]
    B1 --> B2[Acquire desired sample d of n]
    B2 --> B3[Compute output y of n equals w transpose n times x of n]
    B3 --> B4[Compute error e of n equals d of n minus y of n]
    B4 --> B5[Update weights w of n plus 1 equals w of n plus 2 mu e of n x of n]
    B5 --> B6[Increment n to n plus 1]
    B6 --> B1
```

---

### 4.4 MSE Error Performance Surface (2-D Pictorial Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Surf[Paraboloid Error Surface in Weight Space]
        P1[Weight Axis w1] --- P2[Weight Axis w2]
    end
    O[Origin w equals zero] -->|Gradient Descent Path| Opt[Optimum w_opt at Bottom of Bowl]
    Surf -.minimum.-> Opt
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Intuition:** The MSE surface is a paraboloid. The LMS algorithm performs *stochastic gradient descent*, taking noisy zig-zag steps that spiral down toward the bowl's minimum at $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$. The step-size $\mu$ controls the *step length* — small $\mu$ means slow, stable descent; large $\mu$ means fast but oscillatory descent.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define an **adaptive filter**. How does it differ from a fixed (Wiener) filter?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An adaptive filter is a self-designing digital filter whose coefficients are automatically adjusted by an algorithm to minimize a chosen error criterion (typically MSE) in a time-varying environment. It differs from a fixed Wiener filter in two aspects:
1. **Non-stationarity:** The adaptive filter tracks statistical changes in the input, whereas the fixed filter requires wide-sense stationarity.
2. **Online Learning:** The adaptive filter updates its weights recursively using the error signal, while the fixed filter requires prior computation of the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ and cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p}$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Write the **LMS weight update equation** and explain the role of the step-size parameter $\mu$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The LMS update equation is:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

The parameter $\mu$ (step size) controls the *rate* and *stability* of convergence:
- A **small $\mu$** ensures stability but leads to *slow* convergence.
- A **large $\mu$** speeds up convergence but may cause *oscillations or divergence*.
- The stability bound is $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$, where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** *(Module 4, Adaptive Filtering)*

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of an adaptive filter**. Derive the **LMS algorithm** from the steepest-descent method and discuss its **stability condition**.

**(a) Block Diagram and Operating Principle — 7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Draw the block diagram** as in Section 4.1 of these notes, labelling:
   - Input $x(n)$, desired $d(n)$, output $y(n)$, error $e(n)$.
   - Adaptive filter block $\mathbf{w}(n)$ and the adaptive algorithm block.

2. **Principle of Operation (5 key steps):**
   - **Step 1:** Sample the input $x(n)$ and the desired signal $d(n)$ at instant $n$.
   - **Step 2:** Compute the filter output using the current weights: $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
   - **Step 3:** Form the error: $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
   - **Step 4:** Update the weight vector: $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
   - **Step 5:** Increment $n$ and repeat. The algorithm converges when $E[e^2(n)]$ reaches its minimum.

3. **Engineering Justification (1 mark):** Mention that the algorithm uses the *error* as feedback, mimicking biological learning — the filter "learns" by minimizing the discrepancy between actual and desired outputs.

**(b) Derivation of LMS and Stability — 7 Marks**

**Model Solution (follow Section 3.3 of these notes):**

1. **Start from the steepest-descent update:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\nabla J(n)$ ... (1 mark)
2. **Express the true gradient:** $\nabla J(n) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n) - 2\mathbf{p} = -2 E[e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$ ... (1 mark)
3. **Approximate expectation by instantaneous sample:** $\hat{\nabla}J(n) = -2 e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ ... (1 mark)
4. **Substitute and simplify:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ ... (1 mark)
5. **Stability analysis** (Section 3.4): express the convergence along eigen-modes and obtain the bound $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$ ... (2 marks)
6. **Conclude** with a remark on practical choice of $\mu$ and mention NLMS/RLS as variants ... (1 mark)

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]** Explain the **four canonical configurations** of adaptive filters with diagrams. For each, give **one biomedical application**. Derive the **Wiener-Hopf equation** for the optimal filter weights.

**(a) Four Canonical Configurations — 7 Marks**

**Model Solution:**

| Configuration | Block Description | Biomedical Application (1 mark each) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. System Identification** | Adaptive filter models an *unknown plant*; input drives both. | Modelling the electrode-skin impedance transfer function. |
| **2. Noise Cancellation** | Reference noise is filtered and subtracted from primary. | Removing 50/60 Hz mains hum from ECG recordings. |
| **3. Prediction** | Filter predicts a future sample from past samples. | Predicting the next QRS complex for arrhythmia detection. |
| **4. Adaptive Line Enhancer (ALE)** | Input is *delayed* and decorrelated from noise. | Extracting periodic fetal ECG from maternal abdominal signal. |

*Draw a single block diagram* (replicating Section 4.2 of these notes) showing all four configurations. Award full marks for clear labelling of the signal paths.

**(b) Derivation of the Wiener-Hopf Equation — 7 Marks**

**Model Solution (follow Section 3.2 of these notes):**

1. **Define MSE:** $J(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^2(n)]$ ... (1 mark)
2. **Expand** using $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$ to get the quadratic form: $J = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ ... (2 marks)
3. **Differentiate** with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ using matrix calculus (cite the identity for symmetric $\mathbf{R}$): $\nabla J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ ... (1 mark)
4. **Set $\nabla J = 0$** at the optimum: $2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = 2\mathbf{p}$ ... (1 mark)
5. **Solve:** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ ... (1 mark)
6. **Compute the minimum MSE:** $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ ... (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose** in the cross-correlation term — students often write $-2\mathbf{w}\mathbf{p}$ instead of $-2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p}$. Dimensions must match: $\mathbf{w}^T$ is $1 \times N$, $\mathbf{p}$ is $N \times 1$, producing a scalar.
> 2. **Misstating the stability bound** — the bound is $\mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$, **not** $\mu < 1$. Many students confuse the two and lose a mark.
> 3. **Confusing the gradient with the error** — the LMS update uses the *instantaneous* gradient estimate $\hat{\nabla}J = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$, not the true gradient $\nabla J = -2E[e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$. This distinction is a KTU favorite.
> 4. **Omitting the expectation operator** in $J(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^2(n)]$ — examiners specifically check for the expectation symbol, which is the foundation of the entire Wiener theory.
> 5. **Mixing up configurations** — Noise Cancellation uses a *separate reference* input, while Prediction uses a *delayed version* of the same input. This is the most commonly confused pair in the question paper.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Adaptive Filter Definition:** Self-adjusting digital filter; coefficients updated by an error-minimization algorithm.
- **Core Signals:** $x(n)$, $d(n)$, $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$, $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
- **Cost Function:** $J(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^2(n)]$ — the MSE.
- **Quadratic Form:** $J = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$.
- **Wiener-Hopf Equation:** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ — the *theoretical* optimum.
- **Minimum MSE:** $J_{min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.
- **LMS Update:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ — Widrow-Hoff rule.
- **LMS Stability:** $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{max}$; conservative bound $\mu < 1/\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$.
- **Convergence Time-Constant:** $\tau_k \approx 1/(2\mu\lambda_k)$ — slowest along smallest $\lambda_k$.
- **Configurations:** System Identification, Noise Cancellation, Prediction, Adaptive Line Enhancer.
- **Biomedical Applications:** 50/60 Hz ECG notch, fetal ECG extraction, QRS prediction, hearing aids, EEG artifact removal.
- **Algorithm Variants:** LMS ($O(N)$, slow), NLMS ($O(N)$, normalized), RLS ($O(N^2)$, fast).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Adaptive Noise Cancellation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT501 - BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principle of Adaptive Noise Cancellation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Noise Cancellation (ANC)** is a signal processing technique in which an **adaptive filter** is used to cancel an unknown interference (noise) that is **correlated with a reference noise signal**, by producing an anti-noise estimate that is subtracted from the primary (corrupted) input to recover the desired signal.

Mathematically, the principle rests on the assumption that two signals are available at the receiver:

1. **Primary Input** $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ — the desired biomedical signal $s(n)$ corrupted by an additive noise $n_0(n)$.
2. **Reference Input** $x(n) = n_1(n)$ — a noise signal that is **correlated** with $n_0(n)$ but **uncorrelated** with $s(n)$.

The adaptive filter processes $x(n)$ to produce an output $y(n)$ that is an estimate of the corrupting noise $n_0(n)$. This estimate is subtracted from the primary input, yielding the system output (error signal):

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = s(n) + \big[n_0(n) - y(n)\big] \approx s(n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The adaptive filter is adjusted by an algorithm (typically **LMS — Least Mean Squares** or **NLMS — Normalized LMS**) to **minimize the Mean Square Error (MSE)** $E\{e^2(n)\}$. Because $s(n)$ is uncorrelated with $n_0(n)$ and $n_1(n)$, minimizing $E\{e^2(n)\}$ effectively cancels the noise component while preserving the signal.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Anti-Noise Headphone"**

Imagine you are trying to listen to a soft heart-monitor beep in a noisy ICU. The room has a strong **50 Hz mains hum** (from power lines) and a constant **ventilator whirring**.

- You wear **active noise-cancelling headphones** that have **two microphones**: one inside the cup near your ear (catches *beep + hum*) and one outside the cup (catches *hum only*).
- A tiny processor **listens** to the outside hum and generates a sound that is its **exact mirror image** (180° out of phase).
- When played through the speaker, this *anti-hum* **destroys** the inside hum by destructive interference — you hear only the clean beep.

In ANC terminology:

| Headphone Element | ANC Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sound near your ear (beep + hum) | Primary input $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ |
| Sound outside the cup (hum) | Reference input $x(n) = n_1(n)$ |
| Speaker generating anti-hum | Adaptive filter output $y(n)$ |
| Clean beep you hear | System output $e(n) \approx s(n)$ |
| Processor adjusting speaker | LMS weight-update algorithm |

The beauty is that the processor **automatically learns** the right "anti-hum" — it doesn't need to know the hum's exact waveform in advance. This *learning* is what makes the system **adaptive**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** typically **250 Hz to 1000 Hz** for ECG; **50 kHz to 200 kHz** for EMG.
- **Powerline Interference Frequency:** **50 Hz** (India/Europe) or **60 Hz** (USA) — the most commonly cancelled noise in biomedical ANC.
- **Filter Order ($N$):** typically **N = 8 to 64** taps for ECG noise cancellation.
- **Step Size ($\mu$):** **0 < μ < 1/λ_max** for LMS convergence stability.
- **Convergence Rate:** Time constant $\tau \approx \frac{1}{2\mu\sigma_x^2}$ where $\sigma_x^2$ is the reference input power.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does ANC work? The "Why" Behind the Math**

The genius of ANC lies in exploiting **two fundamental statistical properties**:

1. The signal $s(n)$ and the noise $n_0(n)$ are **uncorrelated** (statistically independent).
2. The reference noise $n_1(n)$ is **correlated** with the primary noise $n_0(n)$ (both arise from the same physical noise source).

> Therefore, the **minimum mean-square error** solution will try to cancel the part of $d(n)$ that is correlated with $x(n)$ (i.e., the noise) — because $s(n)$ contributes nothing to that correlation. The signal $s(n)$ is "invisible" to the adaptive filter.

**Operational Pipeline:**

- **Step 1 — Primary Acquisition:** Sensor (e.g., ECG electrode) captures $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$.
- **Step 2 — Reference Acquisition:** A second sensor placed *near the noise source* (but far from the signal source) captures $x(n) = n_1(n)$.
- **Step 3 — Adaptive Filtering:** An FIR adaptive filter with weight vector $\mathbf{w}(n) = [w_0(n), w_1(n), \ldots, w_{N-1}(n)]^T$ processes $x(n)$:

$$y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n)\,x(n-k)$$

- **Step 4 — Error Formation:** The system output is the difference between primary input and filter output:

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$$

- **Step 5 — Weight Update (LMS):** The filter weights are updated to minimize the instantaneous squared error $e^2(n)$:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

- **Step 6 — Convergence:** After sufficient iterations, $y(n) \to n_0(n)$ and $e(n) \to s(n)$ — the noise is cancelled and the clean signal emerges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight — Why Two Inputs?**
> Classical noise filters (Wiener, Kalman) need *a priori* knowledge of signal and noise statistics. ANC's two-input structure **automatically discovers** the noise statistics through the reference channel, making it ideal for *non-stationary* biomedical noise (e.g., time-varying electrode motion artifacts).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Primary input | $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ | Signal corrupted with noise |
| 2 | Reference input | $x(n) = n_1(n)$ | Noise correlated with $n_0(n)$ |
| 3 | Filter output | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Adaptive FIR output |
| 4 | System output (error) | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Recovered signal estimate |
| 5 | LMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Iterative weight adaptation |
| 6 | NLMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \dfrac{\mu}{\varepsilon + \mathbf{x}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)}\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Normalized for varying input power |
| 7 | Convergence bound (LMS) | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$ | Stability condition |
| 8 | MSE objective | $\xi(n) = E\{e^2(n)\}$ | Cost function minimized |
| 9 | Excess MSE (steady state) | $\xi_{ex} = \mu\,\xi_{\min}\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | Misadjustance from optimum |
| 10 | Time constant (LMS) | $\tau_k = \dfrac{1}{2\mu\lambda_k}$ | Convergence rate for mode $k$ |
| 11 | Optimal Wiener solution | $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}$ | Best MSE weights |
| 12 | Autocorrelation matrix | $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$ | Reference input correlation |
| 13 | Cross-correlation vector | $\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$ | Primary-reference cross-corr |
| 14 | Minimum MSE | $\xi_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | Achievable lower bound |

> **Notation:** $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$; $\lambda_k$ is the $k^{th}$ eigenvalue; $\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ is the matrix trace; $\sigma_d^2$ is the variance of $d(n)$; $\varepsilon$ is a small positive constant to avoid division by zero.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the LMS Weight-Update Equation for ANC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Goal:** Starting from the cost function $\xi = E\{e^2(n)\}$, derive the steepest-descent update and its LMS approximation used in ANC.

---

**Step 1 — Express the error signal.**

The error at sample $n$ is the difference between the primary input and the adaptive filter's output:

$$
e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{w}(n) = [w_0(n), w_1(n), \ldots, w_{N-1}(n)]^T$ is the tap-weight vector of an $N$-tap FIR filter.
- $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-N+1)]^T$ is the reference input vector.

**Step 2 — Formulate the Mean-Square Error (MSE) cost function.**

The objective is to minimize the expected squared error:

$$
\xi(n) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\}
$$

Expanding using linearity of expectation:

$$
\xi(n) = E\{d^2(n)\} - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T(n)\,E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\} + \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}\,\mathbf{w}(n)
$$

Defining:
- $\sigma_d^2 = E\{d^2(n)\}$ — variance of the primary input.
- $\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$ — cross-correlation vector.
- $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$ — autocorrelation matrix.

we obtain the **quadratic performance surface**:

$$
\xi(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\,\mathbf{w}^T\,\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the gradient of $\xi$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$.**

Taking the gradient (vector derivative) of the quadratic form:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\,\xi = \frac{\partial \xi}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2\,\mathbf{p} + 2\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}
$$

**Step 4 — Find the optimal Wiener solution.**

Setting the gradient to zero for the optimum:

$$
-2\,\mathbf{p} + 2\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_{opt} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}
$$

Substituting back to get the **minimum MSE**:

$$
\xi_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\,\mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}
$$

**Step 5 — Steepest-descent iterative update.**

To avoid the computational cost of inverting $\mathbf{R}$, the LMS algorithm uses gradient descent:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\,\xi(n)
$$

Substituting the true gradient:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}(n)]
$$

**Step 6 — LMS approximation using instantaneous estimates.**

Since exact $\mathbf{p}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ are unknown, LMS uses instantaneous (stochastic) estimates:

- $\hat{\mathbf{R}} = \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)$
- $\hat{\mathbf{p}} = d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$

Plugging in the instantaneous gradient $\hat{\nabla} = -2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ (because $\frac{\partial e^2}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2e\,\frac{\partial e}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2e\,\mathbf{x}$):

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}
$$

This is the **LMS algorithm** that powers the ANC system. Each new sample $n$ brings a fresh $e(n)$ and a new $\mathbf{x}(n)$, and the weights adapt to track the optimal Wiener solution.

---

### 3.2 Convergence Analysis of the LMS ANC

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Goal:** Determine the conditions on $\mu$ for stable convergence.

**Step 1 — Define the weight-error vector.**

$$
\Delta \mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mathbf{w}_{opt}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the LMS update.**

Since $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ satisfies $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}$, and using $e(n) = n_0(n) - \mathbf{x}^T(n)\Delta\mathbf{w}(n) + s(n)$, the expected dynamics of $\Delta \mathbf{w}$ are governed by:

$$
E\{\Delta \mathbf{w}(n+1)\} = (\mathbf{I} - 2\mu\mathbf{R})\,E\{\Delta \mathbf{w}(n)\}
$$

**Step 3 — Diagonalize via eigen-decomposition.**

Let $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{Q}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^T$ with eigenvalues $\lambda_0, \lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_{N-1}$. Defining modal coordinates $\mathbf{v}(n) = \mathbf{Q}^T \Delta \mathbf{w}(n)$:

$$
v_k(n+1) = (1 - 2\mu\lambda_k)\,v_k(n)
$$

**Step 4 — Stability condition.**

For all modes to decay:

$$
|1 - 2\mu\lambda_k| < 1 \quad \forall k
$$

This requires:

$$
\boxed{0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Time constant of each mode.**

$$
\tau_k = \frac{1}{2\mu\lambda_k}
$$

The slowest mode corresponds to $\lambda_{\min}$, so the **overall settling time** is approximately $T_s \approx \frac{N}{2\mu\,\lambda_{\min}}$ for an $N$-tap filter.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example — LMS-ANC for 50 Hz Mains Hum Removal

**Problem Setup:** ECG signal sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz is corrupted with 50 Hz powerline interference. Use an ANC system with $N = 2$ taps and $\mu = 0.05$. Initial weights: $\mathbf{w}(0) = [0, 0]^T$. Compute the weight update for the first 3 samples given:

- $d(0)=1.02, \; d(1)=1.05, \; d(2)=0.98$ (primary inputs)
- $x(0)=\sin(2\pi\cdot 50/500)=\sin(0.6283)=0.5878$
- $x(1)=\sin(2\pi\cdot 100/500)=\sin(1.2566)=0.9511$
- $x(2)=\sin(2\pi\cdot 150/500)=\sin(1.8850)=0.9511$

**Iteration $n=0$:**

Filter output:
$$
y(0) = w_0(0)\cdot x(0) + w_1(0)\cdot x(-1) = 0
$$

Error:
$$
e(0) = d(0) - y(0) = 1.02
$$

Weight update (with $2\mu = 0.1$):
$$
w_0(1) = 0 + 0.1 \times 1.02 \times 0.5878 = 0.0600
$$
$$
w_1(1) = 0 + 0.1 \times 1.02 \times 0 = 0
$$

**Iteration $n=1$:**

Filter output:
$$
y(1) = w_0(1)\cdot x(1) + w_1(1)\cdot x(0) = 0.0600 \times 0.9511 + 0 = 0.0571
$$

Error:
$$
e(1) = 1.05 - 0.0571 = 0.9929
$$

Weight update:
$$
w_0(2) = 0.0600 + 0.1 \times 0.9929 \times 0.9511 = 0.0600 + 0.0944 = 0.1544
$$
$$
w_1(2) = 0 + 0.1 \times 0.9929 \times 0.5878 = 0.0584
$$

**Iteration $n=2$:**

Filter output:
$$
y(2) = 0.1544 \times 0.9511 + 0.0584 \times 0.5878 = 0.1468 + 0.0343 = 0.1811
$$

Error:
$$
e(2) = 0.98 - 0.1811 = 0.7989
$$

Weight update:
$$
w_0(3) = 0.1544 + 0.1 \times 0.7989 \times 0.9511 = 0.1544 + 0.0760 = 0.2304
$$
$$
w_1(3) = 0.0584 + 0.1 \times 0.7989 \times 0.5878 = 0.0584 + 0.0470 = 0.1054
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The error $e(n)$ is decreasing ($1.02 \to 0.9929 \to 0.7989$) and the weights are growing — the filter is **learning** to mimic the 50 Hz interference so it can subtract it out. After sufficient iterations, $e(n)$ will converge to the clean ECG sample.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Adaptive Noise Canceller

The Mermaid diagram below depicts the canonical ANC architecture, mapping all signal paths, the adaptive filter, and the feedback loop used for weight adaptation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC_S["DESIRED SIGNAL s(n)"] --> SUMNODE["SUMMING JUNCTION +"]
    NOISE_N0["PRIMARY NOISE n0(n)"] --> SUMNODE
    SUMNODE --> PRIM_OUT["PRIMARY INPUT d(n) = s(n) + n0(n)"]
    NOISE_N0 -. CORRELATED PATH .-> NOISE_N1["REFERENCE NOISE n1(n)"]
    NOISE_N1 --> REF_IN["REFERENCE INPUT x(n)"]
    REF_IN --> ADAPT_F["ADAPTIVE FIR FILTER w0...wN-1"]
    ADAPT_F --> FILT_OUT["FILTER OUTPUT y(n) = wT x(n)"]
    PRIM_OUT --> SUB_NODE["SUMMING JUNCTION -"]
    FILT_OUT --> SUB_NODE
    SUB_NODE --> SYS_ERR["SYSTEM OUTPUT e(n) = d(n) - y(n)"]
    SYS_ERR --> FB1["FEEDBACK PATH"]
    SYS_ERR --> OUT_PORT["RECOVERED SIGNAL approx s(n)"]
    REF_IN --> FB2["FEEDBACK PATH"]
    FB1 --> LMS_ENG["LMS ALGORITHM ENGINE"]
    FB2 --> LMS_ENG
    LMS_ENG -. UPDATED WEIGHTS .-> ADAPT_F
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **solid arrows** show the **forward signal flow**.
- The **dotted arrow** from $n_0(n)$ to $n_1(n)$ represents the **physical correlation** between the two noise measurements (e.g., the same 50 Hz source picked up by two electrodes).
- The **feedback arrows** from $e(n)$ and $x(n)$ close the LMS adaptation loop, continuously retuning the filter weights.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — ANC Applied to ECG Powerline Interference Cancellation

```mermaid
graph TD
    ECG_SRC["ECG SOURCE s(n)"] --> ADDER1["SUMMING NODE +"]
    POWERLINE["50 HZ MAINS n0(n)"] --> ADDER1
    ADDER1 --> ECG_ELECTRODE["PRIMARY ECG ELECTRODE d(n)"]
    POWERLINE -. SAME MAINS .-> AUX_ELEC["AUXILIARY ELECTRODE x(n)"]
    AUX_ELEC --> NLMS_BLOCK["ADAPTIVE FILTER NLMS ALGORITHM"]
    NLMS_BLOCK --> EST_NOISE["ESTIMATED 50 HZ NOISE y(n)"]
    ECG_ELECTRODE --> SUB["SUMMING NODE -"]
    EST_NOISE --> SUB
    SUB --> CLEAN_ECG["CLEAN ECG OUTPUT e(n)"]
    CLEAN_ECG --> FEEDBACK1["FEEDBACK TO LMS"]
    AUX_ELEC --> FEEDBACK2["FEEDBACK TO LMS"]
    FEEDBACK1 --> NLMS_BLOCK
    FEEDBACK2 --> NLMS_BLOCK
```

**Engineering interpretation:** The primary electrode sits on the patient's chest and picks up the ECG plus 50 Hz hum. The auxiliary electrode is placed *near* the power cable (or on the patient's right leg, which is a standard driven-right-leg configuration) to capture a "clean" hum reference. The adaptive filter synthesizes an anti-hum that is subtracted to reveal a clean ECG.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Signal Flow Stages

```mermaid
graph LR
    STAGE1["STAGE 1: ACQUISITION d(n), x(n)"] --> STAGE2["STAGE 2: FILTERING y(n) = wT x(n)"]
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3["STAGE 3: SUBTRACTION e(n) = d(n) - y(n)"]
    STAGE3 --> STAGE4["STAGE 4: COST COMPUTATION J = e2(n)"]
    STAGE4 --> STAGE5["STAGE 5: GRADIENT ESTIMATION"]
    STAGE5 --> STAGE6["STAGE 6: WEIGHT UPDATE w(n+1)"]
    STAGE6 -. UPDATED w .-> STAGE2
    STAGE3 --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT: e(n) approx s(n)"]
```

**Each stage in plain English:**

- **Stage 1:** Sample the two input channels using synchronized ADCs.
- **Stage 2:** Convolve the reference signal with current filter weights.
- **Stage 3:** Form the instantaneous error (the cleaned output).
- **Stage 4:** Compute the squared error — the cost the system wants to minimize.
- **Stage 5:** Estimate the gradient using the LMS approximation $-2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- **Stage 6:** Update weights and loop back to Stage 2 with the new filter.
